actions.thought: T9o from UTG is a marginal hand. In 6-max with 100bb stacks, UTG requires a tight opening range. T9o is too weak to open from this position - it plays poorly against the ranges that will call/3-bet me, and I have 5 players left to act. Standard fold.

I have 9♣6♠ in middle position in a 6-handed 50/100 game. The pot is 150 and it costs me 100 to see the flop. 9-6 offsuit is a weak, poorly connected hand with negative implied odds and is difficult to play out of position against the cutoff, button and the blinds. Calling 100 here gives poor equity relative to the cost and leaves me vulnerable to raises and tough postflop decisions. Raising to 200 to try to steal would be irresponsible with this holding and deep stacks — I should conserve chips for better spots early in the tournament. Fold and wait for a stronger hand or better position.

We’re in the cutoff facing an unopened pot (no raise yet), but 7h3c is one of the weakest hands in hold’em. Limping creates multiway pots where we’re dominated and rarely realize equity well, and raising would be pure bluff with poor postflop playability and no relevant blockers. Early in a deep-stacked 6-max tournament, preserving chips and keeping our range strong in late position is best.

I am on the Button with Kh 4s. The action has folded to me. While the Button allows for a wide opening range (typically 40-50%), K4 offsuit is generally considered too weak to open profitably, even in an attempt to steal the blinds. It lacks playability and has poor equity against calling ranges. With this being the first hand and having no reads on the blinds to justify a wider steal, the standard GTO play is to fold.

Everyone folded to me in the small blind. Kd 6d is a strong suited hand in a heads-up situation against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to try to take the blinds or play the hand with the initiative if called.

Early in the tournament (hand 1), facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the SB after all previous players folded. My hand is Q9o, offsuit with mediocre high card strength and poor playability out of position postflop. Pot odds are 200 to call into 600 (33% needed), but equity against even a somewhat wide SB iso-range is likely around 35-40% at best, and postflop OOP dynamics favor folding. Deep stacks (99BB), no need to defend wide here. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

Holding Jd3s UTG in a 6-max game is far too weak to continue. From early position I should be opening a tight range; J3o is easily dominated, has poor post‑flop playability and gives me little fold equity or blocking value. Calling 100 (limping) is passive and invites raises/squeezes out of position, while raising would be an unnecessary bluff with poor expected value. Preserve the stack and fold here.

Preflop is unopened (only blinds in), and 3c4c is a very low suited connector that realizes equity poorly from middle position—often ends up making weak pairs or dominated flushes/straights and plays tough vs the stronger ranges behind (CO/BTN) and the blinds. With 100bb effective and no need to take thin, low-EV opens/limps this early, the best stack-maximizing line is to pass and wait for better spots/position.

In the Cutoff with A6o, this is a borderline hand. Standard 6-max opening ranges often fold A2o-A8o from the Cutoff or mix them at a low frequency, preferring suited Aces or better offsuit Aces (A9o+). With 100BB deep stacks early in the tournament (Hand 2), there is no pressure to steal blinds desperately. Opening opens me up to 3-bets from the Button or Blinds, or domination issues post-flop. I will take a conservative line and fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Eight offsuit. While the button is an ideal position to steal the blinds, J8o is slightly too weak for a standard opening range in a 6-max game, typically sitting just outside the bottom of the recommended 40-45% opening range. Given we are only on the second hand of the tournament and stacks are deep (100BB), I will opt for a disciplined fold rather than risking chips with a hand that has poor playability post-flop.

Folded to me in the small blind with Qc7s offsuit. This is a standard spot in SB vs BB where completing is profitable due to excellent pot odds (pot 150 to call 50 more, ~3:1). Q7o has decent queen-high showdown value and blocks some of BB's strong hands. Raising risks committing more OOP with a marginal hand, and folding is too tight early in the tournament with deep stacks. Postflop, we'll have implied odds if we hit, and can fold to aggression. Prefer seeing a cheap flop heads-up.

I have Ac8c in the BB against a single limper from SB. This is a solid suited ace that plays well heads up. I should raise to build the pot and potentially take it down now. A raise to 300-400 is standard here to punish the limp and get value from my better-than-average hand.

Preflop in SB, completed with Q7o for pot odds and queen-high value heads-up OOP. However, BB 3x raise to 350 shows strength, likely a good hand or good BB defending range. Q7o is marginal offsuit, poor playability postflop OOP vs raise. Deep stacks early tournament; no need to continue with weak hand facing aggression. Folding preserves stack, avoids difficult spots. Standard fold to BB raise after SB limp.

6-max UTG is relatively early but not as tight as full ring; T8s has good playability (can flop strong draws and make nutted-ish straights/flushes) and benefits from taking initiative. With stacks ~100bb, I prefer opening rather than limping to avoid going multiway and to realize equity with position disadvantage. I’ll use a solid 3x sizing consistent with the table’s early opens to pressure blinds and discourage loose flats; if faced with a sizeable 3-bet from tight players, I can comfortably fold.

I hold 9d 7d in Middle Position facing a 3bb (300) raise from the UTG player (FiveTwo). A suited one-gapper like 97s has speculative value, but calling a 3x open from UTG is generally -EV, especially with four players left to act behind me who could squeeze or overcall, leaving me out of position or facing a re-raise. The UTG range is typically strong, and the price (calling 300 to win 450) offers poor immediate odds given the reverse implied odds and lack of position. The most disciplined action is to fold.

FiveTwo has raised from UTG, which usually indicates a strong range. My hand, Q4s, is weak and likely dominated by most of FiveTwo's opening range. Additionally, there are three players left to act behind me who could potentially three-bet or overcall. To preserve my stack early in the tournament, I will fold this speculative hand.

6d9s is an offsuit, unconnected hand with poor playability. Facing a standard 3x open from UTG (early position), who has a strong range including pairs, broadway, and strong aces. Even on the button with position postflop, calling 300 into a 450 pot offers insufficient equity and implied odds against this range. Raising or 3-betting is not justified due to frequent domination. Folding preserves our deep stack for better spots in this early stage of the tournament.
74o is a trash hand. Facing a UTG raise (typically strong range), I'm out of position with one of the worst hands in poker. Easy fold.

I’m in the BB with A♠Q♥ facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 200) and effective stacks ~99BB. AQs is a strong hand here and I don’t want to play a big pot out-of-position by just calling. A min-re-raise to 500 is too small to accomplish isolation or fold equity. I’ll 3-bet to 900 (≈3x the open): this takes the initiative, denies cheap flops, gets value from worse aces/broadways, and keeps the postflop stack-to-pot ratio reasonable for maneuvering. If FiveTwo 4-bets very large (near a shove) I’ll fold; vs a smaller 4-bet sizing I’ll reassess, but my default is to isolate here with a standard 3-bet.

Opened UTG with T8s, but facing a BB 3-bet to 900 our opponent’s range is typically strong and fairly linear (broadways/pairs). Calling would put us out of position with a dominated, non-nutted suited connector that realizes equity poorly; 4-bet bluffing is also unattractive without blockers and risks burning chips early. Best EV is to conserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with Jd 3s, which is a very weak off-suit hand. Opening from early position requires a strong range because there are many players left to act behind me. J3o is not playable from this position. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Ace-Five offsuit is a marginal hand that is generally below the standard opening range for middle position in a 6-max game. While it has an Ace blocker, its post-flop playability is poor and it is frequently dominated by better Aces. Given the aggressive 3-betting seen in previous hands, folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack for better spots.

Pocket fours in cutoff with an unopened pot after two early folds. This is a standard raising spot in late position. 44 plays well post-flop: set mining potential, decent equity vs calling ranges, and good fold equity against blinds. Table seems aggressive from prior hands, but deep stacks (98BB) allow for it. Raising to 3x BB (300) balances stealing blinds and getting value. Min raise is 200, but larger size builds pot and denies equity.
63 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability. Facing a raise from the cutoff, this is an easy fold even from the button. No need to get involved with such a marginal holding.

I have K♠? (Qc Kh — KQ offsuit) in the small blind facing a cutoff open to 300. This is a strong, playable broadway hand that performs well isolating an open and taking the initiative. Because I’m short of position I want to price out draws and weaker callers and seize the lead; a 3x-style reraise is standard here. I’ll raise to 900 total — large enough to generate fold equity and isolate Elon while keeping the pot manageable relative to my 10,300 stack. If I face a large 4-bet I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold), but if called I plan to c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously out of position on dry or A-high/Q-high boards.

SB’s 3-bet versus a CO open is strong but not always nutted in 6-max, and AhTh is a solid suited Broadway that plays well as a defend (good equity, strong top-pair potential, nut-flush potential, and an Ace blocker). With ~95–100bb effective, I prefer not to turn this into a marginal 4-bet bluff out of position that can get shoved on and forced to fold; calling keeps ranges wider (including CO’s continuing range if he comes along) and lets us realize equity with a hand that flops playable draws/top pairs.

Pocket 4s is a speculative hand facing significant resistance: a 3-bet to 900 from aggressive Minni in SB (range: QQ+, AK, some bluffs/squeezes) and a large cold-call of 800 more (total 900) from BB FiveTwo, indicating strength (TT+, AQs+, AK). I've already invested 300; calling 600 more into 2100 pot offers ~3.5:1 but poor post-flop playability multi-way OOP—no fold equity, dominated if behind, vulnerable to overcards/set-mining risks without great implied odds vs two strong ranges. Raising lacks equity/fold equity vs these spots. Early tournament (hand 4/150), deep stacks (~95BB), fold preserves stack for superior positions. Standard fold.

Flop: 8♣ T♣ 4♣. I hold K♥Q♣ — I have a very strong semi-bluff: a high overcard plus a 4-to-a-flush (Qc + three clubs on board). Versus the BB who just called my 3-bet, his defending range contains lots of hands that miss this board (pocket pairs, offsuit broadways) and some club combinations. Betting here accomplishes three things: (1) it can steal the pot immediately from hands that missed, (2) it charges hands with single club or straight/backdoor equity, and (3) it builds the pot when I have ~9 club outs (≈31% to make a flush by river) and additional pair-up outs. I will c-bet roughly 2/3 pot to maximize fold equity but not overcommit — bet 1,400 into the 2,100 pot. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if called I re-evaluate the turn (bet for value if a club hits, check/control if a blank); if he raises big on the flop I will fold this semi-bluff (I don’t have a made hand).

This is a monotone flop where SB’s 3-bet range will c-bet at high frequency (including overpairs, big cards with a club, and some made flushes). Our hand has solid showdown value (top pair, top kicker) and we’re getting a good price: 1400 to win 4900 (~28.6%). Raising without a club is risky because we isolate ourselves versus made flushes/strong draws and can get 3-bet/jammed off our equity; calling keeps their range wide and lets us use position to react to turn sizing/texture (continue on many non-club turns, be cautious versus big barrels/club turns).

Turn: A♣ completes a club on a monotone board (8♣ T♣ 4♣ A♣). I hold K♥ Q♣ — I now have a queen‑high flush. Villain (FiveTwo) called my 3‑bet preflop and my 2/3 pot flop c‑bet, so his calling range contains a fair number of clubs, pocket pairs and AX/broadway hands. Only K♣ (King of clubs) cleanly beats me; many of his hands are worse and will pay to see value. Checking would underextract and give free information/river cards; betting gets value from worse clubs, sets and paired A hands. I want a size that charges those hands but doesn’t overcommit vs the one clear beat, so I’ll bet about 2/3 pot. Pot = 4,900, so I’ll bet 3,200. If called I’ll reassess the river and continue extracting or control the pot accordingly; if I face a very large check‑raise I’ll re‑evaluate (but against most sizes this flush is strong enough to continue).

Turn brings the 4th club, and we hold AhTh with no club. While we have top two pair, on a 4-club board any single club in Minni’s hand is already a made flush, and this line (SB 3-bet pot, c-bet monotone flop, then continue on the 4th club) is heavily weighted toward hands containing a club. Versus a flush we have only 4 outs (A or T) to boat up (~9% equity), far below the ~28% needed to call 3200 into 8100. Raising/jamming won’t generate meaningful fold equity against a range that’s mostly flushes and would just get us called when crushed, so the best stack-preserving play is to fold.

I am in the UTG position with Queen-Five of spades. While it is a suited hand, Queen-Five is far too weak to open from the earliest position in a 6-max game. It is easily dominated by the calling ranges of players behind me, and if I am 3-bet, I would be forced to fold anyway. Standard poker strategy dictates a fold here to wait for a stronger opening hand.

Preflop, middle position, UTG folded. Holding KTo offsuit, a playable hand with good high-card strength and playability in a 6-handed tournament setting with deep stacks (~95BB). Standard to open-raise for initiative and build pot with position advantage over blinds. No limpers or raises ahead. Raising to 3x BB (300) is balanced size. Folding too tight early; calling not ideal OOP.
J2o is a weak hand that doesn't play well against a middle position raise. No reason to call 300 with this trash - easy fold.

Call 300. On the button with Td8h facing an MP open to 300 (pot 450). This is a marginal but playable hand in position — decent showdown and fold equity (can hit top pair, two pair, straights) and I have a large stack advantage relative to the bet size. A 3-bet or a min‑raise here would be over-aggressive with T8o and will just invite 3-bets or multiway pots from the blinds; calling preserves position and lets me see a flop for a small portion of my stack (300 into 450). Plan: play the flop in position, fold to a large 3-bet preflop, otherwise evaluate continuation bets and textures postflop.

We’re in the SB facing a 3bb open from Elon and a flat from the Button, so we’ll be out of position vs both players in a multiway pot. Although the price (250 more) looks tempting, 5c3c suffers heavily from poor equity realization and reverse implied odds: when we make a flush it’s almost always a low flush dominated by higher club holdings in two opponents’ ranges, and our straight potential is narrow. With the BB still to act (squeeze risk) and no good way to profitably apply pressure, this is a clear discipline fold to avoid bleeding EV OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc 3s, facing a raise to 300 from Middle Position (Elon) and a call from the Button (Minni). The pot is 750, and it costs me 200 to call, offering roughly 4.75:1 odds. However, Q3 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. The potential for reverse implied odds (hitting a Queen but being dominated) and the difficulty of realizing equity with such a disconnected hand make a call unprofitable even with the attractive immediate price. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

Postflop heads-up OOP with KTo on a 6c7s4c board that completely misses my range. No pair, no draws (gutshot at best with T but unlikely). Board favors calling range from button: low connected cards, flush draws, straight draws like 53,85,65 etc. Preflop raise reps Ax, broadway, but this flop hits villain's calling range more. Deep stacks (92BB), early in tournament (hand 5/150). Best to check for pot control, fold to bet (easy fold air), or check-call light if tiny bet. No reason to c-bet bluff OOP on scary board vs aggressive button (Minni shows strength in prior hands). Check preserves stack.

Heads-up on the button with Td8h on 6c 7s 4c. I have an open‑ended straight draw (5s or 9s complete it) — 8 outs (~17% to hit the turn, ~31% by the river) plus a strong overcard (T). Elon checked OOP, which represents a wide range (overcards, medium pairs, suited connectors, some pairs checking for pot control). Betting here gets fold equity against his air and builds a pot when I have ~30% equity; a small/medium c‑bet is ideal. I'll bet ~40% pot (300 into 750) — big enough to take down the pot now and cheap enough not to overcommit with a draw. Plan: take it down if he folds; if he calls, reevaluate on turn (value/bet more on good cards, exercise caution and fold to a large check‑raise unless odds justify continuing).

Facing a small 300 bet into 1050 pot (~28% pot) on a 6c7s4c flop that completely misses my KTo (no pair, no draws, just overcards). As preflop raiser who checked first (effectively OOP postflop), villain's button calling range hits this board harder: suited connectors (e.g., 53, 85, 65), club flush draws, low pairs. My equity is marginal (~25-35% vs range), no fold equity if raising, and deep stacks early in tournament favor folding air to preserve stack. Pot odds are good (4.5:1), but implied odds poor with no hand/draw and villain's aggression in prior hands (heavy betting). Check-fold plan executed; no reason to float into tough turn spots.

UTG with Jc6c (suited jack-six) is a clear fold in a 6-handed tournament. This hand has poor playability, low equity multiway, and no business raising or limping from early position. We act first post-flop in a deep-stack spot, and folding preserves our stack for premium hands or better positions. Opponents like Minni are aggressive, but no need to get involved here.

Pocket 4s in middle position with 100BB effective stacks. Small pairs play well for set mining with deep stacks. Standard open raise to 250 to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop, or flop a set for big implied odds.

Kd4c is a marginal, dominated offsuit hand but I'm in the cutoff with position on the button and the blinds. Claude opened to 250 from MP — a small-ish raise relative to stacks — so calling to see a flop in position is the lowest-variance, highest-information play. A 3-bet here would be a pure bluff with little fold equity (and could get squeezed by the button/blinds), while folding would be too tight given the pot odds. I'll call 250 and evaluate the flop: proceed if I pair the King or have a good draw, otherwise control the pot and fold to significant aggression.

On the button with AA versus a 2.5x open and a flat call, we want to build a pot and strongly discourage going multiway (where even AA loses equity and plays tougher postflop). In position, a value 3-bet also targets dominated continues (AK/AQ, JJ-QQ, etc.) and sets up favorable stack-to-pot ratios for future streets. With an opener + caller, I size a bit larger than a heads-up 3-bet to punish the call and reduce odds for the blinds to come along.

I am in the Small Blind holding Q7s facing significant action. Claude opened from Middle Position, Minni called in the Cutoff, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 1050 from the Button. Facing a raise and a 3-bet, my hand is far too weak to continue. Q7s is easily dominated by the ranges involved, and playing a bloated pot out of position against a 3-bettor is a losing strategy. The cost to call is too high relative to the strength of my hand. I must fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2c, which is a very weak hand. Claude opened for 2.5x, Minni called, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 1050. Given the strength of the 3-bet and the fact that I would be out of position with the worst possible starting hand, folding is the only viable option.

Facing a 3-bet with pocket 4s. Need to call 800 into 1700 pot (~32% equity needed). Set mining math: ~12% to flop a set, need good implied odds. FiveTwo has ~5250 behind - if I stack them when I hit, that's adequate implied odds. Minni may also come along adding more value. Calling to set mine with clear plan to fold unimproved flops.

K♦4♣ is a weak, easily dominated offsuit hand. Facing a button 3-bet to 1050 with the original raiser (Claude) also calling, I would be out of position and in a multiway pot where my equity and fold equity are both poor. A call (800 more) is a low-expected-value play here and a light 4-bet/bluff to 1850+ is unlikely to fold out both opponents and unnecessarily risks chips early in the tournament. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Missed my set on a very coordinated J-T-9 two-spade board. This board heavily favors the preflop 3-bettor's range (broadway cards, overpairs). My pocket 4s have minimal showdown value here. As I planned preflop, I should check and fold to aggression since I didn't improve.

Heads-up in a 3-bet pot with a low SPR (~2), AA is still a strong value hand but this flop (J♠ T♠ 9♣) is extremely dynamic: lots of straight draws (KQ, Q8, 87), pair+draws, and two spades. After Claude checks, we should bet for value/protection versus hands like KK/QQ, Jx, Tx, and to charge the many draws. A larger sizing makes sense given how much equity villain’s continuing range can have; it also sets up an easy turn shove line with the current SPR.

Missed my set on a J-T-9 two-spade board. As planned when calling the 3-bet, I'm folding unimproved. This board crushes FiveTwo's 3-bet range (overpairs, broadway cards, straight draws). My 44 has almost no equity here - clear fold.
95 offsuit from UTG is a clear fold. This hand has no playability from early position - it's not connected enough, not suited, and has no high card value. Standard fold.

QcJs is a playable, broadway hand — strong enough to open from middle but not a lock for showdown. With UTG folded and four players to act (cutoff, button, SB, BB), I want to take the initiative, get some fold equity and avoid letting the pot go multiway cheaply. My stack is deep (~131 BB), so a standard 3x open is sensible: it prices in callers, preserves fold equity, and gives a clear plan post‑flop. I’ll raise to 300 total. If I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll re-evaluate; if I get callers I’ll play postflop for value and position where possible.

Minni’s 3bb open from middle position represents a fairly strong range, and JTo is too easily dominated to want a big 3-bet pot. In cutoff we have position, reasonable playability, and can realize equity without inflating the pot; if we face a squeeze behind we can comfortably fold. Flatting keeps weaker hands in and lets us outplay postflop on favorable textures.

I hold 7s 5d on the Button. Facing a raise to 300 from Minni (Middle) and a call from FiveTwo (Cutoff), the action is 300 to me. My hand (7-5 offsuit) is extremely weak and has very poor playability, especially in a multi-way pot. There is no strategic merit in calling or bluff-raising here given the strength shown by the open and call, and players remaining in the blinds. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six suited. While the suitedness adds some value, playing out of position against an open raise from Minni (Middle) and a call from FiveTwo (Cutoff) is risky. Q6s is easily dominated by many hands in their ranges (like KQ, QJ, or even higher pocket pairs). With the Big Blind still to act, there is a risk of a squeeze, and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand if I call. Given it is early in the tournament, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot.

Facing a min-raise from Minni in MP and a call from FiveTwo in CO, with dead SB money in the pot. My T8o (8s Th) is a playable hand with straight potential and decent multiway equity. Pot odds are excellent: 200 to call into a 750 pot (pot odds ~3.75:1, requiring ~21% equity). Against Minni's likely wide-aggressive MP opening range (~12-15%) and FiveTwo's calling range, T8o has sufficient equity (~25-30% multiway) and plays well postflop. OOP but deep stacks allow implied odds. Folding is too tight early in the tournament; squeezing has risk with this hand as it's not premium and OOP; calling balances range and exploits dead money.

Facing the Ks Ah 4c flop first to act in a multiway pot OOP with T8o, which has no pair, no straight draw (gutshot at best unlikely), and minimal equity against Minni's raising range (likely containing Ax, Kx, broadway) and FiveTwo's calling range. The board is dry but hits villains' ranges hard with top pair or better common. No strong draws or implied odds justify check-raising or leading. Checking allows pot control, realizes equity if checked through (unlikely), and sets up fold to aggression, preserving stack early in tournament (hand 7/150). Bluffing or value betting impossible with this hand. Folding not possible since unchecked, calling would be spew later.

Check. My Q♣J♠ is completely behind on K♠ A♥ 4♣ — no pair and only a single-card (Ten) straight draw (4 outs). The pot is multiway (FiveTwo and Elon), so my fold equity is low and a continuation bet would be thin value and likely get called by better hands (A/K, pairs, or draws). With deep stacks I prefer to control the pot and realize equity: check to see a free turn. Plan: call small bets to see the turn because of the four gutshot outs, fold to a large raise, and if I hit a T or pair proceed for value.

Both opponents checked on A-K-4 rainbow, which is a board that strongly favors the preflop raiser—so Minni’s check often caps them to medium pairs, weak Ax, or give-ups, and Elon’s check is also frequently marginal. With JTo we have a gutshot to the Q for Broadway plus some backdoor potential, and in position we can profitably take a low-risk stab to pick up the pot immediately and deny equity. Keep sizing small multiway; if we face significant resistance (call + pressure/raise), we can comfortably shut down.

Facing a flop min-bet of 300 into 1250 from FiveTwo in a multiway pot with Ks Ah 4c board and T8o (no pair, no flush draw, no strong straight draw—gutshot at best is worthless). My hand has negligible equity (~10-15% multiway vs villains' ranges heavy on Ax/Kx/broadway/pairs that hit this board hard). Pot odds ~4:1 require ~17% equity to call profitably, but even then, no implied odds justify continuing OOP with air postflop. Minni's check suggests weakness but doesn't change my hand's lack of playability. Early tournament (hand 7/150), deep stacks—preserve stack for better spots rather than spewing chips on pure float. Folding realizes I'm beat or behind ranges and avoids difficult turn decisions.

The Q♣J♠ missed K♠ A♥ 4♣, but FiveTwo’s flop bet is very small relative to the pot (300 into 1250). Calling is the prudent play: it’s cheap to see a turn where I have 4 outs to the nut straight (any Ten) plus pair-up chances, and it keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. A raise would be a thin bluff that’s likely to be called or re-raised by A/K or strong pairs, so I’ll preserve fold equity and control by calling and re-evaluating on the turn (fold to sizable turn/river aggression if I don’t improve).

Board is K♠ A♥ 4♣ 8♣ and my Q♣ J♠ has missed. I have only four clean outs (the four Tens) to make the Broadway straight (4/46 ≈ 8.7%); pairing Q or J is a marginal improvement and still often loses to any A/K or two‑pair. There is no realistic club flush possibility (even a club on the river leaves only four clubs total). FiveTwo has shown willingness to make small continuation bets and will have a lot of Ax/Kx and strong pairs in his range; a bet here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity heads‑up. With deep stacks and limited equity I should avoid bloating the pot — check and take the free card to the river. If he bets the river I’ll fold to large value bets and only call river bets that give correct pot odds; if a Ten or a clear two‑pair/strong pair hits I’ll proceed for value.

Minni’s check-call on the flop then check on the turn keeps her range weighted toward medium-strength hands (QQ–TT, some Kx, weak Ax) rather than strong value that would more often lead or check-raise. The 8c is largely a brick, so I can credibly continue representing Ax/AK/sets and apply pressure to exactly those medium pairs and Kx that hate facing a second barrel. I also retain a small amount of equity with the gutshot to Q. Use a smaller-to-mid sizing multiway-turned-heads-up to maximize fold equity without risking too much; if I face a raise, I’m done, and if called I’ll mostly shut down on blanks.

Board K♠ A♥ 4♣ 8♣, I hold Q♣J♠ — no pair but the turn 8♣ gives me a one-card club flush draw. Pot is 2150 and the bet is 600, so I’m getting 600/(2150+600) ≈ 21.8% pot odds to see the river. There are 10 clubs left (10/46 ≈ 21.7%) to complete the flush and a few additional non‑club Q/J outs, so my combined chance to improve is meaningfully above the required break‑even. Folding is too tight given the price; raising is thin and will get called or reraised by A/K and stronger holdings. I’ll call the 600 to see the river and fold to large river aggression if I miss, or proceed for value if I complete the flush (or pair up in a way that looks strong).

I hold Q♣J♠ and the river (7♦) bricks — I never paired and my club draw didn’t complete. FiveTwo was the aggressor (small flop c-bet, another bet on the turn) and got called both streets, which skews his range towards value (Kx, Ax, possibly sets or two‑pair combos) rather than pure air. With no showdown value and very limited fold equity against that line, a bluff now is thin EV: better hands will call and worse hands that might fold are not a large part of his continued betting range. Pot control is the priority — checking keeps the pot manageable, preserves a deep stack for future hands, and lets me fold to river aggression. I’ll check and concede unless he gives a very small river price that is mathematically correct to call.

River is a total brick for my hand: J-high with no pair and no completed draw, so checking back has essentially 0% chance to win. After Minni’s check-call on flop and turn, her range is often capped to bluff-catchers (QQ–TT, some Kx) plus weak Ax; sets/two-pair frequently would have raised earlier. I should follow through with a credible triple barrel that targets folding out exactly the Kx and pocket pairs, while accepting that Ax will mostly call. A medium-large sizing applies maximum pressure to those bluff-catchers without risking an overbet that needs too many folds.

Fold. I hold Q♣J♠ on K♠ A♥ 4♣ 8♣ 7♦ — I have no pair, no straight and no realistic flush; the river is the last card so I can’t improve. FiveTwo has led every street (small flop c‑bet, turn bet, now a substantial river bet), which strongly favors value hands (A/K, sets, two‑pair) over pure air. The pot is 4550 and his bet is 1800, so a call requires ~28.3% equity (1800 / (4550+1800)). With nothing to show and essentially zero showdown equity against a value‑heavy line, the opponent would need to be bluffing >28% of the time for a call to be correct — far higher than his line suggests. Raising as a bluff is even thinner and likely to get called by better hands. Preserve the stack and fold.

Fold. 5♦9♣ is a weak, offsuit, disconnected hand with very poor playability from UTG. There are five players to act behind and deep stacks, so calling 100 or opening would put me out of position with little equity and lots of reverse implied-odds against typical calling/3-betting ranges. Preserve my ~119BB stack for better spots and avoid marginal, dominated situations.

6-max, Minni has folded and we’re effectively first-in from MP. A6o is strong enough to open as a steal/initiative hand (ace blocker helps), and raising is preferable to limping given the blind pressure and positional advantage over the blinds. I’ll use a standard small open to keep risk down and maintain fold equity; if we face a sizable 3-bet from CO/BTN, we can comfortably fold.

I hold King-Queen offsuit in the Cutoff facing a standard 2.5BB open from FiveTwo in Middle Position. FiveTwo has shown significant aggression in previous hands (squeezing, triple-barreling), suggesting their opening range is likely wide enough for me to continue. While a 3-bet is an option to isolate, it risks folding out worse hands and isolating myself against dominating hands like AK, AQ, or premium pairs. Calling allows me to play a pot in position with a strong broadway hand against a wide range while controlling the pot size. I will call the 250.

I am on the button with J3 offsuit. FiveTwo raised from middle position and Pro called from the cutoff. J3o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially multi-way against established strength. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

73o is a complete trash hand with no playability, especially out of position in the small blind facing a middle position raise from the aggressive FiveTwo and a call from Pro. Pot odds are about 3.25:1 for calling 200 into 650, but the hand has zero equity against likely strong ranges, and being OOP post-flop makes it disastrous. Folding preserves chips for better spots in this early tournament stage.

K8o in the BB facing a min-raise and a call. Getting 4.3:1 pot odds (150 to win 650). This is a clear call - I'm closing the action, getting good odds, and have a playable hand with a king high. Not strong enough to 3-bet, but too good to fold at this price.

Monotone club board (Ac4cQc) and I hold K8o with no clubs. I have zero equity against any club holding and no pair. I'm out of position against two opponents. Easy check - I'll fold to any significant bet as I have no draw and no made hand.

Multiway on a monotone Ac Qc 4c board, Ad6h is top pair but vulnerable and not strong enough to bloat the pot. Still, a small c-bet performs well: it gets value from worse Ax/Qx and pocket pairs, and it charges single-club hands that have a live flush draw. By sizing small we keep our range protected while making it easy to fold if we face a raise (which will often represent a made flush or very strong equity).

I hold middle pair (Queens) and the Nut Flush Draw (Kc) on a monotone club board (Ac 4c Qc). I am facing a small continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 800 from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive. With the second nut card (King of Clubs) in my hand and the Ace of Clubs on the board, I hold the draw to the absolute nuts. Additionally, I have showdown value with my pair of Queens. Raising here would likely isolate me against sets, made flushes, or strong Aces, whereas calling allows me to realize my strong equity in position, keep the opponent's range wide, and potentially hit my flush or improve to two pair/trips on later streets. The price is excellent (getting nearly 4:1 odds), making a call the most profitable play.

Monotone club board with Ks8h - I have no clubs, no pair, just king high. Getting ~4.7:1 pot odds (300 to win 1400) needing ~18% equity. With no flush draw and no made hand against two opponents who both showed interest, I have virtually zero equity. Clear fold.

Turn pairs the 4, which slightly reduces the nutted density (flushes now worry about boats) and is a decent card to continue for a small, controlled sizing. With Ad6h (no club) we don’t want to face a big bet OOP, but we can still get value from worse Ax that peeled the small flop bet and from one-club hands that are drawing. Betting small also lets us define the hand: if we get raised on this texture it’s heavily weighted to flushes/full houses and we can comfortably fold.

On the Turn, the board is Ac 4c Qc 4s. I hold Qh Kc, giving me Two Pair (Queens and Fours) and the Nut Flush Draw (I hold the Kc with the Ac on the board). The pot is 1400 and FiveTwo bets 500, which is a small bet (approx. 1/3 pot). FiveTwo has been aggressive, so his range includes Aces, made flushes, and semi-bluffs. While my pair of Queens is likely behind an Ace or better, the 4s on the turn counterfeits my two pair strength relative to an Ace. However, the price is excellent: I am getting nearly 5:1 odds to call. With 9 outs to the nut flush and 2 outs to a full house (Queens), plus implied odds in position, calling is profitable. Raising would isolate me against a range that crushes me (Full Houses, Made Flushes, Strong Aces). I call to realize my equity.

River is a brick (2s) after Pro has called flop and turn on a monotone board that later paired. With Ad6h (no club) we have a marginal top pair/weak kicker that’s strong enough to show down but not strong enough to comfortably value-bet three streets. If we bet, worse hands that called twice (missed one-club draws) usually fold, while better Ax/slowplayed flushes/4x can call or raise and put us in a tough spot. Checking keeps the pot controlled and gives Pro a chance to bluff missed club draws; versus a bet we can then bluff-catch selectively based on sizing.

On the River, the board reads Ac 4c Qc 4s 2s. I have Queens up (QQ44A) with the Kc blocker. FiveTwo checks to me after betting the flop and turn. His range likely consists of Aces (which beat me), Flushes (which beat me), trips (4x), or bluffs/worse pairs that have given up. Given the Ace on the board, any Ace makes Aces-up which beats my Queens-up. If I bet, better hands (Ax, Flushes, 4x) will call, and worse hands will fold. There is no thin value to be had, and I have sufficient showdown value to catch bluffs or chop with another Queen (since the Ace on board neutralizes kickers for Qx). Checking back is the correct play to realize equity without risking more chips.

UTG 6-handed we still want a relatively solid opening range. Tc3h is dominated by virtually all better tens and most suited/connected hands that will continue, and it has poor postflop playability (no suitedness, no connectivity). With deep stacks there’s no need to enter marginal spots out of position; folding preserves EV and avoids tough multiway pots.

I hold Kh 2h in Middle Position. UTG has folded. K2s is a weak suited King that is generally below the opening threshold for MP in a 6-handed game. It suffers from domination issues and plays poorly post-flop compared to calling ranges. With a stack of 88 BBs early in the tournament, there is no need to loosen my opening range significantly. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Cutoff with Jack-Six offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand and does not meet the requirements for an open-raise from this position, especially with three active players behind me. Standard poker strategy dictates folding such a weak hand to preserve chips for better opportunities.

Preflop on the button with 9d6h after folds from UTG, MP, and CO. This is an offsuit, unconnected hand with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges. Even on the button with position, 96o is not strong enough for a profitable steal attempt given the deep stacks and aggressive opponents observed. Limping is suboptimal as it bloats the pot OOP for blinds. Best to fold and wait for better opportunities early in the tournament to maximize stack growth.

52o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even heads-up against just the BB, this hand plays very poorly postflop. Minni has shown aggression (3-betting in earlier hands), so completing invites a raise. Folding 50 chips is the disciplined play here.

I am in UTG position with 7d 6h (7-6 offsuit). This is a weak hand to open from the earliest position at a 6-handed table. It lacks the suitedness to realize equity easily and will likely be dominated or difficult to play out of position against calls or 3-bets. With 5 players left to act behind me, the standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in middle position with Ace-Eight offsuit. Since the first player (UTG) folded, I have a decent opportunity to open the pot. A8o is a marginal but playable hand in a 6-max format from this position. I will put in a standard open raise of 2.5x the big blind to 250 to try and take down the blinds or define the hand against the remaining players.

38s is a speculative suited hand with poor high-card strength, low equity against a middle position opening range, and limited playability postflop. Deep stacks (88BB effective), early in tournament (hand 10/150), no position advantage over the raiser justifies calling or 3-betting. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

KTs on the button is a strong hand. Flash opened to 250 from MP. I have position and suited broadway cards that play well postflop. This is a clear 3-bet spot - I can either win the pot now or play a bigger pot in position with a strong hand. 3-betting to around 750.

Facing a button reraise to 750 after an opener to 250. I hold 9♣4♣ in the small blind — a weak, low, suited but disconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling 700 more into a 3-bet pot is marginal: my hand is often dominated by the raiser’s range and I’ll have poor realizable equity and tough post‑flop decisions, especially if the big blind or opener stays in. With a healthy stack and plenty of hands left, preserving chips for better spots is the correct play here.

KQo is too strong to just fold getting decent price, but flatting here leaves us out of position in a likely multiway 3-bet pot with Flash still to act behind, which is a tough realization spot. With K/Q blockers reducing Claude’s strongest value combos (AK/KK/QQ) and a Button 3-bet range that should contain plenty of folds, a 4-bet is the highest-EV line to win the pot preflop and deny equity. I’ll use an out-of-position 4-bet sizing that applies pressure but still allows a clean raise/fold versus a 5-bet jam.

I opened the action with a standard raise, but I am now facing a 3-bet from Claude on the button and a cold 4-bet from FiveTwo in the big blind. Ace-Eight offsuit is significantly behind the ranges of two players showing this much aggression, especially a cold 4-bet from the blinds which usually indicates a premium hand like QQ+, or AK. Calling 2050 more is not mathematically justified given my equity against those ranges, and folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

FiveTwo 4-bet to 2300 after my 3-bet. KTs is a nice hand but facing a 4-bet from the BB, I'm likely up against a very strong range (QQ+, AK, maybe some bluffs). If I call, I'm committing ~30% of my stack with poor SPR, essentially pot-committed. 5-bet shoving with KTs is too aggressive against this range where I'm dominated by AK and crushed by big pairs. This is a disciplined fold - I don't want to stack off with a hand that's often dominated against a 4-bet range.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Jack-Eight of clubs. In a 6-max game, UTG requires a relatively tight opening range. While J8s is a suited hand with some playability, it is generally considered too weak to open from this early position, especially with active players like FiveTwo and Minni behind me. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities in later positions.

3d6d is a speculative hand at best, but very weak from middle position with 6 players behind, many aggressive opponents like Minni and FiveTwo. Poor postflop playability OOP, low equity against calling/raising ranges. Deep stacks favor waiting for better spots to maximize stack long-term. Standard fold preflop.
T2o is a very weak hand, even from the cutoff. With 3 players left to act behind me (Button, SB, BB), this hand has poor playability and limited value. Not worth opening - easy fold.

I'm on the button with QdJd — a strong, playable suited Broadway in a 3-handed spot. Pot is 150 and the cost to see the flop is 100; I don't want to limp/call and play a multiway pot out of position. I should take the initiative, isolate the blinds, and build a reasonable pot with good post‑flop playability. A min-raise to 200 is too small and invites too many callers/3-bets from the aggressive SB (FiveTwo). I prefer a standard 3xBB open to apply pressure and fold equity while still keeping maneuverability with deep stacks. Raise to 300; if I face a re-raise I will re-evaluate (often calling small 3-bets, folding to very large 3-bets), and plan to c-bet favorable flops to continue representing range.

AQs is well ahead of Minni’s button opening range and plays poorly as a flat from the small blind due to being out of position and inviting the big blind in. I want to deny equity, isolate the raiser, and build a pot for value while keeping the sizing reasonable versus stacks ~115bb effective. A 3-bet to ~4x the open is a standard OOP sizing here.

I am in the Big Blind with J9o. The action faced is a Button raise to 300 followed by a Small Blind 3-bet to 1200. My hand is far too weak to cold call a 3-bet, especially with the original raiser still left to act behind me. The gap between the opening raise and the 3-bet is significant, suggesting strength or a strong squeeze play from the Small Blind. I have no equity to justify getting involved here. This is a standard fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♦J♦ and the SB (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1200. QJs is a very playable hand in position — it has good equity and post‑flop playability versus a likely wide/aggressive SB 3‑bet range. Calling keeps weaker hands and suited connectors in my range, lets me use position, and keeps SPR around ~4 after a call (pot becomes ≈2500, effective stacks ≈10.3k), which is comfortable to play postflop. A 4‑bet to the minimum (2100) would commit too much with a marginal hand and can be answered by a shove or a cold 5‑bet; I prefer to see a flop and leverage position. I will call the 900 more (total committed 1200) and proceed based on the flop texture — c‑bet favorable boards, control the pot or fold to heavy action on unfavorable boards.

Heads-up 3-bet pot OOP. This paired, low flop (9c6s6c) favors the 3-bettor’s range because I have all the big overpairs (AA/KK/QQ/JJ/TT) and strong broadways, while Minni has more medium pairs and some suited connectors that miss often. With AhQs I have two overcards and some backdoor equity, but little showdown value right now. A small range c-bet pressures her unpaired overcards, denies equity, and keeps the pot controlled; if I face a raise I can comfortably fold this combo.

Heads‑up to the SB after I 3x'd preflop and called his 3‑bet; I have Q♦J♦ — two clean overcards and good post‑flop playability in position. The flop (9♣ 6♠ 6♣) is dry/paired and he fires a very small c‑bet (600 into a 3100 pot). That size gives me excellent pot odds and is too cheap to fold: I can realize equity with two overcards (~6 outs to pair) and keep his weaker holdings and club draws in the pot. Calling preserves position and lets me reassess on the turn (if I pair I can extract value; if he barrels large on a blank I can fold). I will call the 600 (total committed this hand = 1200 preflop + 600 here = 1800).

Turn brings the Tc completing a 3rd club and adding an overcard to the 9, which improves a lot of Minni’s flop-calling range (Tx, club floats/picked-up flush draws) and reduces fold equity for a second barrel. With AhQs specifically I have very little equity (no club, no real draw) and I don’t block her strongest continues (A♣X♣/K♣Q♣ type hands). Betting again would often commit me to uncomfortable river bluffs in a bloated pot. I’ll check, keep my range protected with my strong hands that also check here sometimes, and reassess versus her turn sizing.

Check. On 9♣ 6♠ 6♣ 10♣ with Q♦J♦ I have two clean overcards and 8 straight outs (K and 8) but no clubs — the turn completes a three‑club board and makes bluffing/continuing without a club hazardous. FiveTwo’s line (3‑bet pre, tiny flop c‑bet, then check on the turn) is consistent with a wide/aggressive range that includes club draws, medium pairs and some made hands; a bet here would often face a check‑raise or call from made hands and put me in a painful fold or commit spot. With ~10k effective stacks and a pot of ~3.7k I’d rather realize equity and keep the pot manageable. I’ll check to the river, plan to extract value if I pair or make the straight, and fold to heavy river aggression if I don’t improve (call small/medium river bets depending on texture and opponent tendencies).

River after c-bet flop and turn goes check/check on the third club, my AhQs has essentially no showdown value versus Minni’s flop-calling range (pairs/9x/club draws/Tx). The best way to win is to apply pressure with a polarized delayed barrel that credibly represents overpairs (AA/KK/QQ/JJ) and strong 9x that often check the turn on the flush-completing card, then go for value/protection on a brick river. A medium-large sizing targets folds from 77–88, some weak 9x (especially without a club), and any floated overcards that didn’t improve. If I get raised, I can comfortably fold.

Board: 9♣ 6♠ 6♣ T♣ 4♥. I hold Q♦J♦ (no clubs). Pot is 6,300 and FiveTwo has bet 2,600; calling costs 2,600 to win a final pot of ~11,500, so I need ≈22.6% equity to break even. Opponent’s line (3‑bet pre, tiny flop c‑bet, check turn, small river stab) looks wide/aggressive and contains a mix of thin value (T9, 99, Tx, club hands) and many missed overcards/bluffs. My hand is unpaired and vulnerable to club flushes and many value hands, but given the pot odds and his tendency to stab thin I expect enough bluffs/weak value in his river‑betting range that a call is justified. Raising would be too thin and polarizing (I have no club blocker and would be crushed by value hands); folding would concede too much with favourable pot odds. Call 2,600 and take it to showdown.

UTG in 6-handed play requires a tight opening range. J9o is offsuit and not strong enough to open from early position. Folding preserves stack for better spots. No previous action, pot has blinds only.

T7o is a weak hand in middle position with 4 players left to act behind me. FiveTwo has been aggressive with a large stack. This is a clear fold - the hand plays poorly multi-way and I'm out of position.

Hand is 3♦6♠ — a weak, unsuited, low hand with little playability. Calling 100 (limping) would put me out of position against the Button and the blinds and will frequently be dominated by overcards; it’s not a set-mining candidate and has poor equity multiway. Open-raising is also a mistake here — I’ll get called or 3-bet by better hands and face difficult postflop decisions. I’m sitting ~75 BB and this is early in the tournament, so preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct. Fold preflop.

Folded to us on the button with blinds relatively shallow vs our stack. Even though 5♠2♠ is weak, suited hands have some playability in position and we profit mainly from immediate fold equity. I’ll open to a standard small sizing to steal; if we face a 3-bet we can comfortably fold, and if called we get to realize equity with position and initiative.

I hold Queen-Ten suited in the Small Blind. The active player, FiveTwo, who is the chip leader and has demonstrated aggressive play (frequent raises and 3-bets), opened to 250 from the Button. Against a wide Button opening range, QTs is very playable and too strong to fold. 3-betting is an option but risky given the opponent's willingness to 4-bet and play large pots; it would also bloat the pot while I am out of position. Calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that retains good playability (flush and straight potential) at a good price (calling 200 to win a pot of 400+). I choose to call to keep the opponent's range wide and control the pot size pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Js 5d. FiveTwo raised from the button and Pro called from the Small Blind. Although the price to call is only 150 to potentially win a pot of 600, J5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against two opponents. Given the aggressive history of FiveTwo, it's better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger hand to contest.

I hold Queen-Ten suited (QsTs) on a flop of 9c-7d-8h. This gives me an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Six makes a straight) along with two overcards. The board texture is very coordinated and generally favors the pre-flop caller's range (my range) over the pre-flop raiser's range. However, the opponent (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression in previous hands, frequently betting and raising. Checking is the standard line out of position to the pre-flop aggressor. It allows me to see if the opponent continues their aggressive tendencies. If they bet, I have a strong enough hand to continue (likely calling to keep the pot manageable or raising for value/protection if the sizing allows). Leading out (donk betting) is an option but risks getting raised by this specific aggressive opponent, which would force me to commit more chips with a drawing hand out of position. Therefore, I will check and react to their action.

Flop 9c-7d-8h is a very connected texture that smashes the small blind’s preflop calling range (pairs, suited connectors, 9x/8x/7x, combo draws). With 5h2h we have essentially no immediate equity (only a backdoor heart draw and a few very specific runouts like a 6), and c-betting into this board risks getting check-raised or simply called by a wide, equity-rich range. Checking back keeps the pot small, realizes our slim equity, and lets us bluff selectively on favorable turns (hearts, high cards that are better for our range, or board-pairing cards).

The turn is the 4c, keeping the board texture relatively similar though adding a flush draw which we do not have. We still hold an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with Q-high. The opponent, FiveTwo, checked back the flop on a wet board (9-7-8), which usually indicates a medium-strength hand attempting to pot control or a hand that missed and gave up. While a probe bet here is an option to fold out Ace-highs or King-highs, FiveTwo has demonstrated significant aggression (raises/check-raises) in previous hands. Betting out risks facing a raise, which would force us to fold our strong draw or commit heavily as an underdog. Checking is the disciplined play: it controls the pot size, induces bluffs that we might pick off if we improve, and allows us to realize our equity (seeing the river) for the cheapest price possible. If the opponent bets a standard amount, we have the odds to call.

After the flop checked through on a very dynamic 9-7-8 texture, Pro’s second check on the turn (4c) signals a lot of capped/medium-strength holdings and outright whiffs (overcards, some weak pairs that don’t want to build a pot, missed broadways). With 5h2h we have essentially zero showdown value and no meaningful draw, so our best way to win is to apply pressure in position with a delayed c-bet. A medium sizing targets folds from his air and some marginal pairs/draws while keeping our risk controlled; if we face a check-raise we can comfortably fold, and if called we can consider barreling only on river cards that credibly favor our range (A/K/Q/J/T, board pairs, some clubs).

I hold Queen-Ten suited with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any Jack or Six) on a board of 9-7-8-4. The opponent, who is the aggressive chip leader, checked back the flop and is now betting 350 into a pot of 600 on the turn. The pot odds are roughly 27% (350 to call into a total pot of 1300). My direct equity for hitting the straight on the river is approximately 17% (8/46). To make this call profitable, I rely on implied odds, needing to extract roughly 700 additional chips on the river if I hit. Given the deep stacks and the opponent's aggressive profile, it is highly likely I can get paid off if I make my hand. Raising is a consideration to attack their capped range (since they checked the flop), but calling is the lower-variance line that realizes my equity efficiently.

I hold Queen-Ten suited (Queen-high) on a final board of 9-7-8-4-2. My open-ended straight draw from the flop has completely missed. I am out of position against the aggressive chip leader who checked back the flop and bet the turn. My hand has practically zero showdown value, losing to Ace-high and any pair. Attempting to bluff (donk-betting) here is risky and lacks credibility because my line of check-calling the turn strongly resembles a missed draw, which the opponent will likely sniff out and call with any pair (which is a large part of their range given the flop check-back). Checking is the prudent play to conserve chips. If the opponent checks back, I lose to their better highs or pairs, but if they bet, I can easily fold.

River gives us a tiny but real showdown hand (pair of 2s). After we bet turn and got called on 9-7-8-4, Pro’s calling range is heavily weighted to pairs (9x/8x/7x/4x, pocket pairs) and some straight draws; the river 2 is a brick that doesn’t pressure those pairs. If we bet, worse hands (missed draws / A-high) mostly fold—hands we already beat—while better hands call, and it’s unlikely we generate enough folds from 7x/8x/4x/pocket pairs to make a bluff profitable. Best is to take the free showdown.
AKo is a premium hand from UTG. Standard open raise to 2.5-3x BB. I'll raise to 250 to build the pot with this strong holding.

Claude opened UTG to 250. I hold Jh8h — a suited J8 that plays well postflop but is marginal vs an UTG raising range. With several players to act behind, a light 3-bet would risk getting isolation or multiway callers and I’ll be out of position vs Claude. The price to see a flop is small (250 into a 400 pot, ~3.3% of my 7,550 stack) and gives good implied odds to hit flushes/straights or top pair. I’ll call to see a flop and evaluate — continue aggressively with strong/connective flops (nut flushes, strong pairs, big draws), fold or proceed cautiously if I miss and face heavy action.

Facing an UTG open and a middle-position flat, Q3o is too weak and dominated to continue profitably. Calling invites tough postflop spots multiway with poor playability and reverse implied odds, and squeezing lacks enough equity/blockers versus early-position strength. Best is to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

I hold Td 7h on the Button. The action began with an UTG raise to 250 (2.5 BB) followed by a call from Middle Position. My hand, Ten-Seven offsuit, is statistically very weak and falls well outside the standard calling or 3-betting range against an Early Position open and a caller. While I have position, the reverse implied odds are high (domination issues) and the hand plays poorly multi-way. There is no strategic merit in entering this pot. The correct decision is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ad6h. Claude opened from UTG for 2.5bb and Minni called. While I am getting good pot odds to call (200 to win 650), A6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an UTG range and a flat-caller. I am likely dominated by Claude's opening range (better Aces) and Minni's calling range. Playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal Ace is a recipe for losing a significant portion of my stack. Folding is the safest and most mathematically sound decision here.

K2o is a trash hand with no playability. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight/aggressive player based on history) and a call from Minni, the range is strong. Pot odds of 650:150 (~4.3:1) require ~19% equity, but K2o has far less against this multiway spot. No position postflop, fold to preserve stack.

I have AK and missed the Q-5-4 flop, but I was the preflop aggressor. This is a standard c-bet spot on a relatively dry board. I can represent the Queen and take down the pot often. Betting around 50% pot to apply pressure.

I call 325. I hold J♥8♥ on 4♥5♣Q♠ — no pair and only a backdoor flush/runner-runner straight potential, so my raw equity is low. Claude opened UTG and made a relatively small c-bet (325 into a 975 pot) heads-up, so calling is cheap relative to my stack (7300) and gives me a chance to realize equity, float the hand, or improve on the turn. Plan: call to see a turn — if I hit a heart or pair (J/8) I can continue (value/bet or raise depending on action); if the turn is a blank and Claude barrels large, I’ll fold rather than invest more out of position. I won’t bluff-raise OOP with only backdoor outs; a flat-call keeps my range sensible and preserves chips while retaining postflop options.

I have AK high and missed the flop. Minni called my c-bet, indicating they likely have some piece - a Queen, pocket pair, or draw. The turn 2s adds a flush draw possibility. With just AK high, continuing to barrel is risky as I'm mostly getting called by better hands. Check to control the pot and potentially see a free river where I could hit an A or K.

I missed — I hold J♥8♥ on 4♥-5♣-Q♠-2♠. Claude opened UTG and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn: that typically shows either a failed c-bet or pot control with a marginal made hand. The turn (2♠) kills the backdoor heart plan — I now have only 6 outs (three J’s and three 8’s) to pair on the river (~12–13% equity). Pot is 1,300 and my stack is ~6,975, so a turn bluff here risks too much with very little fold equity versus an UTG raising range. Best line is to check and see the river; if I pair (J/8) I can pursue value or check-call depending on sizing, otherwise fold to large aggression. Check to control the pot and preserve chips.

The river 3c completes my wheel straight! I have A-2-3-4-5 with my Ace. This is a very strong hand. Minni called flop and checked turn/river, likely holding Qx or a pocket pair. I need to value bet and size it to get called by Queens and other made hands. Around 60-70% pot should extract value without scaring off medium-strength hands.

Claude opened UTG, c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn before firing 850 on the river. I hold J♥8♥ with no pair and no completed flush/straight — there are no more cards to come. Calling 850 into a 2150 pot would commit 850/3000 ≈ 28.3% equity (and ~12% of my stack). I have no outs and only beat pure river bluffs; Claude’s line (UTG open → flop c‑bet → turn check → river value‑sized bet) is exactly the kind of line Qx/Ax (A makes the wheel on 2‑3‑4‑5) or other made hands take. I don’t expect Claude to be bluffing here often enough to justify a call, and bluff‑raising OOP with virtually no blockers is too risky. Follow my plan to preserve chips and fold.

UTG in 6-max with Qh9h is borderline but playable — it has suitedness and decent equity against single opponents but is weak to multiway action out of position. I don't want to limp and get multiway; raising takes the initiative, builds fold equity, and helps isolate one opponent. With ~70BB I can play postflop if called, so I open to a standard 3x the BB (300 total). If I face a large 3-bet from the very active big stacks I'll fold or re-evaluate based on sizing; vs a small 3-bet I can occasionally continue, but my plan is to open here and take control of the hand.

Facing an UTG open to 300, QJo is too often dominated by Minni’s continuing range (AQ/KQ/QT, AJ/KJ) and plays poorly multiway with several players left to act who can squeeze. With deep stacks, calling puts us in tough reverse-implied-odds spots when we make a second-best pair. A light 3-bet here doesn’t have great blocker value versus an UTG range and risks getting 4-bet or called into a difficult postflop. Best EV is to fold and preserve our big stack for better steal/3-bet spots.

Opponent Minni is opening from UTG for 300 (3BB). Minni has been observed to be very active pre-flop, raising in many previous hands, which suggests a looser-than-standard UTG range. Ace-Nine suited (Ac9c) is a speculative hand that plays well in position but is often dominated by a tight UTG range (AT+, AJ+). However, against a loose opener, A9s has sufficient equity to call. I am in the Cutoff, allowing me to play the rest of the hand in position. 3-betting is an option but might isolate me against a stronger range or force a fold if 4-bet. Calling is the best line to realize equity while keeping the opponent's range wide.

I am on the button with Ten-Six suited. While suited cards have some value in multi-way pots due to flush and straight potential, Ten-Six is a weak hand to play against a raise from early position (Minni). UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and there is already a caller (Pro) in the cutoff. Calling here with a speculative hand that is likely dominated is not profitable in the long run, even with the advantage of position. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in the small blind with 3c5h, a complete trash hand lacking connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Minni (UTG) opened to 3BB, Pro flat-called from cutoff, indicating some strength. Defending this wide from SB is unnecessary as it has poor playability postflop and folds to a raise anyway. Pot odds are not compelling enough (needing ~33% equity vs range), and preserving stack for better spots in tournament with 150 hands total. Standard fold.

T3o is a weak hand. Even with decent pot odds (200 to win 750), I'll be out of position postflop against two opponents. T3o doesn't play well multiway and has poor reverse implied odds. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Check. I opened to 300 UTG with Qh9h and got one caller from the cutoff. The flop A K 3 rainbow is very unfavorable for my hand — I have no pair and only backdoor hearts. Pro’s calling range preflop will contain a lot of Ax/Kx and broadway hands that connect with this board, so a c-bet will be called or raised too often and has poor fold equity. I’m out of position and have little equity, so I’ll control the pot by checking and re-evaluating based on Pro’s action. Plan: fold to sizable aggression (large bet or raise), call a small probe bet to see a cheap turn, and only bluff the turn if a heart or a very favorable blank arrives.

I have Top Pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker on a wet board (As Kc 3s). The board connects heavily with a standard UTG opening range (AK, AQ, AJ, Sets). The opponent (Minni) checked to me. While I have showdown value, betting likely folds out worse hands (underpairs) and isolates me against better hands (AK, AQ, AJ, Two Pair) or strong draws. I want to exercise pot control to avoid facing a check-raise and to keep the opponent's range wide. Checking back allows me to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

I check. Holding Qh9h I have no pair and only six clean outs (three Qs + three 9s) to hit on the river (~12%). The turn 5s makes three spades on board (3s, As, 5s) — that increases the chance my opponent has a made flush or at least improved equity (Ax, Kx, pocket pairs, or two spades). A bet here would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity and is likely to be called or check-raised by better holdings. I’ll control the pot: check to see the river and try to realize my equity. Plan: if Pro checks back I get my one-card equity; if he fires a small probe (~<=1/3 pot) I’ll call to see the river; I will fold to sizable bets/raises on this street.

On the Turn, the board is [3s, Kc, As, 5s]. The 5s brings a possible flush (three spades on board) and connects with wheel draws. My hand (Ac9c) is top pair, but my kicker is mediocre, and I hold no spades. Minni has checked twice (Flop and Turn). This line often indicates a marginal hand, a complete miss, or a pot-control line with a made hand. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like Kx or underpairs without a spade) and risk getting raised by flushes, sets, or two pair. This is a classic 'way ahead / way behind' scenario. Checking back controls the pot size, avoids difficult spots against check-raises, and allows me to realize my equity to the river, potentially inducing bluffs on the river or getting to a cheap showdown.

Board: 3s Kc As 5s 5d. I hold Qh9h — no pair and effectively zero showdown value. Pro checked flop and turn, showing a fairly passive/missed line. A small river bluff is the right play: the board pairing reduces the value of many of his hands and makes a small bet credible, and I only need ~25% fold equity to make a 250-chip (≈1/3 pot) bluff +EV. I’ll bet 250 to try to take down the 750-pot; if he calls I’ll concede the hand, and if he raises I’ll fold to avoid overcommitting with air.

On the River, the board is [3s, Kc, As, 5s, 5d]. The pot is 1000 and Minni bets 250 (1/4 pot). I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a 9 kicker. Minni's line of checking the flop and turn followed by a small river bet often indicates a 'blocker bet' with a marginal hand (like Kx, QQ, or a weak Ace) trying to get to showdown cheaply, or a weak bluff. While a flush is possible given the three spades, the bet size offers me 5:1 pot odds, requiring only ~16.7% equity to call. My hand is too strong to fold to such a small bet, but not strong enough to raise for value as I would likely only be called by better hands (AQ+, Flushes). Therefore, a call is the optimal play to realize my showdown value.

9♠5♦ offsuit is far too weak to open from UTG in 6-max: poor high-card value, dominated when we make a pair, and difficult to realize equity out of position versus the active 3-betting we’ve seen (e.g., Minni/FiveTwo/Claude prior hands). With deep stacks, the reverse-implied-odds are significant, so the best EV is to let it go and wait for better spots.

I am in Middle Position with Ten of Spades and Four of Spades (T4s). This is a weak suited hand that performs poorly from early/middle position due to reverse implied odds and a high likelihood of being dominated. With four players left to act behind me, the probability that someone wakes up with a better hand is significant. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Cutoff position with Seven of Diamonds and Six of Spades. While these are connected, they are offsuit and generally too weak to open from this position in a 6-max game. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities, especially since I have a healthy stack of over 95 big blinds.

Preflop on the button with 9c3d offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and no straight or flush potential. Blinds are 50/100, folded to me, pot 150, to call 100. Raising to steal is possible from button but 93o is too weak—low chance of winning at showdown even if stealing succeeds, and vulnerable to 3bets from SB (Claude) or BB (Minni), both active players. Calling risks multiway pot OOP later. Standard fold to preserve stack early in tournament (hand 15/150), deep effective stacks (~60-180BB). Wait for better spots.

Everyone folded to me in the SB with pocket 6s. This is a strong hand heads-up against just the BB. I should raise to put pressure on Minni and potentially take down the pot or get value. Standard raise to 3x BB (300).

I'm in the big blind with KcKd facing a single open to 300. KK is a premium — 3-betting for value and to isolate the opener is standard. Pot is 400, to-call 200; I will raise to 1200 total (putting in an extra 1,100 chips beyond my blind). This size extracts value from worse broadways and mid pairs, discourages multiple callers/speccy hands, and leaves room to navigate postflop with ~63bb effective stacks. If Claude 4-bets large and polarized I'll re-evaluate (call smaller 4-bets, fold or 5-bet shove depending on sizing and range), but overall 3-betting maximizes EV here.

Minni 3-bet me to 1200 after I opened 66 from SB. Pot is 1500, need 900 to call. Minni has ~4025 behind after this bet. Implied odds are roughly 4.5:1 - not ideal for pure set mining (need ~7.5:1), but 66 has some equity against Minni's 3-bet range which likely includes AK, AQ, and some suited broadway. Given the price and position, calling to set mine is reasonable. If I miss the flop, I can fold to aggression.

Missed my set on K95 two-spade board. I'm out of position with an underpair (66) against the preflop 3-bettor. This board texture hits Minni's 3-bet range well (KK, 99, AK, KQ). Donk-betting would be a mistake - I should check and evaluate. If Minni c-bets, I'll likely need to fold as my equity is poor against their value hands.

I have KcKd — I flopped trips (set of kings) on Ks 9s 5h. Pot is 2400 and Claude (who called my preflop 3-bet) checked the flop, so his range is weighted toward Kx, AK, medium pocket pairs and some broadway hands. With one opponent and a near-nut made hand I should bet for value and to charge any backdoor equity. I choose a ~50% pot value bet (1200) — large enough to build the pot and protect against turn equity, but small enough to keep worse Kx and middle pairs calling. If called I’ll keep extracting on safe turns; if I face a sizeable check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (but trips are strong enough to continue in most cases).

Facing a 1200 c-bet on K95ss board with pocket 6s. I'm OOP with an underpair against the preflop 3-bettor. This board heavily favors Minni's 3-bet range (KK, 99, AK, KQs all connect well). I have only ~4% equity to hit my set on the turn. Even with 4:1 pot odds, I don't have the implied odds to continue - Minni only has ~2800 behind. My 66 has no showdown value here and no draws. Clean fold.

I am in UTG position with 9c Th (Ten-Nine offsuit). This is a weak hand to open from early position in a 6-handed game, especially with active players behind me who have shown a tendency to 3-bet (like Minni and FiveTwo). The hand is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position. The standard play is to fold.

I am in middle position with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). In a 6-max game, this is a standard opening hand from the Hijack. While it can be vulnerable to 3-bets from the players behind me—particularly FiveTwo and Minni who have shown aggression in previous hands—it is strong enough to open for a standard 3x raise to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds.

23o is one of the worst possible hands with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Facing a standard 3BB raise from middle position by Flash, who has been aggressive in past hands (e.g., Hand 10). Position is cutoff, but equity is abysmal against any reasonable raising range (~10-15% vs. typical MP open). Deep stacks (87BB), no need to speculate with trash. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Long-term tournament EV positive.

I have pocket Aces on the button - the best starting hand in poker. Flash raised to 300 from middle position. This is a perfect spot to 3-bet for value. I'm in position and want to build the pot. A 3x sizing to 900 is standard and should get calls from worse hands while building a nice pot.

Facing a button 3-bet to 900 after an initial open, I have J♥8♥ in the small blind. J8s is a marginal, speculative hand that plays poorly out of position versus a 3-bet range that will include lots of overcards, broadways and pairs that dominate me. Pot is 1350 and it costs 850 to call (pot would be 2200), so I need ~39% equity to break even — J8s won't have that against a typical button 3-bet. There's also a high chance of multiway action with the big blind still to act, which further reduces my equity and post-flop maneuverability. Raising to the 1500 minimum would commit too much with a weak hand. Preserve the stack and fold to wait for a better, more playable spot.

We’re in the BB facing an open to 300 and a 3-bet to 900, with action potentially reopening to Flash if we continue. Q9o is dominated by the value-heavy parts of both ranges (AQ/KQ/QJ/QT, 99+, etc.), plays poorly out of position in an inflated pot, and doesn’t have strong blocker value to justify a 4-bet bluff. Calling for 800 to see a flop also risks going multiway/sandwiched, which further reduces realizable equity. Best EV is to preserve stack and fold.

I opened for 300 with King-Ten offsuit (KTo) from middle position, and Claude has 3-bet me to 900 from the button. KTo is a marginal opening hand that performs poorly against a 3-bet range, especially when playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Claude's 3-bet indicates strength or a well-timed positional bluff, but since my hand is easily dominated by hands like AK, AQ, AJ, and KQ, and I'll be forced to play a bloated pot without initiative, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with King-Jack offsuit (KJo). In a 6-max game, KJo is at the bottom of a standard opening range from this position. With a stack of over 90 big blinds, I will open for a standard raise of 3x the big blind to 300. This allows me to take control of the pot early, though I must be cautious of 3-bets from aggressive players like FiveTwo or Minni who have shown significant activity in previous hands.

T7 offsuit is a speculative hand with poor playability out of position against an UTG raise from Flash, who has shown aggression in early position in prior hands. The hand has low equity against a strong opening range and is unlikely to make strong hands. Early in the tournament (hand 17/150), preserving stack by folding weak hands against early position opens is optimal to wait for better spots. No fold equity for a squeeze here given villain's stack and position.

Qc8c is a decent suited hand with some playability. Flash opened from UTG which typically shows strength, but I have position on him. Suited queens have flush potential and can flop well. Calling to see a flop in position is reasonable here. Not strong enough to 3-bet against UTG, but too good to fold with position and suited cards.

Flash opened UTG to 300 and Claude called. I’m on the button with Ks6h — a marginal hand against an UTG range and not strong enough for a 3‑bet. Calling 300 is correct: I keep the pot manageable, retain the massive positional advantage, and can fold to significant 3‑bets. Pot is 750 so the call is cheap relative to the implied postflop edge from the button with 75bb effective stacks. Plan: call 300, play postflop in position (look to value-bet top pair, fold to heavy action when missed, chase only strong draws).

We’re getting a very good price (250 to win 1050 now) and Q8s has reasonable playability with flush potential, plus we cover the table so implied odds are decent when we make strong hands. A squeeze here would be too ambitious versus an UTG 3bb open and two callers, and folding is too tight given the pot odds. I’ll complete and play cautiously postflop (mostly fit-or-fold, avoid stacking off with marginal top pair).

I am in the Big Blind facing a standard open from UTG and three callers.
