The pot odds are extremely favorable: I need to call 200 chips to win a pot that will be 1500, offering me 7.5:1 odds (requires ~13.3% equity). My hand, A5o, has decent equity (~18-20% in a multi-way pot) and nut straight potential (wheel). Being out of position in a 5-way pot is difficult, and I must play cautiously if an Ace flops due to kicker problems, but closing the action for this price makes calling the only standard +EV play. Squeezing is too risky against four opponents with a marginal offsuit Ace.

Multiway pot (6 players) on a wet-ish flop (7h8h4d). We have top pair with a middling kicker (Q8) and no heart, so we’re often ahead of overcards but vulnerable to bigger 8x, sets, two pair, and heart/straight draws. Leading into five players risks getting raised by strong made hands/draws and building a pot with a marginal one-pair hand. Best is to check and realize equity, then continue versus reasonable sizing (often call one bet) and avoid bloating unless we improve.

I am in the Big Blind in a 5-way pot with As5d on a 7h-8h-4d board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6) and an overcard. Because this is a multi-way pot with the pre-flop raiser still to act behind me, leading out is ill-advised as it builds a pot I likely won't win and opens me up to raises. Checking is the standard line to see how the action develops and potentially realize equity for free.

I am on the flop in a 5-way pot with King-high and no significant draws. The board (7h 8h 4d) is highly coordinated and likely connects well with the ranges of the callers from the blinds and late positions. Betting into four opponents on this board with nothing but two overcards is extremely risky and unlikely to succeed as a bluff. Given the board texture and the number of players, checking is the most sound strategy to minimize losses and see how the action develops.

I flopped middle pair (8s) with Q kicker on a 7h8h4d board. With 4 opponents still in the hand, my middle pair has marginal showdown value. The board has a heart flush draw and straight draws. Everyone checked to me. In a 5-way pot, middle pair isn't strong enough to bet for value - I'd likely only get called by better hands or strong draws. Checking to control pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown is the best play here.

I’m on the button with Ks6h on 7h-8h-4d. I have no pair and only a backdoor heart flush (needs two hearts — ~4% to hit by river) plus a single overcard (K). Preflop action is multiway (UTG open + several callers), so ranges are relatively strong and betting into multiple opponents with a marginal hand is a losing line. Everyone checked to me, so I’ll exercise pot control and use position to see another card rather than bloating the pot with a thin c-bet. Plan: check and evaluate the turn — fold to significant pressure, consider calling small bluffs or very cheap bets to realize my small equity.

Turn brings the Kc, a high overcard that strongly improves the preflop raiser’s (and callers’) range (Kx, big pairs) while making our 8x second pair relatively weak in a 6-way pot. Betting mostly folds out hands we already beat (air/overcards) and gets called/raised by better (Kx, strong 8x, sets, big heart draws), so it’s a poor value/protection spot. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and decide versus sizing if someone bets (likely continue only versus small bets with good odds).

I am in the Big Blind in a 5-way pot with Ace high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). The flop checked through, indicating potential weakness, but the Turn King likely hits the range of the pre-flop raiser or the other callers better than mine. Leading out into four opponents is too risky as I likely have little fold equity against a King, and I want to realize my straight equity for free if possible. Checking is the prudent play.

I have turned top pair with King-Jack. The turn King is a very strong card for my pre-flop opening range. Since the flop was checked around by all five players, it is unlikely anyone is holding a monster like a set, which they probably would have bet to protect against the straight and flush draws. Now that I've improved to top pair, I must bet for value and to prevent opponents from seeing a free river card with their heart draws or straight draws (65, T9, 96). A bet of 800 in a 1500 pot is a standard sizing to extract value and protect my hand.

Flash checked flop and now bets 800 on the turn when the King hits. This is a classic delayed c-bet or value bet with a King. My middle pair of 8s is weak here - the King likely helped Flash's UTG range. With 3 players still to act behind me (Minni on button, plus FiveTwo and Pro), calling opens me up to potential squeezes. My hand doesn't have enough equity against Flash's betting range to justify calling 800 into this multiway pot. Folding is correct.

I hold K♠6♥ on 7♥-8♥-4♦-K♣ — I now have top pair with a very weak kicker. The turn club killed the backdoor heart flush, so my value is just a one-pair hand vulnerable to better Kx and sets. Flash (UTG preflop raiser) leads for 800 into a 2300 multiway pot — a fairly small probe. Calling 800 is the correct line: it’s a cheap price (~3.9:1) to see the river, keeps the pot controlled in position, and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal kicker against multiple opponents. A min-raise to 1600 would overcommit with a vulnerable kicker and likely get called by better kings or sets; folding would be too nitty given top pair and the small bet. I will call 800 and re-evaluate the river — fold to heavy river aggression if I don’t improve or if the action indicates I’m behind.

We’re getting a good price: 800 to win 3100 (need ~20.5% equity). Our hand is only second pair in a 4-way turn spot, but Flash can easily be betting a wide range after the flop checked through (Kx that improved, some 8x/7x for protection, and missed-heart/straight draws taking a stab). Minni’s call is concerning, yet we still have reasonable showdown value plus 5 clean improving outs (2 eights for trips, 3 queens for two pair). Raising would isolate ourselves versus better hands, and folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call and mostly evaluate river—fold versus large/strong river barrels on bad runouts, and consider checking down when action slows.

I am in the Big Blind facing a bet of 800 into a pot of 3900 (approx 4.9:1 odds) on the Turn with three other active opponents. My hand is Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6). While the pot odds require about 17% equity, my gutshot draw only has 4 outs (approx 9% equity). My Ace outs are likely tainted or dominated in a 4-way pot where three players have shown interest on the Turn. Furthermore, if I hit my straight, it is the lower end of the straight, presenting reverse implied odds risk against hands like 5-9 or 9-10. Given the poor direct odds and the multi-way strength shown, folding is the correct decision.

River completes the front-door heart flush and also brings in the 6x straight, both of which are very plausible given the flop checked through and Flash’s turn stab getting called. Our Q8 is now just a marginal bluff-catcher (pair of 8s) in a 3-way pot and we have no good value-bet target: worse hands mostly check back and better hands/draws will call/raise. Best is to check and try to realize showdown value; versus a river bet we’ll mostly fold to anything substantial and only consider a very small sizing as a thin bluff-catch.

On the river, the board has completed both a potential heart flush (7h, 8h, 5h) and several straight possibilities (any 6 makes a straight). While I have top pair with a Jack kicker, the 5h is a very 'scary' card. Both Minni and FiveTwo called my turn bet, suggesting they have at least a piece of the board or a draw. FiveTwo has checked to me on the river. Given the coordination of the board and the fact that I am in a three-way pot, there is very little value in betting. I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I will almost certainly be raised or called by better hands (flushes, straights, or two-pairs). Checking behind allows me to see the showdown for free and minimizes my losses on a dangerous board.

River doesn’t improve me — I still have top pair K with a very weak 6 kicker in a multiway pot (pot 3,900, my stack 6,475). Both opponents checked to me; that, plus the fact they called the turn raise, suggests at least one of them can easily have a better K or a made two‑pair/set. Betting here would risk getting called by better Kx/two‑pair and fold out the only hands I beat. Pot control and showdown are the prudent lines: check and show down, folding to sizable river aggression if one fires.

Q9o is a standard fold from UTG in 6-max. Offsuit, marginal high card strength, poor playability out of position if limped into or raised. Players show aggression post-flop in history, but no need to enter pot early with this hand on deep stacks (87BB). Preserve stack for premium spots in tournament phase.

9h5h is a weak suited hand in middle position. With several players left to act behind me (including FiveTwo and Minni who have shown aggression with 3-bets), this hand doesn't have enough value to open or call. It lacks connectivity and high card strength. Standard fold.

Cutoff with A♣8♥ — a decent Ace but a weak kicker and not a hand I want to bloat the pot with preflop. The current raise is small relative to our ~103bb stack, so calling is cheap and keeps the pot manageable while preserving position against players to act. A call lets me realize equity (backdoor club possibilities and good chances to hit top pair) and avoid getting 3‑bet or squeezing out by the button/blinds with a marginal hand. Plan: see the flop; proceed aggressively if I hit top pair or a strong draw, otherwise fold to substantial aggression.

Button vs a single cutoff limp, Ah8c is a reasonable iso-raise in position. We’re likely ahead of a wide limping range, and raising wins the blinds/limp often while giving us initiative and position postflop. I’m using a bigger size than a normal open to punish the limp and reduce multiway action from the blinds. If we face a strong 3-bet, we can comfortably fold this offsuit ace due to domination risk.

I hold 3s2h (32 offsuit), which is the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. I am in the Small Blind facing a limp from the Cutoff and a large isolation raise to 450 (4.5bb) from the Button. I am out of position, have zero playability, and poor pot odds to call a large raise. This is a trivial fold to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5d 5s. FiveTwo raised from the Button to 450 after Minni limped from the Cutoff. A pocket pair of fives is a strong hand to call with here, as I have good pot odds and effective stack depth to set-mine. If I hit a five on the flop, I could win a significant pot. The cost to call is 350 into a current pot of 700, and it is likely Minni will also call, providing even better implied odds.

I have A♣8♥ in the cutoff facing a button raise to 450 with the big blind calling. I’ve already put in 100, so calling 350 more (450 total) is cheap versus my ~102bb stack and the ~1050 pot. A8o is not strong enough to 3‑bet light here — calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves position, and lets me realize equity (Ace blocker for opponents’ Ax and the chance to hit top pair or a useful draw). Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit top pair or a strong draw I’ll play aggressively, otherwise I’ll fold to substantial aggression on later streets.

I have flopped a set of fives on a board of 4s-5c-Qs. This is an extremely strong hand. Since I am first to act and the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) is on the button, checking is the standard play to allow him to continue his aggression with a C-bet. If either opponent bets, I plan to check-raise to build the pot and protect my hand against flush or straight draws.

I missed the flop with A♣8♥ — just Ace high on Q♠-5♣-4♠. I was a caller preflop (no initiative) and this is a multiway pot with the preflop raiser (Button) and the big blind still involved. There are no meaningful draws for me and my Ace only blocks some A‑x combos; I don’t want to bloat the pot bluffing into likely Qx/pair ranges or get raised off the board. Pot control is the prudent line: check and see how the original raiser and the blind act. If I face a small bet I can call cheaply to see the turn; fold to substantial aggression unless I improve (pair the Ace or hit a strong draw).

Multiway flop after an iso-raise, Q♠-5♣-4♠ connects well with the callers’ ranges (Qx, 5x/4x, pocket pairs, some spade floats). With A♥8♣ we have only ace-high and very limited backdoors, so a c-bet is likely to get called by better hands and risks getting check-raised off our equity. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our small equity (can spike an A/8), and lets us react to turn action with position.

I have a set of fives on a board of 4s-5c-Qs-2d. After everyone checked the flop, it's clear I need to start building the pot myself to get value from hands like a Queen or a draw. A-3 or 3-6 are possible straights but unlikely given the pre-flop and flop action. I will lead out with a bet of about 65% of the pot to extract value and protect against the flush draw.

The turn bet (900 into a 2300 pot) is substantial aggression and I have not improved — A♣8♥ on 4♠-5♣-Q♠-2♦ is only Ace-high with no realistic draw. I have only 6 clean outs (three Aces, three Eights) to pair on the river ≈ 13% equity, while a call requires ≈28% equity to break even (900 / (2300+900) ≈ 28%). This is a multiway pot and the preflop raiser is still in; the line (check on flop, lead on turn) looks like value or a thin protection bet rather than a pure bluff. Raising as a bluff here into two opponents out of position is too thin. I stick to my plan to fold to substantial aggression with no improvement and preserve my stack for better spots.

Flash’s turn stab comes after a flop check-through multiway, which leaves his range very wide (missed overcards/spade floats as well as some 4x/5x/Qx). We’re getting a good price (900 to win ~3200 now), and with A♥8♣ we still have a gutshot to the 3 plus some overcard/pair outs and, importantly, position to realize equity and potentially win when he gives up on the river. Raising as a bluff is high-variance here because Flash’s stack is short enough to jam over a raise, and we can’t profitably continue with ace-high. Folding is too tight given the odds and likelihood he’s stabbing with air often.

Plan: call, then fold to a big river barrel on blanks; if he checks river, consider betting to fold out his missed hands and some weak pairs depending on sizing/tell.

I have a set of fives on a board of 4s-5c-Qs-2d-As. The river Ace of Spades is a highly coordinated card that completes a potential spade flush and a wheel straight (A-2-3-4-5). FiveTwo called my 900 bet on the turn, which indicates he has a piece of the board or a draw. If he was drawing to a flush or a gutshot straight with A3, he has now improved. By checking, I can control the size of the pot and potentially get to showdown against a Queen or a weaker Ace that might check back. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the bet size and the likelihood of him having the flush or the straight.

River gives us top pair (A) but with a weak kicker (8) and no spade, on a texture where a lot of Flash’s natural turn semi-bluffs (two-spade hands) just got there and can now check to spring a check-raise jam. After leading ~60% on the turn and then checking this river, his range is either (a) give-ups/marginal showdown hands we already beat often, or (b) traps with flushes / turned straights / improved Ax two-pair. A thin value bet is unlikely to get called by many worse hands (Qx/5x hate the ace), while it exposes us to a high-EV disaster if he check-raises all-in and we’re forced to fold. Best play is to take the showdown and lock up the pot equity we’ve realized.

Checking back maximizes EV by avoiding the polarized check-raise range and still winning frequently versus missed hands and weaker pairs.

8h9d from UTG is too weak to open. It's a marginal offsuit connector that plays poorly out of position. Given that FiveTwo and Minni have been 3-betting frequently, opening this hand would put me in difficult spots. Standard fold from early position.

2♠6♠ in middle facing 100 to call (pot 150) is a clear fold. This is a very weak, low suited hand with poor playability and frequent domination; calling requires ~40% equity to breakeven preflop and 2-6s won’t have that against the ranges behind. Players yet to act (cutoff/button/blinds) have shown willingness to raise, so I’m likely to face pressure or multiway pots where this hand performs poorly. With ~99BB effective I can wait for a much better spot to invest chips. Fold to conserve stack and avoid marginal postflop decisions.

Folds to me in the cutoff with blinds in the pot, so this is a good spot to apply pressure and steal. Q6s is not a premium, but it has suited playability and benefits from position; raising is better than limping because it defines ranges and can win immediately. I’ll use a small, standard open size to reduce the cost versus 3-bets while still generating fold equity from the button/blinds.

I hold Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) on the Button. The Cutoff (FiveTwo), who is the current chip leader and has demonstrated aggressive play (frequent raises and 3-bets), has opened for 250 (2.5bb). While I have the positional advantage, A2o is a weak hand that is often dominated by the opener's range (better Aces, pairs) and plays poorly post-flop. Calling invites the blinds to enter, creating a multi-way pot where my hand has poor playability. 3-betting as a bluff is an option due to the Ace blocker, but suited wheel aces would be better candidates for that play. Given the opponent's aggression and stack depth, the disciplined move is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 5h 6h in the small blind. FiveTwo, who has a large stack and has been quite active, raised to 250 from the cutoff. This is a standard opening size. Suited connectors like 56s have good post-flop potential and playability, especially when stacks are relatively deep (I have nearly 100 BBs). Calling the additional 200 to see a flop is a reasonable play given the pot odds (3:1 if the BB folds, better if the BB calls) and the fact that 56s can flop strong draws or disguised hands. Being out of position is a disadvantage, but the price is right to speculate.

J6d suited from the big blind facing a cutoff raise to 250 (2.5BB) and a small blind flat-call. Pot is 600 (6BB), calling an additional 150 (1.5BB) offers over 4:1 direct odds, and post-call pot will be 750 providing excellent implied odds with deep stacks (86BB effective). FiveTwo's cutoff range is wide (~25-30%: pairs, broadways, suited connectors), and Flash's calling range is loose based on history. J6s has strong playability multi-way: flush draw potential, open-ended straight draws possible (e.g., 789T), two overs. Equity ~30% multi-way vs loose ranges, far exceeding required ~17%. Post-flop position on both opponents is ideal for controlling pot and realizing equity. Folding too tight early in tournament; 3-betting J6s risks facing calls/4-bets multi-way and playing OOP if action reopens. Call to play speculative hand profitably.

I have an open-ended straight draw (3-4-5-6) on a flop of 4d-Jc-3d. Since I am out of position as the Small Blind and FiveTwo was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how the action unfolds. My hand has good equity (8 outs for a straight), so I am looking to realize that equity as cheaply as possible or potentially check-raise if the situation warrants it later.

Flop board 4d Jc 3d gives me top pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker but crucially the nut diamond flush draw, making this a very strong hand (~70-80% equity vs typical CO raise + SB call ranges multiway). Board is draw-heavy (flush draws, gutshots like 25, 52; OESD with 25/T5), so betting denies equity to floats/speculative calls and builds pot for value against Ax, Kx, worse pairs (88-TT, Qx), sets that might slowplay. Pot 750, position on both (Flash checked SB), deep stacks; bet 2/3 pot (500) balances value/protection without overbetting, induces calls from medium strength, sets up fold equity if raised by bluffs. Checking risks free cards to draws or giving FiveTwo (aggro history) chance to rep strength; betting realizes equity profitably early tournament.

We whiff this J-4-3 two-diamond flop completely: no pair, no straight draw, no diamond, and only a weak backdoor club possibility. Elon is leading for a strong ~2/3 pot sizing into two players, which is a bad spot to float or bluff-raise multiway (Flash can still continue behind, and our hand has very little equity when called). Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

I have an open-ended straight draw (5-6 on a 4-J-3 board). Elon, the Big Blind, led for 500 into a 750 pot after I checked. FiveTwo has folded, making this a heads-up pot. The pot odds are 500 to call for a total pot of 1750, which is roughly 2.5:1. With 8 outs to a straight (approximately 17% to hit on the turn and 31.5% by the river), I have sufficient equity to continue, especially considering implied odds if I hit my straight. Raising here as a semi-bluff is risky since Elon led into two players, indicating he likely has a piece of the board (like a Jack) or a draw himself. Calling allows me to see the turn and decide based on the next card.

On the turn, the Ten of Diamonds (Td) is a significant card. It completes a possible diamond flush and also connects with potential two-pair hands like J-T. Having checked/called the flop with an open-ended straight draw, I am still looking for a 2 or a 7. However, the presence of three diamonds on the board makes my draw less valuable, as hitting a 2d or 7d could potentially give my opponent a flush. I will check to Elon, who was the aggressor on the flop, to see how he reacts to this board texture and to try and realize my equity as cheaply as possible.

Turn 10d completes my diamond flush (Jd 6d with board 10d 4d 3d for J-high flush), transforming top pair + nut flush draw into a monster (~90%+ equity vs Flash's SB calling range: likely pairs like 88-QQ, two-pair sets, weaker draws that missed or weaker flushes like Qd+/lower). Board extremely wet (three diamonds, gutshots), Flash's check suggests weakness or trap, but history shows loose calling (e.g., Hand 17 multiway calls); betting extracts value from worse (AT+, KQ, sets slowplaying) while protecting against unlikely higher flushes/rivers completing straights. Pot 1750 (17.5BB), deep effective stacks (~80BB); 2/3-pot bet (1200) builds pot, offers good SPR for river jam if called, balances vs bluffs/draws. Checking risks free river card or pot control issues; value bet maximizes stack growth early tournament.

On the turn, the Ten of Diamonds completes a potential flush and potentially gives Elon two pair (JT). Elon has been the aggressor since the flop, betting 500 into 750 and now 1200 into 1750. My open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 2 or 7) is now significantly devalued because the 2 of diamonds or 7 of diamonds could give my opponent a flush. Even if I assume all 8 outs are clean, the probability of hitting on the river is approximately 17.4%, which is far below the roughly 29% pot odds I'm being offered (1200 to call a total pot of 4150). Given the high likelihood that Elon has a strong hand (a flush, set, or strong two-pair) and the fact that I am out of position, I cannot justify the call.

UTG with 6♦8♥ offsuit — a weak, poorly coordinated hand with no suit and limited straight potential. I’m first to act and out of position vs five opponents; open-raising UTG would be far too loose and calling 100 into a 150 pot (40% pot odds) is a negative expectation given multiway play and low equity. With ~99 BB I should wait for a much stronger, playable hand in early position. Fold to preserve stack and pick better spots later.

We’re facing only the big blind (no raise yet), but 9-3 offsuit is far too weak to enter from middle position. Limping creates dominated, low-equity postflop spots, and open-raising as a bluff from MP is unnecessary with several players left to act who can defend/3-bet. Preserve stack and wait for better hands/steal spots from later position.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o). The action has folded to me. T8o is a weak hand, disconnected and unsuited, with poor post-flop playability. Although I am in late position, there are still three players left to act (Button, SB, BB). A standard opening range from the Cutoff typically requires stronger holdings like suited connectors, pairs, or broadway cards. Opening here would be a loose steal with negative expected value against typical calling ranges. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold.

I am on the button with Queen-Trey offsuit. While the button is the best position to attempt a blind steal, Q3o is significantly below the standard opening range for this position in a 6-max game. Given the stack sizes and the presence of active players in the blinds, there is no need to risk chips with a hand that has such poor playability and equity. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play.

Folded to me in the SB with 98s, a suited connector with excellent playability and potential to make strong hands. Standard play is to raise for fold equity against the BB and to take the initiative OOP. Raising to 2.5x the BB (250 total) is a balanced size that works well deepstacked with 6 players left. Limping is too passive, folding too tight.

K2o is a very weak hand. Even though I'm getting decent pot odds (150 to win 350), K2o plays poorly postflop and I'll be out of position against Elon. When I hit a King, I'll often be outkicked. This hand is too weak to defend profitably, folding is the disciplined play.

K7o is too weak to open from UTG in 6-max: it’s dominated by the Kx that will continue, plays poorly multiway when we limp, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position on later streets. With a healthy stack and no dead money beyond blinds, the highest-EV line is to pass and preserve our UTG range integrity for stronger opens.

I am holding J4 offsuit in middle position. FiveTwo folded UTG. This is a very weak hand with extremely poor playability and equity. There is no strategic merit in calling or raising from this position with these cards. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff with 4c 5h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the players before me have folded, opening with this hand is not profitable, especially with aggressive opponents yet to act in the Button and Big Blind positions. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with three folds ahead (UTG, MP, CO), A2o is comfortably in my wide stealing range. Ace-high has decent equity, blocks premium aces, and suited ace would be even better but offsuit is still playable from this position. Deep stacks (mine 98BB, blinds ~70-100BB) allow for postflop play if called. Raising steals blinds frequently (SB and BB have defended wide but folded often in history), builds pot OOP for them, and maintains initiative. Size to 2.5x BB (250 total) is standard for button opens after folds: not too small to get called light, not oversized. Limp weak, fold too tight here.

4s7c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Facing a button raise from Elon, this is a clear fold from the small blind. No high card value, not suited, and minimal straight potential. Not worth defending 200 chips here.

I have T♦9♥ in the big blind facing a BTN open to 250 (2.5bb) with deep stacks (~98bb). This is a defendable hand: it plays well heads-up postflop (straights, two-pairs, occasional top-pair) and the opener looks fairly wide. Calling 150 more into a 400 pot gives good pot odds and keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to realize equity out of position. A tiny 3-bet to 400 would buy little fold equity and needlessly commit chips OOP with a marginal hand, so I’ll call, see the flop, and continue with top pair/strong draws or fold to heavy aggression when missed.

Preflop I cold-called Elon's 250 open with T♦9♥ to see a flop; that was fine. The flop Qc Qd Qs is catastrophic for my hand — I have no pair and no meaningful backdoor draws. Any Q in his hand is quads, and any pocket pair in his range now makes a full house. I have only six clean outs (three 9s and three 10s) across two streets and many of those still lose to better full houses. Betting as a bluff here has very thin fold equity and a high chance of being called or raised OOP by hands that crush me. With deep stacks I don't want to commit chips on a very marginal bluff. Best line is to check and conserve chips — fold to any meaningful bet and only continue if he oddly checks back (then reassess on the turn).

Flop is Qc Qd Qs rainbow, giving every player at least trip queens using the board. My As2h gives me top kicker (ace) to the trips, which beats any king-high or worse kicker. Minni's preflop call from BB is wide, likely including many Ax, Kx, suited connectors, or small pairs. She checked flop, suggesting not a monster full house (pocket pair or better, which would crush me), possibly weakness, mid kicker, or trapping. No flush/straight draws on this dry board. As preflop raiser in position with initiative, a continuation bet for thin value/protection exploits her range: value from ace kicker, gets called/folded by worse, represents strong hands like quads or full houses that hit my button range (e.g., AQ, KQ). Deep stacks allow multi-street play. Half-pot size (300 into 550) balances value and fold equity without overcommitting. Check risks giving free card or losing initiative.

The flop Qc Qd Qs is catastrophic for my hand (T9). I have no pair and only six clean outs (three 9s + three 10s) — ~12.8% to improve by the river if the opponent has no pair — and many of those outs are dead versus hands that already have a full house or quads. Elon’s flop raise into a multiway-ish pot is consistent with very strong holdings (Qx for quads or any pocket pair for an immediate full house) or a polar betting range; either way my equity is miserable. A call to see two cards yields very poor fold equity and leaves me OOP with a marginal hand. Raising as a bluff is too thin and needlessly commits chips with almost no fold equity. Preserve stack equity and fold here.

I am in UTG position with King-Eight offsuit (K8o). This is a weak hand, particularly from early position in a 6-handed game. I have five opponents left to act behind me, increasing the likelihood that someone holds a stronger hand (like a better King, an Ace, or a pair). My stack is healthy (84.5 BB), so I have no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings out of position. Standard strategy dictates folding K8o from UTG.

I am in middle position with J5o, which is a very weak hand. There are still four players behind me, and I have no reason to play this hand profitably from this position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in cutoff with 3h7c offsuit, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card value. Standard play is to fold; limping or raising would be spew as it plays poorly postflop against any defense from button, blinds. Pot has 150 (blinds only), to call 100 for 3:1 but realization equity too low and dominated often. Preserve stack for better spots. Early in tournament (hand 22/150), no need to gamble.
QJo on the button with everyone folding to me is a clear raise spot. I have position and a solid hand. Standard button open of 2.5x should take down the blinds or let me play in position postflop.

Fold. Kc4h in the small blind is a very weak, highly dominated hand against a button open to 250. Calling 200 into a 400 pot requires ~33% equity to break even preflop; K4o vs a button opening range is well below that and plays poorly out of position, especially if the big blind defends or 3-bets. With deep stacks it’s better to preserve chips for hands with real postflop playability or positional advantage. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude is opening from the button with a wide range, and A9o is comfortably within a standard big blind defend. Calling keeps in all his dominated Ax and worse broadways, avoids inflating the pot out of position with a hand that’s sometimes dominated by his stronger Ax (AT+), and gives us good playability with top pair potential. A light 3-bet is possible, but versus a competent button range and his shorter stack, calling is the higher-frequency, lower-variance +EV defend here.

We defend BB and connect with top pair (A9) on an A-5-4 two-heart board, but it’s still a medium-strength one-pair hand that doesn’t want to bloat the pot out of position versus a button range containing stronger Ax (AT+), sets, and some two-pair/straight-draw combos. Checking keeps Claude’s c-bet bluffs and worse hands in (broadways, heart floats, small pairs) and lets us realize equity while controlling pot size. We can comfortably check-call normal sizing and re-evaluate turns, rather than leading and isolating ourselves versus a tighter continue/raise range.

Missed the flop completely with QJo. The Ah is a card that connects well with FiveTwo's BB calling range. I have queen high with a backdoor heart draw. While I could c-bet as the preflop aggressor, FiveTwo is unlikely to fold an ace, and I have little equity. Better to check back, take a free card, and potentially improve or give up cheaply.

Claude checked back the A-5-4hh flop after opening BTN, which significantly caps his range toward a lot of broadway air, small pairs, and heart/straight floats (with some slowplays still possible but less frequent). The 6d adds equity to a bunch of those hands (gutshots like 32, 73, 87; plus heart floats), so I don’t want to just give a free river with A9. At the same time, A9 is only medium strength vs the portion of his range that does have Ax (AT+ dominates), so I don’t want to bloat the pot.

Leading small here accomplishes two things: (1) thin value/protection vs worse Ax that checked flop and vs sticky draws, and (2) denies equity to his overcards/heart floats at a good price. I’ll use a modest sizing and be prepared to fold to a credible raise since his value-raise range on this texture (straights/sets/two-pair) crushes A9.

I have QhJd - complete air on Ah-4s-5h-6d. FiveTwo's small probe bet (250 into 500) after I checked back the flop suggests they're either value betting a weak ace or testing my checking range. I have queen high with no real draws - the Qh gives me a one-card flush draw but that's very weak. Calling here just to float with Q-high against someone who has shown interest would require me to bluff the river, and my hand has virtually zero equity if called. The math doesn't work - I should cut my losses.

Qh6h is a weak starting hand to open from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 6-handed game. Standard opening ranges for this position are much tighter, focusing on higher value hands or suited connectors with more playability. Given the aggressive nature of players like FiveTwo and Minni behind me, opening such a marginal hand is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation or being forced to fold to a 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined choice here.

J4o is a trash hand with very low equity, dominated by any live J or better pairs, and poor post-flop playability. In middle position with no action ahead except UTG fold, standard play is to fold preflop to preserve chips for better spots. Opponents may be aggressive but this hand isn't worth entering the pot voluntarily. Deep stacks favor patience in tournament.

A4o in the cutoff with two folds already. This is a standard open from the cutoff position - Ax suited/offsuit plays well heads up. With only the button and blinds left to act, I should open raise to 2.5x. Standard play here.

I’m on the button with K♦2♦ (king-deuce suited) facing a CO open to 250 into a 400 pot. K2s is marginal and often dominated vs a CO open, but being on the button with ~96BB I want to realize my positional equity rather than inflate the pot with a weak hand. A min 3-bet to 400 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and will frequently get called or 3-bet back; K2s isn’t strong enough for an isolating 3-bet shove or large 3-bet here. I’ll call 250 to see the flop in position, plan to proceed cautiously post-flop (fold to heavy aggression or a clear 3-bet, continue with aggression on favorable flops or good equity like backdoor flush/straight), and use position to pick up the pot or realize implied odds when it improves.

We’re in the small blind facing a CO open (2.5x) and a button cold-call, so we’ll be going multiway out of position. 97o is a poor realization hand: it makes dominated one-pair hands often, has limited nut potential, and plays badly from the SB especially versus two ranges that contain a lot of better 9x/7x and stronger broadways/pairs. Even with a decent price, the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a losing defend, and it’s not a good squeeze candidate either. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with T5o. Facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button, the pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win 650, approx 19%), but T5o is a very poor hand to play out of position multi-way. It suffers from reverse implied odds (domination issues) and lacks playability. Folding is the correct decision to preserve chips.

Flop is Ks Jc Kc. I have A4o - complete air here. This board is fairly favorable for the preflop raiser with two kings on board (less likely either of us holds a king). However, my A-high has some showdown value against missed hands. Minni called from the button and could have various holdings. I'll check here to pot control - a c-bet would fold out worse hands and only get called by better. If Minni checks back, my A-high might win at showdown.

I flopped trips (Kd with Ks Kc on board) heads‑up vs the CO opener. Only a very small portion of his preflop range (KJ combos, pocket Jacks, or Kh+J) currently beats me, while many of his raising hands (Kx, Ax, Qx, Jx, medium pairs) will call. I don’t want to slow‑play and give a free card that can turn him into a full house, but I also don’t want to overbet and fold out my value combos. I’ll make a medium value bet to charge worse kings/jacks and medium pairs while keeping bluffs and weaker hands in the pot. If he check‑raises big, that will strongly indicate a very narrow set of full‑house/quad hands and I’ll re‑evaluate (likely proceed cautiously).

I have A4o on Ks Jc Kc. Minni bet 400 into 650 (about 60% pot). My hand is just ace-high with no real equity on this board - no draws, no pair. While the paired board with two kings makes it less likely Minni has a king, my ace-high has poor showdown value against hands that are betting here (Kx, Jx, flush draws, pocket pairs). Floating with A-high is marginally profitable at best, and I'll face more difficult decisions on turn/river. Folding is the disciplined play.

UTG position in a 6-handed game requires a tight opening range. K4o is offsuit, gapped, and dominated by any king or better hand that might raise behind. With deep stacks and aggressive opponents like FiveTwo, Minni, and Claude who frequently raise preflop, folding preserves stack for better spots. No fold equity worth chasing from early position.
7s 3c is a very weak hand with poor playability. In middle position with 4 players left to act behind me, this is an easy fold. No reason to invest chips with this trash hand.

I’m in the cutoff with 3♦4♠ — a very weak, low offsuit connector. There’s 150 in the pot and it’s 100 to call; min-raise is 200. With aggressive players yet to act (button and both blinds) and deep stacks, opening here risks a 3-bet or getting called by dominating hands. 3-4 offsuit has poor equity multiway and poor postflop playability (reverse implied odds vs better hands). Calling 100 to limp in is marginal — I’d often be out of position postflop and likely face raises. Preserving a 100BB stack and waiting for a better spot in late position or against weaker opponents is the prudent EV+ play. Fold.

Folds to us on the button and the blinds have middling stacks. Q8o is well within a profitable BTN opening range; we can win the pot immediately a lot and, when called, we’ll have position to realize equity and apply pressure on favorable boards. Use a standard small open size to risk little while still denying the blinds an auto-defend with any two.

I hold 6c 4h in the Small Blind facing a Button raise to 250 from FiveTwo. My hand is very weak (6-high offsuit gapper) and performs poorly against even a wide button opening range. Additionally, I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand if I called. The pot odds (calling 200 to win 400) are not sufficient to justify playing this hand OOP with difficult equity realization. Folding is the only standard play.

I am in the big blind with 8d6s. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from the button, which is a standard late-position opening. The pot currently contains 400 chips and I need to call 150 more to see a flop. This gives me pot odds of 400:150 or approximately 2.6:1. While 86-offsuit is a marginal hand, defending the big blind against a button open is standard in a tournament setting with these odds. I have a healthy stack and can afford to see a flop to potentially outplay the opponent or hit a strong connected board.

I have 8-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) on a King-high board. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor, FiveTwo. I will check and evaluate based on their bet size, though with only a gutshot on this dry board, I am likely to fold to a significant bet.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser in position, this K-9-5 rainbow board is fairly static and favors our overall range (we have lots of Kx and strong overpairs), while BB has many hands that miss or have weak underpairs. With Q8o we have mostly air but can leverage that range advantage to win immediately, deny equity to hands like A-high/low pairs, and set up profitable double-barrel opportunities on good turns (A/Q/J/T). Use a small c-bet to risk little while applying pressure.

I have 8-high with a gutshot straight draw on a board of Ks-9h-5d. FiveTwo has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a 550 pot. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75:1, my equity is low—only about 8.5% to hit my straight on the turn. I have no overcards or backdoors to improve my hand significantly. Chasing a gutshot out of position against an aggressive opponent like FiveTwo is not a profitable long-term strategy, so I will fold and preserve my chips.
5d7h is a weak unsuited hand from UTG position. With 5 players behind me, this is an easy fold. No reason to open this hand from early position.

K♦2♠ is a very weak, easily dominated hand with a terrible kicker. I’m in middle position with several players yet to act behind me (Cutoff FiveTwo, Button Pro, SB Flash, BB Elon) — players who have shown they’ll raise and apply pressure. It costs 100 to see a flop into a 150 pot, which does not give me the equity I need vs likely calling/reraising ranges and I’ll be out of position postflop. With ~100bb effective I should conserve chips for better spots rather than limp/call or make a marginal open that’s likely to get played back at. Fold.

We’re first-in from the cutoff after two folds, but 64o is too weak and too dominated to profitably open at these stacks versus a button and two blinds who can defend/3-bet. Limping just invites isolation and plays poorly postflop. Best EV is to pass and keep our stack for better steal candidates.

I am on the Button with T9o, which is a marginal hand. While opening from the Button is standard with a wide range, the opponents in the blinds (Flash and Elon) have shown signs of aggression and activity in previous hands (e.g., Elon betting aggressively in Hand #19). Since I have a healthy stack of 83BB, there is no need to force a steal with a weak offsuit connector that could lead to a difficult post-flop spot against capable opponents who cover me. Folding is the low-variance, prudent play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 6c. It has folded to me. While the cost to complete the bet is only 50 chips, 4-6 offsuit is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against the Big Blind (Elon). Given the depth of the stacks and the lack of potential in this hand, folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack for more favorable situations.

UTG with A♠T♠ and ~100BB. This is a strong, playable hand (nut ace + suited) that I want to take the initiative with — avoid a multiway limp and build a pot with fold equity. A standard 3x open to 300 (instead of a limp or tiny 2x) prices out many speculative hands, reduces the field, and leaves plenty of stack for post‑flop play. I’ll open-raise to 300 and reassess if I face a 3‑bet.

Facing a 3bb UTG open from Minni with T5 offsuit in middle position, this hand is well below a profitable defend/3-bet range. It plays poorly postflop (dominated tens, weak kicker, bad straight/flush potential) and risks reverse implied odds with multiple players still behind who can squeeze or overcall. With healthy stack depth, best EV is to preserve chips and wait for better spots.

I hold King-Three offsuit (K3o) in the Cutoff facing a raise to 300 from Minni in the UTG position. An UTG raise typically signifies a strong range (strong Aces, Kings, pairs), against which K3o performs very poorly due to domination issues and lack of playability. With a stack of 83BB, there is no pressure to play marginal or weak hands. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

I am on the Button with Seven of Spades and Nine of Diamonds. Minni has raised to 300 from UTG. 79o is a weak hand that does not play well against an early position opening range, even with the positional advantage of the button. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve stack size, there is no reason to call or 3-bet this hand.

Preflop in the small blind facing an UTG raise to 300 from Minni, who plays aggressively but UTG indicates a strong range (premium pairs, big aces, broadways). My hand Ah4s is ace-high with a poor kicker, offsuit, and minimal playability OOP. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 are about 2:1, but implied odds are poor postflop with reverse position, dominated ace risk, and multiway potential if BB defends. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a deepstack tournament; no fold equity with a raise given weak blockers and position.

Ad2d suited in BB facing a UTG open to 300. I need to call 200 more into a 450 pot, getting about 2.25:1. Suited aces have decent playability with nut flush potential. Against UTG this is marginal but the pot odds make it reasonable to defend. Calling.

I have Ad2d on 6h4c5h. I flopped a gutshot straight draw to the wheel (need a 3 for A-2-3-4-5). Also have backdoor diamonds and ace-high. This low connected board could hit Minni's UTG range but also gives me some equity. With position acting first and a gutshot, I'll check and see what Minni does. If she bets small I can consider floating, if she checks I get a free card.

I opened preflop to 300 with A♠T♠ and Claude (BB) just checked behind. The flop 6♥-4♣-5♥ is dry and disconnected — it misses a big chunk of the BB’s defending range and gives me a range advantage as the preflop raiser. I also have the nut ace as a high-card blocker and some backdoor spade equity. A continuation bet around 50% pot (325 into 650) applies pressure, folds out unpaired broadways and small holdings, charges the few straight/heart draw combos, and keeps the pot controllable if called. Plan: if I get a single call I’ll reassess on the turn (continue for value on A/T or pot-control vs heavy resistance); if I face a large check-raise I’ll give credit to strong made hands (sets/two-pair/rare straights) and fold or pot-control accordingly.

I have a gutshot straight draw (need a 3 for the wheel A-2-3-4-5), plus backdoor diamonds and an overcard. Minni bet 325 into ~650, giving me 3:1 odds to call. With ~8% equity from the gutshot alone plus backdoor potential, this is a reasonable float. If I hit the 3, I have the nuts.

Minni checked the turn after betting the flop. I still have my gutshot (need a 3 for the wheel). The 8c doesn't help me and I've lost my backdoor diamond draw. With just ace-high and a gutshot (~8% equity), I should take the free card rather than bluff into someone who could easily have a pair or better on this connected board.

I opened UTG with A♠T♠ and c‑bet the dry 6♥-4♣-5♥ flop; Claude called. The turn 8♣ does not improve my hand — I still only have Ace‑high — and it actually helps parts of his calling range (any 8x and any 7 for a wheel/straight using the four board cards). Claude checked the turn, showing either weakness or a trap, but given he called the flop my read is that his range contains many hands that now beat me (pairs, straights, some 8x). A double‑barrel here would be a pure bluff with limited fold equity and unnecessary risk with ~94BB effective stacks. I prefer pot control and to preserve stack for a river decision where I can call or donk‑bet if I improve or face a small bluff. Action: check and re‑evaluate on the river.

River paired 8s, my gutshot missed. I have just Ace-high. Minni checked turn showing weakness, but she still likely has at least a pair from her UTG raising range. Bluffing here is risky - she could easily have a pocket pair or flopped a pair on this connected board. With ace-high I have some showdown value against complete air, and attempting a bluff would require committing chips with poor success probability. Check and hope to win occasionally.

Board: 6♥-4♣-5♥-8♣-8♦. I hold A♠T♠ — only Ace‑high. I c‑bet the flop and Claude called, then both of us checked turn and river; his line (call flop, passive on turn/river) is consistent with a made pair (4/5/6), a 7 (which makes the 4‑5‑6‑7‑8 straight), or an 8 (trips) — all of which beat my hand. There is very little fold equity on the river against a player who called my flop bet, so a river bluff would be low EV and needlessly riskful with ~94BB effective stacks. Pot is small (1,300) relative to stacks; best play is to preserve chips and take showdown. Action: check to the showdown.

6-max UTG, A8 suited is strong enough to open: it has good playability, nut-flush potential, and blocks top pairs (Ace). I want the initiative and to thin the field vs the blinds/button while keeping my sizing consistent with prior opens at this table. A small open also preserves stack if I face a 3-bet and need to continue selectively.

The player 'FiveTwo' opens from UTG to 250 (2.5bb). While FiveTwo has been active and aggressive, opening A2s from Middle Position against an UTG raise is generally a losing play due to reverse implied odds (domination by better Aces) and the risk of being squeezed by players yet to act (Cutoff, Button). Although suited Aces have playability, A2s is at the bottom of that range. With a healthy stack of 83bb, there is no need to take marginal spots out of position. Folding is the disciplined decision here.

I am in the Cutoff position with Ten-Seven offsuit. FiveTwo has raised from Under the Gun (UTG), which typically indicates a strong range. Ten-Seven offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well against an UTG opening range, and I have several players behind me who could still act. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here to preserve my stack.

97o is a complete trash hand from the button facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who plays aggressively but early position raises indicate strength. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 400 for ~1.6:1), and postflop play would be speculative with no suited/connectors value. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a long tournament.

KTs suited is a playable hand against an UTG open. While I'm out of position, the hand has good potential with flush and straight possibilities. Calling is standard here - 3-betting is an option but FiveTwo has been active and likely won't fold light. I'll call and see a flop.

FiveTwo opened UTG to 250 and Claude (SB) called, leaving me heads-up-to-the-flop in the big blind with K♦5♣. This is a marginal, poorly playable hand that’s often dominated by an UTG opener and has weak multiway equity. Calling 150 into a 600-pot gives decent pot odds, but against an UTG range plus an SB defender my expected equity is low and I’ll be out of position postflop. A small 3-bet to 400 would be a thin bluff that risks a 4-bet or a sticky call from the SB; neither is desirable deep-stacked. Prefer to preserve stack and avoid a marginal multiway spot — fold.

Missed the flop completely with KhTh on Qc6d8d. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), only a weak gutshot (need J). I'm out of position against the preflop raiser. Best play is to check and likely fold to a c-bet. No reason to bluff or bet with such weak equity.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser, I retain a strong range advantage on Q-6-8 with all my big pairs and strong Qx. With A8h I have second pair with a good kicker but no backdoor flush; checking is fine for pot control, but a small c-bet is higher EV here to deny equity to two-overcard hands (AJ/KJ/KT), charge diamond draws, and often win immediately vs the SB’s wider preflop calling range. I’ll keep it small to avoid bloating the pot when I’m frequently called by Qx; if I get raised, I can comfortably fold this strength.

I completely missed this flop with KhTh. I have one overcard (K), a gutshot to AKQJT (need J), and a backdoor heart flush draw. Getting 4:1 on a call, but with only ~8% gutshot equity and being out of position, this is a losing spot to float. Even hitting a K could be dominated by KQ. Small c-bet suggests FiveTwo likely has something. Disciplined fold.

I am in UTG position with King-Six offsuit (Kh 6s). This is a statistically weak hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly out of position. With 5 players left to act behind me, opening the pot is negative EV. Given my healthy stack size of 83 BB, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

Holding 2h 4c in middle position, this is one of the weakest possible starting hands. With 6 players remaining and several aggressive opponents still to act, there is no strategic value in entering the pot with such low equity. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Preflop in cutoff after UTG and MP folds. KdTd is a strong suited connector with king-high playability, excellent for position. Standard open-raise size of 3x BB (300) to build pot, take initiative, and deny equity to blinds. Stacks are deep (mine 10100, effective ~6000 with BTN), supporting aggressive play. Opponents' history shows frequent folds to raises, but no need to deviate from GTO fundamentals early in hand 28/150.
75o is a weak hand. Even with button position, calling a raise with this hand is -EV. Two players left to act behind who could 3-bet. Easy fold.

I’m on the small blind with A♠? (Ah) and 8♠ (A8o) facing a cutoff open to 300 (pot 450). This is an off‑suit ace out of position against an opener who has shown a fairly standard 3x raise frequency — many of his A‑hands and broadways dominate mine. The big blind (FiveTwo) has a history of defending/calling, so calling likely creates a multiway, out‑of‑position spot where A8o has poor playability. A min‑3bet to 500 would yield little fold equity and a larger 3bet with this marginal, off‑suit ace is too loose without reads. With ~100BB effective and no reason to gamble here, I’ll conserve chips and avoid tough postflop decisions — fold.

Elon is opening the cutoff fairly actively and has shown a willingness to apply pressure postflop, which implies a reasonably wide range here. We’re getting a good price (200 to win 450) and J2 suited retains enough raw equity versus a wide CO open to defend at some frequency. 3-betting would put us in a dominated, low-realization spot when called, so the best option is to take the price and play cautiously postflop, continuing mainly with decent equity (pairs+draws) and otherwise defending selectively.

We defended pre versus a wide CO open, but this K-8-4 rainbow flop connects far better with Elon's range than ours. J2dd has essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no gutshot; only a very weak backdoor diamond possibility), so betting is unnecessary and would just burn chips. Best is to check our range, then respond based on sizing—mostly folding versus standard c-bets and only considering a peel versus a very small bet if we think we can realize some backdoor/runout equity.

Flop of 8d 4h Kc gives me top pair top kicker (KK with Td kicker) plus a strong nut diamond flush draw (Kd Td with 8d on board). This is an extremely strong hand with huge equity: value bets against Ax/Kx/draws, protection against straight/flush draws. FiveTwo's BB defending call pre-flop and check-flop suggests a wide range including pairs, Ax, suited connectors, but weaker than mine. OOP post-flop after check, betting half-pot builds the pot, denies equity to draws (like 65s, 97s, diamond draws), and gets value from worse like KQ/KJ/AT/Ax. Pot 650, bet 400 (~60% pot) balances value/protection without overbetting deep stacks. FiveTwo's history shows aggression but passivity here warrants thin value.

We defended pre getting a price, but this K-8-4 flop heavily favors the CO’s range. With J♦2♦ we have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush; out of position our equity realization is poor. Facing 400 into 1050 (~38% pot), we’d need ~28% to continue and we’re well short, so floating/bluffing here just burns chips. Best is to cut losses and fold.

I am UTG with pocket tens (TT), which is a premium pocket pair in 6-max play. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise to gain value and thin the field. I will open for 300, which has been my consistent sizing in previous hands.

Preflop, facing an UTG raise to 300 from Flash (3BB), with A3o offsuit in middle position. A3o is a weak ace with poor kicker and no suitedness or connectivity for speculative value. Calling out of position risks difficult postflop spots with dominated aces or missed draws, and the pot odds (450:300) do not justify a loose call given Flash's likely strong range (top 15-20% like pairs, broadway, suited aces). Shoving is overkill with 104BB deep stacks. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this multiway tournament pot.

Pocket 8s in the cutoff facing a UTG open to 300. This is a solid hand with good set-mining potential. I have position on the raiser and decent implied odds with 60BB effective stacks. Calling is better than 3-betting here since 88 doesn't play great against a 4-bet, and I can play the hand well in position post-flop.

I'm on the button with K♦4♦ facing an UTG open to 300 and a CO call. This is a marginal, often-dominated hand vs an UTG opening range (lots of Kx/Ax and strong broadways). Calling 300 into a 750 pot gives ~2.5:1 pot odds (need ~28–29% equity), but with a caller ahead and likely action from the blinds this becomes a multiway pot where K4s' equity drops and my ability to fold out better hands is limited. A small raise is unlikely to fold out the opener and risks a 3-bet; a larger squeeze is too loose with this holding. With a healthy stack and many hands left, the disciplined play is to avoid marginal multiway spots and fold.

J6o is a dominated, low-playability hand from the small blind, and we’d be forced to play out of position versus an UTG 3bb open plus a cold caller, with the big blind still to act (high squeeze/iso risk). Calling creates a multiway pot where we rarely realize equity; squeezing here doesn’t have strong blockers and risks getting played back by stronger ranges. Preserve stack and wait for better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Qc7c facing a raise to 300 from UTG and a call from the Cutoff. The pot is currently 750 (50 SB + 100 BB + 300 + 300). I need to call 200 more to see the flop. This gives me pot odds of 200 to call into a total pot of 950, which is roughly 4.75:1 or ~21% required equity. Although Q7s is a marginal hand and I will be out of position against two opponents, the suited quality and the excellent price justify a call to close the action. I will play defensively post-flop.

I hold Qc7c on a flop of 3d-5h-6d. I have Queen high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4 to make 3-4-5-6-7). Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (UTG) and a caller (CO), leading out is not advisable as this board texture (connected, two-tone) likely connects well with their calling/raising ranges or they may have overpairs. My best play is to check and see how the action develops. If there is a bet, I will evaluate based on sizing, but my plan is to check-fold unless the price is extremely cheap given I only have 4 clean outs.

I hold pocket tens (Tc Th) on a 3d-5h-6d board, giving me an overpair. This is a strong hand, but the board is connected and offers several straight and flush draws. Pro checked from the Big Blind, and I am the pre-flop aggressor. I need to bet for value against smaller pairs (like 77, 88, 99 or pairs of 6s and 5s) and to protect my equity against drawing hands. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to maintain control and build the pot.

I have pocket 8s on a 3d-5h-6d board - an overpair to this low coordinated board. Flash c-bet 500 into a ~950 pot. The board has straight draw potential (47 makes a straight) and a diamond flush draw. My overpair is strong but vulnerable. Raising could fold out worse hands and only get action from better. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me re-evaluate on the turn based on Flash's action and the card that comes. If a diamond, 4, 7, or overcard comes I can play cautiously.

I hold Qc7c on a 3d-5h-6d board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) and one overcard. The action went: I checked, Flash (UTG) bet 500 into 950, and Claude (CO) called. The pot is now 1950, and I have to call 500, offering roughly 4:1 odds (~20% equity required). My gutshot has 4 outs, which gives me approximately 16% equity to hit by the river, and only about 8-9% to hit on the turn. Furthermore, one of my outs is the 4d, which completes a potential flush for opponents. Facing a bet and a call, I am likely behind, out of position, and not getting the direct odds to call with a dirty gutshot. The presence of a caller significantly reduces my equity and increases the likelihood someone has a strong made hand or a better draw.

The 8c on the turn is a slightly coordinated card, but 10-10 remains a strong overpair. While some straight draws (like 7-9 or 4-7) could have completed or improved, it is more likely that Claude is holding a smaller pair, a flush draw (diamonds), or an open-ended straight draw (like 77). I will bet 1000 to continue extracted value from these hands and to protect against the numerous draws on the board. This sizing represents about 50% of the pot, which keeps the pressure on Claude's remaining stack.

I just hit my set! The 8c on the turn gives me three of a kind. This is an excellent spot. The board now has 3d-5h-6d-8c with potential straight draws (4-7 makes a straight) and a diamond flush draw. With such a strong hand on a draw-heavy board, I need to raise to build the pot and charge draws. Flash has been aggressive, so they likely have something like an overpair or a strong draw. I'll raise to around 2500 to extract value while denying proper odds to draws.

I am holding pocket tens, which is an overpair to the current board of 3-5-6-8. While the turn card (8c) is somewhat coordinated and potentially completes some straights (like 7-9), Claude's raise to 2500 on the turn is a significant show of strength. However, the pot is already quite large (5450), and I only need to call an additional 1500 to see the river. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3.6-to-1. Claude's range could include sets (33, 55, 66), straights (79s, 47s), or two-pair (68s, 56s), but it also contains semi-bluffs like flush draws or straight draws with a pair (like 77 or 76). Given the odds and the fact that Claude has relatively few chips remaining (2700), I have enough equity to justify a call, even though I must proceed with caution on the river.

I started the hand with an overpair (TT). The board has become increasingly dangerous with straight and flush possibilities (3-5-6-8-8 with diamonds). Claude raised me on the turn, which usually indicates a very strong hand like a straight or a set. The river 8d is a scare card because it completes the diamond flush and pairs the board, potentially giving someone trips or a full house. I will check to see Claude's reaction. I am likely beaten by his turn-raising range, which now includes many hands that improved or remained ahead on the river.

I have QUADS! Four 8s is an absolute monster. The river 8d completed a diamond flush and any 4-7 straight draws. Flash checked to me, possibly with a flush or straight they're looking to trap with, or they gave up. With only 2700 left and a pot of 6950, I should shove for value. This is less than 40% pot so not an overbet - should get called by flushes, straights, or even trips/full houses that they might put me on.

I started with pocket Tens, an overpair on a low board. However, the board runout (3-5-6-8-8 with three diamonds) is extremely coordinated and hits much of Claude's calling range from the Cutoff. Claude's decision to raise my turn bet of 1000 to 2500 indicates significant strength, likely a straight (7-9 or 4-7), a set (33, 55, 66), or a strong draw. The river 8d is a disaster card; it completes the diamond flush, potentially gives Claude trips with any 8 in his range, or improves his sets to full houses. Given Claude's all-in shove on the river, my pocket Tens have been reduced to a bluff-catcher against a range that is heavily weighted toward value. Even with favorable pot odds, the probability that my hand is best is extremely low given the betting sequence. Folding allows me to preserve a 56BB stack and find better spots in the remaining 120+ hands.

UTG position with K5o, a marginal offsuit hand with poor postflop playability and high domination risk (e.g., vs KQ, AK). Standard tight UTG range folds this to preserve stack and image in a 6-handed tournament with deep stacks (104 BB). No prior action favors patience for premium hands.
J4 offsuit is a weak hand in middle position. Not suited, not connected, and there are still several players to act behind me. This is a clear fold - no need to waste chips on marginal holdings.

QdJs in the cutoff is a clear open — strong, playable hand with decent blockers and good postflop playability. Pot is 150 and calling would be 100; min-raise is 200 but I want more fold equity and to discourage multiway pots. I'll raise to 300 (3x BB): standard cutoff open that builds the pot, isolates the button, and lets me play position against the blinds. If I get called I'll c-bet favorable flops; if I face a large 3-bet from an aggressive opponent I'll re-evaluate (likely fold to an oversized 3-bet), preserving a ~105BB stack for future opportunities.

Button vs cutoff open, AKo is a clear 3-bet for value and initiative. Minni has shown an active opening/3-betting tendency, so we want to isolate her, deny blinds a cheap squeeze/call, and play a high-card hand in position with fold equity. Sizing to ~3.3x keeps her dominated broadways/pairs in while pressuring her marginal opens.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing a raise from the Cutoff and a 3-bet from the Button, I have no equity to justify getting involved. The cost to call is significant, and I would be out of position against two aggressors. This is a trivial fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s7d, a very weak hand. The action consists of an open raise from Minni in the Cutoff and a 3-bet from FiveTwo on the Button. This 3-bet indicates significant strength or a committed bluff from the chip leader. Calling 900 more out of position against two active players with a junk hand like 97o is a recipe for losing chips. Folding is the only sensible move.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 from the button after my cutoff open to 300. I have QdJs — a strong, playable hand but not a 4-bet candidate against a likely value-heavy/polarized 3-bet. The pot is ~1450 and I have to call 700, which means I need roughly 32.6% equity to make a flat call profitable preflop; QJ offsuit versus a reasonable button 3-bet range typically has borderline-to-acceptable equity and plays well postflop (top-pair, straight/straight-draw potential). I don't want to inflate the pot OOP with a marginal hand, and a 4-bet would commit too much against a range that dominates QJ. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and exploit any light 3-betting by FiveTwo, and preserves ~100+BB stack depth to maneuver postflop. Plan: flat-call to 1000, evaluate flop — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry flops.

I called FiveTwo’s 3-bet preflop and now face a K-3-4 double-heart flop with Q♦J♠. I have no pair and no heart draw — only ~6 outs to a pair (poor raw equity) and I’m out of position against the 3-bettor whose range is heavily weighted to Kx/AK and strong broadways that connect with this board. C-betting here would be thin and likely to get called or raised by hands that dominate me. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity and avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal holding. Plan: check-call a small continuation bet (~<=1/3 pot) to see a turn, fold to heavy pressure, and if I pair (Q or J) or pick up clear equity on the turn then start betting for value.

We 3-bet pre and get a favorable K-high flop where our range has a big advantage and we flop top pair/top kicker. Minni’s check contains a lot of underpairs, worse Kx (KQ/KJ), and heart/straight-draw combos (A5, 56, suited hearts). A medium-small c-bet gets value from worse hands while charging the heart draws and denying equity, without bloating the pot so much that we isolate ourselves versus only sets/strong Kx. Sizing ~35–40% also keeps our betting range consistent in 3-bet pots on these textures.

I checked the flop after flat-calling FiveTwo’s preflop 3-bet because Q♦J♠ has poor immediate equity on K♠-3♥-4♥ and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. FiveTwo’s small c-bet (800 into a 2950 pot, ~27% of the pot) is exactly the size I planned to call: calling 800 to potentially win 3750 gives me ~21% pot odds, which makes a cheap turn card worthwhile. Raising here would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and would commit me OOP against a range that contains a lot of Kx/Ax/broadways that dominate me. Plan: call the small bet to see a turn, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and only continue aggressively if I pair a Q/J or pick up meaningful additional equity (heart/runner-runner/straight possibilities).

I called FiveTwo’s small flop c-bet with Q♦J♠ to see a turn; the 6♦ on the turn doesn’t improve me — no pair, no heart draw, no meaningful equity. I’m out of position against a 3-bettor whose range is heavy in Kx/AK/AQ/broadways, so betting now would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and would bloat the pot OOP. With the action checked to me I’ll preserve chips and realize equity by checking to the river; if I pair or pick up a clear draw on the river I can fire for value or a bluff, otherwise I’ll fold to sizeable river aggression. Check is the lowest-variance, most +EV line here.

Turn goes check to us in a 3-bet pot after she check/called flop on K-high. Our range still has an advantage and we have TPTK, so we should continue for value and protection. Minni can have worse Kx (KQ/KJ/KT), pocket pairs (QQ–77/55), and plenty of heart draws/gutters (AhXh, QhJh, A5s-type hands) that will call at a reasonable price. The 6d adds some straight/set possibilities (66, A5s), but those are a small part of her range versus the volume of worse made hands and draws. A ~45–50% pot bet keeps those hands in while charging hearts and denying equity without overpolarizing ourselves into only monsters/bluffs.

Fold. I have Q♦J♠ on a K♠-3♥-4♥-6♦ board with no pair, no heart draw and only ~6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 jacks) — ~13% to hit the river. The bet is 1,800 into a 5,550 pot, so calling requires ~24.5% equity (1800 / (5550+1800) ≈ 24.5%), far more than my raw equity. FiveTwo 3‑bet preflop and has shown a small, value‑oriented line (small c‑bet on the flop, another bet on the turn), so his range is heavy in Kx/AK/AQ and strong broadways that dominate me. Raising as a bluff is unattractive — I have poor blockers, limited fold equity, and the minimum raise to 3,600 would commit a large portion of my stack with low chances of success. Preserving chips for better, more +EV spots is correct here.

A2o from UTG is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. With 5 players behind me and this being an offsuit weak ace that gets dominated by better aces, folding is the correct play. No need to take unnecessary risks with 96.5 BBs.

Holding T5s in middle with 4 players to act behind (including an aggressive cutoff, FiveTwo) — this is a weak, easily dominated hand that fares poorly multi-way. Pot is small relative to stacks but calling 100 to play out of position invites raises and difficult postflop decisions; raising to 200 is too loose and will often get me isolated or 3-bet. With deep stacks I should wait for a better spot in later position or with stronger holdings. Fold and conserve chips for higher EV opportunities.

CO is a profitable steal spot after two folds, and we cover the table. J5o is weak but still works as a low-frequency open here given position and the chance to pick up the blinds uncontested. I’ll use a small sizing to risk little while applying pressure to the button/blinds. Plan: fold to any significant 3-bet; if called, play pot-control and c-bet only on favorable textures (good for range/high-card boards).

I am on the Button with Jc 8h, facing a standard open raise to 2.5bb from FiveTwo in the Cutoff. While playing from the Button provides a positional advantage, J8 offsuit is a statistically weak hand that falls well outside a standard calling or 3-betting range, even against a wide Cutoff opening range. The opponent (FiveTwo) is the chip leader and has shown aggression and capability to play back, making marginal speculative hands less attractive to play. I do not have the pot odds or hand strength to justify continuing. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six offsuit. FiveTwo, who has been quite active and holds the chip lead, has raised from the Cutoff. King-Six offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive player, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Given my current stack size of 55 big blinds, I should prioritize more favorable starting hands to avoid getting tangled in marginal spots out of position.

K7o is a marginal hand at best for Big Blind defense. Facing a raise to 250 from the Cutoff (FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in previous hands, frequently raising and betting postflop successfully), out of position play is difficult. Equity against a late-position opening range (~25-30%) is around 35-40%, but postflop OOP with a non-coordinated offsuit hand risks tough spots and poor implied odds. Pot odds (400:150 ~27% needed) are decent, but folding preserves the stack for superior spots in a deepstack tournament. FiveTwo's stack (17.4k) allows pressure, but no need to engage here.

I'm UTG in a 6-handed game with A♠T♠ — a strong, playable hand for an open. With a deep-ish stack (87.5bb) I want to take the initiative, build a pot when I'm likely ahead, and thin the field. A standard 3x BB open to 300 is size-consistent with the table and gives good fold equity against the blinds while keeping multiway pots manageable. I will proceed cautiously if I face a sizable 3-bet from FiveTwo (big stack) and re-evaluate post 3-bet, but open-raising here maximizes long-term EV and preserves postflop maneuverability.

Minni is opening UTG fairly frequently and has shown a willingness to defend but also fold later streets under pressure. Flat-calling KJo here invites a multiway pot and squeezes from CO/BTN/blinds, and KJo plays poorly multiway when dominated. Using position and my stack advantage, I’ll take the aggressive line and 3-bet to isolate and deny equity; if Minni 4-bets, I can comfortably fold, and if called I can apply pressure on many favorable boards with a manageable SPR for her stack.

I hold 9s2c, which is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from Minni (UTG) and a 3-bet to 900 from FiveTwo (MP), I have zero reason to continue. The action indicates strength ahead of me, and I would be putting ~11% of my stack in with trash equity. This is a trivial fold.

I am on the Button with 9d 4d. Minni opened from UTG for 300, and FiveTwo (the chip leader) 3-bet to 900. Cold-calling a 3-bet with a weak suited hand like 94s, especially when it represents 16% of my remaining stack, is a losing play. The action ahead of me suggests strong ranges, and I have no reason to get involved with such a marginal hand.

87h suited connectors have some playability in multiway pots or in position, but here we're OOP in SB facing a strong UTG open from Minni and a 3-bet from aggressive FiveTwo in middle position. Their ranges are strong (TT+, AQ+ likely for FiveTwo). Pot odds are ~1.6:1 effective after my call (850 into ~2200 with BB to act), but implied odds are limited OOP against tight range. BB Claude still to act and could squeeze. Better to fold and preserve stack for better spots. FiveTwo has been aggressive but shown down strong hands when contested.

AKo is a premium hand facing a UTG raise and a 3-bet from FiveTwo. This is a clear 4-bet spot. FiveTwo has been very aggressive throughout the tournament and has a big stack. I should 4-bet to around 2.2-2.5x their 3-bet size to put pressure on and isolate. If I just call, I'm playing OOP in a bloated pot multiway. 4-betting to ~2300 gives me fold equity and represents strength.

I opened UTG with A♠T♠ and faced a 3‑bet from FiveTwo to 900 and then a 4‑bet from Claude to 2300. To call I'd need 2000 into a 3550 pot (~36% pot odds). Claude's 4‑bet after a 3‑bet is heavily weighted toward value (QQ+/AK+ and the very strongest hands) and ATo is often dominated; calling would put me OOP in a multiway pot with tough post‑flop decisions and large commitment. Raising/5‑betting here with ATo is too thin. No read indicates Claude is light enough to justify a call, so I fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Claude’s cold 4-bet out of the BB after we 3-bet an UTG open is a very strong line, and with his remaining ~5k behind after making it 2300, calling creates an SPR around 1 where we’ll frequently be facing a flop shove. KJo performs poorly in that structure because we’ll often be dominated (AK/KQ/KK/JJ+), and we don’t have the implied odds to realize equity when we miss. A 5-bet jam would need a very high fold frequency to be profitable given how committed his sizing makes him.
