With no clear evidence he’s 4-betting wide here, the best EV/variance tradeoff is to let it go and preserve our stack edge.

UTG in 6-max with 7♠5♦ offsuit has very poor playability and dominated equity versus the ranges that will isolate/3-bet behind. Limping invites multiway, realizing equity badly out of position; opening turns it into a low-EV bluff that gets defended too often. With a healthy stack, best is to preserve chips and wait for better spots.

I hold Kc 6c in Middle Position with 7950 chips (approx. 80 BB). FiveTwo folded UTG. King-Six suited is a marginal hand that is generally too weak to open profitably from Middle Position, as it is easily dominated by better Kings and faces the risk of action from players in position (Cutoff, Button). With four players left to act, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or better position.

I am in the Cutoff with Ten-Deuce suited (Th 2h). While being in late position offers a slight advantage, this hand is extremely weak and does not warrant a raise or a call, especially as I am the shortest stack at the table and need to preserve chips for higher-equity opportunities. The players in the blinds (Claude and Minni) have shown significant aggression in recent hands, making a steal attempt with such a low-equity hand unprofitable.

Preflop on the button with 9h3d offsuit, a complete trash hand lacking connectivity, suitedness, or playability. All players ahead folded, leaving blinds to act after me, but entering the pot with this hand risks getting dominated or outdrawn if called/raised. Deep stacks (10k+ effective), early in tournament (hand 33/150), better to preserve stack and wait for playable hands rather than spew chips with 93o. Standard fold to maintain discipline and maximize EV long-term.
Folded to me in the SB with Q9o against only the BB. This is a standard spot to raise and try to take down the pot. Q9o is a reasonable hand for a steal attempt. I'll raise to 300 (3x BB).

I’m in the big blind with 4c8d facing Claude’s 300 open. The pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total). The hand is weak/off-suit but heads-up I should defend a wide range. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives a post-call pot of 600, so break-even equity ≈33%. Versus a typical small-blind open here my hand has borderline but reasonable equity and can realize equity on many flops, so a call is +EV. Folding surrenders too much dead money; a 3-bet bluff is too risky with poor blockers and opens me to 3-bets or difficult postflop decisions. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and continue only with real equity (pairs/straights/strong draws) or good opportunities to take the pot later.

I raised preflop with Q9o and Minni called from BB. The flop is A-T-7 rainbow. I completely missed - I have queen high with only a gutshot to a straight (need a J). This Ace-high board favors BB's calling range. C-betting into this texture is risky as Minni likely connected with this board or will float with Ace-x. Best to check and give up if faced with aggression.

Board is 7s Ac Td and I hold 4c8d — I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw (flush is impossible and a straight would require both the 6 and 9 on turn+river). Pot is 600 and Claude checked to me. I defended the BB preflop as planned but this flop is very unfavorable for my holding. Betting as a bluff has poor blocker coverage and opens me to check-raises or calls from Ax/Tx/better broadway hands. Best play is pot-control: check and see a turn for free. Plan: check here, fold to significant aggression, and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw or can credibly represent a strong hand on later streets.

I have Q9o on A-T-7-5 board - complete air with just queen high. Minni checked behind on the flop, which could mean a weak Ace, medium pair, or just floating. The 5h changes nothing. I could bet to represent an Ace, but being OOP with no equity (only backdoor straight draws), checking is safer. If Minni checks behind again, I might get to showdown cheaply or potentially bluff the river.

I check. Holding 4♣8♦ on 7♠ A♣ T♦ 5♥ I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draw (only six river outs to make a single pair, ~13% equity). Claude checked both flop and turn, which is consistent with either a medium-strength showdown hand (Ax, Tx) or a missed broadway — I have very little fold equity and poor blockers to credibly represent an Ace or Ten. Pot-control is the correct line: betting here as a bluff is high-variance and invites check-raises or calls from better hands; folding to significant turn aggression preserves my stack for better spots. Plan: check, fold to any sizable bet (I won’t call big turn bets with only 4/8), and only continue to the river if I pair or a clear, profitable bluff spot presents itself based on Claude’s sizing/behavior.

I have Q9o on A-T-7-5-4 board - just queen high. Both players checked flop and turn, indicating mutual weakness. Minni's passive line suggests a weak hand or missed draw. With queen high I have no showdown value against any pair, but I could win against other missed draws (K-high, J-high). Bluffing is risky since Minni could have a weak Ace checking for pot control. Checking to showdown is the safest option - I might occasionally win against complete air and I avoid getting check-raised.

The river 4♦ pairs my 4♣ — I now have a weak made hand (pair of fours). Claude has been completely passive (checked flop, turn and river) after opening preflop, which is consistent with missed broadways or medium showdown hands (Kx/Qx) and some small pairs. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) is appropriate: it extracts value from worse hands that will call (Kx/Qx, small pocket pairs, other missed hands) while keeping the investment low and allowing an easy fold if Claude check‑raises. There’s little evidence he will fire or check‑raise, so betting 200 into the 600 pot maximizes thin value without bloating the pot when I’m likely behind to Ax/Tx. If he raises, I’ll assume I’m beat and fold.

I have Q-high on A-T-7-5-4 board. Minni min-bet 200 into 800 after we both checked flop and turn. This tiny bet sizing usually indicates thin value (weak pair like 7x, 5x, 4x) or a blocking bet with weak Ace. I'm getting 4:1 odds but Q-high only beats pure bluffs. Given the passive line followed by a small river bet, this looks like value - folding is correct.

I am in UTG position with Jack-Eight offsuit (Jh 8d). This is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, especially from early position where I have to act first post-flop against potentially five other opponents. With a stack of nearly 80 BBs, there is no pressure to make desperate moves. Standard 6-max strategy dictates folding this hand from UTG as it falls well outside a profitable opening range. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities.

I am in Middle Position with Queen-Jack offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a 6-max game. While the table has shown significant pre-flop aggression (with frequent 3-bets from FiveTwo and Minni), I should maintain a standard opening range. I will raise to 300 (3 BB) to take control of the pot. If I face a significant 3-bet from the aggressive stacks behind me, I can evaluate based on the sizing, but QJo is often a fold to a 3-bet in this configuration.

Preflop in cutoff with AcTs offsuit, a strong hand with ace blocker and good playability in position against a middle position open from Flash (52BB stack). Flash has shown aggression in past hands but folded to 3-bets at times. 3-betting isolates the raiser, denies equity to blinds, and applies pressure with position advantage. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard, building a pot when ahead and allowing fold equity. Calling is too passive OOP potential postflop; folding too tight.

T8o is too weak to call a 3-bet, even with position on the button. Elon's 3-bet to 900 represents strength, and T8o doesn't have the equity or playability to profitably continue in a bloated pot. Easy fold.

I’m in the small blind with 5♥9♥ facing a 3-bet to 900 after an open to 300. Pot is 1350 and it costs me 850 to continue. 5♣9♥ (suited 5-9) is a speculative hand but plays poorly out of position, especially against a 3-bettor’s likely strong/polarized range and with the original raiser still to act. Calling would likely create a multiway, out-of-position pot where my equity is low and I’d need ~39% just to break even preflop — unlikely vs these ranges. 4-betting or shoving with this hand is not reasonable. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

We’re getting a good price (800 to win 1350) with a strong suited broadway that plays well postflop and can realize equity versus both Elon’s 3-bet range and Flash’s continuing range. A 4-bet here would function mostly as a bluff but creates a low SPR if called (awkward with QJs OOP) and risks getting shoved on by Elon’s 9400 stack, forcing us to fold after investing extra chips. Calling keeps our range protected in the BB, preserves stack, and allows us to navigate postflop with decent implied odds when we make top pair/strong draws.

I opened for 300 with Qh-Js, but Elon 3-bet to 900 and FiveTwo cold-called from the Big Blind. While the pot odds are attractive (600 to call into a pot of 2150), QJo is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by Elon's 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, JJ, etc.) and FiveTwo's calling range. Playing out of position in a three-way, 3-bet pot with a dominated hand is a recipe for losing a significant portion of my stack. I will fold and preserve my chips for a more favorable opportunity.

We’re heads-up in a 3-bet pot and out of position versus Elon, who retains the range advantage on A-K-x (strong Ax/AK, KK/AA, etc.). Our QcJc has essentially no showdown value but does have a gutshot to the nuts (T) plus some backdoor potential, so we want to realize equity without inflating the pot by donk-leading into the preflop aggressor. Checking keeps our range protected, lets Elon continuation-bet his air/medium hands, and allows us to respond based on sizing (often continuing versus small bets, folding versus large pressure).

Preflop 3bet with AcTs got called by FiveTwo in BB after Flash folded. Flop Ad3dKh hits top pair top kicker (TPTK) perfectly, with ace blocker. Board is drawy with diamond flush draw and gutshots (QJT, etc.), plus possible Kx or sets. FiveTwo's BB call range vs 3bet includes broadway, suited aces, pockets; he checked, so likely weak or trapping. As preflop aggressor in position, betting denies equity to draws, extracts value from worse Ax/Kx/draws, and builds pot OOP. Sizing 70% pot (1500) balances value/protection without overbetting into nuts (unlikely). Check risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs.

Elon’s c-bet is large (1500 into ~2150, ~70% pot) on an A-K-x texture where his 3-bet range has a big advantage and will contain lots of strong Ax/AK plus overpairs. With QcJc we only have a gutshot to T (4 outs) and very little clean additional equity; Q/J turns are often dominated and hard to realize with the SPR getting close to 1 if we call. Calling needs ~29% immediate equity and also invites a turn shove that we can’t profitably continue against. A check-raise bluff is also unattractive because he’ll call off frequently with top pair+ (and even some strong draws), and our equity when called is too low. Best play is to conserve chips and fold.

I am UTG (under the gun) with Queen-Four offsuit. This is a very weak hand to open with from early position, especially considering I am currently the table's short stack with 5,200 chips. Standard tight-aggressive play dictates a fold here to conserve my stack for a more premium hand or a better position.

7h2d is one of the worst starting hands in poker: offsuit, low cards, no straight or flush potential. In middle position after UTG fold, no reason to play this speculative hand. Blinds are small relative to stack (115BB deep), so folding preserves chips for premium spots. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression, but this hand has zero playability preflop.
Ad4d suited in the cutoff with two folds in front. This is a standard opening hand from this position - suited ace has decent playability and equity. I'll make a standard 2.5x open raise.

Call 250. On the button with 8♣6♠ facing a cutoff open to 250, calling is the best option: the price is cheap relative to the pot (pot ~400) and I keep position post‑flop. 8-6o is marginal but has some playability and I don’t want to 3-bet bluff into active players behind (SB FiveTwo is loose/aggressive and Pro will defend). With ~87bb effective, I can see a flop and fold to heavy action if I miss, or realize implied odds when I hit. Plan: call, evaluate flop and use position to control the pot or apply pressure when appropriate.

We’re in the small blind with 96o facing a cutoff raise and a button flat, which means we’ll be playing out of position in a multiway pot against ranges that heavily dominate us (9x and 6x dominated, poor straight potential without suitedness). Calling 200 more is a bad price given reverse implied odds and low realization from the SB. A squeeze bluff is also unattractive here: we have no blockers, two players have already shown interest, and we’d often get called and be forced to navigate postflop OOP with a weak hand. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Three offsuit (T3o), facing a raise from the Cutoff (Claude) and a call from the Button (Minni). The pot is 650, and I need to call 150 to stay in. This offers pot odds of about 19%. However, T3o is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability, especially out of position in a multi-way pot. Even though the price is cheap, my ability to realize equity is low due to the lack of connectivity and suitedness, and the risk of domination or reverse implied odds if I hit a Ten is significant. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve chips.

I have Ad4d on a 5d7sTc board - just ace high with a backdoor nut flush draw. This is a fairly dry board but I missed it completely. Minni has position on me. With no pair and only backdoor equity, c-betting into a caller who has position isn't ideal. I'll check and look to see a cheap turn card that could improve me (any diamond gives me the nut flush draw, any ace or 4 gives me a pair).

Heads-up vs the cutoff opener who checked the flop. I have 8♣6♠ on 5♦7♠T♣ — an open-ended straight draw (need a 4 or 9): 8 outs (~17% to hit on the turn, ~31.5% by river). No meaningful backdoor flush. Pot is 650 and stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds. Claude’s check-calling range can include single-pair hands and overcards; a small/medium semi-bluff here applies pressure and folds out many of those hands while still getting good equity if called. I’ll make a modest bet of 250 (~0.38 pot) to buy the pot, keep control with position, and retain fold equity. If Claude raises big I’ll assume strength and fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn — bet for value if I complete, otherwise evaluate based on the turn card and opponent’s actions.

Minni's small bet of 250 into 400 is a weak sizing. I have ace high with backdoor nut flush draw. Getting nearly 3:1 odds (250 to win 650), I have implied odds if a diamond comes giving me the nut flush draw, or if an ace hits. Folding ace high to a half-pot bet here is too tight. Calling to re-evaluate turn.

Turn brought Kh. I have just ace-high on a 5d7sTcKh board - no pair, no flush draw. Minni showed interest on the flop with a small bet. If I bet here, I'm purely bluffing with very weak equity. Better to check and see what Minni does - if they check back I might win at showdown or can bluff river, if they bet I can fold cheaply.

I missed — the turn K does not improve my 8♣6♠. I still have an OESD (8 outs, ~17% to hit the river) but only one card to come and limited equity. Claude’s line (cutoff open, then check‑call on the flop) often contains single pairs (T,7, pocket pairs) and can include hands that will call a turn bluff; he may also have a K now. Betting here would be a pure semi‑bluff with poor odds and the risk of a raise that would force a fold out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity on the river, and re‑evaluate based on the river card and Claude’s action (bet for value if I complete, consider a river bluff only if the board and sizing are favorable).

River paired the 5s. Minni checked back the turn showing weakness. I have ace-high which might actually be good here against a missed draw or weak floating hand. Betting would turn my potential showdown winner into a bluff. Better to check and see if I can win with ace-high.

I missed my open‑ended straight (8♣6♠ on 5♦7♠T♣K♥5♥) and have no pair or showdown value on the river. Claude’s line (cutoff open, call on the flop, passive on the turn) looks like a single pair or other calling/weak value hands (T,7, small pocket pairs, or overcards) that will often call a river bluff — the board pairing reduces my credibility and fold equity. Betting would be a pure bluff with poor expected value and the risk of a costly raise. I’ll check and preserve chips; if Claude fires a sizable bet I’ll fold rather than inflate the pot with nothing.

7sJh is a weak offsuit hand from UTG in a 6-handed game. Early position requires a tight range; J7o has poor playability post-flop, low equity against typical calling ranges, and puts me out of position. Folding preserves stack for premium hands to maximize long-term EV in tournament.
AQo is a premium hand in middle position. With Elon already folded, I should raise for value. Standard open raise to 2.5-3x BB. I'll make it 300 to build the pot and isolate or take down the blinds.

Claude has opened to 300 from MP. I'm on the cutoff with 4♥5♠ — a weak, low, unsuited connector with poor post‑flop playability and high likelihood of being dominated. Calling 300 into a pot of 450 would commit a large portion of the pot out of position against the button and the blinds (Button 15950, SB 7800, BB 5100) and leaves me vulnerable to squeezes or multiway pots where my hand performs poorly. A small squeeze/raise to 500 would be a bluff with very little fold equity and invites 3‑bets from stronger ranges. With 82bb effective and plenty of better spots to pick later, folding and preserving stack equity is the right play.

Claude’s 3bb open from middle position looks fairly standard and he’s been active. On the Button with J9o, we have enough playability and strong positional advantage to defend, but it’s not strong enough to happily 3-bet given the shorter stacks in the blinds (especially BB), where a jam/squeeze would force us to burn chips. Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets us realize equity in position, and we can outplay postflop on favorable textures while comfortably folding to heavy pressure on dominated boards.

I hold 2c 6c in the Small Blind. Facing a raise from Claude (Middle) to 300 and a call from FiveTwo (Button), the pot odds are attractive (calling 250 to win a pot that will be at least 1000), offering 4:1 odds. However, my hand is extremely weak (low disconnected suited cards), and I would be playing out of position against at least two opponents. The risk of domination and the difficulty of realizing equity out of position outweigh the immediate price. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 4s. Claude raised to 300 from middle position and FiveTwo called on the button. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot of 750. While the pot odds are roughly 3.75 to 1, 84 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that plays very poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. My stack is also the shortest at the table, so I should avoid bloating pots with marginal holdings and wait for better spots to commit chips.
I flopped top pair with a queen kicker on Ad-9h-Kc. This is a strong hand heads-up. I should c-bet for value - betting around 2/3 pot to get called by worse aces, kings, or draws.

We have second pair (9x) with a weak kicker on an A-K-9 flop versus a standard MP range that contains a lot of Ax/Kx that has us crushed. However, Claude’s c-bet is small (~40% pot), and in position we can profitably continue by calling to realize equity and keep in his bluffs (broadways like QJ/JT, some underpairs) while avoiding turning our hand into a bluff with a raise that mostly gets action from better. Plan: call once, then proceed cautiously—fold to substantial turn/river pressure on bricks, continue versus small bets or favorable runouts (9/J improving, or checked turns allowing us to control showdown).

The Qh on the turn gave me top two pair (Aces and Queens). This is an excellent hand. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet, likely holding Ax, Kx, or a draw. With two pair I should bet for value - sizing around 65% pot to extract from Kx, weaker Ax, and draws like JT that now have broadway potential.

Turn Qh is a very strong card for Claude’s barreling range: it improves his value region (AQ, AK, KQ, QQ) and also completes the very natural JT straight (which he can have from MP). Our hand is just 2nd pair (9x) with a weak kicker and only a small amount of clean improvement (two remaining 9s for trips + 4 tens for Broadway). Facing ~63% pot, we’d need ~28% equity to continue, and we don’t have it often enough versus a range that’s now heavily weighted to Ax/KQ+ and made straights. Calling also sets us up to face another tough river bet and realize equity poorly. Best play is to fold and preserve stack.
QJs is a solid opening hand from UTG - suited broadway cards with good playability and straight/flush potential. Standard open to 2.5x.

I have 7♥5♦ — a weak, unconnected offsuit hand out of position facing an UTG open to 250. The current pot is 400 and calling 250 requires ~38.5% equity (250/(400+250)), which 7-5o does not have against a typical UTG opening range (true equity is well under that heads-up and gets worse multiway). There are still players to act who may call or 3-bet, putting me in difficult spots out of position. With a deep stack I should preserve chips for better spots rather than spew with a marginal holding, so I fold.

Claude’s UTG open represents a relatively strong range, and QcTc—while playable—can be dominated when we make one-pair (KQ/AQ/QJ). In the Cutoff we have excellent position and good playability with a suited, connected hand, so we can realize equity without inflating the pot. A 3-bet here would often just bloat the pot against a range that continues well, putting us in tougher spots. Flatting keeps weaker parts of his range in and lets us navigate postflop with position and implied odds when we flop strong draws/two pair/straights.

I hold 5s Th on the Button. This is a statistically weak hand (unconnected, unsuited, low ranks). Facing an UTG raise from Claude and a call from the Cutoff (FiveTwo), the pot is multi-way and contested by stronger ranges. Despite having position, my hand has very poor playability and is easily dominated. There is no strategic merit in calling or bluffing here given the action and stack sizes. Folding is the correct decision.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 7s. Claude (UTG) raised to 250, and FiveTwo (Cutoff) called. The pot is 650 and it costs me 200 more to call. While the pot odds are roughly 3.25-to-1, 9-7 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against two active opponents, one of whom is the initial UTG raiser and the other a loose-aggressive big stack. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have positional advantage or stronger equity.

84d is a mediocre suited connector. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (tight-aggressive player based on history) and a flat-call from FiveTwo (loose-aggressive), the range is strong enough that multiway OOP post-flop play will be difficult. Pot odds are 650:150 (~4.3:1, needing ~19% equity), but equity realization is low with low cards that play poorly multiway. Deep stacks don't justify speculative call without position or isolation. Discipline: fold and wait for premium spots to maximize stack long-term.

Flopped top pair (jacks) with queen kicker plus an open-ended straight draw (9 gives me Q-J-T-9-8). Board has two spades creating a flush draw. Need to bet for value and protection. Betting around 60% pot.

We flop top pair (T) with a decent kicker (Q) plus an inside straight draw to the 9 and a backdoor club draw. Claude’s c-bet is small (~40% pot), giving us an easy continue with plenty of equity and good positional control. Raising would bloat the pot versus an UTG range that can have overpairs/sets/strong Jx and may respond with a 3-bet, forcing us into tougher spots with a hand that’s good but not strong enough to stack off. Calling keeps his bluffs and worse draws in, lets us realize equity, and we can apply pressure on favorable turns (9, Q, club runouts, or when he checks).

Turn is a blank 2d. I have top pair with queen kicker plus a gutshot (need 9). I hold the Qs which blocks some flush draws. FiveTwo has been calling, likely on a draw or weaker made hand. Should continue betting for value and to charge flush/straight draws. Betting ~65% pot.

Claude’s turn barrel is still a relatively small sizing (950 into 2400), so we’re getting strong direct odds (~22%) to continue. Our hand has decent equity versus his UTG double-barrel range: we have a pair of tens with a Q kicker (some showdown value) plus a gutshot to the 9 for the straight. Raising would bloat the pot against a range that continues heavily with overpairs/strong Jx/sets and could 3-bet jam, putting us in a tough spot with a medium-strength hand. In position, the best line is to call and re-evaluate rivers—value-betting when we improve (9/T/J/8/2) and folding more often versus a large third barrel on blanks.

River paired the board with 8c. I have top pair jacks with queen kicker (JJ88Q). The board pairing is concerning - if FiveTwo has an 8 they now have trips. Their calling pattern suggests either a draw (now missed) or a medium-strength made hand. Betting for value is risky since worse hands fold and better hands (trips, straights, boats) call/raise. Checking to induce bluffs from missed draws and control pot size against hands that beat me is the better play.

Claude’s line (UTG open, small flop c-bet, turn barrel, then river check) heavily weights toward one-pair hands that want showdown (AA/KK/QQ, sometimes 99) and some give-ups (Q9-type gutters, AQ/KQ that barreled once more). On 8-J-T-2-8 our hand is two pair (Tens and Eights), which is ahead of all overpairs and most Tx, but behind his Jx (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT) and boats/quads. Since he checked, we should go for thin value with a sizing that gets crying calls from overpairs while keeping ourselves safe versus a check-raise (which will be very value-heavy here). If raised, we can comfortably fold.

FiveTwo bet 1100 into 3350 (~33% pot) after I checked. This small sizing suggests either thin value or a bluff with missed draws (spades, straight draws like Q9/K9). I have top pair with queen kicker (JJ88Q). Pot odds are excellent - need ~20% equity to call. I beat all bluffs and possibly thin value from weaker jacks. The small bet size isn't consistent with a monster like trips or a boat wanting max value. Easy call.

UTG in a 6-max table with J8s is too marginal to open. From UTG I need a much tighter range — this is a speculative, easily-dominated hand that plays poorly out of position in likely multiway pots. Several players behind (FiveTwo, Flash, Claude) have shown frequent preflop aggression and big 3-bets, so limp/calling 100 invites isolation or a 3‑bet and raising to 200 is asking for difficult spots OOP. With an 82bb stack and plenty of hands left, preserve chips and wait for a better situation. Fold.

UTG has folded and we’re in MP 125bb deep. A6o is borderline but still viable as a light open in 6-max when the pot is unopened: we have an ace blocker to reduce the chance we run into premium Ax/AA, and we can often take it down pre or play in position versus the blinds. Limping is too passive and invites multiway domination; folding is fine but gives up profitable steal opportunities against players who have shown a lot of folding pre. I’ll open to a standard sizing that discourages loose flats and keeps SPR manageable, and I’m comfortable folding if faced with a sizable 3-bet from later position/blinds.

I hold 7d 5d in the Cutoff facing a 2.5x raise from FiveTwo in Middle Position. FiveTwo has been quite active, often opening the pot. While 75s is a playable speculative hand with flush and straight potential, calling here leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the Button and Blinds, and I would be playing a marginal hand against an MP opening range which is typically stronger than late position opens. In standard strategy, 75s is usually a fold facing an MP open. With 78 BBs, I have plenty of chips and do not need to force speculative spots. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and prudent option.

I have a small pocket pair (22) on the button. FiveTwo raised to 2.5x BB from middle position. With a stack of 5050 chips, I have sufficient implied odds (roughly 20:1) to set-mine. Position on the button is a significant advantage if I miss but want to take a stab at the pot later, or if I hit and want to maximize value. A call is standard here to see a flop cheaply.

Preflop in SB with QhTd offsuit facing an MP raise to 2.5BB and a BTN call. Pot 6.5BB, to call 2BB for ~19% equity needed, but QTo has poor playability OOP, mediocre high card strength, and gappy connectors. FiveTwo is aggressive but ranges likely strong enough to fold this. BB still to act and can squeeze. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 150 hands ahead.

QcJc is a strong suited broadway hand. Getting 4.3:1 pot odds (call 150 into 650 pot) with suited connectors that can make strong flushes and straights is an easy call. Could 3-bet but flatting to see a flop multiway with position on both opponents postflop is solid.

I flopped the nut flush draw (Qc Jc with Ac on board). I have 9 clean outs to the nut flush plus backdoor straight possibilities. With position and initiative, this is an excellent spot to semi-bluff. Betting around 60% pot to put pressure on opponents and build the pot for when I hit.

We flatted pre and see A♣9♣3♠ multiway. With A♠6♥ we have top pair but a weak kicker and no club, so we’re not excited to inflate the pot versus a BB donk that can contain better Ax (A9/A3/AJ+), plus strong club draws. Raising mostly folds out worse and gets action from better, and it also leaves us vulnerable with the button still to act (Flash can continue aggressively with draws/strong aces given his shorter stack). We have plenty of equity and good pot odds to continue, so the best line is to call and re-evaluate turns (especially vs big barrels on bad runouts like clubs/9/3).

On the flop of A-9-3, I have bottom set (22). Claude, the Big Blind, has led out for 500 into a pot of 800. FiveTwo, the original raiser, called the 500. This is a very interesting spot. An Ace is a prominent card on the board, and as the pre-flop caller on the button, my range usually doesn't have many strong Aces, but my opponents might. Claude's lead into two players often signifies a pair (likely an Ace or a Nine) or a draw. FiveTwo's call suggests either a similar hand or a slow-played monster. My pocket deuces are currently just a low pair and are very likely behind any Ace or Nine. However, with 1800 in the pot and needing to call 500, I am getting roughly 3.6:1 odds. Given the action, it's highly probable I'm looking for one of the two remaining deuces to improve, which is unlikely (approx 4.3%). While I have position, calling here against two opponents who have already shown interest on an Ace-high board with only a weak pair is a losing play long-term. I will fold.

The 2c on the turn completed my nut flush! I have QcJc with 4 clubs on board (Ac 9c 3s 2c). Only Kc beats me. FiveTwo called my flop bet, so they likely have an ace, a flush draw that may have also hit, or a set. I should bet for value - around 60-65% pot to extract value from worse flushes, sets, and top pair hands while not scaring them off completely.

Turn brings the 3rd club, which is a very bad card for our exact hand (A6 with no club): Claude can now have a lot of natural flushes from BB defend/call lines, plus strong Ax (AJ+), A9/A3/A2, sets, and also some semi-bluffs that just got there. Raising is high-risk/low-reward because worse Ax without a club often folds and better continues/3-bets, and we don’t block the flush.

That said, folding top pair to a ~38% pot bet is too tight: we’re getting ~27% pot odds and Claude’s line can still include plenty of bluffs/semi-bluffs (one-club hands, pair+club, straight draws with a club) and thin value that we can beat on some rivers. Best is to call and play rivers carefully: continue bluff-catching on bricks versus reasonable sizing, but be ready to fold to big river pressure especially on a club or other strong runouts (A/9/3/2 pairing dynamics depending on sizing).

I have the second nut flush (QcJc) on a 5-club board. Only Kc beats me. FiveTwo has called my flop and turn bets, suggesting they have either a flush draw that completed, a set, or strong Ax. This is a clear value betting spot - I need to bet enough to extract value from smaller flushes and strong hands but not so much that only Kc calls. Betting around 65-70% pot (2600-2800) is optimal to get called by worse flushes and strong hands.

River puts a 4th club on board, and with A♠6♥ we have zero club blockers and only one-pair. Claude’s line (lead/raise flop into two players, barrel turn on the 3rd club, then bet again when the 4th club arrives) is overwhelmingly consistent with hands that contain a single club that just became a flush (or already was a strong draw), and those hands now value-bet thinly because we’ll still sometimes pay off with Ax. The bluff region shrinks massively on this runout since many natural semi-bluffs got there and it’s hard for him to credibly triple-barrel no-club hands into a range that can have plenty of one-club holdings. We’re getting ~28% pot odds, but against a flush-heavy betting range our equity is far below that, so this is a disciplined fold.

6-handed UTG is relatively wide, and 9h8h is a strong suited connector with good playability and implied odds when deeper. Raising is preferred to limping to (1) deny equity to the blinds, (2) give us fold equity to win the blinds, and (3) define ranges against several opponents who have shown aggression. I’ll use a standard small open that keeps my risk low while still pressuring the blinds; if faced with a big 3-bet or a shove from the shorter stacks, I can comfortably fold this hand.

I hold Jack of Diamonds and Five of Clubs (J5o), which is a statistically very weak hand. Facing a raise to 250 from the Under-The-Gun player (FiveTwo), who has been aggressive, I have no reason to get involved with such poor holdings. The hand is unsuited and disconnected, offering very little playability or equity against an opening range. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

I am in the Cutoff with Queen-Jack offsuit. FiveTwo, who has been opening a significant number of pots from various positions, has raised to 250 from UTG. While QJo is a decent hand, it is frequently dominated by a standard UTG opening range (hands like AQ, AJ, KQ, or big pairs). Additionally, there are three active players behind me (Elon, Claude, and Minni) who have shown aggression in previous hands and could potentially squeeze if I just call. Given my stack of 4800 chips, I prefer to wait for a stronger hand or a better situation to commit chips rather than playing a marginal hand out of position against a potentially strong UTG range or facing a three-bet behind me.

Qh4h is a speculative suited hand with good playability for straights and flushes. Position on the button is excellent for realizing equity post-flop. FiveTwo is a frequent and aggressive raiser from early position based on hand history (raises/3bets often: hands 3,6,8,11,12,17,19,24,27,30,31,38), suggesting a wide range that I can exploit with position. Pot odds are favorable (calling 250 into 400 pot, plus implied odds). Folding is too tight against this opponent; 3-betting light risks too much without great blockers. Calling keeps the pot multi-way potentially and allows set-mining/flush-chasing in position. Blinds are deep but will act after; prepared to fold to a squeeze but expect to see flops often profitably long-term.

KTs is a solid hand in the small blind. FiveTwo opened from UTG and Elon called on the button. I'm getting about 3.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 650). KTs plays well multiway with good straight and flush potential. Calling is better than 3-betting since I'll be out of position - I prefer to see a flop cheaply rather than bloat the pot OOP.

UTG raised to 250 and both SB and Button have called — pot is 850 and I must call 150 to see the flop. I have J♠6♦: a weak, easily-dominated offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability in a multiway pot. Calling 150 into a 1000 pot (after my call) gives ~15% break-even equity; against an UTG open and two callers my actual equity with J6o will be below that and I’ll have little fold equity or implied odds. A light 3-bet would be thin and ill-advised out of position. With a healthy 81bb stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Flopped top two pair (kings and tens) on K-T-9 board. This is a very strong hand. Board has straight draw potential (QJ has OESD). I should bet for value and protection. Two opponents still in the pot. Betting around 60-65% pot to build value and charge draws.

Claude’s small lead into a multiway pot on K-T-9 is often a wide range (Kx/Tx/9x, some QJ/J8 type draws, and occasional stabs). With 9h8h we have middle pair with some backdoor improvement (runner-runner straight/flush) and enough showdown value that folding is too tight, but we’re not strong enough to raise and build a pot vs a range that continues mostly with better hands. Calling keeps worse hands/bluffs in, controls pot size, and lets us re-evaluate if Elon applies pressure behind or on later streets.

Preflop call with Qh4h on the button was speculative, leveraging position and FiveTwo's wide UTG opening range for implied odds and playability. However, the flop of 9s Td Kc completely misses my hand: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (Q4 has zero straight potential here; at best a backdoor gutshot to a wheel unlikely). Board favors strong made hands like KT, KJ, QJ, TT+, pairs with draws, or K9/T9. Claude's lead as SB (loose calling range pre) into a dry board, followed by FiveTwo's flat (tight UTG range likely has showdown value), suggests strength. Pot odds (550 into 1950 ~28%) insufficient without equity. Continuing risks dead money with a bluff catcher bluff at best; folding preserves stack for better spots. Deep stacks but no fold equity post-flop justifies check-fold spots like this.

I have top two pair (K-T) on a K-T-9-5 board with two spades. The turn 5s added a flush draw possibility. FiveTwo called my flop bet, likely holding a draw (QJ straight draw, spade flush draw), a weaker king, or possibly a set. My hand is still very strong and I should continue betting for value and protection. Betting around 70% pot to charge draws and extract value from weaker made hands.

Claude is donk-leading flop and then firing a fairly chunky turn barrel on a K-T-9-5 texture. With 9h8h we’ve basically just got a marginal one-pair bluff-catcher with very little equity to improve (mostly only an 8 or 9 helps materially), and we’re in bad shape versus the value-heavy portion of his range (Kx, Tx, two pair/sets, and QJ which also has strong equity). Getting ~29% pot odds isn’t enough when his line is much more often value/protection than a high-frequency bluff, and continuing sets us up for tough river decisions with poor implied odds. Preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with Kd 4d. This is a suited King, but the kicker is very weak. In a 6-max game, opening K4s from UTG is generally too loose as it is dominated by many calling hands (better Kings, Aces) and plays poorly out of position against 3-bets. With a healthy stack of 78 BBs, there is no need to force action with marginal holdings in early position. The standard play is to fold.

I am holding 5c 6h in middle position (HJ). With the shortest stack at the table (48 big blinds) and a very weak hand, there is no reason to enter the pot. Standard strategy dictates folding such low offsuit connectors from this position to preserve stack for higher-equity situations.

Preflop, in cutoff with folds from UTG and MP, Td5d is a standard open-raise hand due to suited connector potential for straights/flushes and good playability in position. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots without initiative. Raising to 3x BB (300) is standard sizing for position and pot odds, maintaining fold equity against blinds while allowing postflop control. Opponents like FiveTwo defend BB wide based on history, but my deep stack (111BB) and position mitigate that. Claude on button is aggressive but I have initiative.

K4o is a weak hand facing a raise from the cutoff. Even with button position, this hand plays poorly post-flop - when I hit a King I'm often dominated, and when I hit a 4 it's rarely good. Fold is the correct play here.

Pocket queens in the small blind vs a cutoff open to 300 is too good to just call and play OOP multiway. I want to 3-bet for value and to isolate Elon while charging speculative hands and weaker pairs — also to avoid playing a big pot out of position. A raise to 1200 (about 3.5–4x the open) is a standard sizing: big enough to fold out lots of marginal hands and punish callers, but leaves us plenty of stack to play postflop. If I face a small 3-bet I’ll often call and proceed postflop; against a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate (QQ can call sizable 3-bets but is cautious versus shove-sized pressure depending on villain).

We’re in the BB with 87o facing a CO open and a SB 3-bet to 1200. This hand has poor high-card value, is easily dominated, and plays badly multiway and out of position in a low-SPR pot if we call. Minni’s 3-bet range here is weighted to strong broadways/pairs (even if some squeezes exist), and we don’t have the blockers or playability to justify a defend. 4-betting/jamming is too spewy with no relevant blockers and deep enough stacks. Best EV is to fold and preserve the stack.

Preflop in cutoff, I opened Td5d to 300, a standard suited connector play for position and playability. Minni's 3-bet to 1200 from SB (4x my raise) indicates a strong range: likely QQ+, AK, AQ, maybe some bluffs but weighted towards value given her aggressive history (e.g., Hand 3, Hand 11, Hand 15). Td5d has poor equity vs that range (~30-35% vs tight 3-bet), blockers minimal. Calling 900 into 2500 pot lays 2.8:1 but requires hitting sets/straights/flushes hard postflop, risky OOP for her and multiway potential gone with folds. 4-betting bluff too thin with deep stacks (her 69BB, me 108BB); fold equity low vs her sizing/stackoff willingness. Folding preserves stack for better spots (e.g., premium hands), +EV long-term in tournament with 110 hands left. History shows Minni values 3-bets; no need to speculate here.

I am UTG with 5c6h, which is a very weak hand to open from early position. Given my current stack size is the shortest at the table (48 BBs), I need to be more selective with my starting hands to avoid bleeding chips in unprofitable situations. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

89o is a marginal speculative hand at best, offsuit and disconnected. In middle position with 5 players to act behind (including tight/aggressive players like Claude with a big stack), folding preflop is standard. No position advantage, deep stacks (mine 108x BB), and pot is small (1.5x BB). Prefer premium or suited connectors in position for multiway potential. Preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 110 hands left.

I have pocket 9s in the cutoff position with two folds before me. This is a premium hand that I should definitely raise for value. Standard open raise to 2.5x the BB (250) is appropriate here to build the pot while not bloating it too much with a medium pair.

I have Q♠T♥ on the button facing a cutoff open to 250. Claude (CO) has been opening fairly wide and my position is valuable — QTo has decent playability and blockers versus his range. I'll call 250 to see a flop in position: calling keeps the pot controllable, avoids isolating myself against deep stacks and a potential 3-bet/squeeze from the blinds, and lets me realize my equity post‑flop. Plan: proceed aggressively with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy resistance when I miss and face significant action.

We’re in the small blind facing a CO open (2.5x) and a BTN flat, so our hand will be out of position multiway with poor realization. T8o is dominated by a lot of the ranges that continue (Tx, 8x, broadways), and calling here tends to create reverse implied odds spots where we make second-best pairs. The price is tempting, but with the BB still behind and the difficulty of navigating postflop OOP, folding is the higher-EV, lower-variance choice.

I am in the Big Blind with J3s facing a raise from the Cutoff and a call from the Button. The pot odds are attractive (calling 150 to win a total pot of 800, requiring ~18.75% equity). However, my hand is very weak and disconnected. Being out of position against two opponents significantly reduces my ability to realize my raw equity, which is likely around 20-22% against their ranges. Defending J3s multi-way is usually -EV due to reverse implied odds (dominance issues with Jacks) and difficulty playing draws OOP. A fold is the disciplined, standard play here.

I have pocket 9s on an A52 rainbow board. Minni called my preflop raise, so they could easily have an Ace. My 99 is an underpair to the Ace on board. Betting here only gets called by better hands (any Ax) while folding out worse. Best play is to check back for pot control and try to get to showdown cheaply.

Flop: 5♦ A♥ 2♣. I hold Q♠ T♥ — no pair and no meaningful draw, but I have position and Claude opened wide from the cutoff and then checked the flop, which signals weakness frequently. This is a very dry board with lots of missed hands in his range, so I can generate fold equity by c-betting rather than just giving up. I'll make a half-pot-sized continuation bet (~325) to pressure his air and weaker hands while keeping the pot controllable. Plan: fold to a significant raise (I have almost no showdown value), call small/noise calls and then proceed cautiously on the turn (check and re-evaluate unless I pick up a real improvement).

I have pocket 9s on A52 rainbow. Minni bet 325 into 650 (half pot) after I checked. This is a small probe bet. While Minni could have an Ace, they could also be probing with missed broadway hands or small pairs. Getting 4:1 pot odds, I only need to be good ~25% of the time. Folding 99 to one small bet seems too weak. I'll call and reassess on the turn.

Turn paired the board with 5s. Minni checked to me after betting the flop. With 99 on A552, I have an overpair to the 5s but underpair to the Ace. Minni checking suggests they may not have a strong Ace or are giving up. Betting here only gets called by Ax hands that beat me - checking maintains pot control and lets me get to showdown with my showdown value. No need to bloat the pot.

I have Q♠T♥ with no pair and no real draw. I c‑bet the flop and Claude called, so his calling range includes Ax, 5x and medium pairs; the 5♠ on the turn pairs the board and improves many hands that already beat me. Claude checked to me, which can be pot‑control with top pair or a trap with a 5 — betting here risks a check‑raise and committing with almost no showdown value. I’ll check to keep the pot controlled (pot ~1,300, my stack 7,925) and fold to significant aggression; I can reassess on the river if he shows weakness.

River completes A2345 on board with paired 5s. Minni's line (bet flop, check turn) suggests they may have been probing with a weak hand rather than a strong Ace. My 99 has showdown value but betting only gets called by Ax which beats me. Checking back is optimal - I get to showdown against their potential bluffs/weaker pairs while avoiding getting raised by better hands.

Board: 5♦ A♥ 2♣ 5♠ 4♠. I hold Q♠T♥ and never improved — I c‑bet the flop and Claude called, then checked turn and checked river. His line looks like Ax (pot‑control), 5x (slowplay), or a medium pair; given that and my lack of showdown value, a river bluff has minimal fold equity and risks a check‑raise from a deep, calling opponent. I keep the pot controlled and protect my stack: check. If Claude fires a meaningful bet I will fold to preserve chips.

Pre-flop UTG in 6-handed play with 89o (offsuit connectors). This is a standard fold from early position as it plays poorly post-flop out of position against a wide calling range. No strong history or dynamics justify deviating; prioritize position and hand strength to maximize long-term stack growth in tournament.
J6 offsuit is a weak hand in middle position. Not worth opening - standard fold.

Cutoff with K♥4♥ (suited King). This is a marginal but playable hand in position with ~79bb effective — better to take the initiative than limp/call and invite multiway action where K4s performs poorly. Table standard open is ~3x the BB; raising to 300 gives fold equity vs the blinds and the button while keeping post‑flop play manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (re‑evaluate vs a small 3‑bet from the button), c‑bet on favorable flops and play cautiously on dry, strong high‑card boards. 300 is a standard, practical open size here.

Minni opens to 3x from the cutoff and we’re on the button with T2 suited. Even with position, this is too weak and dominated (reverse implied odds when we make a ten), and the 3x sizing plus two players behind (SB/BB who can squeeze) makes a flat-call unattractive. Not a good candidate for a 3-bet bluff either because it has poor blockers and plays badly if called. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (AcKs) in the Small Blind, a premium hand that is significantly ahead of Minni's likely opening range from the Cutoff. Flat calling here is suboptimal as it leaves me out of position in a potentially multi-way pot if the Big Blind calls, and fails to build value with a strong hand. A 3-bet is necessary to isolate the opener, deny equity to the Big Blind, and build the pot for value. Given I am out of position and the stacks are relatively deep (~76bb), a larger 3-bet sizing is standard to discourage a call or extract maximum value. Raising to roughly 3.5x-4x the open is appropriate. I will raise to 1100.

I am holding 3-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Cutoff and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, there is no strategic justification for calling or raising. I will fold to preserve my stack.

I opened the cutoff to 300 with K♥4♥ and the small blind (Pro) 3-bet to 1,100, leaving me to call 800. K4s is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a typical SB 3‑bet range (strong Ks, pairs, AKo/AQs) and has poor multi‑street playability even though I would have position. The effective pot odds to call (~800/(1,500+800) ≈ 35%) require much more equity than K4s realistically has vs a value‑heavy 3‑bet. A thin 4‑bet bluff here is unnecessary and can be punished; preserve the stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.
J8o from UTG is a weak hand that's outside my opening range. This is an easy fold from early position - need stronger holdings to open here.

Qc8c is a playable, suited queen in MP with ~76BB. Limping invites the Button/Cutoff and the blinds to play and gives me no initiative; opening allows me to steal the blinds or play the flop as the aggressor. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — a standard open size that gives fold equity, keeps the pot manageable versus potential 3-bets, and realizes my postflop equity with clubs. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (call small 3-bets for position/SPR reasons, fold to heavy 4-bets).

We’re in the cutoff facing a middle-position open to 3bb. K2 offsuit has poor playability, is dominated by Minni’s continuing range (Kx, broadways, better suited hands), and doesn’t realize equity well multiway with the button and blinds still to act. A bluff 3-bet is unnecessary here given our deep stack and lack of blockers/robust postflop prospects. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Current hand is Q9o on the Button. Minni (MP) opened to 3bb (300). Q9o is a weak offsuit hand that is easily dominated by an MP opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ, 99+). Although I have position, the hand plays poorly post-flop, suffering from reverse implied odds. Calling requires a significant chip commitment without closing the action (blinds are still to act). 3-betting as a bluff is risky without better blockers. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from middle position. Given the hand strength and the fact that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a raiser, calling is not profitable. My stack is also the smallest at the table, so I need to preserve chips for better opportunities. Folding is the only sensible move.

A6s is a strong suited ace hand with excellent playability from the BB facing a single middle position raise after folds. We get 2.25:1 on the call (200 into 450), which is favorable given the hand's equity (~35-40% vs typical MP range), flush potential, and implied odds with stacks ~70BB deep. Postflop, we have position advantage in multi-street scenarios and can realize equity well OOP. Folding is too tight; 3-betting is possible for value/protection but calling keeps the pot multiway-controllable and exploits Minni's history of calling stations (e.g., multiple check-calls deep into hands). No strong history of Minni being super tight.

Heads-up on the flop with A6s on Ac8s9d board, giving me top pair top kicker (TPTK). Strong hand with showdown value, protection needed against straight draws (7T) and backdoor flush draw blockers. Board is somewhat coordinated but dry enough for value betting OOP against Minni's MP opening range (Ax, pairs 88+, suited connectors, broadways). Pot 650, effective stacks deep (~70BB for Minni). Betting ~60% pot (400) balances value from worse Ax/pairs/draws while keeping pot manageable if raised. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs; bet extracts value from her calling station tendencies seen in history (frequent check-calls deep into pots, e.g., Hands 7,11,14). Avoids overbetting early street.

I have Qc8c on A♣ 8♠ 9♦ — second pair with a decent queen kicker and a backdoor club-run possibility. Elon was the BB who just check‑called preflop and now makes a small c‑bet (400 into a 1050 pot), a sizing consistent with a wide range (Ax, 9x, pocket pairs, and bluffs). Raising here (min to 800 or larger) is thin: many A‑x hands and better pairs continue and will put me in uncomfortable spots, while a raise also folds out worse hands I want to keep. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves my positional advantage, and lets me realize equity and extract value from worse pairs/draws. I’ll call 400 and re‑evaluate on the turn — if I improve or the turn is a blank I can proceed cautiously; if an Ace hits and he barrels big I will give more weight to folding.

Turn brings Kd, completing diamond draw but adding K-high straight possibilities (QJ). My As6s remains very strong: TPTK blocking AA/AK/AQ/AT, plus nut spade flush draw (with 8s on board), backdoor clubs? Deep effective stacks (~70BB for Minni). Pot 1450, I act first OOP. Her MP raise + flop call range: Ax (weaker blocked), 88/99 sets, T7s/JT straight draws, broadway missed like KQ (now improved). History shows Minni calling station (frequent deep check-calls, e.g., Hands 4,7,11,14,26,30,35). Checking risks free river card to draws or check-raise; betting extracts value from worse Ax/pairs/draws while protecting, pot control if raised. Size ~2/3 pot (1000) balances value/thin value, denies equity, sets up river jam if check-called. Avoids smaller bet she can float cheaply.

I hold Qc8c — second pair (eights) with a decent kicker, but the K on the turn kills the backdoor club-run (only one card to come) and improves a lot of continuing hands. Elon (BB) check‑called preflop, c‑bet a small 400 on the flop and now bets 1000 on the turn; that line is wide and includes Ax, Kx, 9x, sets and some bluffs, but it also contains many hands that currently dominate me. The pot is 2,450 and it costs 1,000 to call, so I’m getting ~29% to call (1,000 / 3,450 ≈ 29%), which is in the right zone to see one more card with second pair in position. Raising here is thin — it would fold out some worse hands I want to keep and commit too much when I’m often behind. I’ll call 1,000 to realize equity, control the pot size, and use my positional advantage to re‑evaluate on the river; if Elon barrels big on the river or the board pairs A/K I’ll be prepared to fold.

River Jd completes a diamond flush for hands with two diamonds and Q10 straight draws, making the board very scary with three diamonds and straight potential. However, my As6s remains a strong top pair top kicker (pair of aces with ace kicker), blocking AA/AK/AQ/AT and many Ax hands in Minni's check-call range. Her MP open + two OOP check-calls on coordinated board suggest showdown value like weaker Ax (AJ, AT), two-pair (KJ, AJ now paired), sets (unlikely given sizing), or missed draws, consistent with her calling station history (frequent deep check-calls in Hands 4,7,11,14,26,30,35,41 without always showing nuts). Pot 3450, her stack 5925 (effective ~60BB), mine deep. OOP river, checking risks giving up value or facing a bluff/value bet I'm behind on (but she rarely leads); betting ~60% pot (2000) gets thin value from worse Ax/pairs/draws she stations with, while sizing denies cheap showdown and can fold to raise/shove from clear beats (flush/straight). Prior value betting streets worked without resistance, continue exploiting her tendencies in tournament mid-game to build stack. Avoid smaller (floatable) or larger (polarized/overbet) sizes.

I hold Qc8c — second pair (eights) with a decent kicker, but the river (A♣ 8♠ 9♦ K♦ J♦) makes my hand very vulnerable. Elon (BB) has shown a coherent, value‑oriented line: check/call preflop, small c‑bet on the flop, a larger bet on the turn, and now 2,000 on the river. That line is consistent with Ax, Kx, 9x, Jx, sets/two‑pair, straights (Q10/10Q) and diamond flushes — almost all of which beat my pair. Pot is 5,450; calling 2,000 would make the total pot ~7,450, so I’m getting ~26.8% pot odds. My hand’s equity versus a value‑heavy continuing range is well below that threshold, and a raise would be a thin bluff that likely gets called by better hands and commits too many chips. With ~59BB I should preserve chips for better spots rather than risking ~20BB here on a marginal second pair, so I fold.

Utg open with K♦6♣ is a weak, easily dominated hand. Offsuit K6 has poor playability out of position and gives negative implied odds in multiway pots; limping invites trouble and open-raising risks 3-bets from deeper stacks. With ~59BB there’s no need to force the action with a marginal hand—fold and wait for a better spot.

KQo is a strong, high-card Broadway hand in 6-max and plays well as an open from middle position. With no raise in front (just the blinds), I want to take initiative, build the pot when ahead of calling ranges, and apply pressure to the shorter stacks on the button/SB while still keeping the open size reasonable versus potential 3-bets from the blinds. I’ll open to a standard 2.5x.

I hold As4h in the Cutoff. The player in Middle Position (FiveTwo) has opened to 250 (2.5 BB). A4o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against an MP opening range which likely dominates me (better Aces, pairs). Calling would be -EV as it invites squeezes from players behind and leaves me with a marginal hand. 3-betting is a possibility as a bluff due to the Ace blocker, but A4o is at the bottom of the bluffing candidates and FiveTwo has shown capability to play back (e.g., 4-betting in Hand #10). Folding is the disciplined and correct standard play here.

I am on the button with 5h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. FiveTwo has opened from middle position to 250. Given that I have the shortest stack at the table (4,650) and this hand has almost no playability or showdown value, there is no justification for calling or 3-betting. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

Facing a middle position raise to 250 from FiveTwo, who has shown aggression in prior hands by frequently raising and putting pressure on opponents. My T5s is a suited connector with straight and flush potential, playable in the small blind due to excellent pot odds: calling 200 more into a 400 pot (roughly 3:1 direct, better post-BB action) with deep effective stacks (~9k from FiveTwo). BB still to act, potentially multiway, which favors speculative hands. Folding is too tight OOP with these odds and playability; 3-betting is too strong for a marginal suited hand without position. Calling to realize equity, control pot size, and exploit implied odds if I connect.

KJs is a premium hand in the BB. FiveTwo opened to 250 and Elon called from SB. With a suited broadway hand, I should 3-bet for value. I have strong equity and can build a pot with this holding. A raise to around 900 is appropriate sizing - roughly 3.5x the open.

We opened KQo and face a BB squeeze to 900 after the SB completed. KQo is too strong to open-fold here given the price (650 more) and our excellent postflop position on both blinds. Claude’s sizing and the dead money from Elon can widen Claude’s 3-bet range (more squeezes/polar bluffs), while a 4-bet with KQo (offsuit) would bloat the pot against a range that continues with hands that dominate us (AK/AQ/KQ/QQ+) and forces us into tough 4-bet-pot spots. I’ll call, keep in the weaker parts of both ranges, and use position to realize equity and navigate postflop.

Facing a 3-bet from Claude in the BB after I called FiveTwo's MP open with T5s suited. Claude has been aggressive throughout the tournament, frequently raising and 3-betting to isolate or apply pressure, showing a wide range but capable of strong holdings. FiveTwo's flat call behind suggests a medium-strength hand that doesn't want to fold but isn't thrilled about the 3-bet. Pot is 2050, calling 650 OOP gives ~3.15:1 direct odds (2700:650), but multiway pot postflop with no position against two players is disastrous for a speculative suited connector like T5s, which relies on hitting strong draws or made hands to realize equity. Implied odds exist due to deep stacks, but the reverse implied odds are high if I hit a weak top pair or marginal draw OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots; continuing would bloat the pot further and put me at a massive positional and initiative disadvantage. Tighten up here to avoid speculative calls multiway OOP after action.

I whiffed this flop completely with KJs on 874 rainbow. However, as the 3-bettor with the initiative, I should c-bet at a reasonable frequency on this dry, low board. FiveTwo's calling range likely missed as well. I have two overcards and a backdoor spade draw. A small c-bet around 35% pot is appropriate - easy to give up if raised.

Claude’s flop c-bet is very small (700 into 2750 ~25%), which gives us an excellent price to continue in position. While KQo has no made hand here, we still have two overcards (can improve to Kx/Qx), plus some backdoor potential, and—importantly—we get to realize our equity and potentially take the pot away on later streets if Claude slows down. Folding to this sizing would overfold our range, and raising would turn a reasonable float into a higher-variance bluff without strong draw support. Call and reassess turn based on Claude’s sizing/texture changes.

Turn brought the 3h which doesn't change much. I have complete air with KJs - no pair, no draw. FiveTwo called my flop c-bet which shows some strength (likely a pair or draw). Barreling again with K-high into a player who called on this low board is too aggressive. I should check and pot control, potentially giving up or bluffing certain river cards.

Claude’s turn check after a tiny range c-bet is a strong indicator of range weakness (a lot of AK/AQ-type hands and some give-ups) and it also caps his range more than usual in a 3-bet pot. Our KQ has little immediate equity but we have a credible value range here (sets 88/77/44, 65s straight, strong 8x) and we can profitably take the pot now while denying free cards to overcards/heart draws. I’ll fire a medium stab that pressures his air and underpairs and sets up a reasonable river barrel if called.

I have KJs with no pair and no draw on 8d4s7h3h. FiveTwo called my 3-bet and flop c-bet, then bet 1800 when I showed weakness by checking turn. I have only ~12% equity with two overcards against any made hand. Pot odds require ~25% equity (1800/7050). This is a clear fold - continuing with K-high against a player showing consistent strength is burning chips.

6-handed UTG with K6o is too weak to open profitably at these stack depths: it’s dominated by the calling/3-bet ranges behind (better kings, broadways, suited aces) and plays poorly postflop out of position. With no dead money beyond blinds, we don’t need to fight for this pot—fold and preserve EV for stronger UTG opens.

I am in Middle Position with King-Deuce suited (Kd 2d). In a 6-handed game, this hand is generally considered too weak to open raise from this position, as it is easily dominated by better Kings and plays poorly post-flop against calling ranges from the Button or Blinds. With a stack of 81 BBs, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff position with 8h 3c, which is a very weak hand. Since it is folded to me and this hand has no potential, the standard and correct play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips, especially since my stack is around 46.5 big blinds and doesn't require desperation moves yet.

On the button with folds from UTG, MP, and CO, Q7s suited is a profitable spot to raise. Excellent position, suited connectors with queen high have good playability and equity against calling ranges. Aim to steal the 150 pot frequently or play postflop in position. Standard sizing 3x BB at 300 balances aggression and fold equity against blinds.

Holding 92o in the small blind facing a button raise. This is a trash hand with no playability - not suited, not connected, and both cards are weak. Even against a likely steal attempt from the button, 92o is too weak to defend. Easy fold.

Facing a straightforward button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with Js4d. To call I must put in 200 into a 450 pot (need ~31% equity to break even). J4o is routinely dominated by a button’s open range, has poor playability and I’ll be out of position postflop. I could 3-bet as a light bluff sometimes, but Elon has shown frequent opens and deep stack — not a great spot to bluff-call or get into a bloated pot with this holding. With 58bb and plenty of future spots, the +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position with Q9o, which is a weak hand to open from early position in a 6-max game. My stack is healthy (81 BB), so there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings out of position. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand to avoid domination and difficult post-flop situations.

I am in Middle Position with Jack-Seven offsuit. This is a weak starting hand that does not warrant an open-raise or a call. With a stack of 4650 chips (46.5 BB), I should look for better spots and maintain my stack rather than playing sub-par hands out of position. I will fold.

Preflop in cutoff with UTG and MP folded, unopened pot. My hand is T3s, a marginal suited connector with poor high-card strength and limited straight potential due to the large gap. While late position allows for a wide raising range (typically 25-35% including low suited connectors like T6s+), T3s is too weak equity-wise against calling ranges from button (Claude aggressive), SB (Minni 58BB), and BB (FiveTwo deep). Postflop playability is mediocre; vulnerable to domination and poor when hitting. Opponents show passive tendencies in history (many limps/calls), but raising risks multiway pots or 3-bets. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 105 hands left. Deep effective stacks (125BB) favor tight preflop to maximize EV.
42o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even on the button with only blinds left to act, this hand doesn't have enough equity to profitably open. Easy fold.

Heads-up from the small blind with K♦5♥ and ~57BB effective. This is a marginal but perfectly playable heads-up hand — I want to take the initiative rather than limp/call and be OOP on a cheap flop. FiveTwo (BB, 114BB) has shown a willingness to defend, so I size to get some fold equity but keep the pot reasonable. I will open to 300 total (≈3×BB), which is big enough to credibly steal the blind and discourage the widest defense, but small enough to fold to a 3-bet without committing a large portion of my stack. If called I plan to c‑bet favorable flops; if 3‑bet I will fold K5o OOP.

Minni is opening from the small blind after it folds around, which is typically a very wide range. Q9 suited is a solid BB defend: it has good playability (suited, decent high-card value, can make strong top pairs and flushes) and we’re getting a great price—200 to win a 400 pot (3:1) with deep enough stacks to realize equity. A 3-bet is possible, but Q9s doesn’t want to face a 4-bet and we’re out of position; calling keeps the pot manageable while defending appropriately versus a likely wide SB open.

I opened preflop to take the initiative and got called by the BB. Heads‑up on a dry 7♦‑2♣‑8♥ flop is a good continuation‑bet spot. I have K♦5♥ — no pair but a high card and a blocker to some Kx hands. FiveTwo defends a wide range preflop, so a half‑pot c‑bet will fold many unpaired hands and collect a decent pot when ahead. I’ll bet 300 (≈50% pot). Plan: if checked around I take it down; if called I proceed with pot control and give up without improvement; if faced with a sizable raise I fold and preserve my stack.

We’re BB vs a very wide SB open, and this small 1/2-pot c-bet will also be wide. With Qc9c on 7d-2c-8h we have two overcards plus a backdoor club draw and some turn cards that let us apply pressure (Q/9, clubs, some high cards that weaken her small-pair region). Getting 3:1 (300 to win 900) we don’t need much equity, and a check-raise bluff here risks running into her many continuing hands (any 7x/8x/2x, pocket pairs) with an awkward stack depth. Calling keeps her range wide and preserves our ability to realize equity and bluff selectively on later streets.

I missed the turn — K♦5♥ has no pair and I was called on the flop, so FiveTwo’s continuing range likely contains pairs (8x/7x/2x/pocket pairs), T‑x, and some floats/draws. The T♠ on the turn helps that calling range (gives them more T‑x combos) and doesn’t improve my hand. A second-barrel bluff here has thin fold equity and will often be called by hands that already beat me or by draws that still have equity. With ~52BB effective and a 1,200 pot, pot control is the priority: check and see the river for a cheaper bluff or a chance to hit a King. If FiveTwo bets the turn, I’ll fold most non‑improving hands; if checked through I can decide on a river plan based on the card and their action.

Minni opened SB wide, c-bet small, then checked this T turn. That check is often a give-up/pot-control with a lot of A/K-highs and small/medium pairs that hate the overcard. As BB we credibly improve on this card (Tx, 98, JT, some slowplayed 8x) and can profitably stab. With Qc9c we also retain equity (J makes the straight; sometimes Q/9 pairs are best if she’s on overcards) but we don’t want to bloat the pot versus her continuing range (mostly 7x/8x/Tx). A smaller pressure bet targets her weakest hands while keeping us able to fold if she check-jams.

I have K♦5♥ and checked the turn after c‑betting the flop. Villain leads the turn for 500 into the 1,200 pot (current pot shown 1,700). Calling 500 here requires ~22.7% equity (500 / (1,200 + 2*500) ≈ 22.7%), but my hand has essentially only 3 clean outs (three kings) to make a one‑pair hand on the river — ~3/46 ≈ 6.5% equity. Villain called my flop c‑bet, which is consistent with a range containing many made hands (7x/8x/2x, pocket pairs, and Tx) plus some floats; the small turn bet can be both value and a probe, but it rarely folds out enough of that range to make a raise profitable. A raise to 1,000 as a bluff would be thin — I risk a large portion of my ~52BB stack with very little fold equity vs the hands that actually call flop and bet turn. Given the math, lack of real outs, and my earlier plan to fold most non‑improving hands to a turn bet, I preserve my stack and fold.

I'm holding pocket 3s in the Under-the-Gun position. While 33 is a marginal hand from early position in a 6-handed game, I have a healthy stack of 46.5 big blinds. Opening for a standard 3x raise allows me to take control of the hand or set-mine effectively if I get callers. If I face significant 3-bet aggression, I can evaluate based on the size, but for now, I want to represent a strong opening range.

Qs7d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by most UTG raising ranges. Flash's UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) indicates strength, and calling from middle position risks playing out of position postflop with a marginal hand. Pot odds (450 pot, 300 to call) are insufficient given the dead money and multiway potential with active players like Claude, FiveTwo behind. Effective stack with Flash is 4350, but folding preserves our 12,500 stack (125BB deep) for better spots in this long tournament (47/150 hands). Standard fold to avoid speculative calls that bleed chips long-term.

4c8c is a weak suited connector. Against an UTG open from Flash, this hand doesn't play well - it's too weak to profitably call or 3-bet. While suited, 84s is at the bottom of playable hands and we'd be out of position against the remaining players. Standard fold.

Holding 9♦3♦ on the button facing an UTG raise to 300 (pot 450). This is a very weak, low-playability suited hand. With the small blind and big blind still to act a call is likely to create a multiway pot or invite a reraise, and my equity vs an UTG raising range is poor despite the pot odds. I have ~52 BB — enough to wait for a better spot — and a 3-bet bluff here is too risky against an early-position raiser and active players behind. Fold to conserve chips and pick a better spot later.

Facing a 3x UTG open from Flash, 7♣5♥ is too weak to defend profitably from the small blind: we’ll be out of position postflop, have poor playability, and suffer heavy reverse implied odds versus a relatively strong opening range. The pot odds (250 into 450) aren’t enough to justify continuing, and a bluff 3-bet is poor here with no blockers and a hand that can’t continue versus a shove/call range. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with A9o facing a 3bb (300) open from the UTG player (Flash). The UTG range is typically strong (top 10-15% of hands), often containing better Aces (AK-AT) which dominate mine. However, getting pot odds of roughly 3.25:1 (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650), I only need about 23-24% realized equity. A9o has approximately 36% raw equity against a standard tight range. While being out of position makes realizing this equity harder, folding A9o here is too tight given the price and stack depth (80bb). I will call to see a flop, planning to play cautiously on Ace-high boards due to kicker trouble.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against an Under-the-Gun raiser on a K-4-7 board with two diamonds. My hand (A9o) has missed the board entirely, leaving me with only Ace-high and no draws. This board texture (King-high) favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (which includes AK, KQ, AA, KK). Leading out (donk betting) would serve no purpose as it folds out bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If faced with a continuation bet, I will likely fold given my lack of equity and position.

I opened from UTG with pocket 3s and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes King-high with a flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor from early position, this board favors my range significantly, as I have all the strong Kings (AK, KQ). Although my pocket 3s have little showdown value and are vulnerable to almost any turn card, a continuation bet here allows me to take down the pot immediately if the opponent missed. If I am called, I will likely check back on the turn unless the board improves my hand.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with Ace-high (A9o) on a King-high board (Kd 4d 7s). The opponent, who opened from UTG (indicating a strong range), has made a continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 650. The board texture heavily favors the UTG range, which contains many combos of AK, KQ, AA, KK, and set candidates like 77. My hand has very low equity against this value range and lacks any flush or straight draws to improve. Even against potential bluffs (like AQ/AJ), I am out of position and will struggle to realize equity. Floating here with no backdoor draws is a losing play. The pot odds of 24% are insufficient given the poor playability and range disadvantage.

Pocket sevens is a strong hand UTG in 6-handed play. Standard to raise for value and to thin the field. Opponents have varying stacks, but mine is deep enough. No pre-flop action yet, so raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot while maintaining fold equity. History shows aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Claude, but 77 plays well post-flop and we want initiative.

9Toff is a marginal hand facing an UTG raise. UTG opening ranges are typically tight, and 9To doesn't play well out of position against that range. While I have a healthy stack, this hand doesn't have enough playability to justify calling 300. Fold is the disciplined play.

Facing an UTG open to 300 with K♦3♣ in the cutoff. This hand is weak and heavily dominated by a reasonable UTG raising range in a 6‑hand game. Calling 300 into a 450 pot (~3 BB to win ~750) requires ~40% equity — K3o has far less vs an UTG range — and calling leaves me with a poor SPR and difficult postflop decisions. A light 3‑bet would be very thin and risky with multiple players yet to act. Preserve my ~52 BB stack for better spots and fold.

We’re on the button with KK versus an UTG open to 3bb. This is a clear value 3-bet: we want to build a pot against UTG’s strong opening range (dominating QQ/JJ/AK), deny equity to the blinds, and avoid going multiway where our overpair realization drops. With ~120bb effective versus UTG, a standard in-position 3x sizing is best to keep worse hands in while still applying pressure.
