Plan: if UTG 4-bets, we’re happy to continue aggressively (often 5-bet jam depending on size).

I hold Ten of Diamonds and Three of Hearts in the Small Blind. The action ahead involves an UTG open to 300 and a Button 3-bet to 900. My hand is extremely weak (T3o), and I am facing significant strength from two opponents while being out of position. Cold calling a 3-bet with this holding is a losing play. The only correct decision is to fold immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 8h. Elon opened from UTG to 300, and FiveTwo 3-bet from the button to 900. Given the strength shown by an UTG open and a button 3-bet, my hand is significantly behind their likely ranges. Calling 800 out of position with a marginal king is not profitable, and 4-betting is too high-risk given the action. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Pocket 77 remains very strong facing a button 3-bet after UTG open. Button ranges are wide here (Ax, broadways, suited gappers, smaller pairs) due to position, and FiveTwo has shown aggression in history (frequent raises/3-bets). Folding is too tight; calling keeps pot multiway potential low but gives up initiative OOP post-flop. 4-betting polarizes our range, gets value from worse, fold equity from bluffs/lights, and builds pot with our premium hand. Sizing to 2400 (about 2.7x the 3-bet) is standard deep-stack size, leaving FiveTwo pot-committed if called while maintaining fold equity. Deep stacks allow post-flop flexibility if flat-called. Maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

We 3-bet KK in position and now face an UTG 4-bet to 2400 with ~100bb effective. With Kings, we’re far ahead of Elon’s value 4-bet range except AA, and the SPR will be low if we just call anyway. Shoving maximizes value versus QQ/JJ/AK, denies equity to hands like AK that can realize well on A-high boards, and prevents awkward postflop spots where we’re effectively committed. If he only continues with AA sometimes, that’s the cooler we live with; otherwise we get called by plenty of worse hands or occasionally take it down preflop.

Facing a massive 5-bet shove from the button after my UTG open and 4-bet with 77. FiveTwo's sizing to 11,150 total (leaving themselves only 900 behind) into a pot of ~3,450 is extremely strong and polarizing. Their range here is likely QQ+, AKo, maybe JJ/TT at best, against which 77 is a significant underdog (~20-30% equity). While FiveTwo is LAGgy in history (frequent 3-bets, value betting, some bluffs shown down), a 5-bet of this size over a 4-bet is not a light play; no prior 5-bets observed from them. Calling 8,750 commits my remaining 10,100 stack (leaving 1,350), with both players tiny-stacked post-call (~1k left), making it effectively all-in preflop. Pot odds are ~1.6:1 but insufficient given equity realization. Shoving all-in adds no value as FiveTwo can only call 900 more. Folding preserves my deep 100+BB stack (10,100) for superior spots in the tournament; 77 plays well multiway but OOP vs button aggression post-flop if somehow continued, but here it's crushed. Discipline > heroism; live to play the 102 remaining hands.
96o from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and I'm in the worst position at the table. No reason to open this.

9♦5♦ (95 offsuit) is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability from middle position and several players yet to act behind me (CO, BTN, SB, BB). Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call, so I’d need roughly 40% equity to justify a call — unrealistic multiway and versus likely raising/3-betting ranges. Open-raising here would be spewy and invite squeezes; limping/calling is a losing, high-variance play. With 52 BB effective I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Folded to us in the cutoff with T8 suited, a standard open in 6-max due to good playability (suited, connected) and strong positional advantage postflop. Raising also attacks the blinds and avoids giving Button/SB/BB a cheap multiway pot. I’ll use a modest 2.5bb sizing to generate folds while keeping risk controlled vs 3-bets.

I am holding 2h 4s on the Button, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a standard raise to 250 from FiveTwo in the Cutoff, I have no strategic reason to enter the pot. Calling is losing chips, and 3-bet bluffing with this specific holding is unnecessary given I have better candidates in my range for bluffs. The correct and only play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with T8s, which is a solid semi-connected suited hand. FiveTwo opened from the Cutoff with a standard raise to 250. My stack is 4850 (48.5 BB), and the pot odds to call are 200 to win 400 (3:1). While T8s plays well post-flop, playing it out of position against an aggressive player like FiveTwo (who just showed major aggression in Hand #48) is tricky. However, closing the action (nearly) and the quality of the hand justify a call to see a flop, rather than a 3-bet or a fold.

K5o is a marginal hand offsuit with poor kicker and playability. Facing a raise from the aggressive Cutoff (FiveTwo) and a call from loose SB (Flash), the range is strong enough that K5o lacks equity and postflop potential. Pot odds are decent (150 into 600, ~20% needed), but better spots available with 10k stack in hand 49/150. Folding preserves stack for premium hands.

On the flop, I have hit top two pair (Tens and Eights) on a very coordinated board of 10h-4h-8h. While I have a strong hand, the board is monotone hearts, and I do not hold any heart in my hand. This means any single heart in FiveTwo's range has a flush draw, and any two hearts already have a flush. Given I am out of position, I should start by checking to see how the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) reacts. Checking allows me to control the pot size if they have a flush or a high heart draw, while also giving them the opportunity to continuation bet with bluffs or weaker hands that I beat.

We flop top two pair (T8) on a monotone heart board. This is a very strong value hand, but it’s not the nuts and is vulnerable (any heart-combo already has a flush; many hands have a heart + pair/straight draws). After SB checks, we should bet to (1) get value from Tx/8x with a heart, overpairs with a heart, and worse made hands, and (2) deny equity to single-heart hands that would otherwise realize for free. I’ll use a fairly chunky sizing to charge draws and set up turn shoves/large bets while still leaving room to react if we get check-raised (where flush-heavy ranges exist).

I have flopped top two pair (Tens and Eights) on a monotone board. While my hand is strong, the presence of three hearts makes it vulnerable since I do not hold a heart myself. FiveTwo's C-bet of 400 into 600 is standard. If I raise here, I risk being shoved on by a made flush or a massive draw (like the Nut Flush Draw), or I force out bluffs that I currently beat. By calling, I keep the pot manageable and allow FiveTwo to continue betting with a wider range, including overpairs or semi-bluffs with a single heart. I will look to see a safe turn card (a non-heart) before deciding to commit more chips.

On the turn card 7c, my top two pair (Tc 8c) remains a very strong hand, though vulnerable to a made flush (any two hearts) or a straight (J9). The 7c does not change the flush possibility, but it adds some straight draws. Since I am out of position against an aggressive opponent (FiveTwo), checking remains the most flexible option. It allows me to see how FiveTwo values his hand after my call on the flop. If he checks back, it's a strong signal I am ahead. If he bets, I can evaluate the sizing to determine if he is likely protecting a made hand or semi-bluffing with a high heart or straight draw. I still have 4 outs to improve to a full house if I am currently behind a flush.

We’re heads-up in position with top two (T8) on a 3-heart board. This is strong, but without a heart we’re vulnerable: Flash can have (1) a made flush (two hearts) that already beats us, or (2) lots of one-heart hands (pair+draw, overcards+draw) that have meaningful equity and will realize too cheaply if we check back.

After Flash check-calls flop and checks turn, I want to keep value/protection betting, but with a sizing that doesn’t auto-stack us vs a check-raise (which will be heavily weighted to flushes/straights). A ~55–60% pot barrel pressures one-heart draws and gets value from Tx/8x with a heart while keeping a clear bet/fold to aggression.

Plan: bet 800; if check-raised big/jammed, fold; if called, often check back many rivers (especially hearts) and value bet cautiously on clean bricks depending on action.

I have top two pair (Tens and Eights) on a board that is still monotone (three hearts). While my hand is objectively strong, the lack of a heart in my hand makes it extremely vulnerable to any made flush or even a single high heart draw. FiveTwo has been aggressive across multiple hands, and his 800 bet into 1400 on the turn represents continued strength. If I raise here, I am likely only called by hands that beat me (flushes, straights like J9, or sets). By calling, I keep his bluffs and semi-bluffs (like an overpair with a heart or top pair with the Ace of hearts) in the hand while controlling the pot size. I have 4 outs to improve to a full house on the river. I will call and re-evaluate on the river, especially if a fourth heart or a straight-completing card appears.

The river is the Queen of Hearts (Qh), putting a fourth heart on the board. I have top two pair from the flop (Tens and Eights), but I do not hold a heart. Any single heart in FiveTwo's hand now beats me with a flush. Given FiveTwo's aggression throughout the hand, it is very likely they have a heart or can easily represent one. Betting here serves no purpose as I won't get called by worse hands and I can't realistically bluff him off a flush. I will check and most likely fold to any significant bet, as my hand is now essentially a bluff catcher against an extremely coordinated board.

River puts the 4th heart (Qh), which is a very bad card for our Ts8s: our flop top two is now only a bluff-catcher and has almost no value-bet targets.

After Flash check-calls flop and turn on a monotone board, his range is heavily weighted to (a) at least one heart (often a made flush already on flop), and (b) some stubborn pairs/draws with a single heart. On this river, any heart in his hand beats us, and those hands will either check-raise or call (and we’re drawing dead). Meanwhile, the hands that *don’t* have a heart (Tx/8x/77/straight draws that missed) are exactly the hands we already beat and are unlikely to pay off a bet on a 4-heart board.

So betting is mostly turning a likely winner into a bigger losing pot vs flushes, with very little chance to get called by worse. Best play is to take the showdown and realize our equity.

UTG with T5o is a weak, non-playable hand. There are multiple players left to act (including deep, aggressive stacks), so an UTG open or even a limp is a marginal line that will often get me in trouble multi-way or face a 3-bet. With 100 to call and 52 BB effective, preserving my stack and waiting for a much stronger spot is the best long-term strategy. Fold.

6-handed after UTG folds, JTo is strong enough to open from middle position, especially with our big stack applying pressure to the shorter stacks behind. Limping gives up initiative and invites squeezes; an open lets us win blinds often and play in position vs blinds when called. I’ll use a standard, relatively small open to keep risk down while still generating fold equity. Versus a tight/large 3-bet I can comfortably fold; versus calls I can c-bet favorable boards and use position/stack leverage.

I hold 9c6c in the Cutoff. The player in Middle Position (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and quite active, has opened to 250 (2.5bb). While 96s has some playability due to flush and straight potential, it is a weak two-gapper. Calling a 2.5x open from MP with this hand is loose and risks being dominated by higher flushes or squeezed by the Button/Blinds. My stack of 77bb allows for some speculative play, but this spot is not favorable enough. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

I am on the button with AKo, a premium starting hand. FiveTwo opened from middle position for 2.5x. Given my stack size of approximately 50 BBs and my positional advantage, a 3-bet is the standard and most effective play to gain value and take control of the hand. I will 3-bet to 800, which is a standard size (3.2x) that puts pressure on the opener and discourages the blinds from entering the pot lightly.

Preflop in SB with 5d6s, facing a 3-bet to 800 after MP open to 250. Pot 1200, to call 750 (1.6:1 odds). 56s is a suited connector but marginal for SB defense vs button 3-bet over strong MP range. Both opponents likely strong; postflop OOP with short effective stacks (Flash 4150) and my 9950, fold preserves stack for better spots. Standard fold.

92s is a very weak hand. Facing a 3-bet from Flash to 800, I need to call 700 into a pot of 1200. Even with suited cards, 92s has very poor playability and equity against a 3-betting range. Clear fold.

Flash’s 3-bet to 800 versus our MP open creates a low effective SPR (he has ~41bb, and if we call the pot is ~1750 with ~3350 behind). Calling out of position with JTo is problematic: we’ll frequently make dominated one-pair hands (Jx/Tx) against a fairly strong/linear 3-bet range, and with this SPR we’re often pressured into tough stack-off decisions with marginal equity realization. 

A 4-bet bluff is also unattractive here: JTo has poor blockers, and given his short stack he can respond by jamming a lot of his continuing range, forcing us to fold after investing a sizeable chunk. With minimal upside and high realization risk, the best EV is to let it go and preserve our big-stack leverage for better spots.

We’re first to act 6-handed and holding 3h4d offsuit—no high-card strength, poor playability, and dominated too often when we make a pair. Limping invites multiway pots out of position with a hand that rarely realizes equity, and opening is unnecessary risk given stack depth and table dynamics. Best is to conserve chips and wait for better spots.

I am holding 5h 6d in Middle Position. This is a weak off-suit connector. With players left to act behind me and no money invested in the pot other than the ante (if any, though none listed here, just blinds), this is a standard fold. The hand has poor playability and equity against likely calling or raising ranges.

I am in the Cutoff with 6c 2h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Since it has been folded to me, I have no reason to enter the pot with such poor equity and connectivity. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

Preflop on the button in an unopened pot with folds from UTG, MP, CO. 8s5c is a marginal hand but playable for a steal raise from the button due to excellent position and fold equity against the blinds. Claude (SB) is deep-stacked and aggressive but often folds to button opens; Minni (BB) has a shorter stack and defends but folds to pressure frequently based on history. Raising to 2.5BB (250) builds the pot when called while maintaining fold equity. Postflop playability is decent with two live cards and position. Folding would be too tight for BTN range; limping is suboptimal.

A7o in the SB facing a button open to 250. This is a marginal hand - not strong enough to 3-bet for value, but has enough equity to see a flop. Getting 2:1 on a call (200 to win 400). Ace-high has some showdown value and can flop top pair. Calling is the standard play here rather than folding or 3-betting light.

I’m in the BB with Kc2d facing a BTN open to 250 and the SB has cold‑called. Pot is 600 and it’s only 150 more to see a flop (total commit to 250). K2o is a marginal, often‑dominated hand, but the price is attractive (~20% of the pot) to try to see a flop. Plan: defend cheaply to realize equity and fold to strong post‑flop aggression unless I make a real hand (top pair/strong draws). If re‑raised substantially preflop or face heavy action on most flops, I’ll concede the spot. Calling keeps pot control and preserves my stack for better spots.

Flopped nothing with A7o on Jd-Th-Ts. I have ace high with no real draws. This board smashes the button's raising range (JT, JJ, TT, QT, KT, etc.) and could easily connect with the BB's calling range too. No reason to bet - checking to see what opponents do is the clear play. If it checks through I might improve, and I can easily fold to aggression.

Check. I defended the BB preflop with Kc2d for price, but on Jd‑Th‑Ts I have no pair and essentially no meaningful draws (only three lone king outs). There are two opponents and the BTN acts after me, so a bluff here is low‑EV — the board is paired and will be called or raised by plenty of hands in the BTN/SB ranges. I prefer pot control and to preserve my 4,975 chip stack: check and fold to any significant bet. If checked through I can reassess on the turn (look for a free card or a favorable card to bluff), but I won't invest chips into this flop without improving.

Flop Jd Th Ts completely misses my 8s5c: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw. Pure air. Preflop I raised as a steal from BTN, got two callers (Claude deep aggressive SB, Minni shorter BB defender). Both checked flop, showing possible weakness but paired board favors their ranges (Tx, Jx, sets likely). C-betting as bluff is low EV here: pot 750 into two opponents, position good but fold equity reduced post-checks on dry paired board; better to check back, pot control, realize near-zero equity for free cards, fold to bets. Avoid spewing stack early in tournament.

Three tens on the board now. I have A7o - just ace high. With trip tens on the board, anyone with a pocket pair has a full house, anyone with a J has a better full house, and anyone with a T has quads. My hand has almost no value here. Betting would be pointless - I can't get value or fold out better hands. Check and hope to see a free river, then likely give up.

Check. I defended the BB preflop for price but K♣2♦ has essentially no showdown value on a board that just turned to J♦ T♥ T♠ T♦. The turn gives the board trips, which hugely favors the raiser/caller ranges (Jx now makes full houses; one remaining Ten in someone’s hand makes quads). This is a three‑way pot with no prior aggression on the flop, so a bluff here is low EV — I’ll be called by many hands that beat me. I want to keep the pot small and preserve my 4,975 stack for better spots, so I check and will fold to any meaningful bet. If checked through to the river I’ll reassess based on the card and opponent actions, but I will not lead out or raise into likely made hands.

Turn brings the Td, pairing the tens into trips on board (Jd Th Ts Td), an extremely strong texture where Jx makes boat, Tx makes quads, and any pocket pair or better is way ahead. My 8s5c has zero equity, no pair/draw. Both opponents checked flop (dry paired) and turn again, showing possible weakness but also trap potential with monsters. Preflop I raised as BTN steal, checked flop pot control on miss. Betting now as pure bluff is -EV: pot 750, sizing say 400-600 risks spew into 2 opponents with reduced fold equity on nuts-heavy board; they can easily have Jx/Tx/draws that call/raise. Checking realizes 0 equity cheaply, keeps pot small, preserves 9700 stack (150BB deep) for better spots in 51/150 hand tournament. No reason to bluff into this disaster.

River with A7o on Jd-Th-Ts-Td-8c. Three tens on board means I play TTT with my ace kicker (TTT-A-J). Everyone checked flop and turn, suggesting no one has a premium hand. My ace kicker is strong against random hands but loses to any pocket pair (full house) or better kicker hands like AK, AQ. Given the passive action, checking to showdown is correct - a bet might only get called by hands that beat me (boats) while folding out worse.

Board: J♦ T♥ T♠ T♦ 8♣. My K♣2♦ has no showdown value — I don't pair the board and there are many hands in BTN/SB ranges that beat me (Txs, Jx, full houses). This was a multiway pot with no prior aggression; bluffing into multiple opponents on this texture is very low EV. There's no bet to call and no value to extract, so the correct play is to check and preserve my ~49 BB stack. If anyone had fired a meaningful river bet I would fold.

The board Jd Th Ts Td 8c is a monster: three tens on turn create full house possibilities with J (boats), quads with T, potential straights (Q9). Preflop BTN raise got calls from SB (Claude, aggressive deep 170BB) and BB (Minni, shorter 50BB). All streets checked by all players, screaming weakness—no medium strength like Jx/Tx would typically bet for value/protection multiway. Their checking ranges: missed draws (KQ, Q9, 98), ace-high, unpaired broadways, pure air from wide defends. My 85o was air preflop/flop/turn but rivers bottom pair (8s pairing 8c kicker 5c), ahead of all worse showdown hands and bluffs. Thin value bet extracts from Ax/Kx/Qx that defended wide pre and checked down. Size 500 (~2/3 pot) into 750 builds pot when called (Minni short, calls light), fold equity vs air, position to realize if check-raised (fold easily to J+/T+). Checking risks free showdown but misses value from worse; betting +EV deep in tournament (97BB stack, hand 51/150). Consistent with prior pot control but capitalizes on river improvement and opponent passivity.

Board has three tens - I play TTT with A-J kickers. Elon's small 500 bet into 750 pot after checking all streets is suspicious but could be thin value or a blocker bet. My ace kicker is strong - beats any hand without an ace or pocket pair. Getting 3.5:1 on the call. Full houses (pocket pairs) beat me, but the passive line and small sizing suggests he may not have one. Calling is correct; folding is too tight with ace kicker.

Fold. The river is J♦ T♥ T♠ T♦ 8♣ and my K♣2♦ has no pair or meaningful showdown value. Elon’s small 500 river bet was met with a call from Claude, making this a multiway pot and strongly indicating at least one made hand (T‑x, J‑x/full house, or even quads). Calling 500 into the roughly 2,750–3,250 range would require ~15% equity to break even; K2o has essentially none versus these ranges. A raise bluff is also very low EV multiway against deep stacks. I preserve my ~4,975 stack for better spots and fold.

I am in UTG position with T4o, which is a very weak starting hand. With 77.5 BBs, there is no need to get involved in a pot with trash cards from early position. The only standard play here is to fold.

I am in Middle Position with T7o. This is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially in an offsuit configuration. With four players remaining to act behind me, including several aggressive stacks, opening this hand would be a significant mistake. The standard and most disciplined play is to fold.

Cutoff position with two folds ahead in a 6-handed game. 78s is a premium suited connector with excellent playability, straight potential, flush draw potential, and postflop flexibility. Deep effective stacks, especially against BB FiveTwo who has shown aggression but also folded to pressure. Raising steals blinds or builds pot in position. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 isolates weaker hands and discourages light calls/3bets from button Claude. Avoid limping as it invites multiway pots where equity decreases.

AJh suited on the button facing a cutoff raise to 300. This is a strong hand with excellent position. I should 3-bet for value and to isolate. Standard 3-bet sizing would be around 3x the raise, so 900.

I’m in the small blind with K♥T♦ facing a button 3-bet to 900 after Elon opened to 300. KTo is a marginal, often‑dominated broadway and I’ll be out of position postflop — plus the big blind still has to act so this can easily become a multiway pot. Calling 850 commits ~17% of my stack into a spot where I’m likely behind and have poor playability; a 4‑bet shove would be an over‑bluff and unnecessary here. Preserve chips for better, in‑position spots — fold.

Facing an open (300) and a 3-bet to 900, we’d have to call 800 more out of position versus at least the button’s 3-bet range and potentially the original raiser continuing. Even with decent immediate pot odds (800 to win 2150 after calling), 63o has very poor raw equity versus these ranges and terrible playability (dominated 6x/3x, reverse implied odds, hard to realize equity OOP in a likely bloated pot). No profitable defend or 4-bet bluff spot here.

78s is a strong suited connector with excellent playability, straight and flush potential, and good equity against a button 3-bet range which is often polarized (strong value hands or bluffs suited broadways/connectors). Facing a 3x 3-bet to 900 after my 3x open, the to-call is only 600 into a 1350 pot (3.25:1 odds), a tiny commitment from my 10k stack (under 6%). Folding is too tight given the position and hand strength. 4-betting as a bluff risks stacks deep OOP with reverse implied odds if called. Calling realizes equity OOP against Claude's likely wide button range (he's shown aggression like in hands 2,15,33), sets up post-flop flexibility to float/raise draws or stack-off with monsters, while pot odds are excellent. Maximizes EV over tournament.

Flop board Ac-5c-Qd is very dry but hits Claude's button 3-bet range hard with top pair, sets, or flush draws. 78s has a gutshot straight draw to the 9 (4 outs, ~16% by river) plus backdoor diamond flush draw and overcard potential, giving ~25-35% equity against his polarized range (value: AQ+, sets; bluffs: suited connectors, Ax suited). OOP first to act in a 1950 pot with deep stacks (10k eff). Donk betting air/draw is -EV as it denies equity realization and faces raises. Checking is optimal: pot controls, induces bluffs/checks from missed value, allows calling reasonable bets (~1/3-1/2 pot) with good implied odds deep, or check-raising monsters later. Folding pre-call would be too tight; now check to realize equity vs his wide aggression (seen in hands 2,15,29,36). Tournament stage favors flexible play over spewy lines.

I flopped top pair top kicker (TPTK minus AK/AQ) with AJh on Ac Qd 5c. Elon checked to me after calling my 3-bet preflop. This is a clear value betting spot - I likely have the best hand against his calling range which includes Ax hands, broadways, and pocket pairs. Need to bet for value and protection against club draws. Betting around 60-65% pot.

Facing a 1200 bet into 1950 pot (~62% pot) on Ac5cQd flop OOP with 78s (gutshot to 6 for 45678 straight, 4 outs ~8.5% turn/river, plus backdoor spade flush and diamond flush draws adding ~3-5% equity, total ~25-30% vs Claude's polarized button 3bet range: value like AQ/AK/AQs/QQ/AA/sets ~70% equity for him, bluffs suited connectors/Ax suited where we have ~40%+). Pot odds excellent: call 1200 to win 3150 (current pot post-bet), need ~28% equity, we have it with position realization postflop, deep stacks (10k eff), implied odds if hit (can stack medium pairs/draws). Folding too tight given preflop call commitment and history (Claude aggressive but folds to pressure e.g. hands 6,15,29). Raising OOP as semi-bluff risky: folds out better bluffs, gets called by value, reverse implied deep; better to keep pot controlled, realize equity by calling (standard vs wide aggro button ranges). Turn play: if blank check/fold, if 6/9/4 stack-off, if drawy raise. Tournament mid-stack preservation favors +EV calls over spew.

Turn board Ac5cQd3s is a blank low card that doesn't complete obvious draws for my range but adds backdoor straight possibilities and a spade, slightly favoring Claude's potential flush draw if he has suited connectors. 78s remains a gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs ~8% equity to river) with backdoor spade flush (8s pairs the single spade) and diamond backdoor (7d with Qd), total ~20-25% equity vs Claude's polarized button 3bet/call range (value: top pair like AQ/AK/Ax, sets QQ/55/AA; bluffs: suited broadways/connectors/Axs where we dominate or have live draws). Pot 4350 OOP first to act with 8850 stack (deep ~18bb effective post-pot). Donk betting a weak draw denies equity realization, faces raises from value with poor implied odds deep OOP, and folds out his bluffs unnecessarily. Check is mandatory: pot controls (~EV+ vs mixed range), induces bluffs/checks from weaker Ax/draws (Claude aggressive but folds to c/r pressure e.g. hands 6,15,23,29,38 where opponents check-fold), allows cheap calls to reasonable bets (pot odds >2.5:1 need ~30%, we have it with river implied if hit ~20bb stack-off vs worse), sets up check-raise bluff if he bets big/small with air. Folding pre too tight; now realize remaining equity in mid-tournament with position post-turn. History shows Claude bets flops/turns aggressively (hands 4,6,11,15,29,30,36,37,38) but vulnerable post-oak OOP pressure. Flexible play preserves stack while extracting max EV.

Turn is a complete brick (3s). I have TPTK with AJh on Ac Qd 5c 3s board. Elon called my flop bet and checked again - his range is likely weaker aces, flush draws, queens, or underpairs. AQ/AK would likely have raised by now. I should continue betting for value against his calling range. Betting around 60% pot to extract value while keeping him in with his weaker made hands and draws.

Turn board Ac5cQd3s is a low spade blank that slightly improves backdoor spade flush draw with 8s and adds minor straight possibilities, but primarily a gutshot to 6x for the wheel straight (3-4-5-6-7-8, 4 clean outs ~8% to river) plus ~3-4% backdoor equity, totaling ~25-30% vs Claude's polarized button 3bet/call range (value: AQ/AK/AA/QQ/55/Ax suited ~65-75% equity for him; bluffs: suited connectors/Axs/broadways where we have 35-50%). Pot 6950 after his 2600 bet (~60% into 4350 pre-bet), call 2600 for 27% equity needed (2600/(6950+2600)=27%), which we exceed with river implied odds deep (~6k effective behind: hit straight stacks most worse hands like Ax/Tx/draws, as seen in Claude's history of folding to turn pressure but value betting aggressively e.g. hands 4,6,11,15,29,36-38). Folding too tight OOP after flop call commitment, denies equity realization mid-tournament (hand 52/150, stack 8850 healthy). Raising semi-bluff to 5200+ risks folding out better bluffs, gets snapped by value OOP with reverse implied (e.g. he has AQ), poor vs aggro Claude who 3bet wide but pots control. Call optimal: pot controls (~37% pot commit), realizes equity cheaply, sets up river check-fold blanks/check-call small or check-jam hits (6/9/4/J etc.), exploits his aggression (folds to c/r often like 6,15,23,29) while preserving stack flexibility. Consistent with preflop/flop strategy vs wide button range.

River 6h is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete obvious draws (no club flush, spade flush unlikely without pair, no clear straights like wheel or 45678/56789 without missing cards). Board: Ac5cQd3s6h favors Ax (top pair/good kicker), AQ two pair, sets (AA/QQ/55), or stubborn Qx/draws from Claude's aggressive button 3bet/Cbet range. My 78s (7d8s) missed completely: no pair, no straight (3-5-6-7-8 skips 4), no flush, pure bluff catcher air (~0% showdown value). OOP first to act in 9550 pot with 6250 stack (~1.5 SPR). Betting as bluff -EV: risks 6250 stack vs his value calling range (Ax/AQ/sets), gets raised/folded to by bluffs we want to keep in; exploits nothing given his history of pot control OOP pressure but value-heavy river bets (e.g., hands 4,11,15,29,37-38). Checking optimal: induces bluffs/value thin bets from wide range (Claude cbets flop/turn aggressively but vulnerable, e.g., folds to c/r or turn pressure in 6,15,23,29), pot controls preserves mid-tournament stack (hand 52/150, healthy 62.5BB), realizes equity by showing down better semi-bluffs/draw misses in range while folding to bets (pot odds-dependent: fold >75% pot bets, call small polarized). Consistent with called draw line: check-fold misses preserves EV long-term vs aggro Claude, avoids spew.

River is a brick 6h. I have TPTK with AJh. Elon has called two streets of betting - his range is likely Ax with weaker kickers (A2-AT), Qx hands, or missed club draws. AQ/AK would likely have raised at some point. Missed draws won't call, but weaker aces and queens might pay off a thin value bet. Betting around 35% pot to extract value from hands I beat while not bloating the pot against better hands.

River 6h is a blank that completes no strong draws for my range (gutshot to 4/9 missed, no flush, backdoors dead) leaving 78s with 0 showdown value: pure air OOP after check-calling flop/turn vs Claude's aggressive BTN 3bet/Cbet range. Board Ac5cQd3s6h favors his value (Ax top pair esp clubs suited like AcXc/AKs/AQs ~60% equity vs air; AQ two-pair; sets AA/QQ/55) over bluffs (missed clubs draw unlikely to 3b/cbet 3x streets IP; suited connectors/gappers like 46/47/24 that missed). Pot 12750 to call 3200 (~20% needed, 3200/15950), but vs polarized range he's weighted to value after check-call history (I rep draws/Ax medium that fold to 3bets), EV negative even if he bluffs 25% (history: aggressive cbets but value-heavy rivers e.g. 4,11,15,29,37-38 where he extracts vs passive lines; folds bluffs to pressure 6,15,23,29). Low SPR post-call (~3050 into 16k) but no fold equity on bet line; check-fold preserves 6250 stack mid-tourney (52/150, ~62BB healthy) avoiding spew OOP vs aggro IP Claude who rarely bluffs 3 streets into passive resistance. Consistent with called draw line: fold air to realize EV long-term, exploits his overvalue tendency.

I am in the UTG position with Nine-Eight offsuit. This is a very weak hand to open from the earliest position at a six-handed table. While I have been relatively active, opening with marginal offsuit connectors here would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations out of position against deep-stacked and aggressive opponents like Claude and FiveTwo. Standard poker strategy dictates a fold here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Preflop, folded to me in middle position with 2d3c offsuit, one of the worst hands in poker. No speculative value, no suitedness, no high card strength. Blinds 50/100, pot 150, to call 100 into multiway pot potential with deep stacks is -EV. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Opponent tendencies from history show aggression, but no need to defend trash here. Early in tournament (53/150), patience key to maximize stack.

J5o is a weak hand that doesn't play well even from the cutoff. While I'm the chip leader with 214 BB, this hand lacks connectivity and suited-ness to justify an open. Minni behind me has shown aggression with 3-bets in previous hands, and J5o plays poorly against re-raises. Standard fold here.

On the button with K♥6♠ and ~49 BB, this is a marginal but playable hand in position against only the blinds. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure rather than just limp; a standard 3x BB open is appropriate to steal the blinds or isolate. I raise to 300 total (3x BB) — if I face a large 3-bet I'll fold given the hand's poor postflop playability; if called I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot when I miss.

Minni is opening from the Button with a likely wide range and has a ~46bb stack, making them sensitive to 3-bet pressure. Calling from the SB with QTo invites the BB in and leaves us OOP in a low SPR pot with a hand that can be dominated; it also realizes equity poorly multiway. A 3-bet here leverages our bigger stack, isolates the raiser, and can win preflop often while still having reasonable playability when called (broadway connectivity, good blockers vs Qx/Tx). If Minni 4-bets, we can comfortably fold given stack depth and hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 2h, a very weak suited gapper. The action has been significant: Minni opened to 300 from the Button, and FiveTwo 3-bet to 1200 from the Small Blind. I am facing a large raise that represents about 14% of my stack. My hand has very low equity against a 3-betting range, and I am not closing the action as the original raiser (Minni) is still to act and could 4-bet. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding. Folding is the only reasonable option.

I opened to 300 on the button with K♥6♠ intending to steal the blinds, but FiveTwo 3-bet to 1200. K6s is a marginal hand with poor post‑flop playability and is often dominated by a 3‑bettor's range (pairs, AK, Kx, broadways). Calling 900 more (~19.5% of my 4625 stack) to play a bloated pot OOP against a big stack 3‑bettor is too risky; 4‑betting to 2100 (the min) would commit ~45% of my stack and would be a thin bluff with weak blockers. I stick to my preflop plan to fold to a large 3‑bet and preserve chips for better spots.

A9o is a strong hand from UTG in a 6-handed tournament. It plays well postflop with ace-high, good kicker, and straight/flush potential. Standard play is to open raise to about 3x the BB to build the pot and take initiative. Given the pot of 150 (blinds) and to-call of 100 (likely indicating BB context), raising to 300 total is appropriate sizing, around 3BB, considering opponent stacks and history of frequent 250-300 opens. No prior action indicated, so proceeding with isolation raise. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots.

Q5 suited is too weak to call an UTG raise. Even with the suited aspect, we're dominated by most of Elon's opening range from UTG. With players still to act behind and poor position, folding is the correct play.

Facing an UTG open to 300 (pot 450) from Elon with 4♠3♣ in the cutoff. This is a very weak, offsuit wheel connector with poor showdown value and poor playability multiway. Calling 300 into a 450 pot (you’d be risking 300 to win 750) requires roughly 40% equity — far more than 4-3o has versus a normal UTG opening range, and even worse if players behind enter. Raising as a bluff is too risky with three players yet to act (button, SB, BB) and I only have ~46 BB — I don’t want to commit chips with a marginal hand out of position against likely stronger ranges. Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot/positioned steal. Fold.

Elon’s UTG open represents a relatively strong range in 6-max, and K2o is heavily dominated (we make lots of second-best top pairs) with poor playability even in position. Flat-calling invites squeezes from the blinds and makes it hard to realize equity. A 3-bet bluff here is too thin versus an UTG range and risks getting played back at by an aggressive opener. Best is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit (Tc 8h). The player in UTG (Elon) has raised to 300 (3BB). Facing an Early Position raise, my hand is significantly below the standard calling or 3-betting range, especially given I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a range that likely dominates mine. The pot odds do not justify a call with this holding. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Trey offsuit. Elon has opened from UTG to 300. While the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, J3o is an extremely weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an early-position opening range. Given my current stack of 5250, there is no need to defend with such a weak hand and risk being dominated on many flops. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K5o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand is easily dominated by better kings and doesn't play well postflop, especially from early position where I'll likely face action. With a healthy stack of 214bb, I should stick to stronger hands from UTG.

3♦7♠ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity. Calling 100 to see a flop requires ~40% equity (100/(150+100)=40%), which 3-7o doesn't have, and if we limp we'll often be out of position against the button and the blinds with multiple players who can apply pressure. Raising is unjustified with such a hand. With a healthy 46bb stack it's better to preserve chips for a better spot, so fold.

We’re in the CO after two folds with a very marginal offsuit holding (T7o). While we could try to steal, this hand is dominated often when called (Tx, 7x, broadways), realizes equity poorly out of position postflop versus the blinds’ defend ranges, and can’t profitably continue against 3-bets. With decent stacks behind that can defend/3-bet, folding keeps our range tighter and avoids low-EV spots.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit (Ad Qs) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. The action has folded to me. With a stack of 76 BB, I am in a comfortable position to open the pot for value and to potentially steal the blinds. A standard opening raise in this tournament context has been around 2.5x to 3x the big blind. Raising to 250 (2.5 BB) is a strong, standard play that puts pressure on the blinds while building a pot with a strong hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind with KTo. Pro has raised from the Button, which is a standard stealing position. KTo is a solid hand to defend with, though playing out of position is challenging. I have the option to 3-bet to take control of the hand, but calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see a flop against what is likely a wide button opening range. Given my stack size and the current stage of the tournament, calling the 2.5x raise is a standard and safe play to evaluate the flop.

Preflop in BB with Q9o facing a button raise to 250 by Pro (wide range expected from button) and a flat call from Flash in SB (also potentially loose). Pot is 600, to call 150 gives good odds (~4:1), but Q9o is offsuit, marginal hand with poor post-flop playability multiway OOP. Multiway pot reduces my equity realization. Pro's button range is wide but Flash's call strengthens the field. Folding preserves stack for better spots; defending too wide OOP leads to tough decisions later. Deep stack (63BB), no need to speculate here.

I have completely missed this flop with KTo, though I do have two overcards. On a board of Jc-6c-8s, the pre-flop raiser (Pro) often has a range advantage or at least a Jack in their button-opening range. I am out of position and have no real draw. The most standard play here is to check and evaluate Pro's action. If they bet, I will likely have to fold unless the sizing is extremely small, as King-high has very little equity against a typical continuation bet on this texture.

I raised pre-flop with AdQs and was called by the Small Blind. The flop comes Jc 6c 8s. This board texture interacts well with a Small Blind calling range, which likely includes pairs, flush draws, and straight draws. As the pre-flop aggressor, while I have two overcards, I have missed the board completely. A continuation bet here might be called by many hands that beat me (any pair) or check-raised by draws/sets, putting me in a difficult spot. My hand has showdown value as Ace-high, and checking back allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size, and potentially catch an Ace or Queen on the turn without bloating the pot against a range that connects well with this board.

The turn 9h adds a gutshot straight draw (Queen) to my King-high. On the flop, both I and Pro checked, which suggests Pro's button-opening range is likely weak or medium-strength (missed high cards or small pairs). The board is now very connected (J-9-8-6), which favors a range that defends from the blinds. However, since I don't have a made hand yet, checking remains the most prudent play to see if Pro will take a stab at the pot or check behind again, which would give me a free look at the river.

I raised pre-flop with AdQs and checked back the Jc 6c 8s flop. The turn is the 9h, making the board J-9-8-6. This card coordinates heavily with the Small Blind's calling range (connecting with T9, 98, 76, T7, etc.). My opponent checks to me again. I now have a gutshot straight draw (a Ten gives me the straight) along with my two overcards. While I hold a Queen which blocks the nut straight (QT), the board is dangerous and hits the opponent's range harder than mine. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better hands, whereas checking back guarantees I see the river to potentially realize my equity (hitting a T, A, or Q) or win at showdown with Ace-high if the opponent is also on a draw. The pot is small (600), and controlling pot size is optimal here.

The board has become extremely connected with J-T-9-8-6. Any Queen or Seven makes a straight. I have hit a pair of Tens on the river with a King kicker. Given that both Pro and I checked the flop and turn, it is unlikely either of us has a strong made hand like a Jack or a high pocket pair, but the button-opening range contains many hands with a Seven or a Queen (like Qx, 7x, or even 77). Checking is the best option here to reach showdown cheaply. If Pro bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking to see if my pair of Tens is good is the most prudent strategy.

I have rivered a Queen-high straight (using the 8, 9, 10, J on the board and my Q). The board is J-6-8-9-T. The only hand that beats me is KQ (King-high straight). The opponent has checked to me on the flop, turn, and river, showing passivity and likely weakness, though a 7 (lower straight) is definitely in their range (e.g., 76s, 87s, A7). Given the four-card straight on the board, it is hard for the opponent to call with just one pair, but they will certainly call with a lower straight (holding a 7) and possibly sets or two pair. I should bet for value. A bet of around 60% of the pot (350 into 600) is large enough to get value from a 7 but small enough to potentially get a crying call from a Jack or two pair.

On the river, the board completes a four-card straight (J-T-9-8-6). While I have improved to a pair of Tens with a King kicker, this is a very dangerous board. Pro, who opened from the button, has a wide range that naturally includes many Queens and Sevens (Qx and 7x) which would have now made a straight. Although Pro checked the flop and turn, which usually indicates weakness, the river bet of 350 into 600 is a polarising size. While it could be a bluff or a thin value bet with a Jack, the presence of a four-straight makes my second pair a very weak bluff-catcher. Given that Pro's button-opening range is saturated with the exact cards that make a straight here, and I have shown weakness by checking three streets, I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

UTG with J♦8♣ (offsuit) is a weak hand from first-to-act. Limping/calling 100 is poor because I’ll be out of position against five opponents and often dominated by better Jx/Ax hands; raising to open invites 3-bets from the big stacks (Claude, FiveTwo) and I don’t have the hand or stack depth to play post-flop comfortably. With a healthy ~46BB I should conserve chips and wait for a stronger, better-positioned spot. Fold and preserve my stack for profitable opportunities.

Unopened pot (only blinds), we’re in middle position with J6o. This hand is too weak to open profitably from MP in a 6-max game: it’s dominated by opponents’ calling/3-betting ranges (AJ/KJ/QJ/JT, better suited gappers) and plays poorly postflop out of position versus the blinds. Limping is also -EV and invites multiway pots. Preserve stack and wait for better open/steal spots.

I am in the Cutoff with Ace-Four offsuit (Ah 4c). The action has folded to me. My stack is roughly 80 big blinds. A4o is a marginal hand from the Cutoff position. While it has some blocker value with the Ace, it plays poorly post-flop due to the weak kicker and lack of flush potential. The players remaining behind me include the chip leader (Claude) in the Big Blind and aggressive players in the Small Blind (Elon) and Button (Flash). Opening this hand risks getting 3-bet or called by better Aces, leaving me in a difficult spot. Standard strategy suggests folding A4o from the Cutoff most of the time, preferring to open suited Aces or better offsuit Aces (A9o+). I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Qc Th. The action has folded to me. This is a standard opening situation to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. QTo is well above the average hand strength in this spot. I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 chips.

AdKd is a premium suited hand with excellent equity against a button opening range, which is typically wide in a 6-handed game. Facing a standard button raise to 300, 3-betting from the SB is optimal to isolate, build the pot, and deny equity to worse hands. Calling OOP is suboptimal as it plays poorly postflop without initiative. Folding is never correct. Standard sizing is 3x the open to 900 total, which is about 12bb, putting good pressure while leaving room for further action. Effective stacks are deep enough (my 6250, villain 4700), and BB may fold or call light. This maximizes EV in a tournament setting aiming to grow the stack over many hands.
T2o is a very weak hand. Flash opened to 300 and Elon 3-bet to 900. Calling 800 more with T2o out of position against a 3-bet is a losing play. Easy fold.

Elon has 3-bet my button open to 900 chips from the small blind. While I have position, Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range consisting of big pairs and strong Broadways like AQ or KQ. In Hand #34, I folded QJs to a similar 3-bet from Elon, and QTo is significantly weaker. Calling here would put me in a difficult spot post-flop in a bloated pot. Therefore, the most disciplined and profitable long-term play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

We’re first to act 6-handed with 7-2 offsuit, one of the worst holdings in NLHE. Limping creates tough multiway spots with dominated equity and poor playability, and opening would be a pure punt from UTG against blinds and in-position players who can 3-bet or outplay postflop. With no dead money beyond the blinds, the best EV line is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in Middle Position with Ad 3h. UTG has folded. A3o is a weak hand to open from this position in a 6-handed game, as it is easily dominated by better Aces and plays poorly post-flop out of position. With active players behind and standard 50/100 blinds, there is no incentive to steal or enter the pot with this holding. The disciplined and standard play is to fold.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Six offsuit. This hand is generally too weak to open for a raise, especially with a massive stack like Claude in the Small Blind and Elon on the Button. Given my current stack of 47 big blinds, there's no need to take marginal spots with weak offsuit kings.

Folded to me on the button with 7h8h, a strong suited connector in an unopened pot. Excellent position to steal the blinds or play post-flop multi-way if called. Standard button open-raise size of 3x the big blind to 300 builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against the blinds, who have shown variable tendencies but are out of position. My stack of 6700 supports this without overcommitting.

Q6s in the SB facing a button raise. This is a marginal hand out of position. While suited queens have some playability, Q6s is on the weaker end for defending. The pot odds are about 1.8:1 which isn't compelling enough for this hand OOP. Better to fold and wait for stronger spots.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the BB with T♣5♦ (T5o) — a weak but defensible hand heads-up. Calling 200 more (300 total) is cheap: I only need ~31% equity to justify the call and I have some showdown value (can hit a T) and occasional backdoor possibilities. 3-betting here with this hand is too loose — I’d be out of position and likely dominated postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy action on dry boards, and continue only with top pair, strong draws, or favorable runouts.

I have T♥5♦ on K♦-5♣-4♠ — second pair (middle pair) with a mediocre kicker and I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser. The board is very dry and many of Elon’s opening hands are Kx or unpaired overcards that will c-bet. Leading out here with a marginal pair out of position bloats the pot and will often be dominated. I’ll check to control the pot and see his action. If he checks back I take it to showdown; if he bets a small-to-medium c-bet (~≤½ pot, ~≤325) I’ll call to realize equity and get value from worse hands/floaters; I’ll fold to large bets or any check-raise since my hand is vulnerable to Kx and bigger holdings. Re-evaluate on the turn if I pair my ten or improve to two pair / a good draw.

Preflop raise with 78hh from the button was standard for position and playability. Flop Kd5c4s hits me perfectly with an open-ended straight draw (4-5-7-8, 8 outs to the nuts), plus suited overcards for additional equity and backdoor flush potential. Board is coordinated but rainbow with no flush draws complete yet; possible opponent ranges include Kx, pocket pairs below K, weak Ax, or draws like 23/36/26. Minni defended BB multiway-ish but checked OOP, showing no strong hand. In position heads-up, a continuation bet realizes equity, denies her equity on her draws/checks, builds pot for when I hit, and has fold equity against her marginal holdings. Sizing 2/3 pot (425 into 650) balances value from her weaker pairs/top pairs and pressure on floats/draws. My stack 6400 effective vs her 4325 SPR ~10 post-bet, comfortable for aggression. Checking risks giving free cards to her draws or inducing bluffs, but betting exploits position and initiative.

Elon c-bet 425 into a 1,075 pot (~40% pot). Calling 425 here gives me pot odds of roughly 22% (425 / (1,075 + 425 + 425) ≈ 22%), so a call is cheap. I hold T♥5♦ on K♦-5♣-4♠ — second pair with a mediocre kicker and decent showdown value against a wide button c-bet range that contains many missed overcards and bluffs. I’m out of position, so 3-betting would be too thin and bloats the pot with a vulnerable hand. I’ll call to control the pot and realize equity, and I’ll fold to significant aggression (big raise/shove) on this street or the turn unless I improve to two pair/trips. Stack impact is small (425 of 4,325), so calling is the prudent line.

Board K♦-5♣-4♠-3♠, I hold T♥5♦ (second pair, mediocre kicker). Elon c-bet the flop and I called; he now checked the turn. That check looks like weakness/pot-control from a wide button c-betting range (missed overcards, medium Kx) or a bait from a very strong but unlikely hand — either way I have no clean draws and am out of position. Betting here bloats the pot and exposes me to a check‑raise or difficult river decisions when I’m likely behind to Kx or sets; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me realize showdown value. Plan: check and take the river — call small-to-medium bets with my pair, fold to large aggression or shove.

Turn 3s makes the board very draw-heavy: low coordinated cards (3s-4s-5c) with spade flush draw open and straight draws like 62, 26, wheel combos completed or improved. My 78hh retains a live gutshot to 6 (4 outs for 4-5-6-7-8 straight), two high card overcards (7/8 for pair + potential), backdoor heart flush, total ~20-25% equity vs typical calling ranges. Minni's check-check OOP after defending BB and calling flop cbet screams capped range: weak Kx (KJ/KT), mid/low pairs (66-QQ unlikely), trapped floats, or weak draws she missed. Strong hands (sets, two-pair, K5/K4) likely lead or raise earlier. In position with initiative, pot 1500 (SPR ~4 vs her 3900 stack), continue aggression to deny her equity on remaining draws (spades ~9 outs, 6x ~4 outs), extract value from her pairs/one-pair hands that can't withstand pressure, and bloat pot for when I hit. Check risks giving free river cards to 9 spade outs or check-raise bluffs. 2/3 pot sizing (~1000) balances value/bluff freq, <1/3 her stack, maintains fold equity while allowing river flexibility (shove/pot if hit). Stack preservation secondary to exploiting dynamics in mid-tournament.

Call 1000. I have second pair (5s) with a T kicker on K♦-5♣-4♠-3♠ and am out of position against a BTN opener who c-bet the flop and barreled the turn. The pot is 2500 and calling 1000 to potentially win 3500 requires ~28.6% equity. Elon’s BTN barreling range contains a lot of missed overcards and air in addition to Kx/sets — combined with the pot odds this call is justified. Raising OOP with a marginal pair is too thin (would bloat the pot and get me in trouble vs Kx and pocket pairs); folding surrenders too much equity given the price. Plan: call to see the river, value or call small bets if I improve or he shows weakness, and fold to heavy shove unless I improve or get a clear price to continue.

I have T5o for second pair on Kd-5c-4s-3s-Qd. Elon barreled the flop and turn then checked the river — that line often indicates pot-control with Kx/Qx or missed overcards rather than pure air, and betting out of position with a marginal pair will just bloat the pot and put me in tough spots versus a raise or a caller with a better pair. With a 3.5k pot and only 2.9k behind, preserving stack equity and taking a cheap showdown is the prudent line. I’ll check to see his action; if he bets a small amount I’ll call for value, but I won’t build the pot here with a vulnerable hand.

River Qd is a reasonably scary card, pairing the queen but completing some straight possibilities (e.g., nut straights with 23, wheel with A2/A5 but less likely) and bricking most spade flush draws and gutshots she might have defended with. My 78hh completely missed: 8-high with no pair, straight, or flush, zero showdown value against her capped calling range (weak Kx like KJ/KT/K5, mid-pairs 66-QQ unlikely given action, bricked draws like spades, 62, wheel combos). She checked OOP after calling two streets, indicating weakness—no sets/twopair/strong K would check river here, likely floating/medium strength planning showdown. Pot 3500, her stack 2900 (SPR ~1), mine 4975. Position + initiative + aggressive history (cbet flop/turn) allows a profitable third-barrel bluff: rep value like KQ, KT paired, or straights/sets. Pot-sized shove (bet 2900) puts max pressure; she needs ~18% equity to call (2900/(2900+3500)), which my air doesn't beat her likely folding hands (weak pairs/draws, ~40-50% fold equity conservatively). Checking loses to her better showdown holdings; bluff exploits her OOP capping and low SPR in mid-tournament spot to build stack vs calling station tendencies observed (e.g., Hands 5,11,14,26, etc.). High EV play to maximize stack.

I hold Ten of Hearts + Five of Diamonds (pair of 5s) on K♦‑5♣‑4♠‑3♠‑Q♦. The pot is 6,400 and Elon shoved/raised 2,900 — calling would commit my entire 2,900 stack and I’d need ~2,900/(6,400+2,900) ≈ 31% equity to break even. Elon’s line (BTN open → small flop c‑bet → turn barrel → large river shove) is highly polarized toward value (Kx, Qx that paired on the river, trips/sets, straights) with only a few bluffs. My single pair is behind most of those hands and only beats a small bluff fraction, so I don’t expect ~31% equity. Preserving my stack and waiting for a better spot is the correct long‑term decision here — fold.

I am in UTG position with A9o. In a 6-handed game, this hand is marginal to weak from early position. With 80 BBs, I have plenty of depth and do not need to take unnecessary risks with easily dominated hands like A9o, which often creates difficult post-flop situations out of position. Standard strategy suggests folding A9o from UTG, as opening ranges should be tighter (usually AT+ or AJ+ for offsuit aces). Therefore, the best play is to fold.

I am holding 6d 7d in middle position. In a 6-max tournament, suited connectors like 67s are strong enough to open for a raise to maintain an aggressive image and capitalize on post-flop playability. My stack is 47bb, which gives me enough room to maneuver. I will open for 3bb to 300 to take initiative, though I must be wary of the aggressive players behind me like Claude and FiveTwo.

Q7o is a weak offsuit hand with a queen-high and poor kicker, offering limited playability post-flop. Facing a standard raise to 3BB from middle position (Flash with 44BB stack), the folding range from cutoff includes this hand. Flash's range likely includes stronger broadway, pairs, and suited aces, often dominating Q7o. Calling risks multiway pots with blinds still to act, and 3-betting has poor equity realization. Pot odds (300 to call into 450) are insufficient for the hand's equity (~30-35% vs typical MP range). Preserve stack for better opportunities in this 6-handed tournament with deep effective stacks.

K8s on the button facing a 3x open from Flash in middle position. This is a decent hand that plays well in position. Flash is relatively short-stacked at 44bb. I could 3-bet but K8s is better as a call - good playability postflop with position. Folding is too tight here.

Facing a raise to 300 and a call on the button, I'm in the small blind with 8c6c (suited, low connectors) and 28.5 BB. This hand is marginal and plays poorly out of position in a multiway pot — my implied odds are limited with the button and (likely) the big blind still to act. Calling 250 to complete to 300 gives only ~20% immediate pot-equity break-even and I'd be OOP for the entire hand; 3-betting as a squeeze would commit a large chunk of my stack with a weak holding and is too high-variance here. Preserving chips and waiting for a better spot (or stealing from a later position) is the best long-term play.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action (200 to win 750) and Flash’s 2.5x open plus a button flat suggests wide ranges. K7o is marginal multiway and can be dominated, but the pot odds justify a defend as long as we play cautiously postflop (mostly fit-or-fold, avoid stacking off with weak top pair). No need to squeeze here OOP with an offsuit, dominated king into two players, especially with Flash’s shorter stack likely continuing tighter vs 3-bet.

Multiway (3 players) and we’re out of position with K-high and no spade/backdoor flush equity. This board (9♠4♠3h) is better for the preflop raiser’s c-bet range and also connects with the button’s flatting range enough that a donk stab won’t generate reliable folds. Best is to check, realize our equity cheaply, and proceed fit-or-fold (continue only versus small bets with decent immediate odds or if we improve to a pair/strong draw).

On a flop of 3h-9s-4s, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) and two overcards to the small cards on the board. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, I am facing two opponents, including Claude who has a massive stack and position on me. C-betting into two players on a board that likely connects with their calling ranges (which include many 9x hands, small pairs, and spade draws) is risky. Checking here allows me to see how the button reacts and potentially realize my equity for free if it checks around, or fold if the action becomes too expensive.

Flop is 3h 9s 4s - very dry board. Both opponents checked to me in position. I have K-high with backdoor spade flush draw. This is a good spot to stab at the pot since neither opponent showed strength. A small bet around 1/3 pot should take this down often enough to be profitable.

We have K-high with no spade, no gutshot, and only very thin ways to improve (basically pairing K/7, which can still be dominated/second-best on this texture). Although the immediate price is decent, we are not closing the action (Flash can still respond), and Claude’s stab multiway after the preflop raiser checks is likely weighted toward at least some real equity (9x, spade draws, pairs). Calling here will often lead to having to fold later with little equity realization, so we take the disciplined fold.

I started the hand by raising pre-flop with 6d 7d, which is a standard play for suited connectors in middle position to maintain an aggressive table image. However, the flop of 3h-9s-4s has mostly missed my range. While I do have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5), I am out of position against Claude, who has a significant chip lead and is the button. Claude bet 325 into a pot of 950 after I checked to him. Although the pot odds are roughly 4-to-1, calling here is risky because I only have 4 outs for the straight, and one of those (the 5 of spades) could potentially complete a flush for Claude if he's holding spades. Without a pair, a flush draw, or even a backdoor diamond draw, I don't have enough equity to justify continuing, especially since I'll likely face another bet on the turn. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am UTG with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 6-handed game. My stack is 4,400 chips (44 BB), making me the shortest stack at the table, but I still have enough depth to play standard poker. Opening to 3x the big blind (300) is a standard play to build the pot and assert strength. I have seen several 3-bets from players like Claude and Elon recently, so I must be prepared to evaluate if I face further action, but AQo is definitely worth an opening raise here.

9c5s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing an UTG raise from Flash to 300 into a 450 pot, their range is strong (likely premium hands like pairs, broadway, suited connectors). Calling 300 OOP with this hand has terrible equity, playability postflop is awful, and raising has no fold equity against a strong range while committing too much of my 8475 stack. Standard fold to preserve stack in a long tournament (59/150 hands).
83 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG raise of 300, I have no business calling with this trash hand. Easy fold.

I’m on the button with K♥J♣ facing an UTG open to 300. This is a playable hand in position but often dominated by an UTG raising range, so I don’t want to bloat the pot or commit too many chips with a marginal holding. A tiny raise to 500 won’t achieve much and a larger 3-bet would commit too much from my ~28.5BB stack. I’ll call 300 to use my positional advantage and evaluate on the flop; fold or proceed cautiously if I face a large 3‑bet.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a button cold-call, holding 9♠4♣. Although the price is tempting (250 more), this hand has very poor playability and realization out of position multiway, and we’re also exposed to the big blind squeezing. A squeeze ourselves is unattractive because Minni’s short stack cold-call is often stronger and can jam over us, forcing us to burn chips with a hand that can’t continue. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack.

I hold Pocket Fives [5h 5d] in the Big Blind. Facing a UTG raise to 300 and a call from the Button. The pot is currently 750, and I need to call 200 more. This gives me excellent pot odds (roughly 4.75 to 1) to call and try to flop a set (set mine). Although I will be out of position post-flop, closing the action guarantees I see the flop cheaply. The stack depths of the opponents (4100 and 2550) offer reasonable implied odds. Folding is too tight given the price, and raising is unnecessary and risky against a UTG range.

I hold Pocket Fives [5h 5d] on a flop of [Qs Ts 2h]. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (UTG) and a caller (Button). This board texture (Queen-High, Ten-High with a flush draw) hits the opponents' ranges very hard (e.g., QJ, KQ, AQ, TT, flush draws). My underpair has very poor equity and showdown value here. Leading out (donk betting) would likely be throwing chips away. My best course of action is to check and fold to any significant aggression. If the hand checks through, I might realize some equity, but I cannot invest more chips on this street.

I have top pair with the best kicker (TPTK) on a flop of Qs-Ts-2h. While the board presents several draw possibilities (straight and flush), I have a strong value hand and need to bet to protect against those draws and get value from hands like weaker Queens, Tens, or straight draws. With a pot of 950, a bet of roughly half the pot (500) is standard for maintaining control and extracting value.

I call 500. On the button with K♥J♣ on Q♠T♠2♥ I have no pair but good equity and position: I have ~11 clean outs (3 kings + 4 aces + 4 nines) — roughly ~40% to improve by the river (straight + top-pair possibilities). Facing a 500 c‑bet into a 1450 pot, the pot odds to call (~3.9:1, ~20% equity required) make a call +EV. Calling keeps the pot under control, lets me realize equity in position and see a turn before committing more of my ~25.5BB stack. A raise here is too thin vs an UTG range and would bloat the pot; I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn (raise for value if I pair or make the straight, fold to strong aggression if I miss).

I hold Pocket Fives on a Qs Ts 2h flop. I checked, the UTG raiser bet 500, and the Button called. This heavy action indicates that at least one, if not both, opponents have connected with this board, which hits their ranges well (Queens, Tens, flush draws, straight draws). My underpair is drawing extremely thin (2 outs for a set) and has negligible showdown value. I am getting decent pot odds, but my equity is likely near zero against two opponents who are showing interest. This is a clear fold as my set-mining attempt failed.

On the turn, the King of Clubs is a very dangerous card for my hand. While I still have top pair (Queens) with an Ace kicker, the King completes several potential two-pair combinations (KQ, KT, KJ) and potentially a straight (AJ or J9) that would be in a Button caller's range. Additionally, the board now presents a four-to-a-straight texture (A-K-Q-T), where any Jack makes a straight. Since I am out of position against Minni, who has shown interest by calling the flop, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size, see how she reacts to the scare card, and potentially realize my equity if I hit a Jack on the river to make the Broadway straight. If she bets, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the standard move to avoid bloating the pot against a range that likely improved.

I have K♥J♣ on Q♠ T♠ 2♥ K♣ — top pair with a decent (but not invulnerable) kicker. UTG (Flash) c-bet the flop and then checked the turn; that check looks like weakness or pot control rather than a value shove. Pot is 1,950 and my stack 2,050, so I can apply pressure without committing my entire stack. I want to extract value from worse queens/tens and small Kx hands, deny free cards that could give broadway straights (A‑J / J9), and keep the pot manageable heading to the river. A half‑pot bet (~1,000) accomplishes that: it gets called by many worse hands, folds air, and leaves me room to fold to a polarized, large check‑raise. Plan: bet 1,000; call a modest raise but fold to an overbet/all‑in that polarizes Flash’s range to straights/two‑pair/sets.

On the turn, the King of Clubs is a significant card that changes the board texture. It completes a possible straight for AJ or J9 and potentially gives any Kx hands the lead. Minni's bet of 1,000 into a pot of 1,950 is a strong lead, especially since it represents nearly half of her remaining stack (she has 1,050 left). However, I have top pair (Queens) with an Ace kicker and, crucially, a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Jack gives me Broadway). The pot odds are currently 2.95-to-1. Given my equity with the nut straight draw and the possibility that my Queens are still ahead of a semi-bluff or a hand like QJ/QT, calling is the most mathematically sound decision. Raising is unnecessary as Minni is already pot-committed, and folding would be too tight given the strength of my draw and current pair.

On the river, the board pairs with a second Queen, giving me Three of a Kind (Queens) with an Ace kicker. While this is a very strong hand, the board texture is dangerous; a straight (AJ or J9) or a full house (KT, TT, KK) are all possible within Minni's calling range from the button. Minni has 1,050 chips remaining in a pot of 3,950. If I bet, she is likely to call with any King or better, but she may fold her total air. By checking, I induce her to bet her remaining stack with a wider range, including potential bluffs from missed draws or value bets with weaker Kings (like KJ or K9) that I currently beat. Given the pot odds, I am committed to calling her remaining stack if she shoves, but checking is the best way to maximize value or minimize loss against her narrow range of better hands.

Board: Q♠ T♠ 2♥ K♣ Q♦. I hold K♥ J♣ — that’s two pair (Ks and Qs) but the river pairing the queen dramatically increases combos that beat me (any Qx = trips or better, QK = full house, QQ = quads, plus some straights like J9). Flash’s line (UTG open, flop bet, turn check-call of my raise, river check) is ambiguous — it can be a medium-strength Kx/pot-control line or a trap with a queen. With only 1,050 behind and a 3,950 pot, any river bet risks a check‑raise/all‑in that would force a painful decision versus many hands that already beat me. A thin river probe isn’t worth the risk (it gets called by worse Kx but is crushed by Qx or a shove), and a larger shove is overly committal. I’ll check and take showdown — this preserves my stack against stronger rivered hands while still winning when Flash was controlling the pot with Kx or missed draws.

UTG position with J5 offsuit is a clear fold. This hand has poor equity, low playability, and is dominated by better jacks or any pair. No reason to enter the pot early with trash, especially with 6 players and varying stack depths including a short stack that might shove. Preserve chips for premium hands to maximize long-term stack growth.
42 offsuit is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Easy fold from middle position.

I have K♠Q♣ in the cutoff with a very short stack (1,050 ≈ 10.5 BB). The pot is only the blinds (150) and it costs 100 to see the flop. At ~10 BB the correct strategy is essentially push/fold — calling 100 or making a tiny raise leaves me shallow and easy to isolate or 3-bet by the button/SB/BB. KQo is a strong shove candidate: it has good equity if called and carries meaningful fold equity to pick up the blinds. I want to maximize fold equity and either double up or take the blinds now, so I am shoving my entire stack.

Minni’s cutoff open to 1050 (10.5bb) is an unusually large sizing in this game, which tightens our profitable calling/3-bet ranges significantly. Td4o is dominated when called, plays poorly postflop, and doesn’t make strong nutted hands often enough to justify defending at this price. On the button we can normally defend wider, but versus this sizing the EV of continuing is very negative, so we let it go and preserve stack for better spots.

Minni has moved all-in for 1050 chips (10.5 BB) from the Cutoff. I hold K3o in the Small Blind. The pot odds require me to call 1000 into a total pot of 2200, needing approximately 45% equity. Against a reasonable 10BB shoving range (pairs, Ax, broadways), K3o is significantly dominated and lacks the required equity. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act behind me. I cannot profitably call off over 13% of my stack with this weak hand.

Minni has shoved all-in from the Cutoff for 1050 chips. As the Big Blind, I have already committed 100 chips. I need to call 950 more to win a total pot of 2150 (including my call). This gives me pot odds of approximately 44%. Given that Minni is shoving from a late position with a relatively short stack (around 10 big blinds), her range is likely quite wide, including many hands that Ace-high is ahead of or flipping against (such as K-x, Q-x, and lower suited connectors). According to standard GTO and tournament play, calling with A4o in the Big Blind against a 10BB shove from the Cutoff is a mathematically profitable decision. I have enough chips to absorb the loss if I lose the hand, and the potential to eliminate or significantly chip up makes this a clear call.
KQo is a strong hand from UTG. Standard opening raise to 2.5x big blind. I have the largest stack at the table and want to build a pot with this premium holding.

I have 5♠5♦ in middle position facing an UTG open to 250. My stack is 2,150 (~21.5 BB). With a small pocket pair and this stack depth, shoving is too loose and a tiny reraise is unlikely to accomplish isolation — calling preserves chips and gives correct pot odds to set-mine or play a controlled pot. I’ll call 250 to see a flop; plan is to extract if I hit a set, otherwise fold to heavy post‑flop aggression or a sizable 3‑bet.

UTG’s 2.5bb open and a flat from Minni creates a strong, condensed range up front, and A6o is frequently dominated (AJ+/AQ+/AK) with poor multiway realization. Calling invites squeezes from the button/blinds and puts us in tough spots versus Minni’s short-stack actions (jam/call dynamics) while rarely making top pair good enough for big pots. A bluff 3-bet is also unattractive into UTG strength and a caller. Best EV is to pass and preserve positional/stack advantage for better spots.

I am on the Button with a pair of Fours (4d 4c). Claude (UTG) raised to 250 and Minni (MP) called. I have a stack of 7600 (76 BB), and Claude covers me, providing excellent implied odds to set-mine. The 'Rule of 20' is satisfied as the potential payoff exceeds 20 times the call amount. Minni is short-stacked (19 BB), which limits implied odds against her, but her call adds dead money to the pot. Being in position allows me to control the action post-flop; I will strictly play for 'set or fold' value. Raising is not optimal as it turns my hand into a bluff against a strong UTG range. Folding is too tight given the price and position.

I am holding 2c 3s in the Small Blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker. Claude raised from UTG and has two callers. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 more into a pot of 900), the hand is nearly impossible to play profitably post-flop out of position against three opponents. I should preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Pre-flop in BB with A9o facing a 250 raise from UTG Claude (loose-aggressive based on history), called by Minni (short stack 1900, likely weak) and Pro (button caller, passive tendencies). Pot 900, calling 150 more (15.8% pot odds, excellent). A9o has strong ace blocker, dominates Ax weaker, live cards for straights/flushes. Multiway pot favors speculative equity realization over raising thin. Minni's short stack reduces raise value. Calling to control pot size, play post-flop OOP with position on some.

Flop: 7h Ks 9c gives me middle pair (9s) with top kicker Ace, decent equity multiway (~30-40% vs typical caller ranges). Board is somewhat drawy (gutshots like 10-8, J-10; backdoor flush/straight potential) but dry enough. OOP first to act in 4-way pot (Claude UTG raiser strong but history shows aggression; Minni short 1900 likely weak; Pro passive button caller). Checking controls pot size, induces bluffs from Claude (loose-aggressive), allows trapping better hands or folding to big bets. Donk betting thin multiway risks check-raises and commits too much OOP when equity realization favors check-call/check-fold dynamics. Pot odds good if bet into; prioritize post-flop control per pre-flop plan.

I flopped top pair with a strong Queen kicker on K97 rainbow board. This is a great spot to c-bet for value against 3 opponents. I want to charge draws (straight draws with 8T, 68, T8) and get value from worse kings and middle pairs. Betting around 60-70% pot is appropriate - around 650-700.

The flop (7♥ K♠ 9♣) completely misses my pocket 5s5♦, but Claude (UTG) has made a relatively small bet (700) into this pot.
