I have a strong hand (ATo) in a heads-up situation from the Small Blind after it was folded to me. Elon in the Big Blind has a relatively short stack of 36 big blinds (3600 chips). Raising here is the standard play to either take the pot immediately or build value against a wider range that the Big Blind will defend with. A raise to 300 is a standard size that exerts pressure while maintaining control of the hand.

4s5c is a suited connector with excellent playability for straights and flushes. Facing a small raise to 300 (3x BB) from the SB after multiple folds, which suggests a wide range. In BB, pot odds are great: calling 200 more into 400 pot (~33% equity needed, and we have ~35-40% vs typical SB open). Defending wide OOP vs SB is standard. Deep stacks allow implied odds if we hit. Raising risks isolation OOP with speculative hand; folding is too tight given price. Call to realize equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high. This low, connected board (4-2-5) is dynamic and hits the Big Blind's calling range well, but I still have a range advantage with all the overpairs. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (50% pot) to fold out his air and protect my hand's equity. If Elon raises, I can comfortably fold given the board texture and his stack size.

Top two pair (fives and fours) on a highly draw-heavy board (4c-2c-5h) with flush draws and straight draws possible. Flop c-bet of 300 into 900 is small (~1/3 pot), consistent with SB continuation betting a wide range including draws, bluffs, and weak pairs. My hand has excellent equity against Flash's likely range (pairs weaker than mine, draws like clubs, gutshots). Raising builds the pot for value, protects against draws completing, and denies equity. Flat calling risks free cards or multiway if others wake up (though folded pre). With 3400 stack (~34BB), raising to 1200 (4x his bet) is balanced: gets value from worse, pressures draws/bluffs, and leaves room post-flop if needed. Folding or checking behind is far too weak.

On this low, connected board (4c-2c-5h), Elon's raise from the Big Blind is very significant. This texture heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many more combinations of two-pairs (5-4, 5-2, 4-2), sets, and straights (A3, 63) than my Small Blind opening range. With only Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3), I am likely behind or facing a high-equity draw. Elon's raise to 1200 into a 900 pot (leaving him with roughly 2200 more) suggests he is prepared to commit his stack. Given my chip lead and the fact that I'm out of position against a range that connects well with this board, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than calling off a significant portion of Elon's stack with just Ace-high.

5-handed ranges are wider, but UTG still needs a relatively strong opening range because we’ll face position from Pro/Flash and a capable BB defend. K4o is frequently dominated by better Kx and broadways, plays poorly postflop (reverse implied odds), and doesn’t realize equity well out of position. With ~79BB effective, there’s no need to take a thin, high-variance open here—just pass and wait for a better spot.

5-handed play, sitting in the Cutoff (UTG+1). Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to open-raise from this position to steal the blinds or play for value. Folding is too tight given the reduced number of players. A raise to 2.5bb (250) is standard to balance risk and fold equity. If 3-bet by the deep-stacked Button or Blinds, I can easily fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey suited, a strong speculative hand that plays excellently in position. Pro raised from UTG+1 to 250. While 3-betting is an option to exert pressure and use the Ace blocker, calling is a highly profitable play that allows me to see a flop cheaply and use my positional advantage post-flop. Given my large stack, I can afford to play this hand speculatively without inflating the pot unnecessarily against an early-position raiser.

Preflop in SB with 4s7c, a trash hand with no playability. Pro raised from UTG+1 (tight range likely), Flash flat-called on button. Pot 650, to call 200 (pot odds ~3.25:1), but equity against raiser and caller is poor (~20-30% multiway). BB still to act and may squeeze. Stack 4250 (~42BB), no need to defend wide here. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

J2s in the BB facing a raise to 250 and a call. I need 150 to call into a pot of 650, giving me ~4.3:1 odds. While J2s is weak, the suited nature gives me some flush potential, and I'm closing the action. With these pot odds and position to close, calling is acceptable.
I completely missed this flop with J2s. Board is 6h Th Kd - I have no pair, no draw (my cards are spades, not hearts). With nothing, I should just check and see a free card.

I hold AcTs on a board of 6hThKd. This gives me middle pair with top kicker. The pot is 800 with 3 players (Claude in BB, myself, Flash on BTN). Claude has checked to me. This board hits calling ranges fairly well, particularly Kx hands and heart draws. Betting here is thin value; hands worse than mine (like 99, 88, smaller Tx) might fold or just call once, while Kx will definitely call and dominate me. Additionally, being in a multi-way pot increases the chance someone has a King. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs or call a bet on later streets with a decent bluff-catcher. I will check back to keep the pot small.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor nut spade flush draw. On this flop (Kd-Th-6h), both the Big Blind (Claude) and the pre-flop raiser (Pro) have checked to me. While Pro's check is somewhat surprising given that this board texture often hits an early-position raising range, betting here into two opponents with only Ace-high is risky. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially improve on the turn with an Ace or a spade, while also keeping the pot small with a marginal hand.

Turn brought the Qs, giving me an open-ended straight draw with my Js (need A or 9 for K-Q-J-T-9 or A-K-Q-J-T). Both opponents checked the flop behind me, suggesting weakness. I could semi-bluff here, but with no showdown value and being out of position against two opponents, checking to see a free river is the safer line. If I hit, I can value bet; if I miss, I can fold to pressure.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades makes the board significantly more dangerous, completing some straights (AJ, J9) and putting two overcards to my pair of Tens on the board. While the flop checked through, suggesting no one had a strong King, the Queen hits the ranges of both the Big Blind and the Button. I now have middle pair and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). Betting serves little purpose as I am unlikely to get value from worse hands, and I don't want to build a pot with a marginal hand on this coordinated board. Checking allows me to realize my equity and hopefully see a free river or catch a bluff later.

On a highly coordinated board of Kd-Th-6h-Qs, both Claude and Pro have checked to me for a second time. While their passivity might suggest weakness, my Ace-high has very little showdown value and limited improvement potential. Betting into two opponents on this texture is risky, as many of their checking ranges (like Jx, middle pairs, or weak Kings/Queens) might still call a single bet. I will check back to realize my equity for free and see the river, potentially winning if the hand is checked down or if I improve to an Ace.

River completed my Broadway straight! With Js and the board showing A-K-Q-T, I have the nuts (A-K-Q-J-T). Both opponents checked flop and turn showing weakness. I need to value bet but not too large since they've shown no interest. Betting around 60% pot should extract value from any King, Queen, or smaller pairs they might have.

The river is the Ace of Hearts, making the final board [6h, Th, Kd, Qs, Ah]. This puts four cards to a Broadway straight (10-Q-K-A) on the board; literally any Jack makes a straight. Additionally, there are now three hearts, making a flush possible. Claude leads out for 500 into two opponents. While the Ace improves me to Two Pair, my hand is extremely vulnerable. Claude's betting range into two players on this texture is likely polarized to straights, flushes, or strong two-pair/sets, most of which beat me. It is highly unlikely he is value-betting a worse Ace (like A2-A9) given the 4-liner to a straight and flush potential. Furthermore, I still have Flash behind me to act. Folding is the disciplined play as my hand has become a bluff-catcher on a board favoring the opponent's range.

On a highly coordinated board of 6h-Th-Kd-Qs-Ah, Claude has led for 500 into a pot of 800 after everyone checked the flop and turn. The river Ace is a very dangerous card as it completes a four-card broadway straight (any Jack makes a straight) and a potential heart flush (6h-Th-Ah). My hand is Top Pair with the worst possible kicker (Ace-Trey). Given Claude's position in the Big Blind and the passive action on the previous streets, his range includes many Jx hands (straight), suited hearts (flush), and certainly better Aces (A4+). Since I am losing to almost every value hand in his range and only beating a total bluff or a smaller pair turned into a bluff (which is unlikely on this texture), I will fold to preserve my stack.

I am in UTG position (5-handed) with JcTd. My stack is 7550 (75 BB). While 5-handed play allows for wider ranges, JTo is still a marginal hand to open from the earliest position. It is easily dominated by hands that call or 3-bet (AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, KQ). With deep-stacked opponents (Flash, Claude) and a short stack (Elon) yet to act, the risk of facing aggression is significant. Being out of position post-flop with a easily dominated hand is a recipe for losing chips. Therefore, the prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding Ace-Ten offsuit (Ac Th) in UTG+1. In a 5-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. I am the chip leader with 21,700 chips, which allows me to play aggressively and apply pressure to the shorter stacks. I will open for 300 (3x the big blind), which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament, to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or define the range of any callers.

Jd6s is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an early position raise from Flash, who plays aggressively but from UTG+1 suggests strength. On the button with 42BB stack, calling 300 into 450 gives poor implied odds due to dominated equity and reverse domination risks. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 70 hands left.

J2s is a weak hand, especially facing an early position raise from Flash. Being in the small blind means I'll be out of position for the entire hand. The suited jack doesn't provide enough equity to profitably defend here. Easy fold.

Flash is opening to 300 (3x) and I’m in the big blind with 75o, a dominated, low-connectivity hand that performs poorly out of position. Although the direct price (200 to win 450) looks tempting, I won’t realize enough equity versus an UTG+1 raising range at 5-handed, especially at 3x sizing. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive with this hand profile (no blockers, poor playability when called). Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack for better defend/3-bet candidates.
I am UTG with Ten-Three offsuit. This is a very weak hand and does not warrant an open from early position, especially as the chip leader. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Q2o is a trash hand from UTG+1 position in 5-handed play. Offsuit, poorly connected, dominated by any other queen or better cards. Facing the BB 100 to limp, but limping weak hands leads to trouble postflop OOP. Raising has no value as it's not a stealing hand with position. Folding preserves my 42.5BB stack for better spots. Opponents have deep stacks, so no need to speculate with marginal equity.

On the button with K3o, two folds in front. This is a standard steal spot with only the blinds behind. K-high has some showdown value and blocking value. Opening to 250 (2.5x) to attempt to steal the blinds.

Claude is opening from the Button with a likely wide range, and flatting A3s from the small blind invites the BB to come along cheaply and puts us in a tough multiway, out-of-position spot. A3s is a good 3-bet candidate: it has strong blocker effects (removes some AA/AK/Ax continues), plays well when called (nut-flush potential, wheel equity), and benefits a lot from taking the betting lead. I’ll 3-bet to a size that pressures a wide open and discourages the BB from peeling.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 7h, a weak offsuit connector. The action in front of me is heavy: the Button (Claude) opened to 250, and the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1000. Facing a 3-bet, my hand has very poor equity and playability, especially being out of position against the original raiser if they call. Calling 900 chips (over 10% of my stack) is not justifiable with this hand strength in this spot. The correct and disciplined play is to fold.

I opened K3o as a steal from the button but FiveTwo 3-bet to 1000 from the SB. K3o is too weak to continue here - it's dominated by most 3-betting hands (AK, KQ, KJ) and has poor postflop playability. Getting roughly 1.8:1 isn't enough with this weak holding. Standard fold.

UTG with T9s suited in 5-handed play. T9s is within an opening range from early position short-handed due to its playability, straight and flush potential. Opponents behind are aggressive based on hand history (FiveTwo, Claude frequent raisers; Pro calls often), but pot odds favorable for a steal (pot 150, minraise 200). My stack of 4250 (~42BB) allows for post-flop flexibility. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 builds pot while maintaining fold equity. Limping weak UTG, folding too passive for tournament progression.

KTs is a solid hand with good playability. Elon raised from UTG with a short stack (~40BB). I have position on him and this hand plays well postflop with straight and flush potential. Calling to see a flop is reasonable here rather than 3-betting with players behind me.

Elon’s UTG open to 3bb is typically a stronger range 5-handed, and Claude’s cold call further tightens the spot. 8♠4♠ is too weak and too easily dominated, and while we have position, we’ll often face squeezes from the blinds and realize equity poorly multiway. A 3-bet bluff here is also unattractive versus an UTG raiser with a shorter stack (39bb) who can continue aggressively. Best play is to pass and preserve stack for better button steals/spots.

I hold 2h 4d in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Facing a raise from UTG and a call from UTG+1, I am out of position against ranges that dominate mine significantly. Even with pot odds, the equity of 42o is too low to justify a call, and the playability is non-existent. Folding is the only correct option.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 6s. Elon raised to 3x from UTG and Claude called from UTG+1. While I am getting 3.75:1 odds to call, playing a weak, off-suit hand out of position against an UTG opener and a caller is generally a losing strategy. 86o doesn't flop well enough to overcome the positional disadvantage and the strength of the ranges I'm likely facing. I'll wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

Preflop raise with T9cc suited connectors was standard from UTG in 5-handed play for playability and potential. Flop Ks Kd 5d is a complete brick: no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw (gutshot unlikely). Board heavily favors opponent's calling range (AK, KQ, sets, etc.), and paired board reduces my fold equity for bluffs out of position. Pot 750 into my 3950 stack (~10% pot check), but with 0 equity, check-fold is optimal to preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 82/150 hands played. Avoid spew; Claude's call pre suggests strength.

Flopped trip Kings with a Ten kicker - a monster hand. Elon checked to me. With this board (Ks Kd 5d), I should bet for value. The diamond draw is the only concern. Elon raised preflop so he has some hand worth defending. A small bet around 40-50% pot should extract value while keeping his range wide.

Facing a 350 bet into a 1100 pot on a Ks Kd 5d flop with T9cc, which has zero equity: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw (clubs missed diamonds). Preflop I raised UTG with playable suited connectors, but Claude's call and flop bet after my check strongly represents Kx, sets (KK/55), or AxK. Paired board caps my range and favors value-heavy calling ranges. With 3950 stack (~40BB), pot odds ~3:1 but 0% equity makes call a pure bluff catcher without one. No fold equity for raise out of position. Preserve stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 82/150); check-fold as planned is GTO.

KTo is a playable hand from UTG but marginal. With a healthy stack of 187BB, I'll make a standard 2.5x open raise. This hand plays reasonably well postflop and can flop strong top pairs. If I get 3-bet, I can evaluate based on the opponent.

Claude is opening from UTG 5-handed to 2.5bb, which should be a fairly strong/structured range. With 9♦7♣ offsuit we’re dominated by his value hands and don’t realize equity well postflop (poor playability, reverse implied odds) even with position. Not worth a defend or a squeeze candidate here given stack sizes and players left to act in position/blinds.

I am on the Button with Ad 3c. Claude opened from UTG to 250 (2.5x BB). Against an UTG opening range, A3o is a weak hand often dominated by stronger Aces (AK-A5) or flipping against pairs. While I have position, the playability of offsuit low Aces is poor, and I risk being squeezed by the blinds or dominating myself if I hit top pair. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. Even though I have a large stack, calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a hand that has very little equity or playability. Folding is the most prudent decision to conserve my chips for better opportunities.

Facing a UTG raise from Claude to 250 (150 to call into 400 pot). My hand K4o is offsuit with mediocre playability and often dominated by better kings or strong hands in Claude's UTG range. Although BB vs EP open defends wide in theory (K4o is in mix), my 38.5BB stack is relatively short for the tournament stage (hand 83/150), and Claude has shown aggression but strength from UTG. Pot odds ~2.7:1 require ~27% equity, but realized equity postflop will be lower OOP—no, I have position as BB. Still, better to preserve stack for premium spots rather than defend marginal king. Fold to avoid tough postflop decisions and wait for better opportunities.

5-handed from UTG, 7♠7♣ is well within an opening range and plays best as the aggressor. Raising builds the pot versus worse pairs/broadways, denies equity to overcards, and gives us initiative to win many flops with a c-bet when we miss. With a shorter stack in the SB (Elon), a slightly larger open also applies pressure without bloating the pot too much versus the deeper stacks behind. If we face a 3-bet, we can continue versus reasonable sizing (set-mine/call in position depending on opponent) and avoid getting stacked in marginal spots when overcards come.

I am holding Ace-Nine offsuit in UTG+1. The player Under The Gun (FiveTwo) has opened with a raise to 250. A9o is a weak offsuit Ace that is often dominated by the opening range of a player in early position. Calling could put me in a difficult spot post-flop, especially if squeezed by players behind me or if I hit an Ace with a worse kicker. 3-betting is not ideal against an UTG range. The disciplined and standard play here is to fold.

I am on the button with 8h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has opened from under the gun with a raise to 250. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I am facing an early position raise, there is no reason to continue. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

K5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by most of FiveTwo's UTG raising range which is likely strong (high pairs, broadway, suited connectors). Facing a 2.5x raise from early position in a 5-handed game, out of position in SB postflop would be disastrous. Pot odds are decent (200 to call into 400, ~3.7:1), but implied odds are poor due to hand strength. Shoving 3800 (~9.5x pot) has little fold equity against an UTG raiser with 7875 behind, and gets called by better. Folding preserves my short stack (38BB) for better spots later in the tournament.

AJo is a strong hand in the BB facing a UTG min-raise to 250. FiveTwo has been active with opens. This is a clear 3-bet spot for value - AJo plays well against a UTG opening range and I can extract value or take it down preflop. 3-betting to 800 is appropriate sizing.

Claude’s 3-bet from the BB versus an UTG open is weighted to strong broadways and bigger pairs, but 7♠7♣ is still too strong to fold given we have position and a playable SPR. A 4-bet doesn’t accomplish much (we rarely get called by worse and we risk getting jammed off our equity), so the best line is to flat and realize equity: we can set-mine with decent implied odds and also navigate postflop on low/medium textures where our pair can still be best or where we can pressure later streets when his range misses.

Flop is 6d Kd Td - all diamonds. I have AcJd with no diamond, giving me only a gutshot (need Q) and two overcards. This is a bad board for my hand: no flush draw, K on board hits FiveTwo's 3-bet calling range (Kx hands, suited broadway, pocket pairs). With no real equity besides a 4-outer gutshot, c-betting into this monotone board is risky - FiveTwo will have many hands that connect. Checking allows me to see what they do and potentially realize my gutshot cheaply.

Claude’s BB 3-bet versus my UTG open is strong, and on a monotone K♦T♦6♦ flop my 7♠7♣ has very little equity when called (mostly set outs). However, Claude checking OOP gives me a good opportunity to take the pot with a small stab, leveraging the fact that my flatting range can contain suited diamond hands (and some made flushes), while a meaningful part of his range (AQ/AJ without a diamond, some low-frequency bluffs) has to fold. I’ll keep sizing small to risk little versus his strong continuing range and comfortably fold if he springs a check-raise.

I need to reconsider - I have the Jd, which gives me a flush draw on this all-diamond board! That's 9 outs for a Jack-high flush, plus 3 non-diamond Queens for the nut straight (Qd would give me flush). That's ~12 outs total, roughly 24% equity to improve by the river. Pot is 2200, I need to call 550, getting 4:1 (20% needed). With my equity and the Jd flush draw being well-disguised, this is a clear call.

Turn is 3h - a total brick. I have the Jd for a flush draw (9 outs) plus 3 non-diamond Queens for the straight (12 outs total, ~24% equity). FiveTwo bet small on flop and I called. Now action is on me with no bet to call. Checking makes sense here - I can see a free river to realize my equity. Semi-bluffing is an option but FiveTwo has shown they'll continue with their hands. Checking keeps the pot controlled and gives me a free look at the river.

We’re in a 3-bet pot where Claude check-called a small stab on a monotone K♦T♦6♦ flop. That call heavily weights him toward any diamond (already a made flush), plus some strong one-pair/overpair hands with a diamond, and occasionally slowplayed monsters. The turn 3h doesn’t change that texture or weaken his continuing range, and with 7♠7♣ (no diamond blocker) our equity when called is basically just two outs to a set.

Barreling here is therefore low-fold-equity and high-variance: he’s not folding flushes, and his non-diamond continues (Kx/overpairs) also often won’t fold to one more small/medium bet. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our tiny river equity, and avoids getting check-raised off the hand.

Plan: check back turn; if we spike a 7 on the river we can go for value, otherwise we’re mostly in bluff-catch/fold mode depending on sizing and runout.

River brings 6h, pairing the board. I have Ac Jd - just Ace-high with no pair. My Jd flush draw missed (only 3 diamonds on board). FiveTwo bet flop small, then checked turn, suggesting a weak hand or missed draw. Checking makes sense here - I have showdown value against busted draws but can't value bet Ace-high. Bluffing is risky since FiveTwo has called my 3-bet and shown interest in the pot. Check and see if my Ace-high is good against missed draws.

Claude’s line (3-bet pre, check-call small flop bet on a monotone K♦T♦6♦, then check turn and river) is still heavily weighted toward hands containing a diamond (made flushes and strong one-pair+diamond holdings) that simply aren’t folding to a river stab. Our 7♠7♣ has very little equity when called and no meaningful blockers, and turning it into a bluff risks running into a check-raise from trapped flushes on the paired river. Checking back takes the free showdown, avoids high-variance spew versus a stronger range, and occasionally wins versus the small part of his range that peeled once without a diamond and gave up.

I am in UTG position with A4 offsuit. Even in a 5-handed game, A4o is a marginal hand to open from early position, especially with deep-stacked, aggressive opponents left to act (Flash and Claude) and a short stack (Elon) who might shove. Opening exposes me to being 3-bet or playing out of position with a weak kicker. With 74 BBs, I have no pressure to make marginal plays. The disciplined action is to fold.

I am dealt Nine-Three of Diamonds in UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak and lacks high-card value or significant connectivity. Open-raising or calling with this hand from early position is mathematically unprofitable over the long term, especially with several active players behind me. I have a healthy stack and do not need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands. I will fold.

Unopened pot to the button after UTG and UTG+1 folds. 2h9h is a suited hand with some playability, especially from the button where I have position on both blinds. Raising wide from the button is standard to steal blinds or build pot OOP. My stack is 38BB, effective vs BB's 94BB, so standard open-raise size of 3BB (300) applies. Blinds are deep but have folded less aggressively in history; however, button range includes this hand for thin value/steal. Folding is too tight, calling risks multiway OOP pots poorly. Raise to apply pressure.
T5 suited is a weak hand from the small blind facing a button open. Playing this OOP will be difficult, and it's not a strong enough hand to 3-bet as a bluff. Clear fold.

Button open to 3bb is likely still fairly wide given 5-handed play and Elon’s shorter 35bb stack. We’re getting decent immediate odds (call 200 to win 650 total post-call, ~31% needed), and Q6o has enough raw equity versus a typical button opening range to defend at some frequency. I don’t want to 3-bet bluff here because Elon can jam over the top and we’d be forced to fold, burning chips. Best is to take the price, keep our range protected, and play straightforward postflop (check/fold a lot of misses; continue with pairs, decent draws, and good Qx on favorable textures).

As the BB defender out of position versus a BTN open, our default is to check range and let the preflop raiser c-bet their advantage. This flop (Th7h2d) is fairly dynamic and still interacts well with Elon’s range (Tx, overpairs, broadways with hearts), while our Qh6s is mostly air: no pair, no meaningful straight draw, only a weak backdoor heart possibility via Qh. Donk-betting here would burn chips and isolate ourselves versus better hands/draws; checking keeps our range protected and allows us to respond appropriately to sizing (mostly fold vs normal c-bets).

Flop of 2d Th 7h hits me hard: bottom pair (2h), nut flush draw (four to flush with both hearts), and gutshot straight draw (8 for 7-8-9-10). Extremely strong multi-way potential hand in position vs BB who checked. Pre-flop, I raised as button raiser, BB called flat – range includes Ax suited, pairs, suited connectors, broadway. Checking risks free cards to their draws (spades flush draw possible, straight draws like JT, 89, etc.). Betting builds pot, gets value from worse pairs/draws (A-high, KQ suited, smaller pairs), protects against overcards. Half-pot bet sizes well into their calling range without bloating pot too much early. Pot 650, bet 400 (~60% pot) balances value/protection/fold equity. Stacks deep (me 35BB, BB ~92BB), but hand crushes their check-call range.

We’re facing a fairly standard BTN c-bet (400 into 1050) on a board that strongly favors the preflop raiser’s value range (Tx, overpairs, strong heart/broadway combos). With Qh6s we have no pair, no straight draw, and only a weak backdoor flush via Qh; our one overcard (Q) has poor realization out of position and is often dominated when it hits (Qx in his range). We need ~28% immediate equity to call, but our practical equity/realization versus Elon’s c-betting range is lower, and raising is bad because his short stack can jam and force us to burn chips. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

I am holding Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) under the gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game. QTo is a standard opening hand in this position at a short-handed table. With a healthy stack of 21,700 chips (217 BBs), I want to play aggressively and exert pressure on the shorter stacks, particularly Elon (41.5 BBs) and Pro (73 BBs). I will open for a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot early or win the blinds.

74o is an extremely weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing an UTG raise from Flash, who plays aggressively but from early position indicates a strong range. I'm in UTG+1, early position myself, and calling would commit chips to a likely multiway pot with poor equity. My stack is 41.5BB, sufficient to wait for premium hands. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in this long tournament.

Ah8h on the button facing a 3x UTG open from Flash. Suited ace with decent playability. I have position which is valuable. Calling is standard here - the hand plays well postflop with flush potential and an ace. Not strong enough to 3-bet for pure value but also too good to fold on the button.

Flash has opened UTG to 3bb and Claude has flatted on the button, creating a strong, multiway range dynamic. K7o is heavily dominated (Kx and broadways), plays poorly out of position, and will realize equity badly from the small blind. A squeeze would need to be fairly large to have fold equity versus two players (and risks getting flatted/4-bet by Flash), which is not a good spot with this hand. Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2h facing a raise to 300 from UTG (Flash) and a call from the Button (Claude). The pot is 750 and I need to call 200 to see the flop. The pot odds are attractive at approximately 3.75:1 (requiring ~21% equity), and raw equity for 92s against two random ranges is likely sufficient. However, my hand is extremely weak (disconnected low cards), and I will be playing out of position against two opponents, one of whom has shown strength by opening from UTG. This scenario drastically reduces my equity realization. With 9-high, I will often miss the flop completely or hit a dominated pair, making it difficult to continue profitably. Preserving my stack of 7300 is preferable to bleeding chips in marginal spots with trash hands.

On a flop of Jc-6d-3d, my Queen-Ten offsuit has not connected, although I do have two overcards to the 6 and 3. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow through with a continuation bet to represent a strong range like overpairs, AJ, or KJ. A bet of 400 into a 750 pot is a standard sizing that pressures Claude's likely range of middle pairs or suited connectors. If Claude raises, I have a clear fold, but a bet here often wins the pot immediately against hands that missed the flop.

Flop completely missed me - I have Ah8h on a 6d3dJc board. No pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. Flash c-bet half pot from UTG after opening. With just ace-high and no draws, continuing is -EV. Even with position, floating here with zero equity is too speculative. Clean fold.

T6o is a very weak hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. It has poor playability post-flop, low equity against typical calling ranges, and no speculative value worth limping early position. With 41.5BB stack, better to fold and wait for stronger hands to maximize EV over the tournament. Opponents have shown aggression in prior hands, but no need to enter pot OOP with trash.
AQo is a strong hand in early position. Standard open raise to 250-300. This hand plays well and I should be raising for value here.

Claude is opening from an early/middle position 5-handed to 2.5bb and we have 9♠3♥, which has very poor equity and playability even on the button. Calling is -EV and invites tough multiway scenarios with the blinds. A 3-bet bluff here is unnecessary given hand quality and Claude’s propensity to continue/4-bet; we preserve our stack for better steal/iso spots.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind, a premium hand in 5-handed play. Claude has opened from the Cutoff (UTG+1) to 250. Given my positional disadvantage post-flop, I prefer to 3-bet for value to isolate the opener, deny equity to the Big Blind (who has a large stack), and take the initiative. My hand is strong enough to play for stacks if necessary, but primarily I want to extract value from Claude's wider opening range. Based on my previous play with strong hands (Hand #42 with AKs), a sizing of around 3.6x the open is consistent. I will raise to 900.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace of Clubs and Six of Clubs. There was an open from Claude (UTG+1) to 250 and a 3-bet from Pro (Small Blind) to 900. While suited Aces have value, A6s is significantly behind the likely ranges of an early position opener and a Small Blind 3-bettor. Calling would put me out of position in a three-way pot with a dominated hand. Given my comfortable stack and the strength shown by two opponents, folding is the most prudent play to preserve chips for better opportunities.

AQo facing a 3-bet from Pro (6400 stack) in the SB. Getting about 2.9:1 pot odds to call 650. AQo is strong enough to continue here - folding is too tight and 4-betting commits too much against a shorter stack who might be 3-betting for value. Will call and play postflop in position.

I 3-bet pre-flop with AQo and the caller (Claude) called in position. The flop comes Jh-6h-Ts. This texture connects very heavily with a standard flat-calling range (sets like JJ/TT/66, two pairs like JTs, straights like KQ, and many flush/straight draws). While I have overcards, the Ace of hearts (backdoor nut flush draw), and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King), I am at a range disadvantage here. Betting into this board is risky as I will likely only be called by better hands or strong draws, and raised by sets/two-pair. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity (see a turn card potentially for free), and evaluate Claude's action. I block the nut flush draw with the Ah, which is good for bluff-catching later, but right now checking is the prudent play.

Pro 3-bet preflop then checked this Jh6hTs flop. I have AQo with two overcards and a gutshot to broadway (K gives me the nuts). Pro's check could indicate weakness (like AK missing) or a trap. With position and decent equity, I should bet for value/protection and to take down the pot. A bet around 60% pot puts pressure on hands like AK/small pairs while building the pot if I improve.

I 3-bet pre-flop with AQo and was called. The flop Jh-6h-Ts is very wet and connects heavily with the opponent's calling range (sets like JJ/TT/66, two pairs like JTs, straights like KQ, and various flush/straight draws). Claude bets 1100 into a pot of roughly 1900 (approx 58% pot), which is a substantial size indicating strength or a strong semi-bluff. While I have two overcards, a gutshot straight draw, and the Ace of hearts (backdoor nut flush draw), I am out of position and likely behind a made hand. Calling would commit a significant portion of my stack (SPR ~1.3 on turn) with a marginal holding. Preserving my 64BB stack is preferable to chasing a draw on a board that favors the opponent.
K6o from UTG is too weak to open. This hand plays poorly from early position - easily dominated by better kings and doesn't have great playability postflop. Standard fold.

5-handed with UTG folded, ATo is strong enough to open from UTG+1. Raising builds the pot with the initiative, denies equity to the blinds, and puts pressure on Elon’s shorter BB stack (40bb) while still keeping the sizing small enough to comfortably fold versus a strong 3-bet range. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open that matches the table’s typical sizing.

I hold 3h 9s on the Button, which is a statistically very weak hand (trash). The player in UTG+1 (FiveTwo) has opened with a raise to 250, indicating a potentially strong range. Despite having position, the hand strength is too low to justify a call or a bluff 3-bet, especially against an early position open. The standard and correct play here is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. FiveTwo, who has a medium-sized stack, opened from UTG+1 with a standard raise to 250. While I am the chip leader and have the odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 400), Ace-Four is a weak hand to play out of position against an early-position raiser. I am likely dominated by most of FiveTwo's continuing range (better Aces) and will be first to act on every street. Given my goal of maximizing my stack over 150 hands, I will take the disciplined route and fold this marginal hand to wait for a more advantageous situation.

Facing a raise to 250 (2.5x BB) from FiveTwo in UTG+1 position (hijack in 5-handed game) after UTG folded. My hand 2d4d is suited but very weak, low cards with poor playability post-flop. Pot odds are favorable (150 to call into 400 pot, getting ~2.67:1 direct odds plus implied), but equity against an opening range (likely 22+, ATs+, KJs+, QJs, AJo+, KQo) is around 35-40%, and reverse implied odds hurt with this hand when behind. FiveTwo has been aggressive in history, often raising and winning pots uncontested or value-betting. With 40BB stack, better to fold and wait for stronger hands or better dynamics rather than defend wide here. Preserves stack for tournament survival and +EV spots.

5-handed but still first to act, 67o is too weak and dominated to open from UTG at these stack depths. It plays poorly versus 3-bets (especially from Flash/Pro behind) and doesn’t realize equity well multiway if we limp. With no ante pressure, the steal incentive is low, so we preserve our stack and wait for better spots/positions.

I am in UTG+1 with 5s 3h (5-3 offsuit), which is a very weak hand. With 64 big blinds, I have plenty of chips to wait for better opportunities. Opening from early position with trash like this is losing strategy. I will fold.

I am on the button with Qd 6s. Both players before me have folded. While this is a standard spot to attempt a blind steal, Q6o is a very weak hand that lacks post-flop playability. Elon in the Small Blind is relatively short-stacked (40 BB), which increases the likelihood of him shoving over a raise, forcing me to fold. Claude in the Big Blind has a deep stack and has shown a willingness to defend and play aggressively post-flop. Given my chip lead and the marginal nature of the hand, I will choose the low-variance path and fold, waiting for a more profitable opportunity.

Preflop in small blind with 6h9d offsuit, facing folds from UTG, UTG+1, and button. Pot is 150 (SB 50 + BB 100), need to call 50 more to complete. This is a weak offsuit hand with poor postflop playability out of position heads-up against the BB. 69o has marginal equity (~40% vs random) but realization problems OOP, especially vs an aggressive BB like Claude who has shown strength in pots. My 40BB stack doesn't necessitate a squeeze or steal here without fold equity. Pot odds are good (3:1), but folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 62 hands left. Standard play is to fold trash like this from SB.

I am in UTG position (effectively Hijack in a 5-handed game) with Jd Td. This is a strong suited connector with good playability and equity retention. With a stack of 64 BBs, I have plenty of room to open this hand. The standard opening size at this table has been around 2.5x (250 chips). Opening allows me to balance my range, which shouldn't consist only of premium pairs and high aces. If I face significant aggression (3-bet), I can easily fold without committing too much of my stack. Therefore, I will raise to 250.

K9o is a marginal hand that performs poorly against an under-the-gun raise. Given Pro's position and the likelihood of being dominated by his opening range (which includes hands like AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, and KJ), folding is the most disciplined play to protect my stack and wait for a better spot.

Pre-flop on the button with Jc4c (suited connectors). Pro raised to 250 from UTG into a 150 pot (50/100 blinds), pot now 400, to call 250 for odds of ~1.6:1 directly but much better implied due to position and deep stacks. J4s plays well multiway post-flop with straight/flush potential, and button position allows controlling pot and realizing equity. Pro's UTG range is strong but not uncallable; folding too tight, 3-betting speculative hand risks domination OOP. SB/BB may call/3bet light, but calling keeps pot small OOP for them. With 40BB stack, speculative call maintains stack flexibility for tournament survival.
36 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise and a caller, I'm out of position with no playability. Easy fold.

We’re getting a very good price (150 to win 650), and A8 suited has solid equity and playability multiway. A squeeze 3-bet is unattractive because Elon’s shorter stack can jam over it, putting us in a poor spot where we’d often have to fold after investing a lot while still being out of position versus Pro’s UTG range. Calling keeps the pot manageable OOP and realizes our equity with a hand that can make strong nutty holdings (top pair + nut flush potential).

Out of position in a 3-way pot on 2h-6s-7c with Ah8h, we have ace-high, no made hand, and only backdoor equity (heart backdoor + some turn-overcard potential). This board connects more with the caller ranges than our defending range, and Pro (preflop raiser) will do a lot of betting here; donking turns our hand into a bluff with low fold equity multiway. Best is to check, keep the pot controlled, and decide vs sizing (we can continue vs small bets due to price/backdoors, and apply pressure on favorable turns if checked through).

I opened JTs from UTG and got called by the Button and Big Blind. The flop comes 7-6-2 rainbow, a low disconnected board. This texture favors the calling ranges (sets, two pairs, lower pairs) more than my opening range (heavy on high cards). Being out of position in a 3-way pot with just two overcards and no significant draws, my equity is low. C-betting into two opponents on this texture is risky and likely negative EV, as I unlikely fold out better hands and get called by anything that beats me. Checking is the standard play here to give up or realize equity if it checks through.

Post-flop on the button with Jc4c on a 2h6s7c board. The board is low and coordinated with possible straight draws (3-5, 5-8, 8-9) and backdoor flush draws. Preflop, I called Pro's UTG raise with suited connectors for implied odds and position. No pair, no strong draw—just overcard Jack, backdoor club flush draw, and backdoor straight potential. Pot is 800, checked by FiveTwo (BB) and Pro (UTG raiser with strong range). Pro's check suggests possible weakness or trap, but my hand has minimal showdown value and is likely behind top pairs or better. Betting risks raising villains with equity or getting check-raised off nothing; checking keeps pot small OOP for opponents, takes free cards for backdoors, and retains position to bet turns profitably or bluff missed draws. With 37.5BB stack and tournament stage (hand 90/150), pot control preserves stack while maximizing fold equity later. Check to realize any thin equity multiway.

Turn pairs the 2, leaving us with just ace-high and no real draw (flush is impossible with only one heart on board and one card to come). In a 3-way pot OOP, a stab here has to work very often to be profitable, and both opponents checking the flop doesn’t necessarily mean they’ll fold turn with hands like 66/77/88–TT/7x/6x that now feel even safer on a paired board. Our hand still has some showdown value versus two overcard floats, so the best EV line is to keep the pot small, realize equity, and be ready to respond to river sizing rather than turning Ah8h into a low-equity bluff multiway.

The turn brings a 2d, pairing the board which is now 2h 6s 7c 2d. The flop checked through, indicating weakness from all players. I hold Jd Td (Jack high) with no immediate draw and very low equity if called. Bluffing into two opponents on a paired board after checking the flop is risky and unlikely to succeed often enough to be profitable, as opponents may look up a delay c-bet with A-high or any pair. Checking back allows me to realize any equity I have (hitting a J or T on the river) or simply give up if the river doesn't improve my hand, without committing more chips with a weak holding.

On the turn with Jc4c on a paired 2h6s7c2d board, multiway pot after calling UTG raise preflop for implied odds and position. Hand has no pair, no strong draws—just overcard Jack, gutshot-ish potential (needing 3-5 for wheel, unlikely), and backdoor club flush. Pot control was correct on flop check; now both opponents check again, suggesting possible weakness (Pro's UTG range may have missed or slowed down, FiveTwo defensive from BB). Betting risks getting called by medium strength (pairs, draws like 5x/8x/9x) or check-raised; multiway bluffs less effective with 3 players. Checking takes free river card, preserves 37.5BB stack (3750) for better spots late tournament (hand 90/150), realizes thin equity if runner-runner improves, and keeps position to bluff or value bet river if board changes favorably. Fold equity low here without initiative; prioritize survival and selective aggression.

We arrive at the river 3-way after flop and turn both check through. With Ah8h on 2h-6s-7c-2d-5h we have only ace-high (no flush, no straight), but importantly we still have some showdown value because both opponents have shown weakness twice and can easily have unpaired overcards (AK/AQ/KQ) or air that will just check back. Turning this into a bluff multiway is unattractive: any 7x/6x/5x/2x, small pairs, or even sticky ace-highs may call, and our line doesn’t credibly represent many strong hands that weren’t betting earlier. Best EV is to check and realize our showdown equity for free.

The board runout is 2h 6s 7c 2d 5h. I have Jack-high (Jd Td) which has effectively zero showdown value against two opponents. The action checked through on the flop and turn, indicating weakness, but ranges are likely condensed to weak pairs (6x, 7x, 5x, 2x) or Ace-high hands that are trying to get to showdown cheaply. While a bet might fold out some better high cards (KQ, QJ) or weak Ace-highs, it is unlikely to fold out any pair, and with two opponents remaining, the probability that at least one of them holds a pair or a sticky Ace is significant. Risking chips to bluff into two players on the river after showing no strength previously is likely a negative EV play. Checking preserves my stack and concedes a small pot that I likely cannot win.

River action on button with Jc4c (jack high, no pair, no draw) in 800 pot multiway after preflop call of UTG raise for speculative suited connectors. Board 2h6s7c2d5h pairs deuces, completes 3-4-5-6-7 straight and 4-5-6-7-8. Pro (UTG raiser, strong range: pairs/overpairs/AK) and FiveTwo (BB wide range) both checked river, showing possible weakness (missed draws, weak pairs) but likely have showdown value (77/66/55/Ax/Kx pairs, 76 two-pair). Bluffing multiway into two opponents has low fold equity; even half-pot bet (400) risks calls from medium strength while committing 10% stack for nothing. Jack high has thin showdown value, might beat bluffs/A-high. Consistent pot control from flop/turn preserves 37.5BB stack (3750) late tournament (hand 90/150, goal maximize stack), realizes equity for free, avoids spew. Check-behind optimal for survival and position exploitation.

I am currently the chip leader with 22,000 chips. Holding 9h 5h under-the-gun in a 5-handed game is a very weak starting position. While I could attempt to use my stack size to pressure the smaller stacks behind me, 95 suited is too weak to justify an open raise from the first position. Folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack and wait for better opportunities.

Js7s is a marginal suited hand with a gap, unsuitable for raising or calling from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. Early position requires stronger holdings to play profitably out of position multiway. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are mediocre (about 2:1), but implied odds are low with this hand's playability issues postflop. Folding preserves my 37.5BB stack for better opportunities in the late tournament stages.

On the button with A5o after two folds. This is a standard open from the button - suited would be better but A5o is still profitable to raise here for stealing blinds or playing in position postflop. Raising to 2.5x.

Claude is opening on the button and we’re in the small blind out of position with 73o, a hand with very poor playability and no relevant blockers for a 3-bet bluff. Even getting ~3:1, we’ll realize equity poorly OOP and face frequent continuation bets plus the risk of the big blind squeezing, turning our call into an immediate loss. Best EV is to give up the small blind and wait for a better defend/3-bet candidate.

I am in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x raise from the Button (Claude). My hand, 6d 4c, is a weak off-suit one-gapper. The pot odds require about 27% equity to call (150 to win 550). While 64o technically has around 32% raw equity against a wide button opening range, my equity realization will be poor playing out of position with a hand that makes weak pairs and few strong draws. With an equity realization factor likely below 1.0, the call becomes marginally negative EV. I choose to preserve my stack and fold.

K7h suited is a playable hand UTG in a 5-handed game due to its playability, flush potential, and straight draw possibilities. With 37BB effective stack, raising builds the pot when ahead or takes down the small blind money (pot 150). Standard 3x raise to 300 pressures the field, especially with some deeper stacks behind but Pro in SB shorter at 60BB. Folding would be too tight short-handed; limping invites multiway pots OOP. Raise to initiate action and leverage implied odds.

K2o is a weak hand facing a UTG open. Even though it's suited to the king, this hand plays poorly and has limited postflop playability. Against Elon's UTG range with players behind, this is a clear fold.

AQs on the button versus a 5-handed UTG open is a clear value 3-bet. Calling invites the blinds in and gives up initiative; raising lets us isolate Elon’s ~35bb stack, deny equity to his opens, and play a strong suited broadway in position. I’m sizing to a firm but not committal 3-bet that pressures his range while keeping my range balanced and leaving room to respond if he 4-bet jams.

I am in the Small Blind facing an UTG open to 300 and a Button 3-bet to 950. Holding pocket Sixes (6c 6d), I am in a difficult spot. The 3-bet size is substantial (9.5bb), representing about 15% of my 60bb stack. To profitably set mine, I typically need much deeper implied odds (usually calling ~5% of stack, not 15%). Furthermore, the original raiser (Elon) is sitting on a ~35bb stack and could easily 4-bet jam, squeezing me out of the pot and rendering my call dead money. Being out of position against the 3-bettor also makes realizing equity difficult if I don't flop a set. The risk-to-reward ratio is poor here.

In a 5-max high roller tournament, Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium starting hand. Elon (UTG) opened to 300, and FiveTwo (Button) 3-bet to 950. With my large stack of 21,900, I am in a position to exert significant pressure. A cold 4-bet from the Big Blind is an extremely strong move that often forces opponents to fold their marginal 3-betting ranges. If Elon, who is short-stacked with only 3,450, decides to jam, I am mathematically committed to calling. If FiveTwo jams (8,875), I will have a tough decision but will likely be priced in given the strength of my hand in a short-handed context. Raising to 2,500 allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate a shorter stack.

Facing a 4-bet to 2500 after my UTG open to 300 and Button's 3-bet to 950. Pot is 3800, to call 2200 leaving me with only 1250 (~12BB) behind from my 3450 stack. K7s is speculative suited but not strong enough versus likely premium ranges from Flash (BB 4-betting) and FiveTwo (Button 3-betting deep). Their ranges: QQ+, AK, maybe some bluffs but positionally Flash defends well OOP. Calling commits too much with dominated hand, weak post-flop playability OOP multiway. Shoving all-in over 4-bet risks stack off with ~35% equity at best vs calling ranges, poor given ICM/tournament dynamics with 58 hands left and 5 players. Folding preserves stack for superior spots; standard discipline against heavy preflop action.

Flash’s cold 4-bet squeeze over my BTN 3-bet (with UTG opener behind) is usually value-heavy, but the sizing (to 2500) and the dead money make it a spot where he can still include some light 4-bets. With AQs we’re not happy 5-bet jamming ~89bb effective (we’d get called by QQ+/AK and have poor equity), and folding here is likely too tight given the price. Calling costs 1550 more to win a pot of 3800 (good immediate odds) and keeps his bluffs in while giving us position. Postflop the SPR will be ~1.2, so I’ll proceed mainly on strong equity flops (Qx, strong draws) and play cautiously on A-high/paired textures where his value range dominates.

On a board of Jc-7s-7c after a 4-bet pot pre-flop, the SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) is very low at approximately 1.37. My opponent's range for calling a 4-bet includes hands like JJ, TT, 99, 88, AK, and AQs. While I have the range advantage with AA, KK, and QQ, checking here is a prudent move. It allows me to control the pot size with my Ace-high and backdoor flush draw. If FiveTwo checks back, I get a free card to hit an Ace or Queen. If he bets, I can evaluate based on his sizing, though his stack size suggests any bet from him will likely be a commitment to the pot.

In a 4-bet pot with a low SPR, this J77 texture heavily favors Flash’s value range (AA/KK/QQ) and is a poor spot to bloat the pot with just two overcards. Flash’s flop check also keeps a lot of AK/AQ-type hands and occasional traps in range; betting here mostly gets called/raised by the hands that beat us and folds out hands we already do fine against by taking a free card. Checking back realizes our equity (A/Q outs + backdoor heart possibilities), keeps our stack protected versus a check-jam, and lets us respond to turn action (call reasonable bets on good turns, bluff selectively if he keeps showing weakness).

The turn brings a third seven to the board [Jc 7s 7c 7d]. In this 4-bet pot, the SPR is low, and the range of my opponent, FiveTwo, is likely very narrow, consisting of AK and pocket pairs (88+). On this board, any pocket pair has now improved to a full house (e.g., 88777). My hand, Ace-Queen, is currently three-of-a-kind with an Ace kicker (7-7-7-A-J). I am currently losing to all pocket pairs and AK. There is no merit in betting here, as I will not fold out better hands and worse hands (like KQ or smaller suited connectors that somehow stayed in) are unlikely to call. By checking, I can potentially see a cheap river and hope to improve to a better full house if an Ace or Queen hits, or simply reach showdown if the action remains checked down.

Turn pairs the board to J777, making hand values mostly about having a Jack (boats) or just kicker strength with trip 7s. With AhQs we have the best possible kicker (A), so we beat all non-J hands like KK/QQ/TT and chop a large portion of Ax hands (AK/AA/A5s-type). Flash’s double check in a 4-bet pot is consistent with overpairs/AK that will often take a showdown line here, and those hands can still pay a small bet. I don’t want to bet big because Jx (especially JJ) has us crushed and will be the one part of his range that can raise; versus a raise we can comfortably fold. A small thin-value/blocker bet targets KK/QQ while keeping losses contained against traps.

On the turn, the board has paired again to show [Jc 7s 7c 7d]. This creates a situation where any pocket pair (88, 99, TT, JJ, QQ, KK, AA) has now made a full house. In a 4-bet pot, my opponent's range for calling pre-flop is heavily weighted toward these pocket pairs and AK. My hand, Ace-Queen, currently holds three-of-a-kind with an Ace kicker (7-7-7-A-J). I am losing to every pocket pair in his range and to AK (which has a 7-7-7-A-K). I only split the pot with other hands like AQ or AT. Given the low likelihood that FiveTwo is bluffing with air in a 4-bet pot after I have shown interest pre-flop, and the fact that I am essentially 'drawing dead' to anything but a split or a bluff, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my significant chip stack.
83o is a very weak hand, especially from UTG position. No reason to open with this trash - easy fold.

5-handed and after Claude folds, A8o is strong enough to open from UTG+1 (effectively HJ). Raising applies immediate pressure to the blinds, takes initiative, and can win the pot uncontested often. I’ll use a standard but not bloated sizing to keep options open versus Elon’s shorter stack in the BB and avoid over-investing if facing a 3-bet/jam.

I am holding Jd 5h on the Button, which is a very weak off-suit hand (J5o). FiveTwo has opened for a raise from UTG+1. Despite having the positional advantage, my hand has very poor equity and playability against an early position opening range. My stack is healthy at 60 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make a loose move here. The standard and correct play is to fold.

FiveTwo raised to 250 from UTG+1. Queen-Ten offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an early-position open. Additionally, Elon in the Big Blind is short-stacked and may look to shove or squeeze. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary risks while maintaining my stack.

Ad6o is a marginal hand facing an EP raise from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in past hands (frequent raises and postflop aggression). However, pot odds are excellent: 150 to call into 400 (getting ~2.67:1 direct, better with implied). Equity vs a typical UTG+1 range (~12-15%: 77+, ATs+, KJs+, AJo+, KQo) is around 33-38%, sufficient given position postflop in BB. Ace blocker helps vs Ax ranges. My 33BB stack allows for postflop playability; fold too tight, 3bet bluff not ideal with poor blockers/postflop playability. Call to realize equity and control pot OOP from raiser.

Top pair top kicker with Ad6c on AhJh7d flop facing UTG+1 raiser FiveTwo, who is aggressive per history. OOP as BB, standard to check for pot control and induce bluffs/checks from his range. Board has flush draw and straight draw potential (e.g., 10T, 89), so vulnerable to stronger Ax (AQ+, AJ) or draws. My weak 6 kicker means I lose to better aces and sets; equity solid vs wide c-bet range (~40-50%) but prefer to realize it passively given position. Stack 32BB allows check-call or check-fold; leading risks too much into strength or gets check-raised off hand. Pot odds post-check bet will guide further; check realizes equity efficiently while controlling pot size.

We opened pre and got a BB defend; on A♥ J♥ 7♦ we retain a strong range advantage and have top pair, but with a weak kicker (A8) and only a runner-runner heart draw. I want value from worse aces (A2–A7), Jx, and heart/broadway floats while denying equity to hands like KQ/QT/T9. A small c-bet keeps the pot controlled versus a short-stack check-jam while still pressuring his wide defend range.

Top pair top kicker (Ad pairs Ah) with weak 6 kicker on a coordinated AhJh7d flop (hearts flush draw, gutshot str8 draws like 10-8, 10-9). Facing small 200 bet (27% pot) into 750 from aggressive UTG+1 raiser FiveTwo (tight preflop range: ~12-15% like 88+, AJo+, KQo+, suited broadways, but c-bets wide/light per history). Excellent pot odds (~3.75:1), ~40-50% equity vs polarized c-bet range (value: sets, two-pair, overpairs, strong Ax; bluffs/draws: flush/straight draws, weak Ax). Ace blocker reduces nutted Ax likelihood, induces bluffs. OOP but heads-up, check-call realizes equity passively, controls pot size (stack ~32BB effective, SPR low ~4:1 post-call). Raising risks isolating vs better (AJ+, sets) or getting check-raised off hand/draws hit turn; folding too tight given odds/strength. Call to pot control, reassess turn (pair vulnerable to 10/8/9/Q flush/straight cards).

Heads-up on turn with Ad6c for top pair weak kicker (pair of Aces) on a highly coordinated board: 7d Ah Jh Kh (heavy heart flush draw, broadway straight potential with Q/10, gutshots). Preflop UTG+1 raiser FiveTwo (aggressive history: frequent raises, small c-bets) bet flop small (200 into ~550 pot), which I check-called OOP. Pot now 950, my stack ~2650 (~2.8 SPR), first to act. Ace blocker reduces nuts (no AA/AKh/AQh), but hand vulnerable to sets (77/JJ? unlikely), two-pair (AJ/AK unlikely), better Ax (AQ+, suited), completed draws (hearts like Qh10h/Jh10h). Equity ~35-45% vs his polarized range (value overpairs/strong Ax/sets; bluffs weak Ax/draws missed). Betting bloats pot OOP into better hands/draws hitting Kh; check induces bluffs from missed draws (e.g., Q10s, flush draws), controls pot, realizes equity passively with good odds likely on river. Positionally disadvantaged but low SPR favors check-call station if bet reasonable; folding too tight given investment/ace blocker/history.

Turn brings K♥, which improves a lot of BB’s flop-calling range (Kx, QT) and adds a stronger draw dynamic, while our exact hand is still just top pair with a weak kicker. However, we also pick up a 4-card heart draw (with 8♥), so we have additional equity and can keep applying pressure. I want a controlled value/protection barrel that gets calls from worse Ax, some Kx/Jx, and charges heart draws, without bloating the pot so much that we’re forced into a marginal stack-off versus a short-stack check-jam. A ~1/3-pot bet accomplishes that and keeps our range strong on most rivers.

Heads-up on turn with Ad6c for top pair weak kicker on draw-heavy board: 7d Ah Jh Kh (three hearts for flush draws, Q10/TQ straight, broadway potential). Preflop called UTG+1 minraise from aggressive FiveTwo (history: tight preflop ~12-15% but wide/light postflop c-bets small sizes like flop 200/turn 350 into growing pots). OOP check-called flop small bet pot control; now facing 350 (27% pot) into 1300 (~3.7:1 odds, need ~21% equity). Equity ~40%+ vs polarized range (value: overpairs/AQ+/sets rare; bluffs/missed draws: weak Ax/flush chases/straights like Q10s, 10x). Ad blocker caps nuts (no AA/AKh/AQh), induces thin value. Low SPR post-call (~1.3:1 my remaining 2650 into ~2000 pot) favors realizing equity passively OOP; raising risks vs better Ax/draws completing (e.g., Qh), isolates vs monsters; fold too tight given odds/investment/position. Check-call station play maximizes EV, reassess river (scary cards help fold equity if bluff bet). FiveTwo small sizing consistent with history (thin value or probes), less likely nuts.

River OOP heads-up with Ad6c for top pair (Aces) weak kicker on a scary, draw-heavy board: 7d Ah Jh Kh 3s (completed heart flush with Ah/Jh/Kh, broadway straight possible with Q10/TQ, sets/two-pair threats like AJ/AK/AJh). Preflop called UTG+1 minraise from aggressive FiveTwo (tight pre ~12-15%: 88+, ATs+, AJo+, KQo; but history shows wide/light small c-bets flop/turn on coordinated boards to probe/induce). Check-called small bets flop (200 into ~550) and turn (350 into ~1300) OOP for pot control realizing equity passively (~3-4:1 odds each street, ~40% equity vs polarized range: value strong Ax/AK/AQ/sets/two-pair; bluffs weak Ax/overpairs/draws). River 3s is a blank (no new draws/pairs, misses most gutshots except unlikely), favors my hand vs missed draws (e.g., Q10s, flush chases without heart, weak Ax). Ad blocker caps nuts (blocks AA/AKh/AQh/AJh), induces bluffs/thin value bets. Low SPR (~1.6:1 post-call history, pot 1650/my stack 2650) favors check: induces bluffs from missed draws/overpairs; pot controls vs better Ax/flush/straight; if bet small (per his sizing history), call profitably with ~35-45% equity; leading bloats pot OOP into monsters/draws, poor vs check-raise. FiveTwo likely polarized (value bets flush/sets/straight/two-pair/AK/AQ; bluffs missed draws), small past bets consistent with thin value/probes but my ace blocks strong Ax. Check maximizes EV: realizes equity passively, exploits aggression history without overcommitting. Fold equity unnecessary given investment/odds.

River is a brick, but the turn K♥ created/confirmed a lot of strong hands in Elon’s continuing range: any heart draw is now a made flush, QT is a made straight, and Kx/Jx/AJ/AK/ KJ combos improved. After he called flop and turn, our A8 is mostly a medium-strength showdown hand and our value target (worse Ax) is relatively narrow and may not call a bet often enough on this texture. Betting also risks getting check-jammed for his remaining ~1850 with a range that will be heavily weighted to flushes/straights/two pair+, and there aren’t many natural missed draws to justify a thin value bet/call. Best play is to take the showdown and avoid turning a decent winner into a tough fold spot.

5-handed from UTG, K9 suited is comfortably within an opening range: it has decent high-card strength, good playability, and blocks some strong continues (Kx). With position disadvantage postflop, I want to take initiative and put pressure on the blinds, especially the short SB who is incentivized to fold or reshove. I’ll use a standard, non-committing open size consistent with the table to steal and keep my range balanced.

I hold A7o in MP (UTG+1) facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG. A7o is a weak offsuit Ace that is frequently dominated by a UTG opening range (AJ+, ATs+, 88+). Calling invites squeeze plays from the button or blinds and leaves me playing a marginal hand post-flop, often dominated if an Ace hits. With 60 BBs, preserving my stack for better spots is the standard play here. I will fold.

I am on the Button with Ten-Four suited. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from UTG. Although I have a large stack and the hand is suited, Ten-Four is a weak hand to play against an early position raiser. UTG ranges are typically strong, and this hand is easily dominated. Additionally, Elon in the Small Blind is short-stacked and may look to jam over any marginal button play. Folding is the most disciplined and standard move here.

Preflop in the small blind with 4s Td (T4o), facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG (early position in 5-handed play). My hand is weak offsuit, with poor playability, dominated potential, and no strong blockers. Stack is 2600 (26BB), short enough to avoid speculative calls OOP. FiveTwo's UTG range is strong (likely premium pairs, broadways, suited connectors). Folding preserves stack for better spots, as calling OOP risks difficult postflop decisions with reverse implied odds. No value to 3-bet bluff here given hand strength and deep villain stacks.
83 offsuit is a very weak hand. Even getting 3.67:1 pot odds, this hand has poor playability and will be difficult to profit with postflop against an UTG open. Easy fold.

I am holding 2h 3c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being in the UTG position (first to act) makes this hand even unplayable, as I would be out of position against almost all opponents if I were to enter the pot. With 60 BBs, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better opportunities. There is no strategic merit in playing this hand. I will fold immediately.

I am holding T2o in UTG+1. This is a very weak hand in a five-handed game. As the chip leader, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with a sub-optimal hand, especially with three active players behind me including two substantial stacks. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with Jc6h offsuit, a weak hand with poor connectivity and no suited potential. Two players folded ahead, leaving blinds with massive stacks (Claude 182x BB, FiveTwo 136x BB). My stack is 26BB, not desperate for shoves. Calling 100 into 150 pot is poor due to weak postflop playability OOP against likely aggressive blinds who defend wide based on history. Raising risks multiway pots or isolation vs strong ranges. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots, especially with 55 hands left in tournament.

Folded to me in the SB with 8d5s - a weak hand. Against just the BB (FiveTwo), I could try to steal but this is a marginal spot. FiveTwo has shown aggression with 3-bets. With such a weak hand, I'll just complete to see a cheap flop rather than risk chips on a steal attempt with no equity advantage.

Blind-vs-blind after SB completes, Claude’s range is extremely wide. J6o has decent raw equity but plays poorly out of position in a bloated pot and doesn’t benefit much from isolating (we’ll get called a lot and face awkward postflop spots). With stacks fairly deep, I’ll take the high-EV, low-variance line: check and realize equity while keeping the pot manageable.

Flopped middle pair with 8d on Ad-8s-7s board. I have showdown value but the ace on board is concerning, plus there's a spade flush draw. Betting would only get called by better hands (any ace, flush draws with overcards). Better to check and control pot size with my marginal holding.

Blind-vs-blind limped pot and SB checks to me on A♣-high texture (A♦8♠7♠). Both ranges are extremely wide, but I have a real range advantage in Ax (I check back some, but I also have plenty) and SB will have a lot of total whiffs that just give up. With J♦6♠ I have no pair and only very thin backdoors, so my best EV is to take one small stab to win the pot immediately and deny equity to random overcards/gutters. If called, I can mostly shut down on bad turns and continue only when I pick up equity (spade, 5/9/T, etc.).

Middle pair with 8d on Ad-8s-7s. FiveTwo's small 100 bet into 200 pot is a half-pot sizing - could be probing with air or value betting an ace. Getting 3:1 pot odds to call with a made hand. Folding is too tight, raising only gets called by better. Calling to see turn and reevaluate.

Turn checked to me with middle pair (8d) on Ad-8s-7s-4c. FiveTwo's check after their flop probe bet suggests weakness - possibly gave up on a bluff or has a draw. My 8s is marginal value that likely only gets called by better (aces, overpairs). Betting thin for value risks being check-raised, and I'm not folding out many better hands. Checking controls pot size and gives me a free shot at showdown.

SB limp/call pre and check-call a small flop stab on A♦8♠7♠ means his range already contains a lot of Ax, 8x/7x, and spade draws (plus some sticky floats). The turn 4♣ is a relative brick that doesn’t improve my hand (J♦6♠ is still just a weak gutshot to a 5 and no meaningful flush equity), and it actually gives him additional pairs (4x) that won’t fold. A second barrel here is likely to get called too often and burns chips with little equity when called. Best line is to check back, realize my small equity, and keep the pot controlled; I can still consider a river bluff on the right card/runout.

River paired the 7s. I have 8d5s for a pair of 8s. FiveTwo bet small on flop then checked turn, suggesting weakness. The 7c doesn't change much. My middle pair has marginal showdown value - betting only gets called by better (any ace, 7x for trips, overpairs). Check to showdown and see if my 8s is good.

River is checked to me in a small blind-vs-blind limped pot. After SB check-calls my small flop stab on A♦8♠7♠ and then checks turn and river, his range still contains a lot of Ax and 7x/8x that will be very inclined to bluff-catch (especially given my line of bet flop, check turn). My hand (J♦6♠) has no showdown value, but it’s also not a great bluff candidate because the 6♠ slightly blocks missed spade-draw combos, and I don’t have key blockers to Ax/7x that I want him to fold. With the pot only 400 and folds uncertain versus a potentially sticky blind defender, the highest-EV, lowest-leak play is to just give up and check back.

4c 6c is a weak suited connector that does not play well from UTG (Under the Gun) in a 5-handed game. While the table is short-handed, this hand is at the bottom of the range and generally doesn't justify an open raise, especially with shorter stacks like Elon and Pro behind who might shove over an open, forcing me to fold and lose chips. As the current chip leader, I prefer to wait for better spots and maintain my stack.

Preflop from UTG+1 with Jc2c, a very weak suited gapped hand with poor playability and low equity against ranges. Stack is 26BB, but early position requires tight play to avoid difficult postflop spots multiway. Pot is 150, calling 100 offers poor implied odds for this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots with 55 hands left.
T5o is weak but I'm on the button with only blinds left to act. Good spot for a steal attempt. Standard button raise to 250.

Claude is opening from the button with a likely wide range, but T7o is a dominated, low-playability hand to defend from the small blind. Calling puts us out of position and invites the big blind to come along/squeeze, and this hand doesn’t have good blocker value to choose as a 3-bet bluff. Best EV is to conserve chips and wait for stronger SB vs BTN defend candidates.

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 7c, a very weak off-suit hand. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. The Small Blind folded. I am facing a raise of 150 more into a pot of 400. While the pot odds are decent (requiring ~27% equity), 74o performs very poorly against even a wide button opening range and suffers significantly from being out of position. It is difficult to realize equity with this hand, and calling would likely lead to losing more chips post-flop. Therefore, folding is the correct decision.

UTG in 5-handed play with 26BB stack (2600). A2s suited is playable as an open-raise: ace-high, suited with flush potential, nut flush draw equity, and blocks AA/AK. In late tournament stage (hand 97/150), need to build stack rather than wait for premium hands. Standard open-raise size 3x BB to 300 given aggression history from opponents like FiveTwo and Claude. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots OOP. Folding too passive for position and stack depth.

54 suited is a speculative hand that needs good implied odds. Facing an UTG raise from a short-stacked Elon (only 2000 behind), I don't have the implied odds to profitably call. Out of position with low suited connectors against an early position raise is a fold.

We’re on the Button with T3o facing an UTG open to 300 from a short 23bb stack. This hand is far too weak and dominated to defend profitably versus an UTG range, even with position, and calling also invites squeezes from the blinds. No reasonable 3-bet option exists here (shoving would be torching chips). Best play is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o). The player in UTG (Elon), who is the short stack with about 23 big blinds, has raised to 300 (3bb). A2o is a weak hand, especially against a UTG opening range which is typically strong. Being out of position and facing a raise from a short stack who is likely playing tight-aggressive creates a situation with poor playability and high reverse implied odds (if an Ace flops, I am likely dominated). Conserving chips is better than engaging in a marginal spot out of position. The standard play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs8s and facing a 3x raise from Elon, who is in UTG and has a relatively short stack of 2300 (23 BB). Suited queens are generally strong enough to defend the big blind against a single raise, especially since I am getting over 2:1 odds on the call. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the potential to hit a flush or a strong pair justifies a call here to see the flop.

I defended the Big Blind with Qs8s against Elon's UTG raise. The flop is Ts-9h-3h, which doesn't directly hit my hand, though I have two overcards and a backdoor spade flush draw. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to him and see how he reacts. Elon is playing a relatively short stack (about 20 BBs remaining), so I need to be cautious about getting involved in a large pot with just Queen-high.

Nut flush draw (Ah for nuts) with 9 immediate outs (~35-40% equity depending on villain range), plus backdoor straight potential, in position heads-up vs BB who checked flop (likely weak or trapping). Pre-flop as UTG raiser with suited ace, continuation bet standard to deny equity, extract value from worse draws/pairs (e.g., smaller flush draws, T9, sets unlikely). Pot 650, my stack 2300 (~3.5x pot), his deep stack. Pot-sized bet applies pressure, gets folds from floats/marginal hands, builds pot if called for all-in later streets. Optimal semi-bluff size for fold equity + value given tournament stage and short stack needing to accumulate.

On a flop of Ts-9h-3h, my Qs8s has very little equity against an UTG raiser. Elon bet 650 into a 650 pot, and with his short stack of 1350 remaining, he is effectively committing himself to the hand. I have only two overcards (one of which, the 8, is only an overcard to the 3) and a backdoor spade draw. Given that Elon is likely to shove the turn on most cards and my hand is currently just Queen-high with no direct straight or flush draws, it is not profitable to continue. I will fold and preserve my large stack.
T8o from UTG is too weak to open. This is a fold in early position - need stronger hands to open from UTG in a 5-handed game.

5-handed after UTG folds I’m effectively in the cutoff, so ranges widen and stealing the blinds is valuable. 9♣7♥ is not a premium, but it has decent playability in position and benefits from fold equity versus the blinds. I’ll open with a standard small raise to pressure the SB/BB, while keeping the risk contained. Plan: fold to a shove from the short BB (Elon) or to a sizable 3-bet from Flash/Pro; continue versus flats and play positionally.
