I hold Ten of Hearts and Four of Clubs on the Button. This is a very weak off-suit hand (T4o) with poor playability. An opponent in early position (UTG+1) has opened for a raise to 250. My hand is well behind their opening range, and I do not have the equity or playability to justify a call or a bluff 3-bet. Preserving my stack of 58bb for better opportunities is the correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands. FiveTwo, who has a significant stack of 13,175, has raised to 2.5x the big blind from early position (UTG+1). While the hand is suited, its connectivity is poor and the stack-to-pot ratio doesn't justify a loose call out of position against a player who has shown aggressive post-flop tendencies in previous hands. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

Pocket sixes is a strong hand from the big blind facing a single early position raise to 2.5BB. My stack is 28.5BB, providing decent implied odds for set mining while having suited connectors-like playability for straights and flushes. Calling gets good pot odds (150 to call into ~550 pot, ~3.7:1) and keeps the pot multiway potential low since only one opponent. 3-betting risks too much stack (SPR would drop low) against an aggressive opponent like FiveTwo who has shown big moves, and folding wastes BB defense equity. Plan: call, check-raise strong flops or float weak ones.

Pocket sixes remains strong heads-up OOP vs FiveTwo's UTG+1 range on this J-7-3 two-club flop. My pair beats bluffs, Ax, KQ, suited connectors, and many floats, while blocking sets. Board favors villain's Jx or club draws, but my equity is ~55-65% vs typical value/broadway range. SPR ~9:1 post-flop allows check-call or check-raise. Donk betting risks folding out worse hands and gets raised off equity; checking controls pot, induces bets from Jx/overs/draws for check-raise value, or check-folds to huge overbets. Aligns with pre-flop plan to check-raise strong flops. FiveTwo aggressive (history of turn/river barrels), so check to realize equity multi-street.

We flop a weak pair (7x) with a mediocre kicker plus a small backdoor (9c gives us some runner-runner club equity). Versus Elon’s short stack, a c-bet often forces us into an awkward spot: he can check-jam and we’d be priced into a tough call with a hand that rarely has the needed equity against Jx/overpairs/strong draws. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our equity, and can induce him to stab turns with worse hands/air while we retain some showdown value.

Heads-up OOP on turn with 66 on Js-3c-7c-Kc board. Middle pair, no draw, but flush completed and K-high added. Villain's UTG+1 range checked flop (missed Js/overs/draws?), suggesting weakness or trap, but turn brings scary club flush and K. My equity ~40-50% vs polarized range (ahead of missed draws/bluffs, behind Kx/Jx/clubs/sets). SPR ~5:1 favors checking to pot control OOP with 27BB stack; betting risks folding out worse (draws pay off) while inviting raises from Kx/flushes. Induces bluffs from aggressive FiveTwo (history of turn barrels). If bet, call small or check-raise jam for value/protection, but check realizes equity better multiway potential low. Plan: check, call/raise villain's bet based on sizing.

Turn brings the K♣ completing a 3-flush and greatly favors my perceived range as the preflop raiser (I have a lot more Kx and strong club combos than BB). Elon has checked twice, which caps him toward marginal one-pair hands, weak draws, and air. With 7h9c I have modest showdown value (pair of 7s) plus a strong backup plan (flush draw needing one more club), so I want to deny equity to his overcards and pick up the pot immediately a good portion of the time.

I’ll use a small-to-mid turn stab that applies pressure without bloating the pot unnecessarily versus a stack that can check-jam. If Elon check-jams, his range is heavily weighted to made flushes/Kx/strong Jx+club and I’m not getting the right price to continue, so this is a bet/fold. If called, I can value-bet club rivers aggressively and often check back many non-club rivers to realize my showdown value.

Heads-up OOP on turn with 66 after checking flop and facing a small 300 bet into 850 pot (~1/3 pot) from aggressive FiveTwo. Board Js-3c-7c-Kc completes club flush, adds K-pair/straight potential, scary for my middle pair. Villain's pre-flop UTG+1 raise + flop check (miss?) + small turn bet suggests polarized range: weak Kx/Jx value thin, missed draws/club semi-bluffs, or traps. My equity ~45-55% vs that (beats Ax/KQo/draws/Jx bluffs, loses to sets/two-pair/flushes); excellent pot odds (300:1150 ~3.8:1, need ~21% equity). 27BB stack favors calling to realize equity cheaply OOP vs barrel-happy villain (history of turn/river aggression), pot control, avoid overcommitting raise with marginal hand post-flush. Raising risks isolating better hands or folding out worse; folding too tight given odds/history. Plan: call, evaluate river (check/fold/call small bet if no scary card, value-raise if brick pairs board).

River OOP heads-up with 66 on Js3c7cKc9s board after calling small turn bet. Pot 1150, stack 24BB. Villain (FiveTwo, aggressive UTG+1 raiser) checked flop (suggests missed Js/overs, weak Ax/KQo/draws), bet small turn (~1/3 pot) after flush completes/scary Kc (polarized: thin Kx/Jx value, club semi-bluffs, bluffs). River 9s bricks most remaining draws (adds T8/QT straights but unlikely), pairs Js weakly. Equity ~45-55% vs villain's range (beats Jx/7x/A-high/missed clubs/bluffs, loses to sets/TPTK/flushes/KJ+). OOP with medium pair favoring pot control; checking induces bluffs/value bets from thin hands/draws per villain's barreling history (e.g., Hands 7,11,29+), realizes equity cheaply. Betting risks check-raise from flushes/Kx or folding out worse; donk too thin for value, invites raises. Check exploits aggression, sets up cheap showdown/call small bet (SPR~2:1 post-check). Plan: check, call reasonable bet/fold overbet.

We improve to two pair (9s and 7s), which is strong versus Elon’s likely check/call range from the turn: lots of Kx (top pair), some Jx, and some 7x/club-in-hand floats that picked up equity on the K♣. He’s checked river again, so we should go for thin-to-clear value rather than check back.

However, this board still contains credible nutted hands (turned club flushes, and now some straights like QT/T8 or better two-pair like K9), and Elon’s remaining stack is short enough that a check-jam is possible. So I want a value size that gets called by worse one-pair hands but doesn’t commit me—planning to bet/fold if he shoves.

A ~50% pot bet accomplishes that: it targets Kx/Jx effectively and keeps my loss smaller if he jams representing flush/straight.

River OOP heads-up with 66 vs FiveTwo's UTG+1 range on Js3c7cKc9s board. Pot 1750, facing 600 bet (~1/3 pot), stack 2400 (call commits ~25%, leaves ~1800 behind, SPR ~1.4:1 post-call). Villain checked flop (misses Js/sets unlikely, favors Ax/broadway/draws), small turn bet after scary Kc/flush complete (polarized: thin Kx/Jx/clubs value, missed draws bluffs), river bricks most draws (9s adds weak pair/straight fillers but pairs Js lowly). Equity ~50%+ vs range (beats Jx/7x/A-high/missed clubs/KQo bluffs thin value ~25-30% range, loses to sets/TPTK/flushes ~20-25%, ties some). Pot odds excellent (600 into 2350 ~20.5% needed). FiveTwo's history aggressive (turn/river barrels Hands 7,11,12,30,44,49,92; thin value check-calls), exploits by calling light OOP. Raise risks isolating better (flushes/KJ call/fold equity poor) or bluff-catch worse; folding too tight given odds/history/check-flop weakness. Call realizes equity, pot controls, cheap showdown vs barreler.

5-handed UTG still plays relatively tight. J5 offsuit is dominated by most continuing ranges and performs poorly when called. With a 17.5bb short stack in the SB, an open also risks inducing a shove that we cannot profitably continue against, turning this into an unnecessary open/fold spot. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better steal/pressure spots.

It is 5-handed play and I am in the Hijack (UTG+1) position. My stack is roughly 58.5 BBs. While A9o is often included in a wide opening range from this position in 5-handed tables, it is at the bottom of the off-suit Ace range. The Small Blind (Elon) is sitting on a short stack of 17.5 BBs, a prime size for reshoving over an open. A9o is not strong enough to call a shove comfortably, and opening just to fold to a shove is a leak. Additionally, the Button and Big Blind are deep-stacked and may apply pressure or float wide. Given the potential for domination and the stack dynamics, folding is the disciplined, low-variance play.

I am on the button with 8c 3h, which is a very weak starting hand. While the button is a prime position for stealing blinds, the small blind (Elon) is very short-stacked with only 1,750 chips. This makes him highly likely to shove over an opening raise, and my hand has zero equity to call such a shove. Given the stack dynamics and the poor quality of my cards, folding is the most prudent play.

Folded to SB with Qd7h, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability OOP vs BB. Stack of 1750 (17.5BB) is short, but Q7o is not in shoving range vs Claude who defends aggressively and has deep stack (18k). Limping OOP is poor; raising risks commitment with marginal equity. Best to preserve stack by folding the dead money.

I am in UTG position with 2d 6s, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em (7-2 offsuit being the worst, 2-6 offsuit is close). With 58.5 big blinds, I have a healthy stack and no reason to get involved with trash cards from early position. Folding is the only standard play here.

I am holding 9-6 offsuit in UTG+1. This is a very weak starting hand and generally does not perform well, especially when opening from early position in a 5-handed game. My stack is very healthy at 191 BBs, so there's no pressure to play marginal or sub-par hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Held 2h Td offsuit on the button, facing unraised blinds (pot 150, call 100). This is a trash hand: low kicker, offsuit, poor post-flop playability, easily dominated by any calling range (e.g., any Tx, Ax). Short stack (17.5BB), but blinds are very deep (186BB and 145BB) and likely to defend wide or 3-bet. Fold equity exists for a steal raise (min to 200, pot odds ok), but hand too weak to realize equity if called--flop OTB often missed, hard to continue profitably OOP initially. Limping invites multiway pots where hand unplayable. Preserve stack for premium spots or better steals (e.g., suited connectors, Ax) with tournament pressure building (hand 100/150). Folding maintains discipline to maximize EV long-term.
Folded to me in the SB with J9 suited - a solid hand heads-up against the BB. This is an easy raise spot to put pressure on FiveTwo. Standard raise to 2.5x.

Claude is opening from the small blind 5-handed, which is typically a very wide range. Getting a good price (150 to call into a 350 pot) makes defending profitable even with a marginal hand like 5♠4♥, since it has reasonable equity vs a wide SB range and can realize value when it flops straights/two pair. I don’t want to 3-bet this offsuit connector and bloat the pot out of position with a hand that plays poorly when called/4-bet. Take the cheap defend and play postflop.

I have J9 suited in hearts on a Kh-3d-Qd board. I have a gutshot straight draw (need a T) and a backdoor heart flush draw. I'm out of position against FiveTwo who flatted preflop. A small c-bet makes sense here to continue my preflop aggression and potentially take down the pot. If called, I still have outs to improve. Betting around 1/3 pot keeps the investment small with my drawing hand.

Claude is opening very wide from the SB and then making a very small c-bet (175 into 675), which is often a range-bet / blocker with lots of air and weak one-pair hands. With 5♥4♠ on K♥ Q♦ 3♦ we have essentially no showdown value and very little equity if we just call, so the best way to win this pot is to apply pressure now. As the BB defender, I still credibly represent strong hands (KQ, Kx, Qx, 33, some strong draws), and a check-raise will pick up the pot a high percentage of the time versus his weak continuations. I’ll use a sizing that meaningfully punishes his tiny bet and forces folds from his garbage while setting up turn barrels on A/J/T/♦/2 when called.

FiveTwo raised my small c-bet from 175 to 850. I have J9 hearts with just a gutshot (need a Ten) and backdoor flush draw. Pot is 1525, need to call 675 - getting ~2.25:1. My gutshot gives me ~8% equity, backdoor flush adds marginally. Even with implied odds, hitting my straight puts 4 to a straight on board, limiting value. FiveTwo's raise suggests real strength (Kx, Qx, or better). This is a clear fold - insufficient equity and poor position.

I am in the UTG position with 3s 6h, which is a very weak starting hand. Given my position and the high probability of being raised or dominated by the players behind me, the most prudent action is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

AQo is a premium hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. With my stack at 1750 (17.5BB), raising is standard to build the pot, isolate, or steal the dead money in the pot (150). Standard raise size of 3x the BB (300) is appropriate given the short stack and deep opponents behind. Folding weak hands preflop as observed in history to maintain pressure.

Q2o is a weak hand. Elon raised from UTG+1 with only 1450 chips left (short stack), so he's likely committed with a reasonable range. I have position but Q2o doesn't play well even against a short stack's range, and I still have SB and BB behind. Easy fold.

Elon is very short (14.5bb) and opened UTG+1 to 3bb, a sizing that often correlates with a tighter/stronger range or an induce line where he’s ready to jam over action. With J5o out of position from the small blind, we realize equity poorly, face awkward SPR spots versus a stack that can easily shove most flops, and we also risk the big blind squeezing. There’s no need to take a thin/negative-EV defend here when we cover and can pick better isolation spots.

The player 'Elon' raised to 300 (3BB) from UTG+1. I am in the Big Blind with [4s Ts]. The pot odds require me to call 200 chips to win a total pot of 650, which is approximately 31% equity needed. My hand, Ten-Four suited, is quite weak, especially against an early position open range from a short-stacked opponent (Elon has ~14.5 BBs behind). While suited hands have some playability, the 3x sizing reduces the attractiveness of the call compared to a min-raise. Being out of position with a dominated hand against a likely strong range makes this a negative expectation call. The prudent play is to fold and preserve the stack.

Short stack at 19BB UTG in 5-handed play. K5s is suited but weak kicker, not in standard UTG raising/pushing range (typically K9s+ suited from UTG). Postflop playability poor OTB vs deep stacks. Opponents deep, likely to 3bet or call wide. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 48 hands left.
T4o is a very weak hand that shouldn't be opened from early position. Folding is the clear play here.

Both players behind are in the blinds and two players have already folded, so this is a strong late-position steal spot. 5h3h isn’t a premium hand, but it has decent playability (suited, can make wheel/flushes) and benefits greatly from position. I’ll open with a standard small raise to apply pressure to the SB’s shorter stack and to win the blinds often; if faced with a sizable 3-bet I’m comfortable folding.

I am in the Small Blind holding 4d 8s, a very weak off-suit unconnected hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Although the pot odds are tempting (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), my hand has poor equity (~32%) even against a wide button range, and playing out of position with such a weak holding will lead to difficult post-flop decisions and realized equity loss. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Six offsuit. FiveTwo has raised from the Button to 250, leaving me with 150 to call. While the price is somewhat attractive, J6o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an active button raiser. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, I prefer to fold and wait for better spots rather than defending with a marginal hand that often gets me into trouble post-flop.

9s8h offsuit from UTG is a weak hand to open. While it has some playability as a connector, it's not strong enough to raise from early position, especially out of position against 4 remaining players. Standard fold here.

5-handed with Claude folding, this is effectively the HJ/CO seat, so we can open wider than full ring. However Q2 suited is still far too dominated versus any reasonable continuing range (better queens, broadways, suited aces), and it plays poorly multiway—especially with a short BB (Elon) who can jam over limps/weak opens and put us in tough spots. The small EV of stealing 150 chips doesn’t justify opening this hand from here. Best is to muck and preserve stack for better open-raise candidates.

I am on the Button with [2h 8d], which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker. Although the action has folded to me, attempting to steal the blinds here is ill-advised for several reasons. First, the Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked (18 BBs) and may be looking for an opportunity to shove all-in, which would force me to fold instantly. Second, the Small Blind (Flash) has a massive chip lead and can easily call or 3-bet. With 57 BBs, I have a healthy stack and no need to take unnecessary risks with garbage cards. The expected value of opening here is negative.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven of hearts. All players have folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind (Elon) left to act. Elon is quite short-stacked with 1,800 chips (18 BB). Ten-Seven suited is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising to 300 chips (3x the big blind) exerts pressure on Elon and gives me the opportunity to win the pot immediately. If Elon shoves, I will have to evaluate the pot odds, but T7s is a standard opening hand here.

Preflop in the big blind with Ts6c, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and dominated by many raising ranges. Facing a standard raise to 300 from the small blind (Flash), who often plays aggressively. My stack is 1800 (18BB), but calling 200 into a 400 pot gives poor implied odds and postflop playability against Flash's likely wide range. No strong blockers or suited potential. Folding preserves stack for better spots in late tournament.

5-handed UTG plays much wider than full ring, and A2o has a useful blocker to the strongest holdings while still having reasonable equity vs the blinds’ defending ranges. With a very short stack in the SB (likely to fold or shove) and only one player left to act behind in position (Pro) plus the Button, raising applies immediate pressure and can win the blinds often. I’ll open small and be ready to fold versus a 3-bet from Pro/Flash or a tight BB; if the short SB jams, I’ll evaluate but generally can fold given domination risk and required equity.

I hold 8d 3c in UTG+1 position. This is an extremely weak hand (8-3 offsuit) with very poor playability and equity. The UTG player (FiveTwo) has opened with a raise to 250. Facing an early position raise with a trash hand, the only correct strategic move is to fold immediately to conserve chips. My stack of 57BB allows me to wait for much better opportunities.

I am holding 3h 2c on the button, which is essentially the worst starting hand in poker. FiveTwo has opened for 250 from UTG. While I have position, this hand has zero playability and no equity against a standard UTG opening range. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

Pocket Queens is a premium hand, top of any shoving range with 17.5BB effective stack facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has been aggressive in past hands. Position is SB, out of position postflop. Calling 200 leaves me short and committed anyway. Shoving 1750 maximizes fold equity against FiveTwo's UTG range, applies pressure to BB Claude, and gets value from worse hands like AK, lower pairs. Deep stacks behind but my short stack justifies jamming premium here late in tournament to build stack.

K2o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet. Elon has only 50 chips behind (essentially all-in for 1750). Even with decent pot odds (calling 1650 into ~2100), K2o is dominated too often against a 3-bet range. I have no blockers to premiums and will be in tough spots postflop. Easy fold.

We opened A2o as a light UTG steal/blocker hand, but the SB’s 3-bet to 1750 is very large relative to our 250 open, which heavily weights him toward strong Ax, broadways, and pairs—exactly the ranges that dominate A2o. Calling 1500 needs ~42% raw equity and, more importantly, the hand realizes poorly even with position because we’ll make a lot of second-best aces and weak pairs. A 4-bet bluff would also require an unrealistically high fold frequency given the sizing/commitment implied by his raise. Best EV and lowest variance line here is to cut losses and preserve our stack.

I hold 9h 6d in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak starting hand, disconnected and off-suit. Opening from UTG with such a weak range is strategically unsound as I have four opponents left to act who could easily wake up with better hands, and I would be playing out of position post-flop. With a stack of 57 BBs, I am in no danger and should simply fold this trash hand to wait for a better spot.

I am holding 9-5 offsuit in an early-middle position. This is a very weak starting hand with little potential for straights or flushes, and it is easily dominated by better hands. Given my position and the fact that there are four players behind me, including several with significant stacks and one short stack who might shove, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my chip lead.

Preflop on the button with 3s4h, a very weak offsuit hand with low cards and minimal playability. Pot is 150, blinds 50/100, to call 100 for a limp, but limping weak hands is exploitable and leads to difficult postflop spots. Raising or shoving 21BB with this hand has poor equity if called by the deep-stacked blinds who have shown aggression in history. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 45 hands remaining in tournament. Position is good, but hand quality doesn't warrant action.

Folded to me in the SB with Q7o. This is a spot where I should raise to try to take the blinds rather than completing and giving position to FiveTwo. Q7o is playable heads up and I have the chip lead at the table. Standard 2.5x raise to 250.

Claude is raising from the small blind after everyone folded, so his opening range is very wide. In the big blind we’re getting a good price (150 to win 350) and we’re deep enough to realize equity and outplay postflop. 8♣5♥ is not strong enough to 3-bet profitably at a high frequency (poor playability when called, no blockers), but it is good enough to defend as a call versus a small blind steal. We keep the pot manageable and see a flop in position disadvantage but with adequate pot odds.

I raised preflop with Q7o and completely whiffed this As 4s 9d flop. I have no pair, no draw, just Q-high. I'm out of position against FiveTwo who called my preflop raise. C-betting here with air into an ace-high board is risky - I'll get called by any ace, any 9, flush draws, and better queens. Check and give up is the best play with this weak holding.

Claude opened SB (very wide) and then checked this A-high flop, which weakens/caps his range and gives us a profitable stab frequency. We have near-zero showdown value with 8c5h, but we do have some backdoor potential (runner-runner straight, plus pairing outs). A small bet targets his broad whiffed range and some underpairs, and keeps our risk low; if we get check-raised we can comfortably fold.

I have Q-high with no draws on As 4s 9d. FiveTwo made a small 200 bet into 500. Calling with Q-high out of position is a losing play - I have no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw. Even if I hit a Q on the turn, I could still be behind. Easy fold.

I am UTG with Queen-Ten offsuit. In a 5-handed game, this hand is on the border of an opening range, but it remains marginal from the earliest position. Considering Elon is sitting on a very short stack of approximately 21.5 big blinds directly to my left, he is in a prime position to shove over an open with a wide range of hands that dominate QTo. Additionally, with Pro (56 BB) also relatively short in the Big Blind, the risk of facing a three-bet or shove is high. I prefer to fold here and maintain my stack for higher-equity opportunities rather than committing chips with a hand that plays poorly against a jamming range.

UTG+1 position with 2h3c, a very weak offsuit low hand with poor playability. Multiple strong opponents behind with deep stacks. My stack is 2150 (~21BB), so folding trash preserves chips for better spots. No reason to limp or raise this hand preflop in early position.
62o is one of the worst hands in poker. Even on the button with only the blinds left to act, this hand has no playability - no high card value, no connectivity, no suited potential. Easy fold.

Folded to me in the small blind, it’s effectively heads-up versus Pro in the big blind. In this spot we should open very wide to leverage positional disadvantage but strong fold equity; Pro has shown a tendency to play straightforwardly and fold to aggression. J5o is plenty strong enough as part of a standard SB opening range, and raising also denies BB a free look with lots of hands that realize equity well. I’ll use a small but standard sizing to risk little while still applying pressure.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, the Small Blind (FiveTwo) raised to 2.5BB. I am in the Big Blind with Q8o, a hand that has reasonable equity against a wide SB opening range. I am getting favorable pot odds (need to call 150 to win a pot that will be 500, requiring 30% equity) and will have position post-flop. Given FiveTwo's frequent raises from the SB, folding here would be too exploitable. I choose to defend by calling.

We’re out of position as the SB raiser on a very BB-favorable, connected low flop (7-6-4) where Pro’s defend range contains lots of pairs/two-pair/straight draws. With Js5c we have an open-ended straight draw (3 or 8), so our hand realizes equity well and benefits from keeping the pot manageable. Checking also protects our range (we’ll have strong hands that check here) and can induce a stab from Pro that we can comfortably call or sometimes punish later depending on sizing/runout. Taking the free card when offered is valuable versus a straightforward opponent.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh8h (Queen-Eight offsuit, though the log says 8h Qc, which is unsuited). The flop is 6c 4d 7s, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needs a 5) and two overcards. The Small Blind (FiveTwo), who was the pre-flop aggressor, has checked to me. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, this texture hits my defending range reasonably well, but my specific hand has equity that I want to realize cheaply. Betting doesn't get much value from worse hands and might fold out hands I am currently ahead of or flipping with (like K-high or J-high), while isolating myself against better hands like pairs. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card to potentially improve to a straight or top pair, keeping the pot small with a marginal hand.

Turn pairs the 4 after a flop that checked through, which heavily caps Pro’s range toward a lot of overcards, weak one-pair hands, and some slow-played pairs. With Js5c we still have an open-ended straight draw (3 or 8), so we’re happy to apply pressure while having solid equity when called. A delayed c-bet here should fold out a meaningful chunk of Pro’s air, denies a free river to hands like A-high/K-high, and keeps our risk controlled versus a shorter stack. If called, we can realize equity and potentially barrel/select rivers well.

In a Blind vs Blind pot, I defended my Big Blind with Q8o against FiveTwo's raise. The flop came 6c 4d 7s, which we both checked. The turn is the 4h, pairing the board. FiveTwo now leads out for 300 (60% pot). Although I picked up a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) and have two overcards, my equity is somewhat marginal. To call, I need about 27% equity. Against a range of made hands (like 6x, 7x, or trips with a 4) that would value bet here, my clean outs are likely just the four 5s (giving ~9% equity). My overcard outs are dirty and might not be enough if I hit. Given the board pairing and the aggressor showing strength after a flop check, it is disciplined to fold this marginal hand and preserve chips.

Q3o is a trash hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. My stack is only 21.5BB, so I need to be selective with shoving or opening ranges. Standard UTG shove range at this depth excludes Q3o entirely (typically 22+, AJo+, KQs+). Limping is poor strategy as it invites multiway pots with a weak hand. Folding preserves my stack for premium spots where I can apply pressure or get value.

J5o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability. From UTG+1 (early position), this is a clear fold. I have no reason to open with such a marginal holding when there are still 4 players to act behind me.

Button vs blinds after two folds is a prime steal spot. Kh7h is comfortably within a BTN opening range: it has decent high-card value, good playability with the suitedness, and realizes equity well in position. I’ll use a small standard open to apply pressure to the SB’s shorter stack and keep risk controlled versus the deep BB who can 3-bet.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 2h, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Facing a raise to 250 from the Button (FiveTwo), I am out of position and my hand has very poor equity even against a wide stealing range. The pot odds of calling 200 to win 400 do not justify calling with this hand, especially given the positional disadvantage and the risk of the Big Blind squeezing. Folding is the only correct play to preserve my stack of 54 BBs for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo opened from the Button to 2.5x. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.6:1 to call (150 to win 400), T2o plays extremely poorly post-flop and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. With a healthy stack of 19,000 chips and 43 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a low-equity hand against a Button open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

K8o in UTG is a marginal hand that's typically too weak to open from early position, even 5-handed. While K-high has some value, the 8 kicker is poor and I'll often be dominated by better kings or face difficult post-flop decisions. Standard fold from UTG.

Unopened pot in UTG+1 with 5♠2♦ offsuit (very low equity, poor playability, and dominated when we make a pair). Even though we cover the BB short stack, opening this hand creates too many tough postflop spots out of position versus the blinds and risks spewing chips for a small steal. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better openers.

I hold Ace-Queen suited (AcQc) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. Action has folded to me. My stack is 5400 (54 BB). The Big Blind (Elon) is short-stacked with roughly 20 BB. My strategy is to open-raise for value. This puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to play a pot in position with a strong hand. A standard raise size of 2.5x (250 chips) is appropriate here, consistent with my previous play (e.g., Hand #55 with AQo). If the short-stacked BB shoves, I have a strong enough hand to call.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. Pro has raised from the Button to 250 chips. While Button raises are often wide, 7-4 offsuit does not play well post-flop, and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Additionally, Elon in the Big Blind is short-stacked (20.5 BBs) and might shove. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand.

Facing a button raise to 250 after two early folds, Pro's range is wide (likely 35-50% of hands) as it's a steal attempt from late position. My J9o is offsuit but has straight potential, decent high-card value, and good playability. In BB with ~20BB effective stack, pot odds are excellent (400 pot to call 150, ~2.7:1), and I get to realize equity OOP against a wide range (~38-42% equity vs typical button open). Calling is standard BB defense here to defend wide and apply pressure postflop while preserving fold equity if I hit. Folding is too tight; 3-betting or shoving oversized with marginal hand risks stack off wide. Plan to c-bet flops that hit or check-fold weak ones.

Flop is Ad 9c 5s rainbow, an ace-high board with coordinated low cards. I flop middle pair with J kicker (9s Jc), which is a strong hand against Pro's wide button opening range (Ax wide, broadways, suited connectors, bluffs). Equity is excellent (~45-55% vs typical c-bet range including Ax, draws, air). OOP with ~19BB effective stack and pot 550, checking is best to pot control, induce wide c-bets from continuation bets/bluffs (Pro likely cbets 60-80% here), and set up check-raise all-in on most bet sizes for max value from worse pairs/draws while folding out pure air. Leading/donking weak with medium strength and risks folding out better bluffs or getting raised off by Ax. Check-fold too tight given showdown value and equity; check-call risks turn commitment without initiative. Post-check, if Pro checks back (weak), great; if small bet, raise shove; larger bet, still shove given SPR ~3.5:1.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcQc) on an Ace-high dry board (A-9-5). This is a strong value hand. My opponent checked to me. Given the board texture, I want to extract value from weaker Aces (A2-AJ) and second pairs (9x) that might call a small bet. The opponent is short-stacked (SPR ~3.5), so I want to start building the pot to potentially get stacks in by the river. A small continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot is standard on this texture to keep the opponent's calling range wide while extracting value.

Flop check-raise plan executes perfectly here. With middle pair good kicker (9sJc on Ad9c5s), I have strong showdown value and equity (~50%+ vs Pro's wide button c-bet range: Ax, broadways, suited connectors, bluffs). Pro's small 200 bet into 550 (~33% pot) is standard continuation but weak, representing wide range including air/draws we crush. OOP with 19BB effective and SPR ~3.5 pre-action, check-raising all-in (1900 total) maximizes EV: extracts value from worse (weaker Ax/pairs/draws), applies max pressure to fold out ~40-50% of c-bets (overpairs might fold to sizing, pure air always), denies equity realization. Calling 200 commits us anyway (SPR <2 post-call), risks turn OOP vulnerability; folding absurd with this strength. Late tourney (hand 108/150), 5 players, building stack requires aggression vs loose button. Pro's history shows calling stations but folds to pressure (e.g., hands 37,38,39). Shove polarizes range, unexploitable.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AcQc) on an Ace-high dry board (A-9-5). I bet 200 and my opponent, who started the hand with roughly 20BB, has check-raised all-in to 1900. Given the shallow stack depth (SPR ~3.5 starting), the opponent's shoving range in a Button vs Big Blind scenario is wide. While he can have sets (55, 99) or two pair (A9, A5), he is also likely to shove with any Ace (A2-AJ) to protect his equity or value bet against what he perceives as a light c-bet. I dominate all single-pair Aces except AK. There are far more combinations of A2-AJ than there are of sets and two-pair hands. The pot odds require me to have roughly 39% equity, and against a range of {Any Ax, Sets, Two Pair}, I have significantly more than that. Folding TPTK here would be too tight given the stack dynamics.

In this 4-handed configuration, the UTG position is effectively the Cutoff. A7s (Ace-Seven suited) is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the Cutoff, as it has good playability post-flop with flush potential and top pair value. My stack of 76 BB is deep enough to play standard poker. I will raise to 250 chips (2.5 BB), consistent with my previous sizing strategies, to put pressure on the blinds and the button.

I am on the button with 5c 4s. Pro has raised to 250 from under-the-gun. While I have position, my hand is extremely weak and does not justify a call or a 3-bet bluff against an opening range. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

5d8d in the SB facing an UTG raise of 250. This is a marginal suited one-gapper that doesn't play well out of position against an UTG opening range which is typically strong. I'd need to call 200 into a 400 pot, but being OOP for the rest of the hand makes this unprofitable. Fold is the disciplined play.

We’re heads-up in the big blind versus a 2.5x UTG open with the button and SB folding. Getting 150 to win 400 (needs ~27% equity) and 7♦4♦ has reasonable playability (suited, can make strong disguised flushes/two-pair/straights). Against an UTG range I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal 3-bet bluff, but folding would be too tight given the price and our stack depth. Defend by calling and play postflop cautiously on dominated one-pair runouts.

Board is 9c-8s-Jc and we hold 7d4d: no made hand, only a weak gutshot to the T (4 outs) plus a backdoor diamond draw. Versus an UTG raiser this texture smashes their range (overpairs, AJ/KJ, QT, big clubs), so donk-leading is low EV and building a pot OOP with minimal equity is bad. Best is to check our range, keep the pot small, and then respond to Pro’s c-bet sizing (mostly fold vs normal/large bets; potentially continue only vs very small sizing with the gutshot/backdoor potential).

On a wet board of 9c 8s Jc, the texture favors the Big Blind's calling range (connecting with straights, two pairs, and pairs). Although I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), my hand has showdown value and equity that I wish to realize cheaply. Betting risks getting check-raised or called by a range that has me beat or has strong equity, forcing me to fold or play a bloated pot out of position effectively (since he checked to me). Checking back controls the pot size and allows me to see a turn card to potentially improve to a straight or top pair.

Pro checked back a very dynamic flop (9c-8s-Jc), which caps his range a bit toward medium pairs (TT-QQ), some A-highs, and occasional slowplays, but also a lot of give-ups like KQ/QT without clubs. The turn Ace is a strong scare card that hits my BB defending range enough (some Ax that checked flop) and puts significant pressure on his flop-check range. With only a gutshot (T) and no real showdown value, the best way to realize EV is to stab small-to-mid and take it down often; if called, we can mostly shut down unless we improve or get a very favorable river. If raised, we simply fold.

The turn brings an Ace, giving me top pair. The opponent leads for a small sizing (250 into 550), approximately 45% pot. This line often indicates a marginal made hand (like a Jack, a nine, or a weaker Ace) or a draw trying to set a price. With top pair, I have sufficient equity to continue. Raising would likely isolate me against better hands (two pair, straights, or better kickers), whereas calling keeps the opponent's range wide, including bluffs and worse value hands. The price is good to call and evaluate the river.

River completes our hand: with 7♦4♦ on 9c-8s-Jc-As-Tc we have a made straight (7-8-9-T-J). This is a strong value hand, but the board is extremely coordinated, so Pro can show up with the higher straight (KQ) and sometimes a club flush (two clubs). After his flop check and turn call, he still has plenty of Ax/two-pair/set type hands that will often check back if we check due to the scary river, so we should lead for value.

I’ll use a small value bet to (1) get crying calls from Ax and some made hands that don’t want to face a big bet, and (2) keep the pot manageable since we are not nutted. If Pro raises big (especially a shove), his range is heavily weighted to KQ/flush, so the plan is to bet/fold to significant aggression.

On the river, the board is [9c 8s Jc As Tc]. I hold the 7h, giving me a straight (7-8-9-T-J). However, this is the low end of the straight; any Queen makes a higher straight (8-9-T-J-Q), and there are three clubs on the board making a flush possible. The opponent's bet of 350 into a pot of 1050 is small (1/3 pot), offering me excellent pot odds (4:1). Raising would be a mistake as I would only likely get called by hands that beat me (Queens or Flushes) or split with another 7. Folding is too tight given the price and the fact that I have a straight; the opponent could be blocking with two pair or value betting the same straight. I will call to realize my showdown value.

I am holding 5-4 offsuit in UTG (under the gun) position. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given that I have a healthy stack and three players are still to act behind me, including aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Claude, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. I will fold to preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation.
QhTh on the button is a solid hand - suited broadway cards with straight and flush potential. With Flash folding UTG, I have position and should open raise here. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Claude is opening on the button into the blinds, so his range is likely wide. From the SB, flat-calling A3o plays poorly OOP and invites the BB to come along, reducing our equity realization. A3o is a good light 3-bet candidate because it blocks strong Ax (reducing the chance Claude can continue with AA/AK/AQ), and we can apply pressure preflop with initiative. Pro in the BB appears relatively tight, so we often isolate heads-up. Plan: take it down preflop frequently; if called, play an initiative-driven 3-bet pot and comfortably fold to a 4-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3c, a very weak hand. The action involves a button raise from Claude and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo) to 950. My hand has no equity or playability against these ranges, and the price to call is far too high relative to my hand strength. This is a clear fold.

QhTh is a suited broadway hand with good playability. FiveTwo 3-bet to 950 from SB. I'm getting ~1.85:1 to call (700 into 1300). With position and a hand that can make strong draws (flush draws, straight draws), calling is reasonable. I have position for the rest of the hand which adds significant value. Folding gives up too much equity with this hand, and 4-betting would be too aggressive here.

In a 3-bet pot OOP, this Q♦J♥T♦ flop is extremely dynamic and connects hard with Claude’s button-call range (broadways, suited connectors, pairs), so our range advantage is reduced and we don’t want to auto-c-bet our weakest A-high. With A♥3♦ we have a gutshot to K for Broadway plus a backdoor diamond possibility, but not enough to happily build a pot or face a check-raise. Checking keeps the pot controlled, protects our checking range (we can have strong hands here too), and lets us respond based on his sizing/frequency (including bluff-catching some stabs).

I flopped top two pair (Queens and Tens) on a very coordinated Qd-Jh-Td board. This is an excellent result, but the board is dangerous with many straight draws possible (AK, K9, 98 all make straights). FiveTwo checked to me after 3-betting preflop, which is interesting - they may have missed or are slowplaying. I need to bet for value and protection against draws. Betting around 65% pot (~1300) is appropriate to charge draws while building the pot with my strong made hand.

Claude’s 3-bet calling range connects extremely well with Q♦J♥T♦ (lots of Kx/2p/sets + strong pair+draws), and his 1300 bet into 3300 is a big sizing that puts immediate pressure on my weakest A-high holdings. With A♥3♦ I only have a gutshot to the K (4 outs) plus a very thin backdoor diamond possibility, so my raw equity and, more importantly, my equity realization OOP are far below the ~28% needed to continue. Raising would be a low-quality bluff here (I don’t block Kx, and he can continue very often). Best play is to cut losses and fold.

6h8h from UTG is a marginal suited connector. While it has some playability, UTG is the worst position to open with this hand. I should be opening a tighter range here. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Claude has folded and we’re on the button with A9o, a strong enough hand to open in position. Raising leverages position, applies pressure to the SB/BB who must defend wider, and A-high provides good blocker value versus their strongest continues (Ax). With stacks deep and a shorter SB, a standard small open keeps our risk low while still generating fold equity and allowing us to c-bet many flops.

I am holding 32o, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a standard 2.5BB raise from the Button, I am out of position in the Small Blind. Even though the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, the playability of this hand is virtually non-existent, and playing it out of position against an aggressive stack is a clear losing proposition. Folding is the only correct strategic option here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 3c. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250, and I need to call 150 more. While the pot odds are tempting, 73 suited is a very weak hand to play out of position against an active Button. FiveTwo has been opening frequently, but defending with this specific hand often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am easily dominated or forced to fold. Given my healthy stack size, there is no need to enter a marginal pot with a low-equity hand.

97o is too loose from UTG even 4-handed: it’s offsuit, frequently dominated by stronger 9x/7x that continue, and it plays poorly out of position versus two aggressive blinds who can isolate/3-bet. With no dead money beyond the blinds and plenty of stack depth, I’d rather pass and keep my opening range cleaner from this seat.

The current game state is 4-handed with blinds at 50/100. I am on the Button with A2o and a stack of roughly 75BB. While having an Ace on the button is often a raising opportunity to steal the blinds, A2o is the weakest offsuit Ace and plays poorly post-flop, especially against the two large stacks in the blinds who can apply significant pressure or dominate my kicker. Given the deep stacks and the potential for being 3-bet or floating out of position, I choose a disciplined fold to preserve chips for better spots with higher playability.

I am in the Small Blind with J6o. The action has folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind (Claude) to act. J6o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against an opponent with a deep stack like Claude who has shown aggression throughout the session. Completing the bet or raising as a steal is generally not profitable with this specific hand in this configuration. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with King-Seven offsuit. My stack is 7475 (approx 75 BB). K7o is a weak hand to open from first position, even short-handed. It is dominated by many hands that would call or raise (better Kings, Aces, pocket pairs). Playing this hand out of position if called is difficult and likely -EV. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.

I am on the Button with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o) in a four-handed game. While the Button is an ideal position for stealing blinds, T5o is too weak to be an open-raising candidate even in a short-handed situation. Both Claude (SB) and FiveTwo (BB) have shown willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary risk with a bottom-tier hand.

K9o in the SB with only the BB left to act. This is a standard spot to raise and try to take down the pot or play heads up with initiative. K9o is well ahead of a random BB defending range. Raising to 250-300 is standard.

Blind-vs-blind spot with a smallish open and we’re closing the action. We’re getting ~2:1 immediate pot odds (200 to win 400), so we can defend quite wide even with a weak offsuit queen. I don’t want to 3-bet Q2o here because it has poor blockers, plays badly when called, and puts us in tougher high-variance spots OOP. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets us realize equity on favorable textures (pair a Q, some backdoor possibilities) while still having room to apply pressure postflop vs Claude’s likely wide SB opening range.

I completely whiffed this flop with K9o - no pair, no draw. Board is Tc 7h 2h with a potential flush draw. Against a BB defender who called my raise, a c-bet with pure air is marginal. If I bet and get called or raised, I'm in a terrible spot with just K-high. Checking to control pot size and potentially seeing a free card makes sense here.

Claude’s SB open is wide and his flop check in a blind-vs-blind pot is often range-capped/contains a lot of air and overcards. With Q2 we have bottom pair and decent immediate showdown value, but it’s fragile on Tc-7h-2h: lots of turn cards (A/K/Q/J, hearts, 8/9) can put us in tough spots and allow his overcards to realize for free. A small-to-mid c-bet here targets his unpaired hands, denies equity, and can win the pot outright. If we face a check-raise, this hand isn’t strong enough to continue (we don’t have the heart blocker or strong draw), so we can comfortably fold.

FiveTwo made a small bet of 250 into 600. I have K-high with two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (need an 8). Getting 3.4:1 pot odds to call. My K or 9 could be good if they hit, and an 8 gives me the nuts. The small sizing often indicates weakness - could be a probe bet. Folding K-high with a gutshot is too tight given these odds.

Turn is a brick 3s. I have K-high with no pair and no real draws (I was wrong about the gutshot earlier - K9 on T-7-2-3 has no straight draw). FiveTwo bet small on flop and I called. Now it's checked to me. Betting here only gets called by better hands. Checking to control pot size and potentially see a cheap showdown or pick up a river bluff opportunity makes sense.

Turn is a low brick (3s) that doesn’t materially improve our hand (Qc2d = weak bottom pair) but also doesn’t drastically change the texture. After Claude’s flop check-call, his range contains a lot of hands that either (a) won’t fold to a small turn bet (Tx/7x/overpairs, heart draws, A2, some pocket pairs), or (b) are unpaired overcards that we already beat and can allow to bluff later. Betting here risks getting called mostly by better hands and draws while folding out the hands we dominate, which is not ideal for thin value/protection with no heart blocker. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our showdown value, and lets us bluff-catch many rivers if Claude takes a stab with missed overcards.

River Ace is a great bluff card. I raised preflop so I credibly rep Ax hands. FiveTwo's line (small flop bet, check turn) shows weakness - likely a weak pair or missed draw. My K-high loses at showdown to almost everything that calls. Betting 700 into 1100 to fold out weak Tx, 7x, and pocket pairs below T.

We’re facing a 700 lead into 1800 on a river A after BvB check-check turn. Our hand is just a pair of 2s, so we’re purely bluff-catching, but the price is good: 700 to win 2500 (~28% needed). Claude’s line (check flop, call our small stab, check turn) keeps a lot of missed heart draws and overcard floats in range, and the river Ace is a very natural card for him to take a stab with to fold out exactly our kind of weak showdown hand. We also block some of his thin value (A2/other 2x) by holding a deuce. Against his value (Ax/Tx/7x/3x) we’re dead, but he doesn’t need to be bluffing that often for a call to show profit, and this sizing looks like it can include plenty of bluffs/blocker bets. So we take the cheap bluff-catch.

I am dealt Jack-Trey offsuit in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position in a 4-handed game. This hand is very weak and does not meet the requirements for a standard opening range even at a short-handed table. With a healthy stack of 18,800 chips, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand from early position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have 2s6d on the button - this is one of the weakest possible hands (offsuit, not connected, no high cards). Even with the positional advantage of being on the button, 62o is a clear fold. It's not in any reasonable opening range and would be -EV to play.

4-handed and folded to us in the small blind, this is a prime steal spot. Kh9h has good high-card strength, plays well postflop with flush/straight potential, and benefits a lot from taking initiative. Pro is shorter in the big blind and will defend some, but a normal SB open size applies pressure and keeps our range consistent while still allowing us to fold to a shove if needed depending on sizing.

I’ll raise to a standard 3x to attack the dead money and play the hand in position relative to the BB postflop (initiative).

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise from the Small Blind (FiveTwo) to 300. My hand is Ace-Five offsuit (A5o). In a Blind vs Blind scenario, ranges are typically wide, and A5o is well ahead of a random hand and certainly strong enough to defend. I have the advantage of position post-flop. While a 3-bet is a strategic option to apply pressure, calling allows me to realize my equity in position and keep the pot manageable against an aggressive opponent. I need to call 200 chips, getting decent odds to see a flop.

Flop comes very dynamic (8c-Td-Jh) and we’ve already made a K-high straight with Kh9h (8-9-T-J-K). BB’s defend range hits this board a lot (Tx/Jx, pair+draws, two-pair/sets, and even smaller made straights like Q9/97), so we should lead for value and start building a pot while also charging his many drawing/one-pair continuations.

I’ll use a ~2/3 pot c-bet size to get called by worse made hands and strong draws and to set up turn/river sizing toward stacks if he continues.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit against the Small Blind. The flop comes 8-10-Jack, which is a very wet, connected board that connects well with the Small Blind's opening range. I have missed the board completely, holding only Ace-high with no straight or flush draws. My opponent bets 400 into a pot of 600 (2/3 pot), signaling strength or a strong semi-bluff. Despite having position, my hand has very poor playability and low equity against a range that likely includes pairs and straight draws. There is no strategic merit in floating here with A-high and no draws. I fold.
T6o is a weak hand from UTG position. Even in a 4-handed game, this hand is not strong enough to open - it's easily dominated and plays poorly postflop. Standard fold.

4-handed and folded to us on the button, we should be attacking blinds very wide. J8o is comfortably within a button opening range: it has decent high-card value, can make top pair with reasonable kickers, and has some straight potential. With Pro in the SB shorter and Flash in the BB deeper, I prefer a small-but-real open size that pressures folds without bloating the pot versus a defend/3-bet. Raising also outperforms limping here because we realize position and can win pre-flop often.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 7h facing a raise to 250 (2.5BB) from the Button (FiveTwo). 78 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position. Although I have some connectivity, the lack of suitedness significantly reduces its playability. Calling would leave me out of position against the aggressor and potentially squeezed by the Big Blind. My equity against a standard button opening range is decent (~36%), but realizing that equity out of position is difficult. Folding is the standard and prudent play here to preserve my stack of roughly 71 big blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 6s. FiveTwo, on the button, has raised to 250. This is a standard opening size. Q6s is a solid defending hand against a button opening range, especially given the pot odds of nearly 3.7 to 1 (I need to call 150 to win a pot that will be 550). I have a healthy stack and can afford to see a flop with a suited Queen, which plays well in many post-flop scenarios.

I am first to act on a paired board of 3-2-3. Pre-flop, I called a standard raise from the Button. My current hand is Queen-high with no direct draw, though I do have two overcards. On a board like this, leading out (donking) is rarely the optimal strategy as it doesn't represent many strong hands in my range. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop aggressor reacts. If they check back, my Queen-high might still be the best hand or I can reassess on the turn. If they bet, I will likely fold unless the sizing is extremely small, as Queen-high with a 6 kicker has very little equity against a standard continuation betting range on this texture.

Heads-up in position as the preflop raiser on a paired, low flop (3d-2h-3h), I keep a high c-bet frequency with a small sizing. This board favors my range (overpairs, A-highs, trips possible) and puts a lot of BB’s unpaired overcards in a tough spot. J8o has two overcards and can improve, but doesn’t want to give a free card to hands like random A-high/K-high or heart backdoors. A small bet targets immediate folds while keeping the pot controlled if called; if check-raised, I can comfortably give it up with this hand.

FiveTwo has C-bet a small amount (about 36% of the pot) on a paired board of 3-2-3. While my Queen-high might occasionally be ahead of his pure air bluffs, I have no direct draws and very little equity to improve. Calling out of position with a weak Queen-high and no backdoor flush draw is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

4-handed UTG is effectively a late position opening spot, so we can play a much wider range than full ring. K8o is a reasonable steal/open, especially with good blockers to strong Kx and decent playability versus blinds. I’ll open rather than limp to apply pressure to SB/BB and define ranges; if we face a strong 3-bet from the shorter BTN (or a tight line from blinds) we can comfortably fold.

I am on the Button with Pocket Tens (Tc Td), a strong starting hand. FiveTwo has opened from UTG to 250. With 4 players left, UTG is effectively the Cutoff, so their range is reasonably wide, but still generally contains decent hands. My stack is 7125 (approx 71 BBs), which allows for post-flop play. A 3-bet here is a valid option to isolate and take initiative, but it risks isolating myself against a range of hands that beats me (JJ+, AK) or flipping. Calling is an excellent option because I have position, it keeps FiveTwo's range wide (including hands I dominate like lower pairs and dominated broadways), and it conceals the strength of my hand. I can navigate post-flop well in position. Therefore, I will flat call the 250.

I am in the Small Blind with 8 of Clubs and 4 of Clubs. FiveTwo raised from UTG to 250 and Pro called on the Button. Although I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 650), 8-high suited is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position against two opponents, one of whom (FiveTwo) showed strength by raising from the first position. Additionally, Claude is still to act in the Big Blind and could potentially squeeze. Given my stack size and the current stage of the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand in a multi-way pot.

AKo is a premium hand in the BB. Facing a UTG open and button flat, I should 3-bet for value. This hand plays best heads-up with initiative. Raising to ~1000 to isolate and build the pot with my strong holding.

K8o is fine as a 4-handed open, but once BB squeezes to 1000 over our open and BTN flat, our hand performs very poorly: we’re often dominated by better Kx, we’ll be out of position to the 3-bettor, and Pro can continue creating a multiway 3-bet pot where our realization is terrible. Even though the direct price (750 to win 1550) looks tempting, we won’t realize anywhere near the equity needed. K8o is also not a great 4-bet bluff candidate here (weak blockers and awkward vs Pro’s short stack). Best play is to cut losses and fold.

Pre-flop, facing a squeeze to 1000 from Claude in the Big Blind after I flat-called on the Button. The original raiser (FiveTwo) has folded. The pot is currently 1550, and it costs me 750 to call, giving me pot odds of roughly 32.6%. My hand, Pocket Tens, is strong and plays well against a typical squeeze range, which includes broadway cards, some suited connectors, and pairs that I dominate or flip with, in addition to premium hands. Shoving (4-betting) would likely isolate me against a range that crushes me (JJ+, QQ+, KK, AA) or coin flips (AK), while folding is too tight given the price and hand strength. Calling allows me to use my position post-flop with an SPR of roughly 2.7, where I can evaluate based on the board texture.

I have AKo on a 2h9dJh flop - missed but have two overcards and backdoor hearts. I 3-bet preflop so I have the initiative. Pro flatted my 3-bet which could indicate medium strength hands. A continuation bet here makes sense to fold out underpairs and maintain aggression. Betting around 1/3 pot (750-800) is efficient - small enough to not commit too much with air but enough to apply pressure.

Pre-flop, I called a squeeze raise with Pocket Tens, which is a strong hand that plays well in position. The flop comes J-9-2 with a heart flush draw. Claude bets 800 into a pot of 2300, which is a relatively small sizing (approx. 35% pot). While the Jack hits his squeeze range (AJ, JJ+), the small bet size suggests he could be c-betting a wide range including missed overcards (AK, AQ) or lower pairs. My Tens are ahead of his bluffs and have about 20% equity against a strong value range, which matches the direct pot odds offered. Folding for this price with an underpair in a squeezed pot is too tight, and raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me. Calling allows me to use my position to evaluate the turn, keeping his bluffing range in the hand.

The Kd on the turn is excellent - I now have top pair top kicker. Pro called my 3-bet preflop and called my flop c-bet, likely holding hands like Jx, flush draws, or pocket pairs. I should bet for value to extract from worse kings, jacks, flush draws, and protect against the heart draw. Pro has 5325 behind with 3900 in the pot. Betting around 2/3 pot (~2500) for value while leaving room for a river shove.

The turn card is the King of Diamonds, making the board 2h 9d Jh Kd. This is a very bad card for my specific hand (Pocket Tens). The King hits the opponent's pre-flop squeeze range heavily (AK, KQ, KK, KJs) and also completes potential straights for QT (though I block that) and gives sets to KK/JJ/99. My hand has been downgraded to a weak underpair relative to the top two board cards. Claude's bet sizing increased from a small flop bet (35% pot) to a large turn bet (~39% pot if we consider the pot before bet, but actually 2500 into 3900 is ~64% pot). This polarizing line on a scare card represents a strong value hand. I am drawing very thin against his value range (JJ+, Kx, Jx) and likely only beating pure bluffs, which are less likely to fire this size on this card. Calling would cost nearly half my remaining stack. Folding is the prudent long-term play to preserve chips.

I am in the UTG position with 63o (Three of Spades, Six of Hearts). This is a statistically very weak hand with no connectivity or flush potential. Given my position, being first to act with three players left behind me, and my status as the shortest stack (though still 53 BB deep), there is no justification for entering the pot. The correct play is to fold immediately and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit (KTo). This is a strong starting hand in a four-handed game, particularly when the action has folded to me. I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately or play the hand in position against the small or big blind.
2c3d is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Even with decent pot odds, this hand plays terribly postflop and I'm out of position against the button raiser. Clear fold.

Button open from Flash is likely fairly wide given the short-handed dynamic and his prior aggression. KQo (with suited diamonds) plays well as a value 3-bet versus that range, and I prefer taking initiative rather than defending passively out of position. A 3-bet also pressures his weaker offsuit broadways and suited connectors while building a pot when we’re ahead of his opens. Sizing to 1000 (a bit over 3x) is standard OOP and keeps his continuing range defined without overcommitting.

I opened with KTo from the button, which is a standard play in a 4-handed game. FiveTwo responded with a 3-bet from the big blind to 1,000. While KTo is decent in a short-handed environment, it performs poorly against a typical 3-bet range, often being dominated by hands like KQ, AJ, or AT, and it doesn't have the same post-flop playability as suited connectors or pocket pairs. Although I have position, calling 700 more into a 1,350 pot (requiring about 33% equity) is a marginal decision. Given that I have a healthy stack and we are nearing the final stage of the tournament (hand 117 of 150), I prefer to avoid high-variance situations with dominated hands. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous spot.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with 9c 3c. This is an extremely weak hand to open from first position, even with the suited value. With a healthy stack of 182 big blinds and 32 hands remaining, there is no reason to take a high-variance, negative expected value risk with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with T4o. Flash folded, leaving just the blinds. This is a standard button steal opportunity. T4 is weak but playable from the button position. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to try to take the blinds.

AJo is strong enough to punish a button open, especially from the small blind where flat-calling creates a tough OOP/multiway spot and gives the BB great odds to come along. Claude has shown a willingness to open and fold to pressure in prior hands, so a 3-bet picks up the pot often and plays well when called (good blockers to AK/AQ/AJ, can make top pair/strong draws). I’ll size to a standard SB vs BTN 3-bet that denies the BB and sets up clear decisions (generally fold to a 4-bet).

I am in the Big Blind with K7o. The action involves a Button open from Claude and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (FiveTwo) to 900. K7o is a weak off-suit hand that performs poorly against a 3-betting range, often being dominated by better Kings or Aces. Additionally, the original raiser (Claude) is still left to act behind me, creating the risk of a 4-bet or a multi-way pot where I would be out of position with a marginal hand. The cost to call (800 chips) is significant relative to my stack of 5225. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips.

I opened T4o from the button as a steal attempt, but FiveTwo 3-bet to 900. T4o is far too weak to continue against a 3-bet - I have no real equity and poor playability. Folding and losing the 250 is the clear play here.
8s4s from UTG is a weak hand. Even suited, it's not playable from early position against 3 opponents. Standard fold.

Claude has folded and we’re on the button vs blinds. Even with a weak hand like 6-2o, this is a prime spot to apply pressure and steal the dead blinds, especially with no antes and position guaranteed if called. I’ll use the minimum raise size to keep risk low while still leveraging fold equity; if either blind 3-bets, we can comfortably fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J5o, a very weak hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has min-raised to 200. Although the price to call is cheap (150 to win 350), playing J5o out of position is difficult and likely -EV due to poor playability and realizable equity. With the Big Blind still to act and a healthy stack of roughly 50bb, there is no need to defend with such a weak holding. Folding is the disciplined and correct choice.

I am in the Big Blind and facing a min-raise from FiveTwo on the button. The raise is to 200 total, and since I already have 100 in the pot as the Big Blind, I only need to call 100 more. This gives me pot odds of 3.5 to 1 (I need to call 100 to win a total pot of 450). While Eight-Deuce suited is a very weak hand, the price is mathematically favorable for a defense in the Big Blind against a wide button opening range. I have the stack depth to see a flop and can easily fold if I do not improve or pick up a strong draw, minimizing potential losses while defending my blind effectively.

On the flop of As-3s-7c, I have missed completely with my 8d2d. The current pot is 450, and my opponent, FiveTwo, who raised pre-flop, has not yet acted. As the Big Blind, I am first to act. Given the board contains an Ace and a flush draw, it heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. However, since the current action is 'check/call 0', it implies both players have currently checked or I am checking to the raiser. I will check here to see what the button does, as I have zero equity and no reason to bet.

Heads-up in position after we open the button and BB defends. This A-high flop is a strong range advantage for the preflop raiser, and Flash’s check caps him somewhat (lots of whiffs and medium pairs). Even though 6♦2♥ has essentially no equity/no useful backdoors, a small c-bet only needs a modest fold frequency to show profit (risking 150 to win 450). If we get check-raised we can trivially fold; if called we’ll mostly shut down and re-evaluate turns that are very favorable to our perceived range.

I have completely missed this flop with 8d2d. The board is Ace-high and contains a flush draw, which heavily favors FiveTwo's pre-flop raising range from the button. I have no pair, no draw, and no realistic way to win the hand without a massive bluff, which is ill-advised out of position against an aggressive opponent. Even though the bet of 150 is small (giving me 4:1 pot odds), calling with 8-high and no potential for improvement is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

4-handed from UTG (effectively CO), A6s is a standard open: it has decent high-card equity, plays well postflop with nut-flush potential, and benefits from fold equity vs blinds. Limping invites multiway and caps our range; raising also lets us represent stronger Ax on many boards. I’ll use a small, consistent steal sizing to risk less while still pressuring the blinds and the shorter BTN stack behind.

I am on the Button with Ace-Six offsuit (A6o). The UTG player (FiveTwo), who is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively, opens to 2.5bb. Although it is 4-handed, A6o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by an opening range (AT+, AJ+, AQ+, AK) and plays poorly post-flop due to reverse implied odds. As the shortest stack at the table (though still ~52bb), I should preserve chips for better spots rather than playing a weak Ace against an aggressive deep-stacked opponent. Folding is the standard and prudent play.

I am in the Small Blind with K4o. FiveTwo, who has been relatively active, opened for 250 from UTG (which is effectively the cutoff in this 4-handed setup). K4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against a raise. Calling would leave me out of position for the rest of the hand against a player with a likely stronger range, and Claude in the Big Blind still has the opportunity to squeeze or call, further complicating the pot. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack and wait for a better opening.

92o is a very weak hand. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has been playing fairly tight/aggressive, this hand doesn't have good playability. Even though I'm getting ~2.7:1 odds, 9-2 offsuit is too weak to defend profitably here. Easy fold.

I am in UTG position (4-handed) with Pocket Tens [Ts, Td], which is a premium starting hand in this configuration. With a stack of over 50 BBs, my standard play is to open-raise to build the pot and define ranges. Raising prevents opponents from realizing equity cheaply and capitalizes on the strength of my pair. A standard open size of 2.5 BB (250 chips) is consistent with table dynamics and allows me to navigate post-flop effectively.

Holding Ace-Four offsuit on the button in a 4-handed game is a standard hand to play. The UTG player (Pro) has opened for 250, which is a 2.5x raise. Pro is the short stack at the table with roughly 50 big blinds. I have a significant chip lead over him and will have position for the remainder of the hand. While A4o is not strong enough to 3-bet for value against an UTG range, it's too strong to fold on the button. I will call to see a flop and utilize my position post-flop.

T9o in the SB facing a UTG raise and button call. Getting 3.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 650). T9o has decent connectivity for straights and can flop well. With deep stacks and good implied odds if I hit, calling is reasonable here despite being out of position. The hand plays well multiway.

We’re getting a very good price in the big blind: 150 to win 850 (about 5.7:1) and we close the action in a 4-way pot. While 3♠2♠ is very weak and can be dominated, the suitedness gives us some flush/straight potential, and the small additional investment is justified versus folding. A squeeze isn’t attractive here because Pro is short and already has two callers, so we’d need a large size to generate folds and we have terrible equity when called.

Flopped middle pair with T9 on K97 board in a 4-way pot. Middle pair is vulnerable in a multiway pot - any King beats me and I'm out of position. Checking is standard here to control pot size and see what develops. No need to build a pot with a marginal holding.

We’re 4-way on a K97 rainbow flop with 3♠2♠: no pair, no straight draw, and only a backdoor spade flush. Multiway our fold equity is low, and this board connects well with the UTG raiser’s range (Kx, strong broadways) and the callers’ ranges (Kx/9x/7x, pairs). With no meaningful blockers and minimal equity, a bluff/donk is burning chips; best is to check and realize what little equity we have.

I hold Pocket Tens on a K-9-7 rainbow flop. This is a 4-way pot. The presence of the King hits the defending ranges of the blinds and the calling range of the button reasonably hard. With three opponents, the likelihood that one holds a King is significant. C-betting here turns my hand into a bluff catcher or forces folds mostly from hands I already crush, while building a pot I likely lose to a King. Checking allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially catch bluffs or get to a cheap showdown. My hand has showdown value, but it is not strong enough to value bet into three players.

On this King-high board (Kd 9c 7h), the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) checked, followed by both blinds. I have Ace-high with no direct draw. In a 4-way pot, betting as a bluff is less effective as it's more likely one of the three opponents connected with the board or is slow-playing a King. By checking behind, I realize my equity, keep the pot small, and get to see a free turn card where I might improve to top pair or pick up a better bluffing opportunity if the turn is an undercard and everyone checks again.

Turn brought Qd, giving me a gutshot straight draw (need J for a straight) in addition to my middle pair of 9s. Board now shows Kd9c7hQd - two diamonds out there. Everyone checked the flop, suggesting no one has a strong king. However, the Q could have helped someone. My hand is still marginal in this 4-way pot - middle pair with a gutshot. Best to check again and control pot size while keeping options open. If someone bets big, I can fold; if it checks through, I might improve on the river.

Turn brings the Qd, adding a second diamond and improving a lot of the ranges that called pre (Kx/Qx/diamond draws/broadways). With 3♠2♠ we have essentially no equity (no pair, no straight draw, no meaningful spade draw) and we’re still 4-way, so a lead is very unlikely to get through multiple opponents and just burns chips. Best is to keep the pot small, check, and be prepared to fold to any real pressure.

The turn card is the Queen of Diamonds, making the board [Kd, 9c, 7h, Qd]. This is a poor card for my Pocket Tens as it adds a second overcard to the board and completes the potential Jack-Ten straight draw (though I hold two Tens, reducing the combos of J-T significantly). With three opponents in the hand and two overcards on the board, my hand is now a weak bluff-catcher or marginal showdown hand. Betting yields no value as better hands (Kings, Queens, two pairs) will call, and worse hands will fold. My goal is to get to a cheap showdown. Both blinds have checked to me, and checking gives me the best chance to control the pot size.

On the turn Queen of Diamonds, the action has once again checked around to me. While everyone's repeated checking indicates a lot of weakness, a 4-way pot is still difficult to bluff effectively, especially when the board is becoming more coordinated with straight and flush draws. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, is short-stacked and might be looking to check-shove a marginal hand or a draw. By checking behind, I realize my equity with Ace-high for free, keep the pot small, and remain in position to see what happens on the river. If an Ace falls, I likely have the best hand, and if the river is another blank and everyone checks again, I can consider a small value bet or a bluff depending on the final board texture.

River paired the King, making the board Kd9c7hQdKc. I have T9s giving me two pair (Kings and Nines). Everyone checked flop and turn in this 4-way pot, suggesting no strong Kings. However, any K gives trips, any Q makes KKQQ which beats my KK99. My hand has showdown value but is too marginal to bet for value in a 4-way pot first to act. Checking is optimal - I'll call a small bet but fold to significant aggression.

River is K♦ 9♣ 7♥ Q♦ K♣ in a 4-way pot where everyone has shown passivity. With 3♠2♠ we have pure air and no relevant blockers (we don’t block Kx, Qx, or missed diamond draws). A river stab into three players is unlikely to get through often enough, and it also exposes us to getting raised (especially by a slow-played Kx) with zero ability to continue. Given we can take a free showdown for 0 and preserve stack, checking is best.

On the river, the board is [Kd 9c 7h Qd Kc]. I hold Pocket Tens. The action has been checked to me by the Small Blind and Big Blind in a 4-way pot. The pot is 1000. My hand is effectively Two Pair, Kings and Tens with a Queen kicker.
