I am beating pairs of Nines, Sevens, and lower pocket pairs, but I lose to any King (Trips), any Queen (Two Pair, Kings and Queens), or Jacks. 

Given the double broadway runout (Turn Q, River K) and the multi-way nature of the pot, betting for value is very thin. Worse hands (like a 9 or 7) are unlikely to call a bet, and better hands (Qx, Kx) will certainly call or raise. Checking back ensures I get to showdown cheaply to see if my hand holds up against the blinds' weak ranges and the button's checked-down range. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff or value bet into a range that likely has me beat if they continue.

The board is King-Nine-Seven-Queen-King. The hand has been checked all the way through the flop, turn, and river by all four players. My Ace-high has significant showdown value in this situation, as the repeated checking from everyone suggests no one has a strong hand (like a King or a Queen) and likely very few players even have a marginal pair (Nines or Sevens). In a 4-way pot, betting is risky because a player with a small pair might feel compelled to call a small bet, and I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand. Checking is the most prudent play to see if my Ace-high is the best hand without committing more chips to a pot where my relative strength is uncertain.

I am dealt T5o in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack of 177 big blinds and there are 28 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to open-raise or call with this hand. Folding is the standard and most profitable play in this situation to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Q3o on the button is a marginal hand, but with only the blinds left to act after Flash folded, this is a reasonable steal spot. I have position and good fold equity. Pro is short-stacked at ~58bb which could make him more likely to play back, but a standard 2.5x open is +EV here given the positional advantage.

Claude’s button open is likely wide, and flat-calling from the SB with A7o invites the short BB in and leaves us out of position in a multiway pot. A7 also blocks strong Ax continues, making it a good candidate to 3-bet as a semi-bluff/merge to win preflop often, deny equity, and take initiative. If Claude 4-bets, we can comfortably fold; if called, we can leverage range advantage on many boards.

I am facing a button open raise to 250 followed by a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1,000. My hand, Jack-Eight offsuit, is extremely weak in this scenario. The pot odds require roughly 40% equity to call, but against a strong 3-betting range, my hand has significantly less than that. Additionally, I am the shortest stack at the table and should preserve chips for better opportunities. Folding is the only correct play.

FiveTwo 3-bet me to 1000 from the SB. Q3o is far too weak to continue here - it has poor equity against a 3-bet range and plays terribly postflop even with position. Not a good hand to call or 4-bet bluff with. Easy fold.

Pocket 4s in UTG at a 4-handed table. Small pocket pair with good implied odds given deep stacks (189 BBs). Standard open raise to 2.5x to build the pot and set-mine. If I hit a set, I can potentially win a big pot.

Claude’s UTG open is likely fairly wide 4-handed, but K3o is still a dominated, low-playability hand. Flatting invites tough spots versus squeezes from the SB/BB and leaves us frequently dominated by better Kx and Ax holdings even with position. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive with this exact combo (poor postflop realization when called, better candidates exist). Preserve EV and wait for a stronger/button-3betable hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Ah 6h (A6s). The UTG player (Claude), who is the chip leader, has raised to 250. I am the shortest stack at the table with ~58BB. While A6s is a playable hand, calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position against a strong UTG opening range that dominates my Ace (AK-A8). Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall, further complicating the post-flop play. Given my stack disadvantage, I want to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots out of position. Folding is the prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Four suited. Claude, the chip leader at the table, has raised to 250 from UTG. I only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 550. These pot odds (approximately 3.6:1) are very favorable for a suited King. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the hand has enough potential equity and playability to justify a call rather than a fold or a high-variance 3-bet against an UTG range.

On the flop of 2h 7h Qs, I have a King-high flush draw. As the Big Blind in a heads-up pot against the UTG raiser (Claude), the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out into the pre-flop raiser is generally weak here as the board texture favors his range. By checking, I allow Claude to continuation bet with his air or medium-strength hands, and I have more than enough equity with my flush draw and overcard to comfortably call a bet. If the hand checks through, I get a free card to my draw.

I have pocket 4s on Q72 with two hearts - an underpair to the queen. Flash checked from the BB. With this weak holding, I should check back for pot control. I beat very few hands that would call a bet, and I'm vulnerable to overcards and the flush draw. A free turn card is valuable here - if I improve to a set I can extract value, if scare cards come I can get away cheaply.

On the turn 2s, the board pairs but the heart flush draw is still live. Claude checked back on the flop, which suggests he does not have a strong Queen or a high pocket pair. I have significant equity with my King-high flush draw and one overcard (the King). I will check here to see if I can get a free look at the river or call a bet to realize my equity. Betting as a semi-bluff is an option, but given Claude's position as chip leader and his aggressive history, checking allows me to control the pot size and see the final card.

Board paired with the deuce on turn. I still have just pocket 4s - an underpair to the queen. Flash has checked twice showing weakness, but betting here doesn't accomplish much. I'd only get called by hands that beat me (any queen, 7, or higher pair). My hand has some showdown value against busted draws. Check back for pot control and see a free river.

The river has completed a double-paired board (7-7-2-2) with a Queen. My final hand is two pair, Sevens and Deuces, with a King kicker (7-7-2-2-K). Pre-flop, Claude raised from UTG and I called from the BB. Claude then checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests he does not have a Queen, a Seven, or a big pocket pair. However, his UTG opening range contains many Ace-high hands (like A-J, A-T) that would now be 7-7-2-2-A, beating my King kicker. Since there is no value in betting (better hands like Ace-high won't fold, and worse hands will rarely call), checking to realize my showdown value is the most logical play.

Board is double paired: 2277Q. My pocket 4s essentially play the board since Q is the kicker. I have no value to extract - any bet would only get called by hands that beat me (any Q, 7, 2, or pocket pair 5+). Flash has checked the whole way showing weakness, but checking back is correct since I'm just playing the board and have no value to bet.

4-handed UTG is effectively a late position open; with AcAd we’re way ahead of all calling/3-betting ranges. I want to build the pot and deny cheap realization while keeping my sizing consistent with standard opens so I still get action (and leave room for Pro’s short stack to 3-bet/jam).

I hold King-Eight offsuit (K8o) on the Button. The player Under the Gun (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. K8o is a weak hand that is often dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically consists of stronger Kings, Aces, and pairs). Although I have position, calling is not profitable due to the risk of domination and the likelihood of the blinds entering the pot or squeezing. With approximately 58 big blinds, I should preserve my stack for better opportunities. The standard play here is to fold.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit in the Small Blind. This is mathematically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. FiveTwo has opened for 2.5x from the UTG/Cutoff position. There is no strategic reason to call or raise with such a poor hand out of position, especially with another player (Claude) still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Holding T5o in the BB facing a min-raise from FiveTwo. Getting 2.7:1 odds (150 to win 400), but T5o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well postflop. It's difficult to make strong hands and I'll often be dominated when I hit. Against FiveTwo who has been active, I'd rather fold this weak holding and wait for better spots.

I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed play) with a very weak hand, 8s 5d. My stack is 57.75 BBs, so I am not in a push/fold situation where I need to take marginal spots. With three opponents yet to act who all cover me significantly, opening with 8-5 offsuit is a negative EV play. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I am on the button with A6o in a 4-handed game. Pro has folded from UTG. Ace-six offsuit is a standard opening hand from this position to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Claude and FiveTwo are both active players, but folding here would be too tight. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

T8 suited in the SB facing a button raise of 300. This is a decent suited connector with straight and flush potential. Against a likely wide button opening range, I have playable equity. Calling to see a flop with good implied odds makes sense, though I'll be OOP. 3-betting isn't ideal with this hand, and folding is too tight against a button steal.

Button is opening to 3bb and SB cold-calls, giving me a great price (200 to win 700 ≈ 22%). A6o has enough raw equity versus a wide BTN range and a likely capped/medium-strength SB flatting range to defend. A squeeze/3-bet is tempting with the ace blocker, but out of position and multiway it risks building a pot with a hand that realizes poorly when called and is often dominated when we make top pair. Best EV is to take the direct odds and see a flop.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw with T8d on K97. Any 6 or J gives me the straight (8 outs). However, I'm OOP against the preflop raiser (Flash) and another caller. No flush draw since board has clubs. Best play is to check and see what develops - if it checks through I get a free card, if someone bets small I can call with my draw equity, and if there's heavy action I can fold cheaply.

Multiway flop (BTN raiser + SB caller) on Kc-9h-7c is a relatively good board for the preflop raiser’s range, and my Ah6c has no pair and no direct draw—only thin backdoors (runner-runner clubs, occasional ace high showdown). Leading out here is mostly a low-EV bluff into two players who will have plenty of Kx/9x and will peel appropriately. Best is to check, keep the pot small, and react to BTN’s c-bet sizing (folding most bets, occasionally continuing vs very small sizing).

On a board of Kc-9h-7c, I have missed the flop completely with my Ace-six offsuit. While I was the pre-flop aggressor, the pot is now three-way after both Claude and FiveTwo called from the blinds. In a multi-way pot, continuation betting with total air (only Ace-high and no real draws) is significantly less effective than in heads-up play, as the likelihood that one of the two opponents has connected with the King, Nine, Seven, or a club draw is high. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and can potentially hit an Ace on the turn or see how the action develops if my opponents continue to check.

Turn brought 3h, a blank. I still have my OESD (8 outs to a straight with J or 6). The flop checked through showing weakness from all players. I could semi-bluff here, but with position against me and a dry board that favors Flash's preflop range (K-high board), a check is safer. I'll take the free card and see if I hit my straight on the river. If I miss, I can check-fold to aggression.

Flop checked through multiway on a board that usually favors the BTN opener; that’s a strong signal of capped/medium-strength ranges (a lot of A-high, small pairs, and some 9x/7x that don’t want to bloat). The turn 3h is a brick, and if I check I’m often just facing a delayed stab and folding ace-high anyway. So the best way to realize EV with Ah6c is to take a one-and-done stab that pressures their air and marginal pairs while keeping risk controlled. If I get raised I simply fold; if I get called I’ll mostly shut down unless a very favorable/scary river arrives.

On the turn card 3h, the board is Kc-9h-7c-3h. I have Ace-high with no draws. After I checked back the flop in a three-way pot, FiveTwo (Big Blind) has led out for 500 into a 900 pot (total pot now 1400). While FiveTwo has been aggressive throughout the tournament, I have almost no equity here. I have no flush or straight draws, and only three outs to an Ace which may not even be good if an opponent has a King. Additionally, Claude is still left to act behind me. Calling with just Ace-high in this multi-way scenario is losing in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

FiveTwo bet 500 into 900, making pot 1400. I need to call 500 to win 1400 (2.8:1). With 8 outs for my OESD (J or 6 for straight), I have ~17% equity with one card to come. Direct odds aren't quite there, but with stack depths around 18k and implied odds if I hit the nuts, this is a profitable call. If I hit the straight on the river I can likely extract value; if I miss, I check-fold.

River brought 2h completing a heart flush. I missed my OESD and have T-high - no showdown value. FiveTwo bet turn then checked river on the flush-completing card, suggesting either a medium-strength hand scared of the flush, or a busted club draw. While I could bluff representing the flush, FiveTwo's turn bet shows some strength and they may call with any K or pair. Better to just check and minimize losses rather than bluff into a pot where I'll often get called.

River after my turn stab gets called: Claude’s range is now heavily weighted to made hands with showdown value (Kx/9x/7x and pocket pairs) plus some missed club draws/straight draws that called once. The 2h is a brick that doesn’t add a credible new scare card, so a second barrel won’t fold out enough of his pairs, and I also don’t want to bet and fold out the very missed draws I currently beat with A-high. Best EV is to take the free showdown and realize whatever equity I have versus his floats/draws.

I am dealt Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo) in UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a strong starting hand for short-handed play. My goal is to open for a standard raise of 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and potentially thin the field. Given the stack sizes, especially Pro's shorter stack in the Big Blind, a 300-chip raise is standard for my play style as seen in previous hands.

7c9s on the button facing a UTG raise of 300. This is a weak offsuit hand that doesn't play well against an UTG opening range. While I have position, 97o is too marginal to profitably call or 3-bet here. Easy fold.

Flash’s 3bb open from first position 4-handed is likely fairly wide, and QTo has enough connectivity to realize equity with good pot odds (250 to win 700). I don’t want to 3-bet this offsuit hand from the small blind because Pro’s shorter stack in the big blind can jam and put us in a marginal, higher-variance spot, and if Flash continues we’re bloating a pot out of position with a hand that’s often dominated. Calling keeps the pot manageable while still defending a reasonable portion of our range.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ts8s with a stack of roughly 57BB. The UTG player opened to 3x, and the Small Blind called. The pot is currently 700, and I need to call 200 to see a flop, offering me excellent pot odds of 3.5:1 (requiring ~22% equity). T8s is a solid speculative hand that plays well in multi-way pots due to its flush and straight potential. Given the price and the fact that I close the action, calling is clearly the highest EV play compared to folding (too tight) or raising (too risky against an UTG range).

We flopped top pair (T) with a decent kicker in a 3-way, very dynamic board (two hearts + a lot of straight connectivity). Out of position with a medium-strength hand, leading doesn’t accomplish much: better hands continue, worse hands often fold, and we open ourselves up to getting raised and playing a bloated pot. Checking keeps ranges wide, lets the preflop raiser c-bet his range, and allows us to respond based on sizing (generally check/call reasonable bets, avoid stacking off versus big multiway aggression).

I hold Ts8s on a flop of 9h-7h-Td. I have flopped middle pair (Tens) and an open-ended straight draw (needing a Jack or a Six). The pot is 900. The Small Blind has checked to me. As the Big Blind in a multi-way pot against an UTG raiser, I should generally check to the aggressor. The board texture is very wet and connects well with UTG's range (overpairs, sets, higher straights) and the Small Blind's calling range (sets, two pairs, draws). Leading out (donk betting) here is risky as I could easily be raised, forcing me to make a tough decision with a hand that has good equity but is currently vulnerable. Checking allows me to see what the pre-flop raiser does; if they bet, I can comfortably call given my pair and strong draw. If it checks through, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or improve my pair.

I have flopped top pair with top kicker on a coordinated board of 9h-7h-Td. Both opponents have checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. Given the presence of numerous straight and flush draws (8x, Jx, and two hearts), I need to bet for value and protection. A bet of 600 (approximately 2/3 of the pot) is appropriate to charge any draws while extracting value from worse made hands like middle pairs or weaker tens.

We have top pair (TT) with a decent kicker in a 3-way pot on a very dynamic board (T97 with two hearts). Flash’s c-bet is a reasonable ~40% pot sizing that keeps his range wide (overpairs, Tx, 9x, heart draws, straight draws, and some air), so folding would be far too tight.

Raising isn’t attractive: it bloats the pot out of position with a medium-strength one-pair hand, folds out a lot of the hands we want to keep in (worse Tx/9x/some draws), and importantly gives Pro (shorter stack in the BB) a clean jam option that would put us in a high-variance, awkward spot versus a range that can be quite strong/heart-heavy.

Best line is to check/call and reassess turn based on card and sizing (continue versus reasonable barrels, but tighten up versus big multiway aggression on bad turns like hearts/8/J/overcards).

I hold Ts8s on a flop of 9h-7h-Td. I have flopped Top Pair (Tens) along with an Open-Ended Straight Draw (outs: Jack or Six). The pot is 2100, and the UTG player (Flash) bet 600, which the Small Blind (FiveTwo) called. I need to call 600 to win a total pot of 2700, giving me pot odds of 3.5:1 (approx. 22% equity required). My hand has significant equity; even against an overpair or strong range, the combination of pair outs and 8 straight outs makes this a clear call. Raising is unnecessary and risky as it isolates me against stronger hands (sets, straights) and bloats the pot out of position. Calling closes the action and lets me realize my equity cheaply.

Turn brings the 3rd heart, completing the front-door flush and making this already connected board (T-9-7-6) significantly more dangerous multiway. With QTo and no heart, our one-pair hand drops in relative strength: a lot of the flop-calling range for both Flash and Pro includes heart draws that now have us crushed, plus some straights/two-pair that can also continue.

Leading doesn’t accomplish much: worse hands will often check back anyway, better hands (flushes/strong made hands) won’t fold, and any bet can induce a shove from Pro’s shorter stack and force us into a high-variance, uncomfortable spot out of position. Best is to keep the pot controlled, protect our checking range, and react to sizing (potentially continue versus small bets, but be ready to fold to big multiway pressure).

I have turned a straight (6-7-8-9-T) on a board of [9h 7h Td 6h]. However, the board is extremely coordinated: there are three hearts making a flush possible, and the four-card straight sequence means my straight is the lower end (J-8 would make a higher straight). Given the multi-way action and the pre-flop raiser (Flash) yet to act behind me, leading out is risky. A bet would likely only be called by flushes or higher straights, essentially value-owning myself. My stack is relatively short (SPR < 2), so checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate the aggressor's action. If checked through, I avoid facing a tough raise; if bet into, I can decide based on sizing.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more dangerous with the arrival of the 6 of hearts, which completes a potential flush and various straight possibilities (like 8-5). Although I have top pair with an Ace kicker, the presence of two opponents who called my flop bet suggests they likely have pieces of this coordinated board. By checking behind, I am practicing pot control and realizing my showdown value. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I risk being check-raised by a flush or a straight. Checking allows me to see the river for free and decide based on the final card and my opponents' actions.

River is a relative brick (3c) after the flush card hit on the turn and checked through. With three hearts on board, a flush requires *two* hearts in hand; many strong made hands (flushes/straights/two-pair) would often bet turn for value/protection, so the double-check keeps both opponents’ ranges fairly capped and full of one-pair hands (Tx/9x/7x) and some give-ups.

Our QTo is still a medium-strength showdown hand, but checking invites Flash to take a bigger stab and puts us in a tougher bluff-catch spot. Best is a small block/value bet that gets called by worse pairs and sets the price; if we face a raise/jam (especially from Pro), we can comfortably fold given how value-heavy that line is multiway.

Sizing: ~25% pot to target calls from worse and keep our bet/fold clean.

The river brings the 3c, completing the board as [9h 7h Td 6h 3c]. The pot is 2700. FiveTwo leads into me for 700. I hold [Ts 8s] for a straight (6-7-8-9-T). The board contains three hearts, so a flush is possible, and a higher straight (J-8) beats me. However, the turn (which brought the third heart and possible flush) checked through, suggesting neither opponent has a strong flush. FiveTwo's small river bet (approx 1/4 pot) looks like a blocking bet with a weaker hand (two pair, set) or a value bet with a similar straight/weak flush. Given the excellent pot odds (~17% equity needed) and the fact that the turn action showed weakness, folding a straight here is too conservative. Raising would only get called by better hands (flushes, J8), so the correct play is to just call and realize my equity.

On the river, the board is 9h-7h-Td-6h-3c, which is highly coordinated with both flush and straight possibilities. I hold top pair with the top kicker (AsTh). FiveTwo has led with a small bet of 700 into a pot of 2,700, and Pro has called. This small sizing (roughly 25% pot) from FiveTwo often indicates a blocker bet or a marginal hand like a weaker ten rather than a monster, and Pro's call also suggests a one-pair hand. Given the pot odds are nearly 6-to-1, I only need to be correct about 15% of the time to justify a call. In a short-handed game, my top pair top kicker is strong enough to call this small price, even if there is a risk of a flush or straight being present. Raising would be unwise as it would only be called by hands that beat me, so I will call to realize my showdown value.
Q3o is a weak hand from UTG position. Even 4-handed, this doesn't meet the threshold for an open raise. Folding is the disciplined play.

Claude has folded and we’re on the button with position against the blinds. KTo is comfortably ahead of the blinds’ random holdings and plays well as an open-raise hand, especially with our stack edge over the SB. Raising here applies maximum pressure to their ranges, often wins the pot preflop, and keeps initiative when called. I’ll use a standard small open size to risk little while still generating folds and keeping my range balanced.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 6s, a very weak offsuit hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has opened to 2.5bb. While the price to call is relatively cheap, playing 96o out of position against a button raiser is a losing strategy due to poor playability and low equity realization. I have no reason to get involved in this pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 3s, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. FiveTwo has raised from the button to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a pot of 400), this hand has very poor playability post-flop and will be difficult to realize any equity out of position. Folding is the most prudent move to avoid losing more chips with a junk hand.

4-handed UTG is effectively a late position spot; we should open fairly wide to pressure the blinds and build a lead. A3o is not a premium, but it has an ace blocker (reduces chance we run into strong Ax/AA) and plays fine in position versus the blinds, especially when we can often win preflop or take it down with a c-bet on many boards. Limping invites multiway pots and lets aggressive players like Flash/Claude punish us; folding is too tight given the blind-steal value. I’ll use a standard small open to keep my range wide and my risk controlled.

I am on the Button with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o). The player in the Cutoff (UTG in 4-handed), FiveTwo, has raised to 250. FiveTwo has been opening frequently, but A8o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by an opening range (AQ, AJ, AT, A9). Calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the blinds and puts me in a spot where I can lose a big pot if I hit top pair with a weak kicker against a better Ace. Given my stack depth (~90BB), I can wait for better opportunities rather than contesting this pot with a easily dominated hand.

In this 4-handed situation, FiveTwo opens from the UTG/Cutoff position with a 2.5x raise. While QTo is a reasonable hand in a short-handed game, playing it from the Small Blind (the worst position) against an active open is problematic. Calling often leads to playing a dominated hand out of position, and it also invites the Big Blind (Claude) to squeeze. Given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament, I prefer to avoid this marginal, out-of-position spot and wait for a more advantageous situation.
J2o is a weak hand facing a UTG raise. Even getting decent pot odds (150 to win 400), this hand plays poorly postflop with minimal equity against an UTG opening range. Easy fold.

I am holding 6h 2d, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unsuited, unconnected, low ranks). I am also in the UTG position, which is the most disadvantageous position pre-flop as everyone else has yet to act. Given the deep stacks (approx 89 BB) and the late stage of the tournament (Hand 129/150), preserving chips is more important than taking low-probability risks. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am on the button with 4-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While the button is a good position to attempt a steal, Claude (SB) and FiveTwo (BB) both have large stacks and have shown a tendency to be active pre-flop. Risking chips on such a low-equity hand this late in the tournament (Hand 129 of 150) is not strategic. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 2c5d - one of the worst hands in poker. Even though it's only 50 to complete against the BB, this hand plays terribly postflop with no high cards, no connectivity, and no suited potential. FiveTwo has been aggressive and will likely raise if I complete. Folding this trash and saving 50 chips is the correct play.

I am UTG with 5c6s, which is a very weak starting hand. Given the stack sizes and the fact that we are approaching the end of the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips with a sub-par hand from the worst position at the table. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have QQ on the button - a premium hand. Flash folded, leaving only the blinds behind me. With pocket Queens, I should raise for value. Standard raise to 250-300 to build the pot while likely getting action from weaker hands.

Facing a 3x BTN open from the small blind, 6c2c is too weak to defend. Even with decent immediate pot odds, we’ll realize equity poorly out of position, get dominated when we make a pair, and we’re exposed to the BB squeezing behind. This is a low-EV, high-variance call with no compelling exploit, so we muck and preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 7h facing a Button raise to 300 from Claude. The pot is 450 (300 raise + 50 dead SB + 100 my BB), and I need to call 200 to see a flop. The pot odds are 200/650 ≈ 30.8%. Although 73s has raw equity of roughly 33-35% against a wide button opening range, it suffers significantly from poor playability and being out of position. I will likely under-realize my equity. Against a 3x raise, defending 73s is generally -EV. Therefore, the disciplined play is to fold.
A2o from UTG in a 4-handed game is playable. With 183 BB deep, I'll open with a standard raise. Ace-high has decent equity and can flop top pair occasionally. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Facing a 2.5x UTG open 4-handed, we can defend the Button fairly wide, but 64o is near the bottom: it’s offsuit, has poor playability, gets dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t provide useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. Calling will struggle to realize equity even in position, and 3-betting risks getting flatted/4-bet by a range that interacts well with our hand. Best EV is to preserve chips and fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 9c. The UTG player (Claude) has raised to 250. I am out of position against an UTG opening range which is typically strong. My hand is an off-suit connector with low playability and equity against a standard opening range. Additionally, the Big Blind is still left to act, so calling could invite a squeeze or a multi-way pot where I am out of position with a marginal hand. Given my stack size relative to the others (I am the shortest), preserving chips is important, and this spot is not profitable. Folding is the standard and disciplined decision.

I am holding Pocket Kings (Kh, Kc) in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand. Claude (UTG) has raised to 250. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I am out of position, I should 3-bet to build the pot and gain value. A standard 3-bet sizing of roughly 3.5x the initial raise is appropriate here, so I will raise to 850.

A2o facing a 3-bet from BB. This is a weak ace that plays poorly postflop, especially OOP. Against Flash's 3-bet, I'm likely dominated by better aces and don't have good implied odds. Standard fold here - A2o isn't strong enough to continue vs a 3-bet.

4-handed, AJo is a clear open from UTG: it dominates a lot of the continuing range (worse Ax, broadways) and plays well in position against the blinds. Limping invites multiway and makes it harder to realize equity; folding is too tight. I’ll use a small, standard open size to keep worse hands in and avoid bloating the pot versus stronger 3-bet ranges while still applying pressure on the blinds.

I hold 2c 6s on the Button, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in the UTG position, my hand has very poor equity against their opening range. Although I have position, the hand is unplayable, and preserving my stack of 8775 (approx 88 BB) for better opportunities is the correct play. There are no pot odds or strategic dynamics that justify entering the pot with this holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Clubs and Six of Hearts. FiveTwo has raised from UTG in a four-handed game. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given the stack sizes and the strength of a typical UTG opening range, there is no reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack as we approach the final hands of the tournament.

K7 suited in the BB facing a min-raise from UTG. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (150 to win 400). K7s is a reasonable BB defend - has some playability with flush potential and high card value. Not strong enough to 3-bet but definitely worth a call at this price.

Flopped middle pair (7s) with king kicker on Q74 board. Standard play is to check OOP with medium-strength hands. I have showdown value but not strong enough to lead out. Will check and evaluate FiveTwo's action - likely calling a reasonable c-bet given pot odds and my pair + backdoor diamond draw.

Claude’s BB flat range has a lot of broadways, suited connectors, and small/medium pairs, but this Q74r flop is still a clear range advantage for the UTG raiser (overpairs, AQ/KQ/QQ). With AJo we have little showdown value and want to pick up the pot immediately while denying equity to hands like 55–TT, 8x/9x, and worse ace-highs. A small c-bet keeps our bluffs cheap, pressures his marginal holdings, and sets up profitable barrels on good turns (A/J/K/T).

Flopped middle pair (7s) with king kicker. FiveTwo c-bets small (200 into 550). Getting nearly 5:1 pot odds. My hand has decent showdown value and I have a backdoor diamond flush draw. Easy call - folding is too tight and raising only gets called by better.

Turn brought a blank 2s. I have middle pair 7s with king kicker on Q742 board. Standard line is to check with this medium-strength hand for pot control. Don't want to bloat the pot OOP with a vulnerable hand. Will check and re-evaluate based on FiveTwo's action.

Claude’s flop call on Q74r heavily weights him toward at least some pair (Qx, 7x, 44/77, or stubborn mid pairs like 55–TT) and he’s shown a tendency to continue versus small bets. The turn 2♠ is a pure brick that doesn’t improve my range in a meaningful way (no real draws created for AJo) and doesn’t meaningfully reduce his continuing range either. Barreling here would be a mostly unprofitable second shell into a range that is still very sticky, and I also risk getting check-raised by his strong made hands. By checking back, I realize my equity (A/J outs) and keep the pot controlled, while preserving the option to value-bet if I improve or to bluff selectively on true scare rivers (A, J, K, T) if he checks again.

River 8s completes the board Q742-8. I have middle pair 7s with king kicker. FiveTwo c-bet flop then checked turn, suggesting either a weak hand that gave up or pot control with a medium hand. My 7s beats missed draws and A-high but loses to Qx, any 8x (now pairs), and overpairs. This is a pure showdown value hand - betting only gets called by better. Checking to showdown is optimal.

Claude’s line (BB defend, call small flop c-bet, check turn and river) is very consistent with a one-pair bluff-catcher range: lots of Qx, 7x/4x, and mid pairs (55–JJ) that don’t want to bet but also don’t want to fold to a single river stab. The river 8♠ isn’t a meaningful scare card for that range, and after I checked back the brick turn, my story is capped and looks like A-high give-ups often—making it harder to generate folds at a price that’s worth it. Since we’re unlikely to reach the fold frequency needed for a profitable bluff, and we still have a small chance to win at showdown versus the occasional ace-high/backdoor floats, the best play is to take the free showdown.

Current hand is 133/150, 4 players remaining. I am in UTG (effectively Cutoff in 4-handed) with [Js, Ks] and a stack of 8,775 (87.75 BB). KJs is a strong starting hand, especially short-handed. It falls well within a standard opening range from this position. The table play has seen standard opens of 2.5x-3x. I will open-raise to 250 to build the pot and assert initiative, looking to play post-flop in position against the blinds or out of position against the button with a strong flush/straight potential hand.

Holding 9d 2d on the button, I'm facing a 2.5x open from Pro in the UTG position. 92s is a very weak hand that doesn't play well against an early position opening range, even with the advantage of being on the button. There is no strategic reason to call or 3-bet here, so folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.
Holding 3s6s in SB facing UTG raise. This is a weak suited gapper - while it has some playability, it's too weak to profitably continue against an UTG opening range. Easy fold.

4-handed, Pro’s UTG opening range is meaningfully wider than full-ring, and we’re getting a strong price closing the action: 150 to win 400 (~27% required). A2o has sufficient raw equity versus a reasonable UTG range and realizes decently in many runouts. 3-betting would bloat the pot out of position with a weak kicker and no great playability, so the best EV line is a straightforward defend.

Heads-up OOP versus Pro’s UTG open. On T95 with two hearts, our A2 has essentially no made value, no meaningful straight draw, and no heart to continue on many turns. This board interacts well enough with Pro’s range (overpairs, Tx, 9x, heart combos) that leading is likely to get called/raised too often, and we don’t benefit much from fold equity with this exact hand. Best is to keep the pot small, check our range, and decide versus his c-bet sizing (mostly fold to pressure, continue only versus very small bets with the Ace as thin equity).

I hold KsJs on a 9h 5h Td board. I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). The board texture is wet and connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). Betting here risks getting check-raised or called by a range that has good equity against me. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a straight or top pair on the turn, and controls the pot size. My King-high also has some showdown value against missed draws. Therefore, checking is the prudent play.

Flop checked through, which caps Pro’s range toward a lot of overcards, some medium pairs, and some delayed barrels with hearts/straighty hands. The 2c turn improves us to a (weak) made hand and is a card that connects much more with BB than UTG (Pro has very little 2x). If we check, Pro can comfortably stab a wide range and we’ll often be forced to fold our thin showdown hand. A small donk lead lets us (1) deny equity vs his unpaired broadways, (2) set a cheap price to reach showdown, and (3) fold cleanly if he shows real strength with a raise. Sizing small keeps our risk low while still generating folds from his pure air.

On the turn, the board is [9h 5h Td 2c]. I hold [Js Ks], giving me two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). The opponent bets 200 into a pot of 550, which is a small bet (~36% pot). I am getting pot odds of approximately 21% (200 to call into a total pot of 950). My hand has reasonable equity: 4 outs to the nuts (Queen) and 6 outs to top pair (King or Jack), though the pairs aren't guaranteed to be good, they are likely strong enough to win at showdown or induce a check on the river. Given the small sizing, the price is too good to fold a hand with this much potential and position. Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling is the most +EV play to realize my equity.

We improved to bottom pair on the turn and used a small donk to deny equity and buy a cheap showdown. Now on this river (9h5hTd2c8c) our hand is still very weak: it only really beats unpaired overcards, and it loses to essentially any pair and to the occasional 67 straight.

Betting here doesn’t get called by many worse hands (A-high will fold a lot, while any made pair can call/raise), so a thin “block/value” bet is unlikely to outperform just taking our showdown. Checking keeps worse hands in, avoids getting raised off our equity, and lets Pro make the mistake of checking back A-high often enough.

Plan: check and mostly fold to meaningful pressure, only consider calling versus a very small stab.

The board runout is 9h-5h-Td-2c-8c. This river card (8) is very coordinated, completing several straight draws: any Q makes a straight (with my Jack acting as a connector, creating 8-9-T-J-Q), any 7 makes a straight (7-8-9-T-J), and 6-7 makes a straight (5-6-7-8-9). Given that the opponent called pre-flop and bet the turn, they could easily have a 7, Q, or just a pair. My King-high has very little showdown value, but converting it into a bluff is risky given how many straights and pairs are in the opponent's range. The opponent checked the river, potentially inducing action or giving up. Checking back minimizes loss and allows me to see a showdown for free, though I likely lose to any pair or Ace-high.

I am dealt 2s 6c in the UTG position in a 4-handed game. This is a very weak starting hand, and with only 16 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips by attempting a bluff or playing a hand with such poor equity and playability. Folding is the standard and most prudent action.

23o is one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm on the button with only blinds to act, this hand has virtually no playability post-flop. With 18500 chips and 16 hands remaining, I don't need to take unnecessary risks with garbage hands. Easy fold.

Blind-vs-blind and folded to us: we should attack the big blind very frequently. T8o is well within a profitable SB raising range, with decent high-card/straight potential and good playability in position postflop. Pro is the shorter stack (effective 8.2k) and has shown a tendency to play straightforwardly, so applying pressure with a standard open is higher EV than limping. I’ll use a 3x size to reduce BB’s realization and win the pot immediately at a good clip without bloating it too much when called.

The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 3bb (300 chips). In a Blind vs Blind scenario, opening ranges are typically wide (often 40-50%+ of hands). King-Nine offsuit is well ahead of a random hand and has sufficient equity to defend against a standard opening range. Additionally, as the Big Blind, I will have a positional advantage post-flop against the Small Blind, which makes calling with marginal to good hands more attractive than 3-betting or folding. The pot odds require me to call 200 more to play a pot of 600 (33% equity needed), which K9o easily satisfies. Defending by calling allows me to keep the opponent's wide range in play while utilizing my position.

Heads-up blind-vs-blind on A♦ J♥ 9♥, we have a strong semi-bluff: an open-ended straight draw (7/Q are clean outs) plus a backdoor heart draw with T♥. As the preflop raiser we retain enough range advantage to c-bet small and deny equity to BB’s random floats while risking little when he continues (he’ll call mostly with Ax/Jx/heart-heavy hands). Versus Pro’s generally straightforward profile, a small bet should pick up the pot often enough and still lets us realize equity cheaply when called; we can barrel on good turns (7/Q/heart, sometimes K/T) and otherwise slow down.

On a board of [9h Jh Ad], I have bottom pair with a King kicker. The opponent (Small Blind) made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot). This texture favors the aggressor's range (Ax, Jx), but the small bet size suggests a high-frequency c-bet strategy or a merge. I need 20% equity to call. Even against a stronger range, my 5 outs (improving to two pair or trips) plus the possibility that I am currently ahead of pure bluffs or draws gives me sufficient odds to continue. Folding a pair to a single small bet in a Blind vs Blind scenario is too tight. I will call in position and re-evaluate on the turn.

Turn is an excellent barrel card for our range: the board pairs the ace (A♦A♥), which strongly favors the preflop raiser and puts a lot of pressure on BB’s flop-calling range (Jx/9x and many heart draws/gutters). We also have a very strong combo draw ourselves: OESD (7/Q) plus a flush draw with T♥ (15-ish outs when he’s on trips Ax, with some caution that boats like AJ/A9 already have us crushed). Betting a solid, not-too-small size maximizes fold equity versus his one-pair/two-pair holdings and charges draws, while still letting us comfortably fold if he check-raises big (likely Ax/boats).

The board is extremely coordinated: [9h Jh Ad Ah]. There are three hearts and a pair of Aces. My hand, [Ks 9d], constitutes Two Pair (Aces and Nines) with a King kicker, but effectively acts as a bluff-catcher. The opponent (Small Blind) has bet 700 into 1000 (70% pot) on the turn after c-betting the flop. Given the board texture, the opponent's range includes many hands that beat me: any single heart makes a flush, any Ace makes trips, and any Jack makes a better two pair. Even pocket pairs like QQ or KK beat me. The only hands I beat are pure air bluffs without a heart, which are unlikely to fire a large barrel on such a scary board. My equity is likely near 0% against their value range, and I cannot profitably call.
KTs is a solid suited broadway hand that plays well from UTG. With 18500 chips and only 4 players remaining, I should open raise to build a pot. Standard open to 2.5x BB.

4-handed, Claude’s UTG open range is wider than full-ring and we have position. Calling invites Pro/Flash to come along cheaply and K8o plays poorly multiway. A small-to-standard 3-bet leverages our button position, uses the K blocker to reduce the strongest continues (AK/KK), and puts pressure on Claude’s opening range while often picking up the dead money immediately. If called, we’ll play a positionally advantaged, initiative-driven pot and can proceed cautiously on K-high dominated textures.

Facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet, A4s is too weak to continue. Cold-calling from the Small Blind leaves me out of position against two strong ranges, likely dominated by better Aces or pairs, and vulnerable to a 4-bet from the original raiser. Cold 4-betting is too risky against an UTG open. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Claude opened from UTG and FiveTwo 3-bet from the Button. J8o is a weak hand that does not play well out of position, especially against two opponents where one has shown significant strength with a 3-bet. Given my healthy stack and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to commit chips to a high-variance, negative-equity spot.

KTc suited facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. I'm OOP but have a suited broadway hand with good playability. The 3-bet is standard sizing (3x). With effective stacks around 175BB, I have good implied odds if I hit a flush draw or strong pair. FiveTwo has been active with 3-bets throughout the tournament. Folding KTs here is too tight, and 4-betting OOP as a bluff isn't ideal. Calling to see a flop is the best play - I can re-evaluate based on board texture.

Flopped a gutshot straight draw (need J) and backdoor club flush draw with KcTc on Qd3s9c. This board doesn't connect well with my range and I'm OOP against a 3-bettor. FiveTwo likely has strong hands like AQ, KQ, QQ, or big pairs. Checking to see what FiveTwo does is standard - I can evaluate calling a reasonable c-bet with my draws or fold to heavy aggression.

Claude’s check in a 3-bet pot lets us leverage our range advantage (we have more AQ/KQ/QQ/overpairs) and win the pot immediately a good amount. With Kh8s we have little showdown value, so checking back mostly just realizes weak equity and invites turns/rivers where we’re guessing. A small c-bet pressures his underpairs (66–JJ), some suited broadways that missed, and denies equity while keeping our risk low; if he check-raises we can comfortably fold.

I have KcTc with a gutshot straight draw (need J) and backdoor club flush draw. FiveTwo made a small c-bet of 500 into 1650 (~30% pot). Getting 4.3:1 odds (need ~19% equity). Gutshot gives ~8%, backdoor flush ~4%, and I have two overcards that could improve (though risky against 3-bet range). The small sizing gives me a good price to peel and see the turn. Calling is correct - folding would be too tight given the odds, and raising as a semi-bluff OOP isn't ideal.

Turn 8c gives me a strong flush draw (Kc Tc with 9c 8c on board) plus my gutshot to J. That's 12 outs (~24% equity). I'm first to act with 0 to call. Best play is to check and let FiveTwo act - I can call a reasonable bet with my draws or check-raise as a semi-bluff if sizing is right. Leading out as a donk bet would be awkward OOP against the 3-bettor.

Claude’s flop check-call in a 3-bet pot heavily weights him toward made hands (Qx, 99/88, 9x) and some sticky underpairs/draws. The 8c turn improves us to second pair, giving real showdown value, but it also connects with parts of his continue range (88, 98s, T7s, club draws). Firing a second barrel mostly just folds out hands we already beat (some TT/JJ without a club) while getting called by better (Qx, sets/two pair) and putting us in tough river spots versus a check-raise or a call-then-lead line. With position and a hand that can reasonably check back and bluff-catch some rivers, the best EV/variance tradeoff is to take the free card and realize our equity.

River Qh paired the board and killed my flush draw. I now have just King-high. FiveTwo checked the turn, suggesting medium strength or giving up. The paired Queens make this a scary board to bluff - any Qx in their 3-bet range (AQ, KQ, QQ) now has trips. With K-high I have no showdown value, but bluffing into a 3-bettor who could easily have a queen or pocket pair is too risky this late in the tournament. Best to check and accept the loss if FiveTwo has any piece.

River pairs the Q, and after Claude’s flop check-call in a 3-bet pot his range is still heavy in Qx and big pocket pairs (AA–TT) that now make stronger two-pair/trips and will continue versus a bet. The hands we beat (missed club draws, some lower pocket pairs like 44–77) are either folding often or are a relatively thin part of his flop continue range, so betting is likely to get called mostly by better and occasionally induce an expensive check-raise. With K8 we now have decent showdown (QQ88 with a K kicker), so the highest-EV line is to take the free showdown and avoid turning a medium-strength hand into a bluff/value-bet that’s dominated by his calling range.

4-handed ranges widen, but UTG still needs playable cards to avoid dominated/low-equity spots out of position versus three opponents. 32o has terrible equity, poor playability, and can’t realize equity well postflop; limping just burns 100 chips and raising is spewy. Preserve stack for better steal/defend opportunities.

I am on the Button with J4o, which is a very weak starting hand. The UTG player has folded. Although I am in position, the two players in the blinds (Flash and Claude) have deep stacks (over 150 BBs) and can easily defend or 3-bet. My stack is roughly 78 BBs, so I am not desperate to steal. The potential reward of 1.5 BBs does not outweigh the risk of playing a dominated hand post-flop or facing a re-raise. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit. Both the UTG and Button players have folded, leaving me heads-up against Claude in the Big Blind. While blind-vs-blind situations often warrant wider ranges, J2o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack (over 150 BBs) and we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, there is no need to enter a pot with such a marginal hand against a capable opponent like Claude.

I am in UTG position with King-Four offsuit (K4o), which is a weak hand. Although the table is short-handed (4 players), opening from the first position with K4o is generally a losing play due to the likelihood of being dominated or out-flopped, and having to play out of position against up to three opponents. With 78 big blinds, I have plenty of stack depth to wait for better starting hands, especially given there are only roughly 13 hands left in the tournament. Folding is the disciplined and correct decision here.

I am on the button with 6s 4s, which is a speculative suited gapper. While it can occasionally be used for a positional steal, both Claude and FiveTwo have been active and hold larger stacks than me. Given that there are only 13 hands remaining in the tournament and I have a healthy stack of over 150 big blinds, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for stronger cards to protect my position and avoid unnecessary variance against aggressive blinds.

Holding 7c8s in the small blind with only the big blind left to act. This connected hand plays reasonably well heads-up. I'll make a standard raise to 250 to put pressure on FiveTwo and take the initiative in the hand.

Claude is opening from the small blind in a short-handed game, so his range should be wide. Pocket 8s performs well versus that range and benefits from taking the betting lead OOP, denying equity to his suited/connected hands, and building a pot when we’re likely ahead. With deep stacks, flatting is fine for set-mining, but 3-betting is higher-EV here given his likely frequency and our ability to win preflop or with a single c-bet on many boards. If he 4-bets, we can comfortably fold this hand most of the time.

Facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo after my SB open with 78o. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet range. The pot odds are ~1.8:1 (600 to win 1100), but 78o lacks the equity needed to profitably continue. FiveTwo has been active with 3-bets throughout the tournament. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game. While 6c4c is suited, it is far too weak to open-raise from the first position, especially against aggressive opponents like FiveTwo and Claude who have larger stacks. Opening here would likely lead to being out of position post-flop or facing a 3-bet. With only 12 hands remaining in the tournament, I should prioritize stack preservation and wait for higher-equity opportunities.
J8o on the button with only the blinds left to act. This is a standard button steal spot - I have position and a playable hand. Standard open to 2.5x.

We’re in the small blind facing a BTN open (2.5bb) with KK, a premium hand that wants to build the pot and deny equity. Flatting risks going multiway with the big blind and gives BTN a wide range realization in position. A standard, strong-value 3-bet size OOP here is ~4x the open to pressure the BTN range and isolate while still keeping in dominated hands (AQ, AJ, KQ, TT-QQ) and inducing occasional 4-bets we can continue against. If BTN 4-bets, we’ll be happy to continue (often for stacks depending on sizing) given stack depths and opponent tendencies.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9s 2c, which is a very weak hand. The action started with Claude raising from the Button, followed by a 3-bet from FiveTwo in the Small Blind to 1000. Facing a 3-bet cold with trash cards, I have no viable strategic option but to fold. My equity against a 3-betting range is extremely low, and the cost to call (900 chips) is far too high for this hand strength.

FiveTwo 3-bet me to 1000. I have J8o which is a weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet. Even with position, J8o doesn't play well postflop - I'll often make second-best hands. Pot odds are about 36% (750 to win 2100) which isn't enough for this speculative hand. FiveTwo has been active but that doesn't justify calling with such a marginal holding. Disciplined fold.

3h5c is a very weak hand - unsuited, disconnected low cards. From UTG position, I should only be opening strong hands. With a healthy stack of 168bb, there's no need to take risks with marginal holdings. Easy fold.

4-handed and on the button after UTG folds, AJo is well within a profitable opening range. We have maximum positional advantage and can win the blinds often, while still having strong playability when called. I’ll use a standard small open size to pressure the blinds (especially the shorter SB) without bloating the pot unnecessarily deep-stacked.

In a 4-handed game, QJo is a strong hand against a Button opening range. However, I am out of position against the chip leader (FiveTwo) who has been showing aggression. 3-betting would bloat the pot and potentially isolate me against a stronger range or face a 4-bet. Folding is too conservative given the width of Button ranges 4-handed. Calling is the most prudent play to see a flop and realize equity while keeping the pot size manageable.

I am in the big blind with 2c 4d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (175 to win a total pot of 825), 2-4 offsuit is difficult to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser like FiveTwo and a short-stacked small blind. With only 12 hands left in the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack rather than committing chips with a bottom-tier hand.

On an Ace-high flop (Ah 8h 7d), the board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) on the Button, who has a wide range of Ax hands. As the caller in the Small Blind with QJo, I have completely missed the flop with no significant draws. Being out of position against the chip leader, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Donk betting here would be ill-advised as I have no equity to protect or value to extract. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold, but checking gives me the chance to see a free turn if they check back.

We opened the button and got called by the SB, then he checks on an A-high, somewhat draw-heavy flop (two hearts, some straight connectivity). With AJo we have top pair with a strong enough kicker to value-bet versus worse Ax (A2–AT), 8x/7x, and heart/straight draws, while also denying equity. I’ll use a small c-bet size to get called wide and keep the pot manageable against stronger aces that exist in his preflop calling range.

On the flop of Ah 8h 7d, I hold QJo and have completely missed, holding only Queen-high with no immediate straight or flush draws. The opponent (FiveTwo), who raised pre-flop from the Button, has continued with a bet of 250 into a pot of 650. This board texture (Ace-high) heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Being out of position with a weak hand and facing a bet from the chip leader, I have insufficient equity to call. Floating is not profitable here given my stack size relative to the field and the lack of backend equity. The most prudent play is to preserve my stack for a better spot.

4-handed ranges widen, but 72s is still near the bottom of any UTG opening/limping range and plays poorly out of position versus the Button and blinds. With a healthy chip lead, there’s no need to take low-EV, high-variance spots—better to preserve stack and apply pressure with hands that realize equity more reliably.

I hold 4c 2h on the Button, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands. Although being on the Button provides a positional advantage often suitable for stealing blinds, the recent history shows the blinds (Flash and Claude) have been active and capable of 3-betting pre-flop. Risking chips with near-zero equity in this spot is unnecessary given my stack size and the stage of the tournament. The standard and prudent play is to fold.

With 75o in the small blind and the action folded to me, I am faced with a decision against Claude in the big blind. 75o is a very weak hand to play out of position, and Claude has shown significant aggression and willingness to defend in previous hands. Given that there are only 10 hands left in the tournament and I have a healthy stack, there is no reason to risk chips in a marginal blind-versus-blind battle with such a poor hand. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here.

The tournament is 4-handed, making UTG effectively the Cutoff position. I hold Kh 7h with a stack of 74 BBs, which is the shortest at the table but still deep. While suited Kings can be opened from the Cutoff, K7s is on the lower end of the opening range and is easily dominated by the calling or 3-betting ranges of the deep-stacked opponents behind me (Flash, Claude, FiveTwo). Given the aggressive tendencies of the opponents (particularly FiveTwo in the Big Blind who has been active), opening this hand risks facing a 3-bet or playing a dominated pot out of position. Preserving chips for better spots is the more prudent strategy here.

I am on the button with JTo (Jack-Ten offsuit), which is a solid hand to open in a 4-handed game. The player in the Small Blind (Claude) and the Big Blind (FiveTwo) both have larger stacks than me, but I am in position. Opening here allows me to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 3BB (300) to keep my standard opening size.
6d8h is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a button raise. Out of position with a marginal hand that doesn't play well postflop. Easy fold.

Flash’s button open is a larger 3x sizing, which tightens my big-blind defense range substantially. T2o is at the very bottom with poor playability and reverse implied odds (dominated tens, bad kickers, tough to realize equity OOP). While the direct price (200 to win 650 after calling) is tempting, the realization is poor versus a competent button range and I don’t need to take thin, high-variance spots as the covering stack late in the set. Best EV is to release and preserve my stack for better steal/3-bet opportunities.

Holding T3o in the UTG position is extremely weak. In a 4-handed game, while ranges are wider, T3o still lacks any significant equity or playability. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Folding here is the standard and most disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

On the button with A3o, folded to me. This is a standard button steal spot. A3o is playable from the button against only the blinds. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to put pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable with my marginal ace.

Claude is opening from the button fairly frequently and has shown a willingness to open/fold versus 3-bets. In the small blind, flat-calling with A4o plays poorly out of position and invites the big blind to come along, reducing our equity realization. A4o is a solid light 3-bet candidate here: we block strong Ax continues, can win the pot preflop a lot, and when called we retain some playability (wheel/ace-high boards) with the initiative. Plan versus a 4-bet is to fold.

I hold Jack-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. The action involves a raise from the Button and a substantial 3-bet from the Small Blind to 1000. My hand is extremely weak (trash) and poorly playable post-flop, especially in a 3-bet pot. Facing this aggression, I am getting poor odds and likely dominated. With 73 big blinds, I should preserve my stack for better spots. The only logical play is to fold.

FiveTwo 3-bet me to 1000 from the SB. I have A3o which is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against 3-bets. While I have position, A3o is easily dominated by better aces in their 3-betting range. The pot odds are roughly 1.8:1 which isn't compelling enough to continue with this weak ace. Folding preserves my healthy stack of 16.5k with only 8 hands remaining.

J8o from UTG is below my opening range even 4-handed. This hand plays poorly out of position and is dominated by many hands that will call or 3-bet. With 165bb effective stack and only 8 hands left in the tournament, no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. Standard fold.

Folded to us on the Button with a medium pair (55). This is a standard profitable open: we can steal the blinds, realize equity well in position, and have good implied odds versus either blind if we flop a set. We keep sizing modest to avoid bloating the pot against a potentially aggressive 3-bettor while still pressuring the blinds’ ranges.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five of Clubs (Qc 5c). The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250. Although my hand is suited, Q5 is a weak speculative hand, and playing it out of position against an aggressive Button raiser is generally -EV. Additionally, the Big Blind is still to act and could squeeze. Given I am the shortest stack (72.5 BBs) and approaching the end of the tournament (Hand 143/150), conserving chips by folding marginal hands out of position is the correct strategic decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. FiveTwo (Button) has raised to 2.5bb (250). With the pot at 400 and needing 150 to call, I am getting very good pot odds (2.67:1) to defend. FiveTwo has been very active from the button in recent hands, suggesting a wide opening range. While Ace-Six isn't a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend against a button steal, even out of position. I will call to see a flop and keep the pot manageable as we approach the end of the tournament.

On a very dry board of 2c-9d-3s, I have Ace-high with a six kicker. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has some showdown value against the Button's wide range, but it is not strong enough to lead out. I will check and see how FiveTwo reacts.

Heads-up in position after we opened the Button and the BB defended. This 2-9-3 rainbow board is relatively dry; BB will have some 9x and small pairs, but also a lot of unpaired overcards that will fold to pressure. With 55 we have modest showdown value and we benefit a lot from denying equity (overcards like A/K/Q/J) while also getting thin value from worse pocket pairs that may continue once. A small continuation bet keeps the pot controlled, folds out the hands that beat us on later streets via overcards, and avoids risking much versus a check-raise.

On this dry flop of 2c-9d-3s, my Ace-high is often the best hand against a Button opening range. FiveTwo has bet 200 into a 550 pot, which is a small continuation bet. Folding would be too weak given my overcards and the pot odds (getting 3.75:1). Raising isn't necessary as I want to keep their bluffs in and reach showdown or improve on the turn. I will call the 200 chips and see what the turn brings.

On the turn card 4h, the board remains very dry (2c-9d-3s-4h). I currently hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). As the out-of-position player who called pre-flop and on the flop, I will continue with the standard play of checking to the aggressor. My hand still has significant showdown value against FiveTwo's wide button opening range, and checking allows me to see how they value the turn. If they check back, I am happy to take a free river card and potentially see a cheap showdown. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on their sizing, but for now, checking is the most disciplined action.

Turn is a low card that interacts with BB’s defending range (4x/5x/6x), so we don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal pair and risk facing a credible check-raise. That said, 55 still has decent showdown value versus floats/underpairs and we picked up a double-gutshot (A or 6), so a small, controlled barrel is good: it denies equity to overcards, can get thin value from 2x/3x/4x and stubborn underpairs, and keeps our risk low. If Flash check-raises, we can comfortably fold given the strong value density on this texture.

On the turn, the 4h is dealt, which gives me a gutshot straight draw to go along with my Ace-high. FiveTwo has bet 325 into a pot of 950, which is approximately one-third of the pot. This small sizing suggests a wide range, potentially including many hands that I am still beating or have significant equity against. My pot odds are roughly 3.9:1, meaning I only need about 20% equity to make this call profitable. Given that I have 4 outs to a straight, 3 outs to top pair (though an Ace might not always be good, it often is against a button range), and existing showdown value against total air, calling is the most appropriate action. I will check-call and see the river.

On the river, the board is 2c-9d-3s-4h-Js. My hand is Ace-high (As6c). The Jack on the river is a significant card as it completes some of the broadway draws FiveTwo might have opened from the button (like AJ, KJ, QJ). Additionally, the turn 4h completed straight draws for 5-6 and A-5, both of which are in a button's range. Having called two streets with Ace-high and a gutshot, the river has not improved my hand. Since I am out of position and have some marginal showdown value against missed high-card bluffs, checking is the most appropriate play. I will check and hope for a free showdown or evaluate if FiveTwo decides to bet again.

River checks to us after BB has called two small barrels on 2-9-3-4. That line is heavily weighted toward made hands that beat 55 (most 9x, 66-88, occasional slowplayed sets) plus some turned straights (A5/56). The J river doesn’t improve our hand and is a card that can also give BB additional top-pair holdings (J9/J4/J3 type hands) that will never fold. If we bet thinly, most worse hands (A-high floats) likely fold and we mostly get called/raised by better, while our hand has clear showdown value vs 2x/3x/4x and missed overcards. Best is to take the free showdown and avoid turning a marginal pair into a thin value/bluff spot.

4-handed from UTG we can open fairly wide to pick up the dead blinds/antes (150 in the pot) and leverage our big stack. Q4o is weak and dominated when called, so I’m not looking to play big pots; the plan is a small steal open and to comfortably fold versus any significant 3-bet or heavy pressure postflop on bad textures. A standard 2.5x keeps risk controlled while applying fold equity to the blinds.

I hold 4c 7h on the Button, which is a statistically very weak hand (7-4 offsuit). The player in UTG (FiveTwo), who has a large stack, has opened with a raise to 250. Given the strength of an UTG range and the poor playability of my hand, calling or 3-betting would be a losing play. I have a healthy stack of 72.5 BB and should wait for a better spot, especially with only 6 hands remaining in the tournament session.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand, especially in a 4-handed game. FiveTwo opened from UTG to 250. I am in the Small Blind. Calling is an option, but playing AKo out of position in a multi-way pot is sub-optimal. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take initiative, define FiveTwo's range, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. This sizing (4x the open) is standard when 3-betting out of position.
Holding 36o - one of the worst hands in poker. Facing a 3-bet to 1000 from the SB after UTG open. This is a trivial fold - no equity, no playability, out of position against a 3-bet. Easy fold.

Flash’s small blind 3-bet versus our UTG open (with BB already folded) is typically a fairly strong/value-heavy range plus some suited bluff candidates. Q4o is dominated by the hands that continue and performs poorly even with position—too many flops leave us with weak pairs or dominated top pair, and we can’t profitably defend often enough at this depth without better blockers/connectivity. With only 6 hands left, preserving stack and avoiding negative-EV spots matters more than fighting for this pot. We opened light to steal; facing real resistance, we execute the plan and fold.

I am holding Nine-Four offsuit (9h 4s) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being in the worst position with a trash hand and the shortest stack at the table (72.5 BB), there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. The blinds are small relative to my stack, so I am not under immediate pressure to make a move. The correct play is to fold immediately and wait for a better opportunity in the remaining 5 hands.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. With only 6 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack of 15,025 chips, there is no strategic reason to attempt a steal or enter the pot with this hand. Both Claude and FiveTwo in the blinds have significant stacks and have shown a willingness to play back at raises. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

Heads-up against the BB with T5o. This is a marginal hand but completing for 50 is cheap. Out of position makes raising less attractive, and folding seems too tight when I'm getting good odds. Will complete and play postflop cautiously.

Claude has limped the small blind and we’re heads-up with a reasonably connected hand (9-7) that plays fine against a wide limp range. Checking is acceptable, but raising is higher EV here because (1) we often win the pot immediately versus folds, (2) we deny equity to a bunch of random overcards/weak holdings, and (3) we take initiative for postflop c-bets. I’ll use a small iso size to keep risk controlled while still pressuring the limp range OOP.

T5o is a weak holding, especially out of position against FiveTwo's BB raise to 300. I only completed initially for 50, and now facing a raise of 200 more. With poor hand playability OOP and FiveTwo being an active player, this is a clear fold. No need to invest more with such a weak hand.

I am in the UTG position in a 4-handed game holding 9c 3d, which is a very weak hand. Given the stack sizes and the stage of the tournament (Hand 146 of 150), there is no reason to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand. Folding is the standard and most prudent play to conserve my stack.

6c2d is one of the weakest hands in poker - no connectivity, no high cards, offsuit. Even on the button with just blinds to act, this hand isn't worth stealing with. FiveTwo has shown aggression throughout and may 3-bet light. Folding is the disciplined play here.

Folds to us in the small blind, so this is a prime spot to attack the big blind’s range. Ah9h is comfortably ahead of a typical BB defending range, has strong playability postflop (top-pair potential + nut-flush possibilities), and benefits from taking initiative. Pro’s stack is smaller and they’ve shown a generally straightforward, fold-prone profile, so a standard small-blind open should show immediate profit while keeping our risk controlled. I’ll use a 2.5x open to apply pressure without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad4d, facing a raise to 250 from the Small Blind (FiveTwo). In a Blind vs Blind scenario, the Small Blind's opening range is typically very wide. A suited Ace is a premium hand to defend with, possessing excellent equity against a wide range and good playability in position post-flop. The pot odds are favorable; I need to call 150 to play for a pot that will be 500, requiring only 30% equity, which A4s easily exceeds. While a 3-bet is a viable aggressive option, calling is the standard, lower-variance line that keeps the opponent's range wide and utilizes my positional advantage.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser, this K-K-4 flop strongly favors my perceived range and puts a lot of Pro’s BB-defend holdings (random overcards, small pairs without a K, weak 4x) in a tough spot versus a continuation bet. Even though I don’t have a king, Ah9h gives me a high-equity nut-flush draw, so betting accomplishes two things: (1) immediate fold equity on a board that’s hard for him to continue without Kx, and (2) building a pot for when I improve. I’ll use a small sizing to keep his range wide, deny free equity, and set up profitable barrels on hearts, aces, and some high cards.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, facing a small c-bet (175 into 500) on a K-K-4 board, my hand (Pair of Fours, Ace kicker) is a strong bluff-catcher and has good showdown value. The board is paired, making it less likely the opponent has a King compared to their wide opening range, though it is certainly possible. My hand beats all air, missed high cards, and draws. Raising would likely only get action from better hands (Trips, better pairs) and fold out the bluffs I am beating. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn in position. The price is excellent (requiring ~20% equity), and my hand is far too strong to fold.

Pro checked to me on a K-K-4-Q runout after calling a small flop c-bet. This turn is a good barrel card for my perceived range (I have a lot of Kx/strong Qx after opening SB), and it puts maximum pressure on the parts of Pro’s range that called once (small/mid pairs, some 4x, occasional floats) while still allowing Kx to continue. I also have strong equity when called with the nut-flush draw (Ah9h), so betting both leverages fold equity now and builds a pot for when a heart hits. I’ll use a medium sizing that pressures without overcommitting against trips/full houses.

I am in the Big Blind facing a turn bet from the Small Blind on a Kh-4h-Kd-Qd board. I hold Ad4d, giving me two pair (Kings and Fours) with an Ace kicker, which is currently a bluff-catcher, but I have significantly improved to the Nut Flush Draw. The opponent bets 500 into 850, offering me pot odds of roughly 27%. Even if I am behind a King or a Queen, I have substantial outs: 9 diamonds for the nut flush, 3 Aces to make a higher two pair (beating a Queen), and 2 Fours for a full house. Against a Queen (Two Pair), I have ~30% equity. Against a King (Trips), I have ~20-25% equity. Since the opponent's range also includes semi-bluffs and pure air which I currently beat, my total equity is well above the required 27%. Raising would likely isolate me against superior hands, while calling keeps the opponent's bluffing range wide and allows me to realize my strong equity.

River bricks our nut-flush draw and we have no showdown value (Ah9h on K-K-4-Q-3). Given our line (SB open, small c-bet, turn barrel on the Q) we credibly represent a king (Kx) or boats (QQ/4x/KQ), while Pro’s check-call/check-call range still contains a meaningful chunk of bluff-catchers like Qx and stubborn pocket pairs. Pro has also shown a generally straightforward, fold-prone profile, so converting to a final-barrel bluff is higher EV than checking and almost certainly losing at showdown. I’ll use a solid, non-all-in sizing (~70% pot) to apply real pressure to Qx/pairs without needing an unrealistic fold rate like an overbet shove would.

I hold Ad4d on a board of Kh-4h-Kd-Qd-3s. I have Two Pair (Kings and Fours) with an Ace kicker. Facing a triple barrel from the chip leader in the Small Blind. 

1.  **Relative Hand Strength**: On this double-paired board (Kings), my pair of Fours is counterfeit by the Queen. I now lose to any Queen (which makes Kings and Queens) and any King (Trips). I only beat pure bluffs.
2.  **Opponent Range**: The opponent's line (Raise Pre, Bet Flop, Bet Turn, Bet River) is strong.
