The Queen on the turn improves many of their semi-bluffs or broadway floats to a hand that beats mine. The large river bet (1300 into 1850) is polarizing, representing a King or better, or a missed draw.
3.  **Blockers**: Crucially, holding the Ace of Diamonds [Ad] blocks the nut flush draw (e.g., AdJd, AdTd), which is a major part of the opponent's bluffing range. Blocking their bluffs reduces the likelihood they are empty.
4.  **Tournament Situation**: I am the short stack with 4 players left. Preserving my remaining stack (approx 5000 chips if I fold vs 3700 if I call and lose) is vital for fold equity in upcoming hands. The risk/reward of bluff-catching here with bad blockers is poor.

AQs is a premium hand, especially in a 4-handed game. UTG position requires some caution, but AhQh is strong enough to open-raise. I'll raise to 3x BB (300) to build the pot while still getting value from worse hands that call.

Claude’s 3x UTG open (4-handed) is still a reasonably strong range, and K2o is heavily dominated by his Kx/Ax/broadways. Flatting puts us in tough postflop spots (reverse implied odds) and also risks a squeeze from the blinds, especially with Flash covering the BB. A light 3-bet would be high-variance and not necessary with our chip lead this late. Best EV here is to pass and preserve stack for better steal/3-bet spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Sixes (6c 6s) facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from the UTG player (Claude). While my stack of ~63BB allows for set-mining mathematically (implied odds are present), playing a small pair out of position against an Under-The-Gun opening range is generally a losing proposition in the long run. The UTG range is typically strong (88+, ATs+, KJs+), often dominating my pair or leaving me flipping against overcards. Additionally, calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze, which would force a fold. Given the 'maximize stack size' objective and being essentially at the end of the tournament (Hand 147/150), preserving chips by avoiding marginal, out-of-position spots is superior to gambling on hitting a set (~12% chance) and trying to extract value OOP.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd 7c. Claude has raised to 3x (300) from UTG in a 4-handed game. Although the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position. With only a few hands left in the tournament, I want to avoid losing chips in marginal spots where I am likely to be dominated or forced to fold post-flop without improvement. I will fold and preserve my stack for the remaining hands.

4-handed UTG plays wider than full ring, but 7♣3♥ offsuit is still at the very bottom: poor playability, dominated when it makes a pair, and not worth opening or limping into the blinds with three players left to act. With a big stack, the best way to maximize EV is to avoid unnecessary low-equity, multiway spots and wait for better steal candidates.

I am on the Button with 4s 3h, which is a very weak unsuited hand. UTG has folded. While being on the button allows for a wider opening range, 43o is at the very bottom of the range and generally not profitable to open, especially with active opponents in the blinds (Flash has been 3-betting recently). With only 3 hands remaining in the tournament and a stack of roughly 63 big blinds, preserving chips by folding trash hands is the optimal strategy to maximize final stack size.

I am in the Small Blind with 4h-2c, one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given that there are only three hands remaining in the tournament and the Big Blind has a substantial stack, it is not worth the risk to attempt a bluff or play out of position with such low equity. I will fold to preserve my stack.

It is Hand 149 of 150. I am playing 4-handed from UTG (effectively the Cutoff) with King-Nine suited (K9s). My stack is 6275 (approx 63 BBs), which is the shortest at the table. While K9s is often a standard open from the Cutoff in 4-max play, there are strategic reasons to fold here. Firstly, being the short stack against three significantly larger stacks (Flash, Claude, FiveTwo) exposes me to ICM pressure or aggressive 3-betting which would force a fold. Secondly, K9s can be dominated by stronger Kings (KQ, KJ, KT) that opponents might call with. Thirdly, with only one hand remaining after this, preserving my stack to play the Button in Hand 150 allows me to use position to my advantage with a wider range, rather than playing out of position or first-in from UTG now with a marginal holding. Therefore, minimizing variance and waiting for the positional advantage next hand is the prudent play.

I am on the button with Hand 149 of 150. With only two hands left in the tournament, I want to maintain or increase my stack. Queen-Eight suited is a solid opening hand from the button in a four-handed game. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early.

7s4s is a weak suited connector in the small blind facing a button raise. While it has some playability, I'd be out of position post-flop against the raiser. With 16500 chips and only 2 hands left, no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands. Fold.

Flash is opening to a full 3x from the button and we’re out of position with 5♠3♦ offsuit. Despite getting decent immediate odds (200 to win 650 after calling), this hand has very poor playability, realizes equity badly OOP, and will often make dominated weak pairs with no strong draws. It’s also not a good candidate to 3-bet bluff (no blockers to strong continues, and terrible when called). Best EV is to let it go and preserve chips for a better defend/3-bet spot.

As this is the final hand of the tournament (Hand #150), my objective is to maximize my final stack. Holding pocket fours (44) in a 4-handed game is a standard opening hand from the UTG position. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing to pick up the blinds or to define the hand against the remaining three players. Given the stack sizes, I am comfortably in third place and a small raise allows me to play for the pot without overcommitting my stack unless I hit a set on the flop.

Last hand of the tournament with pocket tens on the button - a premium hand in great position. Flash opened to 300. TT is too strong to flat here; I should 3-bet for value and to isolate. Standard 3-bet sizing to around 900.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet. Q4o has very poor playability, is frequently dominated (Qx and better), and we’ll be out of position against at least one strong range with a short-stacked BB still to act (increasing squeeze/jam and multiway complications). The pot odds aren’t good enough to justify continuing with such a weak offsuit hand, and turning it into a 4-bet bluff is high risk with low blocker value and bad realization. Best EV is to cut losses and fold.

This is the final hand (#150) of the tournament. I hold 7s 3s in the Big Blind. The action facing me is significant: an UTG open to 300 followed by a Button 3-bet to 900. My hand is extremely weak relative to the ranges of an UTG opener and a Button 3-bettor. Even though I need to maximize stack size, the Expected Value (EV) of playing 73s suited in a 3-bet pot out of position is heavily negative compared to preserving my current stack of roughly 6,075 chips. I cannot realistically improve my tournament standing (4th place) with just this hand given the large gap to the next stack (14k+), so preserving the current stack is the optimal play to maximize ending chips compared to a likely loss.

On the final hand of the tournament, I am holding pocket fours (44) in the UTG position. I opened for a standard raise of 300, and Claude responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the button. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (600 more to win a pot of 1350), I would be playing out of position for the remainder of the hand. In a four-handed game, a 3-bet from the button over an UTG open typically indicates a strong range. Given that my goal is to maximize my final stack size, calling with a small pair that misses the flop approximately 88% of the time is mathematically -EV, especially since I will likely be forced to fold to any continuation bet on most board textures. Folding allows me to preserve 14,425 chips, securing my current standing, whereas calling and likely folding the flop would leave me with a significantly smaller stack. Therefore, the most professional and stack-maximizing play here is to fold.
